description: Extension of concepts developed in Biochemistry I to illustrate biochemical pathways including DNA replication, transcription, microtubule assembly/function, muscle excitation-contraction, G protein signaling, neuronal signaling, and others. Part I (nucleic acid biochemistry), Part II (cellular biochemistry) and Part III (signal transduction) are integrated in order to emphasize concepts in macromolecular assembly, regulation of enzymes, the dynamic nature of gene structure/expression, and the relationship between dysfunctional biochemical pathways and disease state. Prerequisite: -419. Spring.

title: Biochemistry II
description: Extension of concepts developed in Biochemistry I to illustrate biochemical pathways including DNA replication, transcription, microtubule assembly/function, muscle excitation-contraction, G protein signaling, neuronal signaling, and others. Part I (nucleic acid biochemistry), Part II (cellular biochemistry) and Part III (signal transduction) are integrated in order to emphasize concepts in macromolecular assembly, regulation of enzymes, the dynamic nature of gene structure/expression, and the relationship between dysfunctional biochemical pathways and disease state. Prerequisite: -419.

title: Solution Kinetics
description: Currently interesting aspects of kinetics and mechanisms of chemical reactions in liquid solutions. Techniques, methods, and theories that concern those reactions. The course will cover organic, inorganic, and biochemical reactions. Specific topics covered will vary from year to year. Prerequisite: Physical Chemistry. Spring.

title: Advance Topics in Nano-Chemistry
description: This course provides fundamental understanding of technologically important nanomaterials by specifically focusing on their synthesis, properties, and applications in nanobiotechnology. The course is aimed to provide fundamental backgrounds in order to promote better understanding of nanotechnology and nanobiotechnology. This course is suitable for graduate students in chemistry as well as physics and biology. Although no prerequisite is required, students should be familiar with basic concepts covered in the areas of general chemistry, physical chemistry, and general physics. The course contents include, but not limited to key concepts in solid state physics, nanomaterial synthesis, nanomaterialsâ properties, and nanomaterialsâ applications. Specifically, this course will focus on technologically important materials such as quantum dots, carbon nanotubes, semiconductor nanowires, conductive nanorods, nanoscale polymers, and nanosized biomaterials. Fundamental properties of these nanomaterials will be emphasized. The course will also cover the details of key measurement techniques that are commonly used to characterize these 0D and 1D materials. This course will also look into current and future applications of these materials in biology and medicine by covering the operating principles of these promising techniques in nanobiotechnology.

title: Advanced Topics in Inorganic Chemistry
description: Aspects of the structure, synthesis, and chemical and physical properties of inorganic compounds and related materials. Techniques, methods, and theories related to inorganic chemistry. Currently open problems and recent developments of broad importance are presented. Specific topics covered will vary from year to year. Prerequisites: Physical Chemistry and CHEM-366 or equivalent.

title: Advanced Topics in Organic Chemistry
description: Different topics of current organic chemistry, varying with instructor. Includes Heterocyclic Compounds (synthesis, reactions, mechanisms of aromatic and reduced heterocyclic nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur compounds) and Organic Free Radicals & Photochemistry (basic principles and techniques of photochemistry, especially as applied to elucidation of mechanisms of photoreactions or organic molecules). Prerequisites: Organic and Physical Chemistry.

title: Advanced Topics in Organic Chemistry: Organic Materials
description: Different topics of current organic chemistry, varying with instructor. Includes Heterocyclic Compounds (synthesis, reactions, mechanisms of aromatic and reduced heterocyclic nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur compounds) and Organic Free Radicals & Photochemistry (basic principles and techniques of photochemistry, especially as applied to elucidation of mechanisms of photoreactions or organic molecules). Prerequisites: Organic and Physical Chemistry.

title: Advanced Topics in Biochemistry
description: Amino acids, nucleic acids, carbohydrates, lipids, and biomembranes. Emphasis on rational drug design and biomolecular engineering. Topics will include incorporation of synthetic amino acids, catalytic antibodies, catalytic properties of rRNA, biosynthesis and mechanisms of action of selected natural products, functional models of biopolymers, drug delivery. Prerequisites: Organic Chemistry or equivalent and Biochemistry or permission of instructor. Spring.

title: Computational Methods for Biological Macromolecules
description: Computational methods for proteins, nucleic acids, and lipids. Energy minimization and molecular dynamics simulations for structure/function studies and for NMR/X-ray structure determination. Bioinformatic analysis including sequence and phylogenetic analysis. Quantum chemistry calculations of biochemical reactions. While an introduction to the theories of the methods will be given, concentration will be on applications. Prerequisites: physical chemistry and biochemistry or permission of instructor. Fall.

title: Photochemistry
description: An introduction to the theory and practice of organic photochemistry and free radicals. Unimolecular and intermolecular dynamic processes of electronically excited states, intermediates (including radicals, radical ions, excimers, and exciplexes), energy transfer mechanisms, chemiluminesecence, and photochemical equipment (including fluorimeters, time-correlated single photo counting systems, lasers, filters, and fast-time detection systems) will be discussed. Kinetic aspects of photochemistry will be emphasized.

title: Introductory Seminar
description: Discussion of current departmental research by the faculty members of the Department, plus introductory participation in the program described under CHEM-703, 704.

title: Introductory Seminar
description: Discussion of current departmental research by the faculty members of the Department, plus introductory participation in the program described under CHEM-703.

title: Introductory Seminar
description: Discussion of current departmental research by the faculty members of the Department, plus introductory participation in the program described under CHEM-703, 704.

title: Introductory Seminar
description: Discussion of current departmental research by the faculty members of the Department, plus introductory participation in the program described under CHEM-704.

title: Advanced Seminar
description: Students will be required to conduct intensive literature investigations on approved topics. Presentation of these topics in a formal manner to the seminar group. Lectures by faculty and visiting authorities.

title: Advanced Seminar
description: Students will be required to conduct intensive literature investigations on approved topics. Presentation of these topics in a formal manner to the seminar group. Lectures by faculty and visiting authorities.

title: Advanced Seminar
description: Students will be required to conduct intensive literature investigations on approved topics. Presentation of these topics in a formal manner to the seminar group. Lectures by faculty and visiting authorities.

title: Advanced Seminar
description: Students will be required to conduct intensive literature investigations on approved topics. Presentation of these topics in a formal manner to the seminar group. Lectures by faculty and visiting authorities.

title: Thechniques in Chemical Education
description: This course is designed to familiarize the student with the department expectations and resources in regard to teaching duties and to provide opportunities to work on the "soft skills" needed to make the student more successful in his or her role as a teaching assistant.

title: Introduction to Chemistry Education
description: An experiential course providing practical application of education principles to labs and recitations.

title: Introduction to Graduate Research
description: An introduction to the graduate research programs and opportunities offered by the department faculty.

title: Chemistry Research
description: Graduate research. Choose the section taught by the faculty supervising the research.

title: Thesis Research--Chemistry
description: Thesis research in chemistry.

title: Thesis Research--Chemistry
description: Continuing registration for chemistry graduate students who have completed course work.

title: Thesis Research--Chemistry
description: Continuing registration for chemistry students finishing course work.

title: Intensive First Level Chinese I
description: For students with no background in Chinese. An introduction to Mandarin Chinese emphasizing pronunciation and grammatical patterns. Introduction to Chinese characters.

title: Intensive First Level Chinese II
description: Continuation of -011.

title: Intensive First Level Chinese for Advanced Beginners
description: This accelerated 6-credit course is designed for students of Chinese heritage and advanced beginners with good speaking and listening skills. It covers in one semester the materials equivalent to that of one academic year's Intensive First Level Chinese (011 and 012). The focus is on reading, writing, and grammar, along with continuing improvement of oral communication skills. The purpose of instruction is to utilize previous language background to lay a solid foundation for further Chinese language study (second level). Placement test and instructor's permission are required. Satisfactory completion of this course fulfills the prerequisite for Intensive Second Level Chinese (-111 or -114). Fall.

title: Intensive First Level Chinese for Advanced Beginners
description: This accelerated 6-credit course is designed for students of Chinese heritage and advanced beginners with good speaking and listening skills. It covers in one semester the materials equivalent to that of one academic year's Intensive First Level Chinese (011 and 012). The focus is on reading, writing, and grammar, along with continuing improvement of oral communication skills. The purpose of instruction is to utilize previous language background to lay a solid foundation for further Chinese language study (second level). Placement test and instructor's permission are required. Satisfactory completion of this course fulfills the prerequisite for Intensive Second Level Chinese (-111 or -114). Fall.

title: East Asia: Texts and Contexts
description: An examination of the culture, literature, and art of East Asia through intensive analysis and discussion of primary texts in translation, critical articles, and visual materials. Priority given to Chinese and Japanese majors. Required of majors in their first or second year. Writing intensive; satisfies ENGL-022. Conducted in English. Spring.

title: East Asia: Texts and Contexts
description: An examination of the culture, literature, and art of East Asia through intensive analysis and discussion of primary texts in translation, critical articles, and visual materials. Priority given to Chinese and Japanese majors. Required of majors in their first or second year. Writing intensive; satisfies ENGL-022. Conducted in English. Spring.

title: Intensive Second Level Chinese I
description: Continuation of -012. Interactive student participation in classroom discussions. Readings and dialogues on practical topics and political and social issues. Continued practice in traditional and simplified characters.

title: Intensive Second Level Chinese II
description: Continuation of -111. Interactive student participation in classroom discussions. Readings and dialogues on practical topics and political and social issues. Continued practice in traditional and simplified characters.

title: Intensive Second Level Chinese for Advanced Beginners
description: This accelerated course is designed for students of Chinese heritage and advanced beginners with good speaking and listening skills. It covers in one semester the materials equivalent to that of one academic year's Intensive Second Level Chinese (111 and 112). The focus is on reading, writing, and grammar, along with continuing improvement of oral communication skills. The purpose of instruction is to consolidate the foundation which students have built in their first level Chinese courses, to expand their vocabulary, and to introduce them to more complex grammatical structures. Placement test and instructor's permission are required. Satisfactory completion of this course fulfills the prerequisite for Third Level Chinese I (211). Note: Students are expected to enroll in both -013 and -114 during the same academic year. Spring.

title: Intensive Second Level Chinese for Advanced Beginners
description: This accelerated course is designed for students of Chinese heritage and advanced beginners with good speaking and listening skills. It covers in one semester the materials equivalent to that of one academic year's Intensive Second Level Chinese (111 and 112). The focus is on reading, writing, and grammar, along with continuing improvement of oral communication skills. The purpose of instruction is to consolidate the foundation which students have built in their first level Chinese courses, to expand their vocabulary, and to introduce them to more complex grammatical structures. Placement test and instructor's permission are required. Satisfactory completion of this course fulfills the prerequisite for Third Level Chinese I (211). Note: Students are expected to enroll in both -013 and -114 during the same academic year. Spring.

title: Third Level Chinese I
description: Continuation of 112. This course offers continued training in listening, speaking, reading, and writing in Chinese at an advanced level. Its aim is to guide students through the difficult transition from colloquial Chinese to literary Chinese, and to help students express their ideas about cultural and social matters in Chinese.

title: Third Level Chinese II
description: Continuation of 211. This course offers continued training in listening, speaking, reading, and writing in Chinese at an advanced level. Its aim is to guide students through the difficult transition from colloquial Chinese to literary Chinese, and to help students express their ideas about cultural and social matters in Chinese.

title: Chinese Composition and Style
description: A study of advanced rhetorical skills through reading and composition exercises in various modern Chinese writing styles. Readings include essays of well-known Chinese writers such as Hu Shi, Zhu Ziqing, Bing Xin, Ai Wu, and Wang Wenxing. Students are required to keep a weekly journal of 800 Chinese characters, in addition to regular homework assignments. For non-native speakers of Chinese only. Prerequisite: 212 or above.

title: Advanced Oral Communication
description: This course provides students with selected techniques of oral expression to develop greater fluency and accuracy in spoken Chinese. Conversational topics are based on contemporary issues in China.

title: Introduction to Classical Chinese
description: Classical Chinese is the language of the bulk of the Chinese textual tradition from early historical and philosophical writings down to the early twentieth century. This course introduces the basic structures and vocabulary of that language, which still has a large influence on the formal written prose of modern newspapers and documents. Prerequisite: -212 or permission of instructor.

title: Business Chinese
description: Development of advanced skills in reading materials related to Chinese business. Prerequisite: -212 or equivalent.

title: Business Chinese II
description: Development of advanced skills in reading materials related to Chinese business. Prerequisite: -321 or equivalent.

title: Advanced Readings in Chinese
description: Using an upper-level Chinese textbook (Chih-p'ing Chou, et al., China's Peril and Promise: An Advanced Reader), this course covers a variety of modern Chinese literary genres including essays, short stories, biographies, and criticisms. Emphasis on reading comprehension and expansion of vocabulary. Class discussions are on some substantive issues related to the readings. (This course does not fulfill the humanities and writing General Education requirements.)

title: Topics in Current Affairs
description: Readings of current events from Chinese newspapers and periodicals published in mainland China, Taiwan, and the U.S. Emphasis on the development of reading ability of newspaper articles on different subjects and the analysis of sentence structures. Not open to native Chinese speakers. (This course does not fulfill the humanities and writing General Education requirements.)

title: Images of Women in Contemporary Chinese Films
description: This course explores the images of Chinese women constructed by Chinese art cinema, mainly since 1980s. For historical background, films will also include two from the revolutionary era and one silent movie of the 1930s entitled "New Woman." This course will examine stereotypes and male fantasies in the films directed by well-known male directors such as Zhang Yimou and Chen Kaige, and will also examine films produced by women directors which take up the theme of women's experience and struggle. Conducted in English.

title: Reading Chinese Landscapes
description: Starting from the basic observation that without humans there is no landscape but only land, this course examines how landscapes have been constructed, represented, interpreted, and altered in China, from early to modern times. Course materials will include literature (landscape writing in poetry and prose), visual arts (painting, gardens, and cityscapes), and recent writings that reexamining Chinese culture's complex relationship with landscape. Course taught in English.

title: Legal Chinese
description: This course is a fourth-level Chinese language course, with the focus on Chinese law and legal aspects of contemporary Chinese society. It will examine the changing legal system and its impact on Chinese culture and society. The readings will include selected Chinese legal documents, newspaper articles, online discussions, as well as audio-video material.

title: The Tradition of Chinese Fiction
description: A reading and discussion class examining major themes in the Chinese narrative tradition from the late Zhou to the Qing dynasty, with particular emphasis on classical tales from the Tang period. All readings are in Classical or late imperial vernacular Chinese, with glosses.

title: Senior Seminar
description: Each year the senior seminar will examine critical approaches to literary, historical, and cultural studies in the context of a specific topic in Chinese culture. Utilizing the approaches discussed, students will undertake individual research papers on topics of their own choosing, mastering fundamental research skills for Chinese studies along the way. Required for all majors.

title: Survey of Chinese Literary Genres
description: This course offers an introductory overview of some of the most influential and cherished masterpieces of the rich Chinese literary tradition, covering a wide range of works from the Zhou dynasty to the Qing dynasty. Works studied include selections from the ancient Book of Songs, the teachings of Confucius and Laozi, the poetry of the Tang and Song dynasties, and the novels Journey to the West (Xiyou ji) and Dream of the Red Chamber (Honglou meng). An essential course for students of Chinese language and culture, it aims to enhance students' appreciation and knowledge of Chinese philosophy and literature while emphasizing the development of advanced oral skills to communicate abstract concepts. Readings in modern vernacular Chinese are supplemented with the original classical Chinese texts; no knowledge of classical Chinese is required. Conducted in Chinese. (This course does not fulfill the humanities and writing General Education requirements.)

title: Modern Chinese Drama
description: Modern Chinese drama--"Huaju" (word drama)--features realistic spoken dialogue rather than the sung poetic dialogue of the traditional Chinese dramatic forms. It resembles Western-style stage drama and often contains social commentary. In addition to readings on the history of modern Chinese drama, we will spend most of the time reading three representative modern plays and watching videotaped performances of them: Thunderstorm (Cao Yu's script, 1936), The Teahouse (Lao She's script, 1957), and Bird Men (Guo Shixing's script, 1997). We will pay close attention to cultural meanings and to the use of language, particularly Beijing dialect, in both the written scripts and the stage performances. Conducted in Chinese. (This course does not fulfill the humanities and writing General Education requirements.)

title: Readings in Chinese Humanities
description: The course introduces students to a variety of writings by contemporary Chinese scholars and public intellectuals on Chinese literature, history, thought, the arts, both modern and pre-modern. Writings will be drawn from books, intellectual magazines, the web, and journals, and most will straddle the border between academic and serious popular writing. The course is aimed at students who have completed at least one course in Chinese at the 300-level. Attention will be given to reading comprehension and to discussion of the approaches and ideas of the readings, as well as to presentation and translation skills. We may also employ some English-language writings for comparative purposes or background.

title: Greek Tragedy
description: Students read the tragedies of Aeschylus, Sophocles, and Euripides, the fifth century B. C. Athenian playwrights whose works represent the remarkable birth of a genre that has captured the Western imagination for 2500 years. We consider the plays within the context of the culture saw their first productions: Athenian democracy and empire. We also study the genre of tragedy itself, thinking about what we can learn from the extant plays themselves as well as from Aristotle, in the Poetics, and other theoreticians who have speculated on the origin and meaning of the tragic and the drama meant to enact it. Whatever is profound and provocativeâand indeed, originalâin Shakespeare, Schiller, OâNeill, or Wilson must be set against the foundation of tragedy: the Oresteia of Aeschylus; the Oedipus Tyrannus and Antigone of Sophocles; or the Medea and the Bacchae of Euripides. This is the course for students who want to know that foundation. *Readings in English.*

title: Ancient Greek I
description: An intensive introduction to the ancient Greek language with primary emphasis on the acquisition of reading skills. Drills in grammar and syntax. Programmed reading selections from a variety of ancient authors.

title: Ancient Greek 2
description: A continued intensive introduction to the ancient Greek language with primary emphasis on the acquisition of reading skills. Drills in grammar and syntax. Programmed reading selections from a variety of ancient authors.

title: Intermediate Ancient Greek
description: This course will feature readings from Plato's Apology and review of the fundamentals of Greek morphology and syntax.

title: Latin I
description: An intensive introduction to the Latin language and the culture of the ancient Romans. Readings and composition exercises will focus on the acquisition of solid reading skills. At the same time, the study of Latin will enlarge students' English vocabulary and their understanding of the structures of their own language.

title: Latin 2
description: An intensive introduction to the Latin language and the culture of the ancient Romans. Readings and composition exercises will focus on the acquisition of solid reading skills. At the same time, the study of Latin will enlarge students' English vocabulary and their understanding of the structures of their own language.

title: Intermediate Latin
description: This class, for students with one year of college Latin or equivalent, combines grammar review with continuous reading of what must be Cicero's most amusing speech, the Pro Caelio. While aimed primarily to teach students practical strategies for reading Latin prose, the course also introduces the day-to-day life of late Republican Rome's high society.

title: Intermediate Latin
description: This class, for students with one year of college Latin or equivalent, combines review of Latin grammar with continuous reading of what must be Cicero's most amusing speech, the Pro Caelio. While aimed primarily to teach students practical strategies for reading Latin prose, this course also introduces the day-to-day life of late Republican Rome's high society.

title: Medieval Latin
description: A survey of how the Latin language was put to use at different periods during the middle ages and what those uses reveal about different periods of medieval culture. Some authors to be read include Bede, Gregory of Tours, Einhard, Alcuin, Thomas Aquinas, Abelard and Petrarch.

title: Petronius
description: In this course, we will read substantial portions of Petroniusâ Satyricon, which recounts the exploits of the lovers Encolpius and Giton. We will explore such issues as the novelâs relationship to other literary forms, the representation of imperial extravagance and excess, the innovative prosimetric language; we will supplement our investigations with reading from contemporary scholarship.

title: Tacitus
description: Students will read selections from the Annales, paying attention to Tacitus' deployment of rhetorical devices and distinctive literary style, as well as to his 'annalistic' or year-by-year organization of his history, and to his peculiar form of moral and political criticism. In addition to readings in Latin, the course also will include discussion of modern scholarly approaches to the Annales.

title: Seneca
description: An introduction to the writings of Seneca. This ruthless politician, who served as Neroâs tutor and then ran Rome in the early years of the emperorâs reign, also wrote a series of popular philosophic works that criticize the court society in which he was so enmeshed. By reading from across Senecaâs prose, students will examine how this provincial, born in southern Spain, fashioned a key role for himself in the Rome of the early Caesars and for posterity. We will also read one of his verse tragedies, and investigate what they contribute to Senecaâs literary program. Throughout the course attention will be given to the relationship between literary style and content in the Senecan corpus, and also on Senecaâs role in the larger history of European literature.

title: ADV LATIN READING:CAESAR/LUCAN
description: Both of these works take as their topic the civil war between Caesar and Pompey that led to Caesar's rise to power. We will read Lucan's epic De bello civili in light of Caesar's Commentarii (as well as the Roman epic tradition more generally) and consider, e.g., differences and similarities between poetry and history, the Roman gods, and power at Rome. Strong command of Latin and a willingness to do more than translate are required for this course.

title: Introduction to Communication, Culture and Technology
description: This course introduces students to some of the main approaches and issues that animate the Communication, Culture & Technology program. Students will explore interdisciplinary approaches to solving intellectual problems in communications, cultural, media, and technology studies. Students will also consider issues in the movement ofrom theoretical work to intellectual and interpretive practice. What does an interdisciplinary practice of communication, culture and technology studies look like? How do contemporary media environments create conditions for or against social change? How are distributed production and social media technologies changing how we work, relate, and interact with one another? How have communication practices influenced--and been influenced by--these new forms of interaction and space? What are the issues of power and agency which cut across these social, cultural, and individual media practices? After an opening section suggesting frameworks for interdisciplinary practice, the course is organized into macro and micro case studies under the following overarching themes: * Media Industries, Media Use and Political-Social Change * Social Relationships, New Technologies, and Distributed Production * Subcultures, Work and Media These themesâand the individual case studies grouped beneath themâare not comprehensive. Rather, students will explore vital CCT problems using a consistent set of questions from radically divergent disciplinary, theoretical, and methodological perspectives. In this sense, the students will learn approaches to think through problems, rather than a set body of knowledge, theory, or method.

title: Fundamentals of Technology
description: This course explores the fundamentals of technology through the historical lens of the rise of computing and the Internet. Topics to be covered include information theory, human computer interface, recording and input technologies, computer security, and the world wide web. The Internet has been one of the most rapid bases for transformation in technological history, and we appear to be only in the first stages of observing and understanding these shifts. We will begin to unpack the wide implications of this rapid change through a four-pronged approach: (a) understanding the historical rise of the Internet and information technologies; (b) identifying a broader set of transformations that arise from a globally networked communication infrastructure; (c) familiarity with the content and functioning of these technologies (i.e., how they work, both inside and out); and (d) discussion of particular issues and controversies which have emerged from the Internetâs development (including privacy, access, the digital divide, and debates surrounding open-source). Throughout the course, we shall continually consider the convergence of a broad array of information technologies and how this convergence preserves and/or breaks with previous forms of order information. Classes will be conducted primarily as a lecture, with two in-class exams addressing the content of lectures and assigned readings. Weekly lab sections are also required. A series of hands-on assignments will encourage students to engage both practically and reflexively with the technologies that surround us. The final project will present group research exploring the history, design, and use of a particular technology. Research findings will be collected and mediated through a variety of communication technologies (formal reports, blogs and twitter feeds, an oral and visual presentation, and video).

title: CCT Internship
description: This course is intended for CCT students who want or need to earn credit for an internship. This course is graded on a Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory basis and counts for one credit. This internship course can only be taken once while enrolled in CCT. In order to register: 1. The student is responsible for finding an appropriate internship position. 2. Student must present to the Academic Program Manager an offer letter from the internship organization during the course registration period. The letter must include how many hours per week you will work, general duties you will perform, and the start and end dates of the internship, which need to match the semester dates. 3. If the CCT Director approves the course, CCT will request to register you for the course. Once the semester begins, 1. Bi-weekly short email reports describing your work must be submitted to the Academic Program Manager 2. Submit a a final 5-8 page written report at end the of the semester describing one or more aspects of your internship work and relate them to your CCT studies. 3. Submit a letter from your internship supervisor at the end of the semester describing your overall performance, and confirming the hours worked per week and period of employment.

title: CCT Internship
description: Course is limited to students who have written approval from the CCT Program. This course is intended for CCT students who want or need to earn credit for an internship. This course is graded on a Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory basis and counts for one credit. This internship course can only be taken once while enrolled in CCT. In order to register: 1. The student is responsible for finding an appropriate internship position. 2. Student must present to the Academic Program Manager an offer letter from the internship organization no later than the course registration period. The letter must include how many hours per week you will work, general duties you will perform, and the start and end dates of the internship, which need to match the semester dates. 3. If the CCT Director approves the course, CCT will request to register you for the course. Once the semester begins: 1. Bi-weekly short email reports describing your work must be submitted to the Academic Program Manager. 2. Submit a a final 5-8 page written report at end the of the semester describing one or more aspects of your internship work and relate them to your CCT studies. 3. Submit a letter from your internship supervisor at the end of the semester describing your overall performance, and confirming the hours worked per week and period of employment.

title: Remix Culture
description: This seminar course explores the "remix" as both an analytic framework and a collection of cultural forms. Remixes can be found in music, text, images, education, garnes, art, technology, and wherever something intended for a particular meaning or use is redeployed and reinterpreted within a new set of constraints. Digital technologies have catapulted the idea of remix to a new level of visibility. But the remixing of culture precedes these technologies. This course will examine the notion of the remix through a theoretical opening discussion in which the idea of remix is unpacked, unmixed, and re-assessed as a process of cultural hybridity accelerated and emphasized by new media technologies. We will then explore the remix at work across a spectrum of media forms: art, music/sound, film. The course will end with a series of cultural concerns raised by the prominence of remixing in current society. Issues to be discussed include plagiarism, open-source development, gaming/play, and the "remixing" of history. By the end of the course, you will have practice applying the remix as an analytic paradigm. You will remix your own work and the work of others throughout the course, and you will be able to create your own remix. You will also become familiar with a variety of cultural forms inspired, or made possible, by the remix.

title: Marketing, Advertising and Public Policy
description: This is an inter-disciplinary graduate level course designed to provide students with a solid overview of current themes in marketing, advertising and public policy. The course examines the impact of public policy on commercial marketing and advertising practices in todayâs new media environment. We review the role of government in the regulation and limitation of marketing and advertising, particularly the roles and rulings of the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and Federal Communications Commission (FCC) on contemporary marketing issues. The course also examines the First Amendment as it applies to commercial speech and its evolving interpretation by the U.S. Supreme Court. Finally, the course takes a critical look at the policy implications of several marketing and advertising practices including: marketing to children and youth; marketing of so-called âviceâ products such as alcohol, tobacco and gambling; the use and collection of personal information and consumer data for targeted and social marketing purposes; behavioral targeting and consumer privacy; and embedded advertising, branded content, product placement and advertising transparency in online and offline media. Organization In the first half of the course, we study the basic federal laws that govern the marketing and advertising of commercial products. We review the role and authority of the federal government, including Congress, the courts, the FTC and the FCC. We look at the major court rulings on the First Amendment and commercial speech. We also review issues relating to self-regulation of marketing and advertising. In the second half of the course, we look at the business of commercial marketing and advertising in todayâs multimedia environment. Our examination will include several marketing and advertising campaigns involving selected categories, including consumer goods, food, children and youth products, prescription drugs, beer and alcohol, and emerging trends in online and offline marketing such as product placements, advergaming, sponsored content and social media. This review raises a number of public and social policy issues that will be focused on throughout the course, namely: marketing and advertising ethics; the right of Americans to keep their private data away from marketers; responsible marketing of so-called âsinâ products (such as tobacco, alcohol, and gambling); Internet advertising, and the relationship between marketing, advertising and media. Finally, the course critically examines the effect of marketing and advertising on society. Among the themes we explore: marketing as a reflection or a determinant of popular culture; the proliferation and intrusiveness of commercial messages, and the cultural implications of media images. We finish with an analysis of whether marketers owe a duty of corporate social responsibility to consumers and society, or do they have the right to market anything to anyone, anywhere at anytime, as long as itâs legal? Grading and Requirements The course is divided almost evenly into 50% lecture and 50% seminar format. I also will bring in two or more guest speakers who are involved in the creation, production or regulation of broadcast advertisements. Taken together, the lectures, class discussion and guest speakers will provide students with a sound overview of the major public policy issues involved in commercial marketing and advertising. The final grade will be determined by class participation and a final research paper on a topic to be selected by the student and approved by the professor. Students are expected to attend and participate actively in class. Text and Reading Materials Watch This, Listen Up, Click Here: Inside the 300 Billion Dollar Business Behind the Media You Constantly Consume, David Verklin, Bernice Kanner, John Wiley & Sons, 2007. The Future of Advertising: New Approaches to the Attention Economy, A Report of the Aspen Institute Communications and Society Program, by Richard Adler (Rapporteur), The Aspen Institute, Washington, DC, 1997. The First Amendment and the Mediaâ2003: Free Speech and Free Press Since September 11. Richard T. Kaplar (ed.), The Media Institute, Washington, DC, 2003. Additional course materials will be adapted from periodicals, journals and online sources and made available to students through the library. About the Professor Adonis E. Hoffman, Esq. is Senior Vice President and Counsel for the American Association of Advertising Agencies in Washington, DC and president of the American Business Leadership Institute. He was appointed by the Chairman of the FCC to serve as Deputy Bureau Chief of the Cable (now Media) Bureau and as Senior Counsel for International Telecommunications Policy. He previously served in the U.S. House of Representatives as Counsel for the House Committee on Foreign Affairs. Mr. Hoffman was a Senior Associate at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, a Senior Fellow at the World Policy Institute, and a Distinguished Visitor at the New School University. He is the director of the Center on Responsible Media and Marketing and serves on the First Amendment Advisory Board of The Media Institute and the Board of the Corporate Counsel Institute at Georgetown University Law Center. He is admitted to practice in the District of Columbia, Pennsylvania, the U.S. Court of International Trade and the U.S. Supreme Court. Hoffman received an A.B. from Princeton University and a J.D. from Georgetown University Law Center. Contact Adonis E. Hoffman, Esq. | Senior Vice President & Counsel American Association of Advertising Agencies 1203 19th Street NW, Fourth Floor | Washington, DC 20036 202-331-7345 - office / ahoffman@aaaadc.org 703-627-0400 - cell

title: Looking at Photography
description: Photography is many things: it is a technical process, a documentary tool, a personal memento, an art form, a propaganda medium, a device to generate commerce, or any combination of these elements. It is both an act and the thing created by that act. Its apparent subject matter can be portraiture, still life, landscape, couture fashion, criminal evidence, scientific data, or any combination thereof. Why a photograph was taken and what it means to a viewer can vary wildly depending on its physical condition, its provenance, and the different âgiven circumstancesâ (a term borrowed from the theatre) under which is was taken and under which it is viewed: the who, what, when, where, why, and how of these separate acts are frequently at odds with one another -- when they can be determined at all. Perhaps most importantly, photography is simultaneously ubiquitous and nearly invisible. From its earliest days, now nearly 200 years ago, photography has been thought of as a window on reality. Even the most sophisticated viewers tend to see the thing represented, accepting it as truth, with little awareness of the thing that is the photograph itself. But as the late Richard Avedon put it, âAll photographs are accurate. None of them is the truth.â The âthingnessâ of the photograph and the processes that create it affect our understanding of the thing represented. How the photographer sees affects what the viewer sees. This course will examine photography as a way of seeing and a mode of rhetoric. We will approach the topic historically, but the markers of change over time â the technical innovations, the varying subject matter, and the generations of men and women who have made photographs for innumerable reasons â will not be considered as milestones of progress. Our purpose will not be to gauge the distance that separates us from Niepce and Daguerre and Fox Talbot. Rather, we will explore these historical markers as accretions, like the layers of a pearl, on a core human need to make images of the world.

title: Looking at Photography
description: Photography is many things: it is a technical process, a documentary tool, a personal memento, a form of art, a tool for propaganda, a device to generate commerce, or any combination of these elements. It is both an act and the thing created by that act. Its apparent subject matter can be portraiture, still life, landscape, couture fashion, historical artifact, criminal evidence, scientific data, or any combination thereof. Why photograph was taken and what it means to a viewer can vary wildly depending on its physical condition, its provenance, and the different âgiven circumstancesâ (a term borrowed from the theatre) under which is was taken and under which it is viewed. The who, what, when, where, why, and how of these separate acts are frequently at odds with one another -- when they can be determined at all. Perhaps most importantly, photography is simultaneously ubiquitous and nearly invisible. From its earliest days, now nearly 200 years ago, photography has been thought of as a window on reality. Even the most sophisticated viewers tend to see the thing represented, accepting it as truth, while failing to see the thing that is the photograph itself, let alone the intellectual, artistic, technical, and political process of which it is the end result. But as the late Richard Avedon put it, âAll photographs are accurate. None of them is the truth.â The âthingnessâ of the photograph and the multiple processes that create it affect our understanding of the thing represented. How the photographer sees affects what the viewer sees. This course will examine photography as both a way of seeing and a mode of rhetoric. We will approach the topic historically, but the markers of change over time â the technical innovations, the varying subject matter, and the generations of men and women who have made photographs for innumerable reasons â will not be considered as milestones of progress. Our purpose will not be to gauge the distance that separates us from photography's 19th century pioneers, Niepce, Daguerre, and Talbot â nor, for that matter, from such famous 20th century practitioners as Adams, Weston, or Evans. Rather, we will explore these historical markers as accretions, like the layers of a pearl, on the core human need to make images of the world.

title: Arcade Theory
description: During the first few weeks of 2010, the World Bank launched a Net-based alternative reality game (ARG) dedicated to crowdsourcing novel approaches to global problems; the South Korean Supreme Court effectively decriminalized the flow of currencies between real and virtual worlds; Zynga's Farmville, a popular "casual game," boasted the participation of over 1% of the world's population; and the GDP of Azeroth, a virtual state in the game World of Warcraft, eclipsed that of Hungary. Conventionally, games have always been relegated to special places, sites separate and apart from the everyday world: The casino, the gridiron, the playground, the court, the arena, the diamond, the arcade. But the advent of digital games in a networked environment is quickly eroding the previously sacred boundary between games and the real. Students are learning from historical simulations; the military is recruiting via first person shooters; politicians are campaigning in virtual worlds; newspapers are publishing games as online op-eds. Videogames and digital simulations represent rich sites for interdisciplinary interrogation. In this course, we will consider these technologies from a number of positions. Topics include: * Procedural rhetoric and the polis; * Cognition and simulation; * Games and embodied learning; * Simulated science; * The phenomenology of gaming and the persistence of the body; * Virtual-world governance * Hacking, modding, and digital play * Ubiquitous games and ubiquitous computing * The end of the interface

title: Media and Communication in the Arab World
description: Since September 11, 2001, much public and scholarly attention has been drawn towards the operations of Arab media institutions. This course delves into the increasingly influential realm of international broadcasting as it applies to the Middle Eastern media milieu. By engaging primary issues pertaining to the development, structure and impact of contemporary Arab media institutions and their content, the course allows students to explore the theoretical approaches underlying a burgeoning "industry" in the region. Topics include the political and economic structures of regional media, the history of Arab journalism, identity-construction and representation, operations of government broadcasting, the advent of satellite television and new media technology and their ramifications. The course also problematizes such notions as the "Al-Jazeera Effect" and "the Arab street" as well as issues including reporting styles in the Arab press, legislation and "freedom of the press" policies, religious and doctrinal broadcasting, and entertainment/pop culture in the region. Through the evaluation of exemplar case studies, the future of Arab media in light of new technological innovations and the advent of "alternative notions" of information delivery including mobile phone messaging, blogging and other forms of digital communication.

title: Propaganda â Old Methods and New Tools for âInvisible Rulers"
description: In 1928, when the world was awash with new tools of mass communication â cinema, cheap newspapers, and radio âthe âfather of PR,â Edward Bernays, called propaganda the tool by which the âinvisible rulersâ of society control and direct âthe masses.â Bernays argued that the bad reputation that the word âpropagandaâ had earned was a testament to the power of messaging that plays on empathy, fear, superstition, envy, and all the other emotions that define us as humans. Rather than vilify propaganda, Bernays urged, we should learn how to master it, harnessing it to our own ends. Today our brains and our emotions remain as they were in Bernaysâs day (though behavioral psychology has helped us at least to understand them better) but Twitter, Facebook, YouTube, Wikipedia, and millions of blogs are amplifying the volume, reach, and scope of propaganda light-years beyond anything that Bernays ever dreamed of. Part seminar and part workshop, this course will sketch the birth of modern propaganda in World Wars I and II, and then move on to study the psychological constants of human behavior that appear to make propaganda (whether âwhiteâ, âgray,â or âblackâ) so powerful. At least half the course will be devoted to exploring the ways that the new technologies of communication are changing propaganda, in part by dissecting some successful ânew propagandaâ campaigns, and in by part by having students propose ways that they might use the new tools and old methods of propaganda to achieve some specified end. Course materials will include books, articles, videos, demonstrations of student projects, and presentations by âpropaganda practitioners.â

title: Cultural Economics and Policy
description: This course counts as a core theory and as an IBD elective. Behind every piece of art is a policy â an emperorâs wish, a cityâs museum, a municipalityâs graffiti, or a nationâs film. Sometimes the policies are indirect; Florentine Renaissance gained from the Medici family patronage but also from that of the Church and a general encouragement given to creative enterprise. Social media in our own times, encouraged by all kind of policies, bring us close to everything from Senegalâs hip-hop to South Koreaâs soap operas. Creativity is as much about heavenly imagination and genius as it is about earthly costs, revenue, and policies. This course examines the basic economics underlying creative enterprise, and its links with cultural policy and identity. How do music, film, television, performing arts, and tourism industries organize their production and distribution? What policies encourage or discourage such enterprises? These questions carry special import not just because of the raging debates on cultural identity worldwide, but also because cultural industries now comprise anywhere from 7 to 10 percent of the national income and employment in most countries. The gross world output of cultural industries â alternatively labeled entertainment, copyright, creative industries â is calculated to be above $1.5 trillion. In the United States, it is also the biggest export industry. The ways in which we represent ourselves through our images, songs, and monuments matter both for our economies and our identities. The course is organized into three parts emphasizing valuation, production, and policies. The first part of the course examines the way we value cultural products. These valuation mechanisms can vary from market-based art auction pricings to institutional tastes such as a snooty professorâs or an art criticâs chatter about commercialized Hollywood. Next, we examine how these industries are organized from the stage of creation to production and distribution. How do television programs recover their costs? Why is tourism the biggest industry in the world? Finally, we examine policies that encourage creative products from the local to global levels including subsidies, tax incentives, intellectual property laws, arts education, and heritage conservation and preservation. Most importantly, we link these policies to cultural identities. Why do museums promote national identities, for example? How does the European Union encourage an idea of Europe? How are gender, sexual, racial, and ethnic identities linked to cultural economics? PLAN Class participation and presentations will count for 10 percent of the grade. Each class participant will be the designated leader for one session of the class and offer a five-minute critique of the readings in the second half of the class each week. Students will also write three papers of less than 2000 words each (30 points each). The first paper will evaluate the value of a cultural good or service deploying one of the several mechanisms available, including market pricing, contingent valuation, or institutional valuations. The second paper will analyze an aspect of a cultural industry, and the third will discuss a cultural policy.

title: Critical Studies in Journalism
description: This course combines theoretical perspectives on news with primary source material produced by and about journalists. Students will analyze theoretical material on journalism alongside articles and broadcasts appearing in the media, interviews with journalists, trade articles, and professional reviews about crises and turning points in journalism. Topics include models of journalistic practice, journalistic values and norms, gatekeeping and sourcing practices, storytelling formats in news, challenges to journalism such as ethical problems and celebrity, and changes in the current news environment. Emphasis will be placed on technological adaptation within journalism, and new and rising journalistic enterprises. Students will develop skills in the critical analysis of journalism to recognize existing analytical frames for understanding and to uncover the implicit givens of news-work.

title: Architecture as Visual Narrative: Capital Culture----Cultural Capital
description: Are we what we build? Shelter is among the most basic needs of man, but the art of building is more than the sum of its parts. Architecture incorporates political, economic, social, cultural and technological influences and transforms them into tangible physical form, often outlasting any and all these forces. Architecture is considered a defining barometer of cultureâin other words it is the cultural capital of a civilization. How then does the architecture of the nationâs capital define American culture? Are Washingtonâs buildings billboards? What do Washingtonâs public and private buildings communicate beyond their functionality about who we are? How does the city reflect the changes and transformations of the past two centuries? This course explores the physical transformation of Washington DC over its 220-year history examining in the process the myriad of political, economic, social, cultural and technological forces that shaped and defined not just the city but America. From its inception, Pierre LâEnfantâs visionary plan represented Americaâs grandest intentions but the first 100 years of its history was anything but grand. It was not until the turn of the century that the city re-emerged from its provincial shell through the efforts of a few individuals. The re-making of Washington in the 20th century mirrors the emergence of the United States as a world power. What do the unbuilt projects of the city tell us about the culture of Washington, of America? How did the technological innovations that transformed America since the industrial revolution impact the public and private structures in the capital? How is the current century reflected in the cityâs built form? This course will attempt to read architecture and address the cultural meaning behind Washingtonâs physical form through visual narrative and engaging interaction that includes field trips and visits to selected buildings and sites. No Prerequisites. Texts & Readings ? Required book: Richard Longstreth, ed. The Mall in Washington 1791-1991, National Gallery of Art, Yale University Press, New Haven & London: 1991, 2002. ISBN 0-300 09537-6 Reading assignments and selected texts for each session will be posted on Blackboard. Assignments & Expectations of Students ? Students will be required to have a weekly journal that will include written and visual exercises. There will be between two and four field trips to visit selected buildings including the Society of the Cincinnati, the Building Museum, the National Gallery, the Mall, Freedom Plaza, the Finnish Embassy, the Swedish House and the Newseum. Students will produce a written paper and make an oral presentation of their selected building analysis. Class attendance and contribution to discussions 30% Weekly Journal 30% A presentation and final paper 40% What Students Should Know ? Syllabus: ? Week 1 Introduction to architecture as communication and visual narrative, reading the city in the style of Walter Benjamin; space-time Washington as we know it today represents the intersection of public and private forces that shaped it over its 200+ years. spatial form as the expression of self-conscious invention and experimentation as space assumes meaning beyond function. Week 2 Visions-Visionaries: The city is a culmination of multiple visions and pioneering visionaries whose legacy continues: Pierre LâEnfant, George Washington, Thomas Jefferson. Week 3 ? Power of Place: Washington remains the cultural property, the cultural real-estate of a nation through its evolution from a revolutionary renegade country to a world power. It represents the power of place over time. Capitol & White House. Week 4 ? Federal-Local: Federal ownership, local ownership dichotomy, with its inhabitants caught inbetween. A child of an implicitly conflicting marriage, an orphan child the city even today has been denied the rights afforded all American cities. ?? Week 5 ? City of magnificent distances was how Charles Dickens described Washington at mid 19th century. How did the civil war change the city? What impact did the industrial revolution and technological transformations have upon city form? Week 6 ? City Beautiful and monumentalization of the Mall at the turn of the century, the architects who reinvented LâEnfantâs vision. Impact of progressive minds and progressive technologies.? Week 7 ?The Federal presence, the Federal triangle and New Deal America reflected in built form. What impact did new transportation technologies have on Washington. Selecting research topics: learning to reading buildings in their contexts. Week 8 Unbuilt and Iconic; The city that could have been: Frank Lloyd Wright and Eero Saarinen designed but never built. An increasingly modern city at war with modernism. ? Week 9 Postwar urban surgery; Southwest Washington became the countryâs largest urban renewal initiative and its most controversial case as race and architecture became intertwined. Week 10 Recapturing history and mainstreaming modernism; Washington at the forefront of the nationâs historic preservation movement at the same time that it embraced modernism. ? Week 11 ?Another monumentality: museums and monuments in late 20th century; the Mall as sacred cultural space, secular pilgrimage site and national playground. Technology and communication redefine architecture. Week 12 Contextualism galore and reimagining the city in the 21st century; global information age is transformative; reflecting the future. Weeks 13-14 ?Student presentations. ?

title: Global Governance and Deliberation
description: Course Goals & Description This is a gateway course for the globalization cluster designed to introduce you to the role of international communication and media processes in global governance. Global governance, a salient area of concern in international relations, refers to explicit and implicit forms of global coordination and cooperation in various issues such as security, commerce, and human rights. Governance itself is contingent upon the way in which the various international actors (governments, firms, transnational civil society) communicate and deliberate. Particular emphasis will be placed in the course on the way in which technologies constrain or expand various forms of deliberation. The first part of the course will critically situate the evolution of the term global governance, drawing upon various schools of thought especially in the U.S. and Europe. Next we will examine forms of deliberation such as communicative action, negotiation and problem-solving, formal institutional procedures such as in the UN, coalition-building, multilateralism. The last part of the course will apply our understandings to global issue-areas such as security, poverty, commerce, human rights, and Internet. Assignments & Expectations of Students Students will participate in class debates each week with designated student leaders leading the debate. The final requirement will be a 25-page research paper that will be developed over the course of the semester. Class participation and debates will comprise 20 percent of the grade with 80 percent accorded to the final paper.

title: Contemporary Corporate Communications
description: The Course in Context Corporations today must communicate with a number of important audiences if they are to remain competitive in the global marketplace. For large, publicly-traded companies, the communications burdens are significant. CEOs and corporate leaders must communicate effectively with key constituencies, including: (1) shareholders and investors; (2) customers; (3) employees and business partners; (4) governments, policymakers and regulators; (5) the news media, and (6) stakeholders, non-governmental organizations and other critical observers. Communicating with each of these audiences requires most corporations to develop different methods and vehicles to deliver their desired corporate message(s) in both online and offline contexts. Typical communication modalities include, but are not limited to: (a) annual reports and corporate brochures; (b) marketing, promotional and public relations materials; (c) investor-focused communications; (d) legal, regulatory and compliance reports; (e) statements on corporate policy; (f) statements on public policy, and at some point in the life of every company, (g) crisis communications. Increasingly, companies are adding a range of cutting-edge social and interactive media to their communication mix. Course Themes and Objectives: This is a highly interactive course designed to provide students with a solid understanding of contemporary corporate communications practices, methods and strategies. We will examine the present-day challenges companies face in light of heightened expectations from shareholders, stakeholders, policymakers, employees and the media. And we will examine the importance of corporate communications to the implementation of corporate strategy; investor relations; corporate branding, identity and image; government relations; advertising; media relations, and crisis management. In addition to the textbook, we will study real-world cases and contemporary articles in business magazines and journals. At the end, students are expected to develop a facility and proficiency with written and oral communications that can be employed in a corporate context. Grading and Requirements: Effective written and oral communications are intrinsic course requirements. Students are expected to read all of the course materials, participate actively in classroom discussions, and demonstrate solid communication skills. â¢ Classroom participation will account for 20% of the final grade. â¢ From their reading of current articles and periodicals, students are required to submit two (2) Communications Briefs (between 5-7 pages) that analyze an issue faced by a major corporation. Students will present their Brief to the class, discuss the strategy and solution they recommend, and defend their decisions with colleagues. Together, the Communications Briefs will account for 40 % of the final grade. â¢ Students will be required to submit a final paper which presents a comprehensive communications strategy for a corporation of their choice. This paper should establish an overall communications strategy for the company and its key constituencies; identify key message points for each audience; outline an implementation timetable; and establish metrics for evaluating its success. This paper will account for 40 % of the final grade. Text and Reading Materials Required Text: Paul A. Argenti, Corporate Communication, 5h edition. Boston, MA: McGraw-Hill / Irwin (2008). ISBN-10: 0-07-337773-2 Additional course materials will be adapted from The Conference Board Reports, Harvard Business Review, periodicals, journals and online sources, and provided by the professor. Students are expected to read The Wall Street Journal, Business Week and Fortune Magazine. Contact Adonis E. Hoffman, Esq. | Senior Vice President & Counsel American Association of Advertising Agencies 1203 19th Street NW, Fourth Floor | Washington, DC 20036 202-331-7345 - office / ahoffman@aaaa.org 703-627-0400 - cell

title: Communication and the Public Sphere
description: This course will investigate historical and contemporary conceptions of the public sphere and public sphere theory, including the potential virtual and networked public spheres that new technologies have enabled. Topics will include: public sphere versus public space, forms of civic dialogue, multiple public spheres (with a lowercase "p") versus one all-encompassing Public Sphere (with a capital "P"), media involvement in the public sphere, privacy and the public sphere (such as Bentham and Foucault on the panopticon), and different forms of participation in the public sphere. Also addressed will be the intersection of public opinion and public sphere, and behavior in public spaces. Main texts may include: Craig Calhoun's Habermas and the Public Sphere, Goffman's Behavior in Public Places, Vincent Price's volume in the Communication Concepts series on Public Opinion. Other texts might include: Noelle-Neumann's The Spiral of Silence, Putnam's Bowling Alone, or Jamieson's and Capella's Spiral of Cynicism.

title: Knowledge Management: Theory and Application
description: In today's world the sheer amount of data and information created by and accessible to individuals and organizations overwhelm us. But, the real challenge is the continual struggle to transform these into usable and reusable knowledge. This challenge faces all organizations - corporate enterprises, non-profits, educational institutions and government agencies. This course reaches behind the current fads regarding organizational knowledge management to explore the theoretical foundation for knowledge, its meaning and value, as well as policies and approaches for building capabilities to manage knowledge within and across organizational boundaries. By the end of this course, students will be able to articulate key knowledge management concepts, issues and trends regarding a variety of related topics, which include: Nature and topology of knowledge Knowledge economy Theory, policies, practices and problems for: - Knowledge capture/acquisition - e.g., mentoring, storytelling, social network analysis - Knowledge conversion/transfer e.g., documentation, cognitive learning, collaborative sharing and situated learning - Knowledge reuse/application- e.g., accessibility, infrastructure and reliability - Research strategies and methods - Roles in knowledge management - e.g., managers, intermediaries, facilitators, seekers, practitioners

title: E-Government
description: Just as the Internet and information technology are changing the way companies do business, they are changing how governments deliver services to the citizen. This course will explore the different phases in the development of e-government services, from simple Web pages to online transactions, Web 2.0, virtual worlds, and more. Students will examine a variety of projects--at the Federal, state, and local level--and determine the technological, economic, cultural, and bureaucratic factors that determine why some succeed and others fail. They will learn how new technologies could transform the relationship between citizens and their government. This course will help students interested in working in the public sector or in firms helping develop and deploy new online services.

title: Communities of Practice
description: Communities and collaboration are incubators of innovation, knowledge and intellectual capital creation. This course will explore collaboration and communities from both a theoretical and a practical perspective. On the theoretical side, weâll explore the structural elements (community identity, scoping the domain, defining practice), their natural lifecycles, the culture of collaboration, and key roles and models that lead to successful outcomes. On the practical side, weâll explore enabling technologies (Web 2.0 and 3.0), the strategic placement of communities, community design and implementation, and measurement of the impacts of communities. Students will collaboratively develop a community of practice assessment framework, and apply the framework to case studies and real world communities in education, health care and nursing, disaster management, military sciences, science and engineering communities, hobbies and craft circles, online gaming, and spiritual/religious communities.

title: Architecture and Cinema: From Metropolis to Avatar
description: Cinema and architecture both rely on the primacy of vision. Like architecture, cinema is visual narrative, articulating and guiding the spectator through various spatial experiences. Like cinema, architecture is a "technique" of the time reflecting societyâs aesthetic and intellectual makeup. The shared experiential ground between architecture and cinema is space, time and the representation of ideas. Both have the capacity to create and manipulate the experience of space; both reflect time and become relics of their time. This course uses the rich intersection of cinema and the built environment as the platform to explore spatiotemporal relationships and nuanced cultural meanings while revealing the transformational impact of technology. Modernism and multiple modernities are defined by architecture and cinema. The indelible image of âmodernâ as it was transformed from avant-garde to mainstream is crystallized in celluloid as much as stone. The city has been the subject and backdrop of films revealing its layered evolution, so too has the skyscraper, often with associations laden with cultural meaning and gender specific iconography. Visionary unbuilt architecture too narrates delightful possibilities and like fictional film is sometimes the inspiration for built form. Cinematic films that feature the cityâespecially in America and architectural tours of the city will be supplemented by readings of critical theories including Juhani Palaasmaâs Architecture of Image: Existential Space in Cinema. Students will be expected to produce a written/visual essay.

title: Using and Managing Information Technology
description: Overview Digital technologies, including the Internet, cellphones, digital video, and databases, have transformed large segments of the economies of developed countries and are beginning to have a major impact in developing countries as well. Twenty-five years ago, the CEO and board of a company paid almost no attention to decisions regarding the technological infrastructure used to run the company. The discussion usually ended after the budget for the IT department was approved. Neither the CEO nor the Board felt it was necessary to really understand the technology and how it was being used. Today, in many sectors, picking the right technologies and the right technology strategies can be the difference between growth and bankruptcy. As a result, CEOs and corporate boards, government agency heads, and NGO executives are much more involved in addressing IT-related issues. More and more of these issues are not purely technological. Instead, they require understanding how people and organizations use existing technologies and how they adopt or resist new technologies. They require understanding the economics of the digital marketplace. In some cases, government policies influence which technologies can be used and how. Many CCT graduates find jobs in the high-tech industry or in technology consulting. Others take jobs in the media sector, often working with new media like social media. Still others work in more traditional sectors or in government but end up working with technology or managing technical teams. While most CCT students are not trained as Webmasters and are not looking to become a Webmaster, many of them end up working with Webmasters and their companyâs IT staff to develop business plans, new products, and new services. So it is essential that CCT graduates understand the fundamentals of digital technologies. Objectives Students will gain a better understanding of how digital technologies developed, how they work, and how they are evolving. More importantly, they will explore the many organizational and management issues related to digital technologies. The class will be organized around a series of critical IT-related questions that executives and managers are struggling with today, and which will become even more difficultâand importantâin the future. Class assignments are designed to give students opportunities to become acquainted with a range of tools and technologies and to better understand how companies and other organizations adopt and use them. Some of these assignments will include exercises designed to give students a chance to âplay withâ a range of tools, including: - Web authoring tools - Social network sites - Digital video - Animation - Blogs - Instant messaging and microblogs (e.g. Twitter) Students will be expected to take at least two short courses or workshops on specific digital technologies that interest them, in order to become more adept at using them. I believe that class discussion is essential to getting the most out this class and your education at Georgetown. In both class discussions and on-line postings, I will encourage vigorousâbut respectfulâdiscussion and debate. Please follow the rules set out by the Georgetown Honor Council. See //gervaseprograms.georgetown.edu/hc/honor_system.html Grading 1. Weekly reactions. 1-page written reaction each weekâs readings and assignments to be submitted before each class. In these reactions, share any questions you have about the material covered, any problems you had in accomplishing the assignments, and any insights or additional resources you might want to share with your professors and classmates. They are expected to be informal, and once students have completed the reading and the assignment, these reactions should take no more than 30 minutes to write. These will be graded Pass/Excellent/Outstanding. For each student, the 8 best will count toward 20% of the course grade. Please post these assignments on the class blog. 2. Practicum projects. A primary goal of this course is to help students understand and feel more comfortable with a range of digital technologies. The case studies which you lead will count toward 40% of the course grade. 3. Class participation. Your grade will depend on how your participation in discussion (both in class and on the blog) reflects your preparation for class, as well as attendance. (20%) 4. Final paper. For your final paper you are develop one component of an IT strategy for a specific company or organization. You will be given a range of projects to choose between. For instance, you might have to: (1) choose a collaboration platform for a non-profit organization with dozens of chapters scattered around the US, or (2) recommend how a Federal agency could use Twitter to publicize its activities and events, or (3) analyze whether a small business should migrate from Microsoft Windows to a Cloud-based system such as Google Apps. Your final paper and the presentation based on it will account for 20% of your grade.

title: Technologies of the Text
description: In this seminar, we consider the term "text" from many perspectivesâincluding digital media, experimental books, art objectsâand even musical scores and dance-notation "texts." We ask how such forms are redefining the terms "author" and "reader"âand how they are changing our definitions of "literature" and "news" and "social networking." Each term, we develop special topics in the seminar according to the particular research interests of each class member. In past years, for instance, we have read the most popular hypertext ever writtenânamely, "Patchwork Girl," a powerful take-off on the novel Frankenstein (which we also discuss during the course). We have also read graphic novels such as the award-winning Fun Home, Akira, and Persepolis (we saw the movie-version of the latter as well). And we also considered many texts from a cross-cultural perspective, including American versions of Japanese anime and other non-Western genres. During previous terms, we have also looked at the historical origins of today's digital cultureâincluding the history of libraries, museums, and art collections. We have discussed the origins of copyright law, comparing the first amazing trials with today's debates about digital copyright (including the famous Napster and Mickey Mouse cases). And we have investigated notorious internet hoaxes (including the Lonely Girl scandal)âand traced their origins to the most intriguing art and literary forgeries (such as those by Thomas Chatterton). Students with specifically literary interests might also consider how various textual forms affect novels, plays, and poetry. How is literature influenced by, say, Thackeray's emphasis on his novels' illustrations, Joyce's preoccupation with pagination, or Emily Dickinson's use of stationery and paper scraps? How did the serialization of Dickens's novels in periodicals affect their composition, distribution, and readership? What do William Blake's bizarre paintings tell us about his poetry? In the past, we have also read influential literary by Chaucer, Defoe, Sterne, Pope, Wordsworth, Byron, Hemans, Austen, the Brontes, Joyceâas well as some of the more recent, experimental developments within literary and electronic textuality. If you would like more information about it, please email Michael Macovski at msm44@georgetown.edu.

title: History of the Book: Theory of Text
description: This course is for those interested in Graphic Novels (Comic Books), Videogames, Fan Fiction, Anime, Mash-ups, Blogging, Online Journalism, RSS-Feeds, Podcasting, Wikis, Online Communities, Web 2.0, Hypertexts, the new E-booksâand other recent movements and experiments in New Media.
