title: Gateway: 18th/19th Century Lit/Cult
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR REBECCA BOYLAN ENGL 041.01 Through a Lens Darkly: Phantoms Haunting 19th C Literature This course explores the individual hidden by others and hiding the self in 19th C literature (and painting and photograph) as this obscurity reflects the centuryâs sociological, psychological, aesthetic, and philosophical wrestles. We begin by pondering several conundrums: how this century of realism and technological advancements in vision celebrates illusions and masked identities; why obscurity provokes the desire to discover; why the observer desperate to see often veils his vision or covers up the object of his gaze; how those in hiding mysteriously reveal a multi-faceted self-consciousness; how discomforting truths struggle out of confusing ambiguities. As readers traversing misty moors and seas, opium dreams, foggy streets, and phantasmagoric interiors, we will meet real ghosts, sprites, transforming portraits, and haunted houses. As scholars, we will study how, why, and to what effects the shadows of the 19th C crept into the early 20th C to determine to what extent historical perspective adjusted the lens on these ephemeral Victorian personas. We might well ask, does Alice come UP the rabbit hole? Is the Other discovered only to disappear behind a different barrier? How do changed expectations for Artâs functions and purpose provoke either our urgency to see or our complacent blindness? Participants will respond in several short response essays, a mid-length paper, and a longer final paper. Dynamic and consistent class participation is expected. Critical and theoretical essays are available via electronic reserve. Novels include: E. BrontÃ«âs Wuthering Heights, Jamesâs The Turn of the Screw, Braddonâs Lady Audleyâs Secret, Wildeâs The Picture of Dorian Gray, Forsterâs Howardâs End, Woolfâs To the Lighthouse, and Lawrenceâs The Rainbow. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR MARGARET DEBELIUS ENGL 041-01 (Colloquium) Sections of ENGL 041 described as colloquia (COL) are organized as writing intensive workshops, involving a team of instructors and close coordination with advanced under- graduate tutors. This course will focus on learning how to read a variety of texts from the turn of the nineteenth century. We will consider works by Robert Louis Stevenson, Henry James, Joseph Conrad, Charlotte Perkins Gilman, Arthur Conan Doyle, Edith Wharton, and others as expressions of a host of cultural anxieties â anxieties about race, class, gender, technology, and sexuality. We will develop the close reading skills required to read literature intelligently and learn the terms and concepts necessary to understand the critical discourse surrounding these texts. Be prepared for frequent writing and rewriting.

SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR DENNIS TODD ENGL 041.02 The âIâ and the Other In this class, we will explore a literary question and a cultural question. The literary question is, How did 18th and 19th-century English and American authors exploit the areas of experience that a first-person narrator made available to them? The cultural question is, How did these authors deal with the existence of âOthersââthose radically different peoples and cultures that 18th- and 19th-century exploration, colonialism, and slavery brought them in to contact with? I hope that we might even begin to see a connection between these two questions. We will read five novels: Daniel Defoeâs Robinson Crusoe; Jonathan Swiftâs Gulliverâs Travels; Charles Brockden Brownâs Edgar Huntly; Emily BrontÃ«âs Wuthering Heights; and Mark Twainâs Huckleberry Finn. In the class, we will be introduced to the skill of close reading, to some of the rich criticism and scholarly analysis that surround these novels, and to some of the concepts and theoretical constructs that are foundational to the discipline of English. Finally, we will be attentive to writing, and you will be assigned numerous reader response papers and several longer analytic papers.

title: Gateway: 18th/19th Century Lit/Cult
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR JOY YOUNG ENGL 041.03 19th-century Melodrama This class will introduce students to early nineteenth-century popular culture through the study of its most popular form: melodrama. With its familial narratives, depictions of bodily torture, scenes of criminal conduct and female sexual endangerment, expressions of excessive emotion, and plain themes of good and evil, melodrama emerged as a behavioral paradigm, manifest in the drama, minstrel shows, and literature of a newly urbanized America. We shall focus on melodramaâs voyeuristic appeal and pay special attention to melodramaâs relationship to the body, gender roles, working-class culture, and race. Readings will include Stowe Uncle Tomâs Cabin, Melville Pierre or the Ambiguities, Hawthorne The Blithedale Romance, Smith The Drunkard, Boucicault The Octoroon. Students will work on close critical reading and writing skills by way of numerous reader response papers and three longer analytic papers. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR DENNIS TODD ENGL 041.03 The âIâ and the Other In this class, we will explore a literary question and a cultural question. title: Gateway: 18th/19th Century Lit/Cult
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR MAUREEN CORRIGAN ENGL 041.04 Victorian Extreme Adventures The extreme adventure tale is a genre that has become enormously popular in our own time through non-fiction narratives such as Sebastien Junger's /The Perfect Storm/ and Jon Krakauer's /Into Thin Air/ in which man is pitted against the forces of nature. This course proposes to look at nineteenth-century British fictional antecedants of the extreme adventure tale in which both men and women face tests of superhuman endurance--not only in the great outdoors--but also in the marriage market, the teeming metropolis of London, the sick room, and the realm of the supernatural. Among the novels and romances we'll be considering are: /Pride and Prejudice/ by Jane Austen, /Jane Eyre/ by Charlotte Bronte, Great Expectations by Charles Dickens, News from Nowhere by William Morris, and Dracula by Bram Stoker. Classes will be conducted like a seminar and informed participation is mandatory. Two essays, a midterm exam, and a final project are required. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR JOSEPH FRUSCIONE ENGL 041.04 Writing, Race, Nation, and American Romanticism This course will span roughly a century of American literature, from the late-18th century to the post-Reconstruction era. We will pay particular attention to matters of: race, slavery, and Reconstruction politics; rhetoric, literacy, and literary art; nation, nationhood, and nationalism; notions of geographic, historical, and intellectual quests; gender dynamics, constructions, and hierarchies. Authors: Phillis Wheatley, Ralph Waldo Emerson, Edgar Allan Poe, Henry David Thoreau, Herman Melville, Frederick Douglass, Harriet Jacobs, Mark Twain. Assignments and Requirements: Reading Quizzes; Analysis Papers; Final Exam; Class Participation.

title: Gateway: 18th/19th Century Lit/Cult
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR LYNDON DOMINIQUE ENGL 041.05 Race and Gender in Eighteenth-Century British Literature The Rights of Man, Thomas Paineâs classic argument in defense of the individualâs right to assert freedom in the face of tyranny, was a popular late-eighteenth century refrain originating from the 1789 French Revolution. But generalized arguments about individual human rights also gave rise to specific debates concerning the rights of women and Negro slaves. What rights were these individuals denied in eighteenth-century Britain and how did the periodâs literature reflect their dilemmas? This course will consider these questions as a way of introducing you to the study of race and gender in a British colonial context. But it is particularly concerned with occasions in literature where British writers combined simultaneous discourses about race and gender in ways that sometimes helped and at other times hindered the fights against tyranny that Negro slaves and female advocates fought. We will read plays, poetry, novels, short stories, travel literature, and non-fiction prose as well as recent theories about gender and racial construction in the eighteenth century to discuss representations of British men and women, and colonial Others like Negro slaves, Creoles and Jews. We will consider an assortment of issues ranging from slavery, anti-slavery, abolition, miscegenation, mimicry, ambivalence, hybridity, blackness and whiteness, to marriage, libertinism, and sexual double standards in a variety of canonical and obscure texts. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR PATRICK O'MALLEY ENGL 041.05 Novel and Nation in Nineteenth-Century Britain This course will position a number of representative British novels of the nineteenth century within a literary and historical context that stresses the relationship of narrative literature to the broader social and political issues that engaged Englandâs attention in the course of the century. Some of those issues are race and national identity, the changing nature of social classes, marriage and the family, and Britainâs relationship to its own colonial structure. Throughout the entire course, we will devote particular attention to the ways that the novels formulate âEnglishnessâ itself, whether that notion is constructed in relationship to society, culture, race, gender, or history; in particular, we will think about how British writers articulated their relationship to Scottish, Irish, Jewish, Indian, African and Continental cultures and identities. Texts will include Sidney Owensonâs The Wild Irish Girl, Walter Scottâs Ivanhoe, Joseph Conradâs Heart of Darkness, Bram Stokerâs Dracula, and Rudyard Kiplingâs Kim, in addition to theoretical and critical accounts of literary and cultural representations of national formation and identity. Regular analytic writing, group presentations, and participation in class discussion will be required.

title: Gateway: 18th/19th Century Lit/Cult
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR LYNDON DOMINQUE ENGL 041.06 Race and Gender in Eighteenth-Century British Literature The Rights of Man, Thomas Paineâs classic argument in defense of the individualâs right to assert freedom in the face of tyranny, was a popular late-eighteenth century refrain originating from the 1789 French Revolution. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR BRETT TOMLINSON ENGL 041.06 Into the Wild: Nature, Freedom, Madness in 19th C American Literature This course will consider the tension between civilization and the wild in 19th C American literature and culture. In a century when the frontier was progressively colonized and made into spectacle, and when scientific revolutions in natural history created doubt about human exceptionality in the natural order, the wild became a space of both spiritual refuge and hidden menace. As wildness shifted from an exterior landscape to an interior presence, human nature became a source of intense cultural and literary speculation. By tracing a path from transcendentalism to naturalism we will also explore the competing ideas of regeneration and degeneration, of self-definition and loss of identity, of ecstatic connection and destructive mania, that inform literary treatments of the wild during this period. Among many questions, we will ask: how are American values, particularly individualism, rooted in âself-relianceâ and a free-spirited relationship with nature? Why is the wild often a source of liberation for individuals and of paranoia for the dominant culture? Why did the performance of privilege during this period entail the construction of gender and racial others as dangerously wild and in need of domestication? How did Darwinism haunt the latter half of the 19th C and influence the rise of naturalism? Students will contribute weekly short responses to a class discussion forum, will lead a class discussion on a relevant topic, and will write a midterm paper and a final paper. Major works to be discussed include: Thoreauâs Walden, Douglasâs Narrative of the Life, Melvilleâs Moby Dick, Twainâs The Diaries of Adam and Eve, Chopinâs The Awakening, and Norrisâs McTeague.

title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR DAVID GEWANTER ENGL 042.01 Voice and Form This course explores the productive conflicts between a writerâs several voices, and the literary forms they inhabit. A writerâs several voices, characters, sensibility, and imagined world will fight and befriend the literary form that houses them. In modern and contemporary literature, this struggle between voice and form becomes especially acute, sending out sparks, flames, stinging smoke, and not a few misfires. This course will offer a close study, and some practice, of four modes of writing in which the writerâs presence battles with form: fiction, stage-dialogue, poetry, and personal prose. We will explore and discuss great writers of these genresâboth those that with apparent loyalty fulfill a formal promise, and those others intent on mayhem and sabotage. We will hear craft lectures from the excellent writers in our Department who work in these forms; we will write critical prose on selected works; and we will try our hand at composingânot for gradesâour own poems, stories, personal essays, and a short radio-dialogue. Our frame-breaking authors include D.H. Lawrence, Michael Ondaatje, and Anna Deavere Smith, poets such as Heaney, Eliot, Moore, Toomer, and several others. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR JENNIFER FINK ENGL 042.01 Playing Modern: Modernism, Postmodernism, and Theatricality In this course, weâll investigate performanceâs troubled relationship with modernism and postmodernism. Pairing key theoretical texts with play texts, weâll explore how theatrical performance reflects, refracts, and refutes notions of modern and postmodern subjectivity. The fraught relationship between texts and bodies will be a key site of our investigation. From Bertol Brecht to Judith Butler, from Samuel Beckett to Tony Kusher, from Oscar Wilde to Moises Kaufman to Jacques Derrida and beyond, weâll trace the (dis)appearance of the modern subject and the emergence of the postmodern notion of the performative. Also making a guest appearance will be the relationship between theory and practice. Expect to write, read, and perform, both critically and theatrically.

title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR WAYNE KNOLL ENGL 042.02 Because this Gateway course is principally geared for the prospective English major, our principal objective is to assist the student to further develop skills in close textual reading and in effective writing. We will read key texts of seven modern American writers of fiction and poetry who significantly interacted with each otherâHemingway, F. Scott Fitzgerald, Gertrude Stein, Ezra Pound, Archibald MacLeish, Hilda Doolittle, T. S. Eliot, and E. E. Cummings. We will discuss these texts as their authors interacted with each other, principally as they experimented with innovative literary techniques and thus revolutionized American literature, both fiction and poetry. Writing requirements: two short papers and a literary journal reflecting personal analysis and class discussion of these texts, occasional quizzes, and voluntary power point presentations on the lives of these American Expatriate writers. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR SCOTT HEATH ENGL 042.02 "New" Black Aesthetics As historically disenfranchised people of color have been collectively occupied with attaining recognition as citizens and with determining how to exercise fully the rights indicated by this citizenship, they have been represented (by others and by themselves) politically, socially, and artistically in ways that work both within and against the boundaries of Americanness. From slave narratives to hip_hop tracks, many otherwise race-specific expressions of marginal resistance in the United States have been appropriated to the extent that they have become in some ways synonymous with the conventions of mainstream Americana. In this course we will explore the ways that the notion of blackness has been and continues to be constructed, commodified, challenged, and reconceptualized in literature and in other contemporary media. We will think through various approaches to the interpretation and critique of culture, taking particular interest in those expressive modes that have been called âpopularâ and that have not been generally classified as âblack.â And in this context we will begin to reevaluate exactly what these descriptors mean. With this project in mind, our class meetings will be arranged thematically, crossing disciplines to incorporate conversations about books, films, and music in which this discussion happens and about genres of text, such as speculative fiction and punk, with which African Americans have not been typically associated in the public imagination. We will examine works that exhibit aesthetic tendencies and discursive styles that might be deemed ânewâ or âalternativeâ (perhaps even radical and interventionist) to the extent that black people have not been heavily represented as producers or subjectsâeven as we locate certain cultural continua that would contradict this assumption. Among our consistent talking points will be ideas of cultural property and authenticity, feminism and the performance of masculinity, cosmopolitanism and globalization, and public versus private text. Considering that identity and community are inevitably mediated through conceptions of race, class, gender, sexuality, and nationality, we will work to think more critically about the ways that black cultural production can be read.

title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR EDWARD MALONEY ENGL 042.03 What are modernism and postmodernism? Do these terms define historical periods? Literary genres? Aesthetic concepts? How do we understand these terms in relationship to each other and in the various cultural and historical contexts in which they are often used? Are there differences between the use of these terms in the visual and the literary arts? Through careful analysis of a number of films, literary works, and critical theory, this course will begin to answer these and other fundamental questions about modernism and postmodernism. We will pay particular attention to the ways in which certain writers self-consciously foreground the act of writing in their works. Texts for the course will include works by Virginia Woolf, James Joyce, Katherine Anne Porter, John Fowles, Vladimir Nabokov, Thomas Pynchon, Manuel Puig, and Margaret Atwood, as well as a number of critical essays. This is a writing intensive course. Students will be asked to contribute to an online course reading portfolio throughout the semester and complete multiple elements of a research prospectus. title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR MARK MCMORRIS ENGL 042.04 Interventions in Modern Poetry This course will examine diverse currents in the explosive, hybridizing, deregulated, re-forming poetry of the early to mid-twentieth century: the revolution of the word. Film and visual art will necessarily supplement our study, since the â-ismsâ or movements we will be most concerned with found expression in a variety of media. Call it the rise of inter-artistic vanguards. The chief tendencies within Imagism, Vorticism, Dada, Surrealism, Futurism (s), Expressionism, & Negritude will surveyed. Careful to understand that these movements do not account for the entire poetic field of Modernism, we will position the impulses ranged under these names--as is customary--in relation to broad historical and social changes then underway in the United States & Europe, taking special notice of responses to World War I, developments in machine technology, and an ongoing urbanization. Our special focus will be the problem of critical approaches to these works. Taking lyric poetry as a point of departure, we will search for ways of writing about texts that, in some ways, resist or evade the habits of lyric address & subjectivity. Students can expect to write occasional analytical responses & three short papers; there will be a midterm & a final exam. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR MARK MCMORRIS ENGL 042.04 Interventions in Modern Poetry This course will examine diverse currents in the explosive, hybridizing, deregulated, re-forming poetry of the early to mid-twentieth century: the revolution of the word. title: Gateway: Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR JENNIFER NATALYA FINK ENGL 042.05 Playing Modern: Modernism, Postmodernism, and Theatricality In this course, weâll investigate performanceâs troubled relationship with modernism and postmodernism. Pairing key theoretical texts with play texts, weâll explore how theatrical performance reflects, refracts, and refutes notions of modern and postmodern subjectivity. The fraught relationship between texts and bodies will be a key site of our investigation. From Bertol Brecht to Judith Butler, from Samuel Beckett to Tony Kusher, from Oscar Wilde to Moises Kaufman to Jacques Derrida and beyond, weâll trace the (dis)appearance of the modern subject and the emergence of the postmodern notion of the performative. Also making a guest appearance will be the relationship between theory and practice. Expect to write, read, and perform, both critically and theatrically. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR ROBERT PATTERSON ENGL 042.05 Civil Rights and African American Literature Throughout the past forty years, African American writers have used their literary works to think about the modern Civil Rights Movement. Although historians often argue that the Civil Rights era began in 1953 and ended in 1968, feminist historian Jacqueline Dowd recently has encouraged civil rights era scholars to consider the âlongâ civil rights movement. By expanding the time period that we use to classify the Civil Rights era, we might think about several issues related to that time period in more nuanced ways. If, for example, we consider the labor movements of the 1930 as part of the Civil Rights Movement, our understanding of gender and leadership during the Movement in the 1950s might be enhanced. In this course, we will undertake Dowdâs challenge, examining how African American literature produced since the mid 1960s assists us in this process of ârevisionary historiographyâ about the Civil Rights Movement and even the Black Power Movement. As we examine the historical period and literary texts, issues of leadership, activism, gender, and periodization will inform our analyses. Moreover, we will interrogate the definition of civil rights, including the historicity of this phrase as it specifically relates to African Americansâ quests for political, economic, and cultural enfranchisement in the United States. Alongside this concern, we will consider how feminist, black feminist, and gay rights movements have appropriated rhetoric and tropes of the Civil Rights Movement, as well as how those movements in turn have re-shaped our understanding or the gender and sexual politics of the Civil Rights Movement. Consistent and engaged reading will be essential for success in this course. Students enrolling in this course should expect to read fictive texts by the following authors: James Baldwin, Toni Cade Bambara, Ernest Gaines, Gayl Jones, Alice Walker, Randall Kenan, Anthony Grooms, Toni Morrison, and Charles Johnson. Potential Assignments/Grade determination: class participation (15%), two mid-length papers (5 pages) (30%); 1 Midterm Examination (20%); 3-4 quizzes (15%), and 1 final exercise (20%)

title: Gateway: Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR LIBBIE RIFKIN ENGL 042.06 New York, San Francisco and the Literature of Place This class will consider the relation of literature to place, using New York and San Francisco as two interconnected examples. Weâll read poetry, nonfiction, fiction, and a play about those two cities (including works by Jack Kerouac and Maxine Hong Kingston, Allen Ginsberg and Amiri Baraka) but weâll also complicate that approach, examining the idea of âplaceâ through a number of different lenses: geographic, ethnic, mythical, psychological, and most importantly, social. Along with the work of a range of poets and writers who lived and wrote in these two coastal nodes in the last 50 years, we will explore the explosion of little magazines associated first with the âmimeo revolutionâ and later online; weâll also tap into the readings, gatherings, happenings, and scenes that writers developed in order to make these cities their own, and weâll examine how literary community shaped both their lives and their art.

title: Gateway: Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR LIBBIE RIFKIN ENGL 042.07 New York, San Francisco and the Literature of Place This class will consider the relation of literature to place, using New York and San Francisco as two interconnected examples. title: Gateway: Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR JESSE OAK TAYLOR ENGL 042.08 Fin de SiÃ¨cle/ Fin du Globe (1880-1910, 1980-2010) âEnd of the century, end of the world.â This phrase encapsulates a widespread sense of malaise, apprehension, and even elation at the prospect that the passing of a century might herald the end of world as we know it. Such periods of transition are both freeing and frightening. The literary period at the end of the 19th century known as the âfin de siÃ¨cle,â was one of remarkable invention, giving birth not only to genres such as horror, detective, and science fiction but also the aesthetics of high modernism. In this course, we will pair the period from 1880-1910 with the most recent âfin de siÃ¨cleâââthat of our own time, the years 1980-2010. While we will treat each period as distinct in its own right, many recurrent themes emerge: imperialism and globalization; ecological vulnerability and concern over the âend of natureâ; increased visibility of (and reaction against) changing gender roles and sexualities; debates over the role of both science and art; and questions about the status of the human. In the process, the course will serve as an introduction to the methods of literar studies, and its relationship to history, science, and other modes of cultural inquiry. Texts will include fiction, poetry, and plays by authors such as: Algernon Swinburne, Bram Stoker, Oscar Wilde, Amy Levy, Alice Meynell, George Bernard Shaw, Ted Hughes, Tom Stoppard, Jeanette Winterson, Amy Gerstler, and Indra Sinha, as well as contextual material and samplings of relevant literary and cultural theory.

title: Gateway: Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR WARD TIETZ ENGL 042.09 Literatures of Sight and Sound: from "Serious" Comics to Digital and Sound Poetry Literature and the visual arts exist largely as separate aesthetic categories. There are many works, though, that mix forms from these categories, examples from books to paintings, to video, to digitally animated text, that combine pictures, drawings or other graphic elements with printed words or text. For decades such combinatorial strategies have been common in advertising and in popular forms like comics. Increasingly, they are now used to literary ends, in work, that in aggregate, seems to beg the construction of a new, hybrid category: a visual literature. While it can be argued that examples of visual literature can be found in many historical periods, the application of the term to work from the second half of the twentieth century seems to be the most productive, given the unusually wide range of examples, and especially given the recent proliferation of electronic forms that demonstrate a similar hybridity. The focus of this class will be to examine contemporary âvisualâ literature and spillover forms in sound and performance and consider their implications. We will be especially interested in understanding how so-called visual works merge the traditions of literature and the visual arts and challenge the way we view, read and value aesthetic objects, in a culture increasingly dominated by technology as seen on the Internet, in television, film, video and graphic images.

title: Gateway: Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR JOY YOUNG ENGL 042.10 Remaking the News: From Article to Screen In this course we will study how films use news articles and contemporary histories to create compelling narratives and popular entertainment. We will read and analyze newspaper reports and non-fiction literature and then study how they are turned into the core narrative material of films. Our short written analyses will lead to the composition of three essays in which we will study the transformation of ânews materialsâ into popular art.

title: Gateway: Mod and/or Post-Mod Lit
description: SUMMER 2011 Professor Chris Shinn GLOBAL POSTMODERN CULTURE: FROM POP ART TO FACEBOOKÂ© This course will investigate the shifting meanings of postmodern art and culture. What does it mean to be âpostmodern,â and how does the term challenge âmodernistâ worldviews? What does the California sushi roll have in common with Lady Gaga? What does the transition from analog to digital mean for contemporary art, social networking and global media? How are the modern designs of prisons similar to new Orwellian technologies of security and surveillance? How does âbeing and nothingnessâ become questionable again in postmodern philosophy? What will the future look like in 2195? We will start by looking at a brief selection of modernist works from the early to mid-twentieth century. The class will then observe the key transitional phases from the modernist to the postmodern age and will read literary works by Kobo Abe, Haruki Murakami, Margaret Atwood, Philip K. Dick, John Yau and Frank Miller. We will be watching a selection of international films and Japanese anime and reading French and German philosophy and criticism about the postmodern condition. We will also be examining the artwork of Andy Warhol, Jean-Michel Basquiat and Barbara Kruger and will be building a time capsule. Requirements: two short papers, a midterm, a class presentation and a final paper or exam.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR NADINE EHLERS Theorizing Race Race is generally taken to be something that we 'know' â it is something that is alternatively taken for granted and unquestioned, thought of as a biological and social given, or contested as a trope and social fiction with embodied effect. This course unpacks the contested terrain of the term and idea 'race' and critically examines the construction of race and racism within Western thought. We will examine the work of thinkers such as Immanuel Kant, Thomas Jefferson, Patricia J. Williams, W.E.B. Du Bois, Franz Fanon, Edward Said, Patricia Hill Collins, and Richard Dyer and consider racial theory through the prism of: enlightenment thought; scientific racism; whiteness studies; black philosophy; critical race theory and; postcolonial theory. The aim in this course is to interrogate the assumption that race exists as a natural category and, instead, question the intricacies of race as a production. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR NATHANIEL RIVERS (Colloquium) Sections of ENGL 043 described as colloquia (COL) are organized as writing intensive workshops, involving a team of instructors and close coordination with advanced under- graduate tutors. The Rhetoric of New Media Our public and private lives are increasingly lived across electronic networks. Wireless technologies and portable smart devices have only accelerated this development. ENGL 043 explores such questions as: what is the impact of new media technologies on the composition and distribution of texts, on the work of persuasion and identification, and on the cultivation of ethos and identity? These are central questions for the critical methodology of rhetoric, which this course, borrowing from George Kennedy, defines as âthe energy inherent in emotion and thought, transmitted through a system of signs, including language, to others to influence their decisions or actions.â Merging form and content, students in this course use new media technologies (e.g., Facebook, Wikipedia, and online games) to write frequently (and in a variety of ways) about rhetoric, technology and culture. For instance, students are encouraged to incorporate their own wireless and smart device technologies (e.g., laptops, iPhones, and iPads) into the classroom. Students also collaborate online through social networking sites. Audio (or video) podcasts take the place of weekly writing assignments and exams. Class time is devoted to traditional discussions but also to engagements with new media technologies (e.g. workshops, demonstrations, and tutorials). Students likewise develop a research project, which can take a variety of forms utilizing new media technologies. Students with a variety of academic and research interests are encouraged to enroll, as are students with varying comfort levels with new media technology. This course hopes to generate a diverse set of critical practices and responses to technology: a diverse student population is therefore crucial.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: Reading and Writing Critical Theory: Crimes Against Humanity This course is a reading and writing intensive course that will examine the ways in which literary theory and cultural studies may help us extract meaning from the texts that we will study or that you will later encounter both in your academic careers and your life. These texts will range from various literary genres, films and popular cultural objects from across the globe. We will investigate contemporary conflicts in the world and consider the ways in which these conflicts are, or are not, considered crimes against humanity as the United Nations defines them. We will seek to achieve an understanding of how these conflicts have arisen, their affects and consequences, and what the diverse responses are by the global community. Some critical approaches that we will investigate are feminism, queer and gender studies, postcolonial theory, disability studies, deconstructionism, poststructuralism and postmodernism.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR LIBBIE RIFKIN ENGL 043.03 The Poetry and Culture of Washington, DC This course uncovers a hidden side of Washington--the diverse literary communities that have flourished in the shadow of the Capitol for the past 150 years. We'll begin with the poetry and prose Walt Whitman wrote while nursing Civil War wounded in the hospitals that crowded the city. Then we'll focus our attention on the New Negro Renaissance in the 1920s and 30s, reading such writers as Sterling Brown, Jean Toomer, Georgia Douglas Johnson, and Langston Hughes. We'll look critically at poetry read at presidential Inaugurations and political protests, and weâll read, listen to, and visit with poets and editors working in DC today. Students will participate in literary historical walking tours of DC neighborhoods and will incorporate these experiences into their writing for the course. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR GAY CIMA Human Trafficking This course introduces students to critical methodologies central to the study of English by focusing on how human trafficking is represented in contemporary novels, plays, and poems. In an effort to learn to think critically and write clearly, we will read a wide variety of fictional accounts of twenty-first century slavery. We will compare these fictional renderings with the actual testimonies and memoirs of those trafficked.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR LIBBIE RIFKIN ENGL 043.04 The Poetry and Culture of Washington, DC This course uncovers a hidden side of Washington--the diverse literary communities that have flourished in the shadow of the Capitol for the past 150 years. We'll begin with the poetry and prose Walt Whitman wrote while nursing Civil War wounded in the hospitals that crowded the city. Then we'll focus our attention on the New Negro Renaissance in the 1920s and 30s, reading such writers as Sterling Brown, Jean Toomer, Georgia Douglas Johnson, and Langston Hughes. Students will participate in literary historical walking tours of DC neighborhoods and will incorporate these experiences into their writing for the course. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR PAMELA FOX ENGL 043.04 Women Writers, Feminist Readers This Gateway methods course focuses on the field of feminist literary theory and criticism, teasing out its history, aims, contributions, and conflicts. In order to explore questions and theories surrounding the vexed category of âwomenâs writing,â we will read a series of literary texts that now occupy a centralâsome might say âcanonicalââplace in the feminist critical archive by authors such as Phillis Wheatley, Charlotte Bronte, Nella Larsen, Virginia Woolf, Sylvia Plath, Toni Morrison, and Julia Alvarez. At the same time, we will examine a diversity of feminist critical and theoretical approaches to each text (a mix of early and more recent work) as a way of charting its development as a field. Student work includes 3-4 papers and bi-weekly blogs.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR GAY CIMA ENGL 043.05 Empathy & Activism This course introduces students to two particular critical methodologies, performance studies and critical race studies, to investigate how nineteenth-century abolitionists used literature to fight against slavery. Weâll study theories of how sympathy and empathy function, so that we can understand abolitionist literature, from poems and plays to essays and speeches. Is empathy necessary toâor a danger toâthose who use literature to fight for human rights? Weâll trace the genealogy of abolitionist literature from the 1820s to present-day anti-human-trafficking literature, in an effort to address this question. The diverse reading list will include philosophical treatises on sympathy and political theories about citizenship as well as anti-slavery literature. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR PATRICIA O'CONNOR ENGL 043.05 Narratology In this course we will note structuralist and deconstructivist theories of narrative as we read closely, critique, and craft three major genres which employ narrative: autobiography, historical fiction, and the short story. Early in the course, students will learn how to do discourse analysis to locate and analyze spoken narratives in everyday speech following methods of linguists William Labov and Deborah Tannen. Students will tape, transcribe, and analyze sample conversations for their narrative thrust. Students will also read the brief autobiography of Eudora Welty --One Writerâs Beginnings; the historical fiction novel Out of the Dust (written as a series of poems) by Karen Hesse -- and the Short Stories of Breece DâJ Pancake. Students will explore these texts as well as the oral and written narratives collected from everyday life using structural approaches as found in On Narratology by Mieke Bal and Michael ToolanâNarrative: A Critical Linguistic Introduction. Using the work of Wolfgang Iser in Prospecting we next delve into reader-response theory wherein we react to how writers set up for readerly engagement as they and we compose texts. Thus, in the course we will move from a structuralist understanding into a variety of deconstructive positionings as we examine the narrative choices made by the authors, paying particular attention to matters of external and /or internal narration, focalization, and manipulation of time. As a major project, we will put all this exploration together as students use historical information, print media, and digital archives as well as family stories, to produce a piece of historical fiction. Diana Hackerâs A Pocket Style Manual will be used for matters of clarity and style in writing and for producing MLA citation forms where appropriate.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: This course is an introduction to some of the prevailing theories and methods of cultural criticism practiced in Europe and America in the 20th century. We will discuss why critical cultural analysis is important and why and how to think theoretically. Methods covered will include Marxism, Feminism, Postmodernism, Postcolonialism, Cultural Studies and Media Literacy, among others.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR ANDREW RUBIN ENGL 043.07 Criticism between Culture and System This course introduces students to the major theoretical development that have informed cultural and English studies in the twentieth-first century. It introduces students to a range of conceptual developments and a critical vocabulary that has fundamentally transformed English Studies to include the critical examination of culture, and analyzed how exactly culture functions as a process in its relationship to power, knowledge and society. In this seminar we shall examine how the study of culture in general and literary texts in particular has been shaped by attending developments by major intellectual movements such as psychoanalysis, structuralism, and post-structuralism, feminism, postcolonialism, and Western Marxism. We shall investigate and analyze the tensions between these developments and cultural studies, focusing on subjects such as the relationship between aesthetics and politics, the politics of cultural representation, hegemony (how certain ideas prevail over others in non-totalitarian societies), and the way that so-called Western narratives contribute to the management, administration, and control of territories abroad. The courseâs aim is to introduce the role and responsibilities of the literary and cultural critic to the studentâone who is intellectually committed not to rehearsing methods of interpretation, but, on the contrary, responsible for actively engaging the relationship between the various strategies of interpretation and the text itself in order to do more than simply criticize a textâs ideological function. Readings in Hegel, Marx, Nietzsche, Freud, Saussure, LukÃ¡cs, Gramsci, Benjamin, Adorno, Lacan, Althusser, Foucault, Derrida, Said, Jameson, Spivak, Zizek, Butler and Agamben.

title: Gateway: Mod &/or Postmodern Lit
description: Fall 2010 Prof. Caetlin Benson-Allott This course is an introduction to the history of film, its technological, cultural, and economic development over the past 115 years. It is also a survey in the discipline of film studies; weâll read famous and foundation articles in the field of film and media studies and analyze how our predecessors have chosen to approach motion picturesâand what their approaches exclude or overlook. This course will focus specifically on the relationship of technology to filmâs social and political significance in the US in order to examine the role it plays in the âculture industries.â Film and the cinema may seem like well-established cultural institutions today, but they have been the newest of new mediaâmultiple times! Although ânew mediaâ often seems synonymous with âdigital mediaâ in contemporary discourse, in fact thereâs nothing inherently digital or electronic about the new or newness. This course will study the evolution of film as a new medium, from its birth in the 1890s through its popular and technical reinventions and the (alleged) fantastical future of digital cinema. By studying various incarnations of ânewnessâ in films like Singin' in the Rain, Cloverfield, and Coraline, we will come to understand how ânew mediaâ have always already been with us and how the term is deployed strategically to serve different economic, cultural, and political ends.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: This course will be held on Georgetown's Qatar campus. Please contact the Office of International Programs for further information.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: This course will be held on Georgetown's Qatar campus. Please see the Office of International Programs for further information.

title: Medieval British Literature
description: This course will involve readings in medieval English literature, both in translation and in the original Middle English. Its readings will range from the Old English epic poem Beowulf, through such Middle English works as Sir Gawain and the Green Knight, numerous lyric poems, and selections from Chaucer's Canterbury Tales, and including as well works reflecting late medieval religious practices, Julian of Norwich's Revelations, the Cloud of Unknowing, and the Book of Margery Kempe. It will include three papers and a final exam, and there will be a creative option offered for two of the papers.

title: Chaucer
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR JOHN HIRSH ENGL 108 Chaucer A reading and discussion of Chaucer's great (if unfinished) master-work, the Canterbury Tales, a work of first importance to anyone who reads (or writes) narrative fiction. Students will read the work in original Middle English in which Chaucer wrote it, and discuss it in small and in large groups, focusing upon the work's narrative strategies, intellectual constructions, and artistic accomplishment. They will also write, in a variety of ways, about those aspects which particularly take their interest.

title: English Literary Renaissance
description: This course surveys the literature of Tudor England in its abundance, exuberance, and generic variety. Readings include lyric and narrative poetry, narrative and critical prose, and (of course) the drama. A particular focus will be on the Renaissance poetic theory and on the self-conscious engagement by writers of the period with the status of English as a literary language. Among the topics we will consider are the anxiety about the capacity of the English language for effective literary presentation, controversies over the expansion of the lexicon through borrowing and conversion, and the eventual âTriumph of the English Language.â TEXTS (provisional) The Longman Anthology of British Literature, Volume 1B: The Early Modern Period (2nd Edition), eds. David Damrosch, Clare Carroll, Constance Jordan Shakespeare, The Complete Sonnets and Poems, ed. Colin Burrow An Anthology of Elizabethan Prose Fiction, ed. Paul Salzman Thomas Nashe, The Unfortunate Traveller and Other Works, ed. J. B. Steane Thomas Kyd, The Spanish Tragedy, ed. David Bevington Christopher Marlowe, Edward the Second, ed. Charles R. Forker Shakeseare, The Winterâs Tale, ed. Stephen Orgel

title: Shakespeare
description: SUMMER 2011 Professor Michael Collins The course will look closely at four of Shakespeareâs best known plays, two comedies (As You Like It and The Merchant of Venice) and two tragedies (Hamlet and King Lear). It will focus largely (but not exclusively) on two significant questions: how might these plays have been brought to life by actors on a stage in Shakespeareâs time and how might they continue to be brought to life by actors in our own time. Through the process of seeking an answer to that question the course will hope also to discover what the plays of Shakespeare, on the page and on the stage, might say to us now, some five hundred years after they were written. SUMMER 2010; FALL 2010 PROFESSOR MICHAEL COLLINS Shakespeare The course will look closely at five of Shakespeareâs best known plays, including King Lear. It will focus largely (but not exclusively) on two significant questions: how might these plays have been brought to life by actors on a stage in Shakespeareâs time and how might they continue to be brought to life by actors in our own time. Through the process of seeking an answer to that question the course will hope also to discover what the plays of Shakespeare, on the page and on the stage, might say to us now, some five hundred years after they were written. Students will be asked to take part in a number of acting exercises throughout the semester to enhance their understanding of how a script is brought to life on the stage (the course carries four credits in the fall in order to provide them an opportunity to do so) and also to attend a production of a Shakespeare play (probably Allâs Well that Ends Well at the Shakespeare Theatre). A lab fee for the course will cover the cost of the ticket.

title: Women in Shakespeare
description: This course will focus on Shakespeareâs treatment of female characters in eight different plays across his playwriting career: Titus Andronicus, Richard III, The Merchant of Venice, Much Ado About Nothing, Twelfth Night, Othello, and Antony and Cleopatra. All plays will be read in the context of their source material and some modern criticism. What function do these characters serve in each play? How do they conform to or overturn assumptions about women in the early modern period? What does it mean in this period to be voicing women onstage, especially in light of the fact that all these parts were played by men? We will also be reading John Websterâs Duchess of Malfi at the end of the semester to situate Shakespeareâs women in the context of other early modern playwrights. Assignments will include short reading responses to each play, one research paper, and scene performances.

title: The Problem Plays
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR CARTER HAILEY ENGL 132 The Problem Plays The term âproblem playâ was originally applied to a new, more realistic form of drama that emerged in Europe in the second half of the nineteenth century. These plays concerned themselves with contentious social issues, often through the lens of a moral choice facing one or more characters. Later, the term came to be applied to a group of Shakespearean playsâusually including Allâs Well That Ends Well, Measure for Measure, and Troilus and Cressidaâbut here the problem is initially one of genre. What kinds of plays are they? Are they comedies, histories, or tragedies? (Early title pages and prefatory matter from Troilus and Cressida, for example, claim at one time or another that it is each of the three!) In this course weâll range widely in the Shakespearean oeuvre, from the early comedy The Taming of the Shrew to the late romance The Winterâs Tale, with additional texts to include The Merchant of Venice, Henry the Fourth Part 1, Othello, Allâs Well, and Measure for Measure. In examining these works weâll consider both social and generic definitions of âproblem play,â and ways in which the two senses may intersect or overlap. We will focus particular attention on the evolving forms of dramatic language Shakespeare deploys in his explorations of various problemsâsexual, political, religious, and genericâinherent in the human drama. Course members will be expected to attend a live production of Allâs Well That Ends Well at The Shakespeare Theatre (a lab fee is charged to cover the cost of the ticket).

title: Sem: Shakespeare's Exotic Romances
description: From puckish sprites to island savages, from man-eating bears to speaking statues, Shakespeareâs plays are populated with otherworldly or extraordinary elements that we might term exotic. Interestingly, most of these examples of exoticism occur within the context of romance, understood in two primary senses: as a genre encompassing Shakespeareâs last four plays, and as a gendered narrative trajectory of the knight-wins-damsel variety. Rather than simply survey his last plays, this course will try to deconstruct that generic category by drawing connections to two earlier plays and asking how Shakespeare makes exotic the conventions of romance. There will be a strong theoretical/critical component, as well as some archival research. Primary texts may include A Midsummer Nightâs Dream, Othello, A Winterâs Tale, Pericles, and The Tempest.

title: Milton
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR JASON ROSENBLATT ENGL 136 Milton By starting with the early literary productions in poetry and prose and concluding with /Samson Agonistes/, written in old age, when Milton, like his champion, was blind and disillusioned, we will give attention to the development of his literary powers. But we will spend most of our time on /Paradise Lost/. With all due reverence for Chaucer's /Canterbury Tales /and Shakespeare's /Hamlet/ and /King Lear/, Milton's epic is the greatest single work ever written in English. We will try to understand it mostly on our own but also with help from a few of the best contemporary essays.

title: Shakespeare's Tragedies
description: This class will explore Shakespeareâs many different approaches to tragedy -- its causes, its effects, its ramifications -- through reading eight of his plays across the semester. These will include Titus Andronicus, Romeo and Juliet, Richard II, Hamlet, Othello, King Lear, Coriolanus, and Antony and Cleopatra. What makes these plays tragic? How do they both conform to and diverge from our ideas of what tragedy is? What do they say about ideas of tragedy in Shakespeareâs own time? How do performance choices and cultural pressures influence the way we view them? As a point of comparison, we will begin by reading Thomas Kydâs enormously successful play, The Spanish Tragedy, which influenced a number of other plays from this period, including several of Shakespeareâs. Plays will be read in conjunction with contextual material and modern criticism. This class will have a strong performance element -- all students will be required to do planned scene performances as well as read aloud regularly in class. Assignments will include short reading responses to each play, one research paper, and scene performances.

title: Literature of the Atlantic Empire
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR DENNIS TODD ENGL 152 Literature of the Atlantic Empire: From Colonies to Republic In this course, we will examine some British, Native American, Anglo-American, African-British, and American literature that grew out of the collision of cultures in the Americas in the late seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, particularly, though not exclusively, in the Chesapeake and Caribbean regions. In these fictional and non-fictional narratives and poems, we will be interested in several interrelated concerns: the construction of ideas of civilization and savagery; the working out of the dynamics of slavery and freedom (understood historically and economically, but also as figures for psychological and spiritual processes); and the articulation of selfhood and subjectivity in a colonial context. In addition, we will pay attention to the question of whether there are significant and growing differences between Old World and New World identities. Among the works we will read are Oroonoko; Robinson Crusoe; Byrdâs Secret History; Equianoâs Interesting Narrative of the Life; Franklinâs Autogiography; Fosterâs The Coquette; Brownâs Wieland; and James Fenimore Cooperâs The Prairie. There will be several shorter papers, two longer papers, and a final exam.

title: Colonial Rise of the Novel
description: In The Colonial Rise of the Novel, Firdous Azim states, âthe novel is an imperialist project based on the forceful eradication and obliteration of the Otherâ (37). This course will explore the implications of this statement as it relates to eighteenth-century British novelists of both genders and the âOthersâ described in their texts. We will consider the validity of Azimâs claim as well as excavate the origins of the modern novel form by using a very specific thematic lens: colonialism. Since a significant number of the canonical and non-canonical texts that are associated with the origins and rise of the novel frequently employ the colonial Otherâboth as geographic space and narrative individualâwe will approach the novelâs development as a form that went hand-in-hand with Britainâs plan for colonial expansion. The novel, perhaps, provided Britons with justification and inspiration for this plan. To explore these ends we will consider eighteenth-century British novelistsâ obsessions with colonialismâobsessions that, curiously, begin and reach their height in novels written by British women. Readings will include some of the following: Aphra Behnâs Oroonoko, Daniel Defoeâs Robinson Crusoe, Jonathan Swiftâs Gulliverâs Travels, William Beckfordâs Vathek, Jane Austenâs Mansfield Park, Charlotte Bronteâs Jane Eyre, Anonymous The Woman of Colour, Samuel Johnsonâs The History of Rasselas, Unca Eliza Winkfield The Female American as well as critical essays by F.R. Leavis, Ian Watt, Michael Mckeon, Edward Said, Firdous Azim, Joseph Lew and Gayatri Spivak.

title: Reading American Sentiment
description: FALL 2010 ENGL 172 READING AMERICAN MELODRAMA This class introduces students to early nineteenth-century popular culture through the study of its most popular form: melodrama. With its familial narratives, depictions of bodily torture, scenes of criminal conduct and female sexual endangerment, expressions of excessive emotion, and plain themes of good and evil, melodrama emerged as a behavioral paradigm, manifest in the drama, minstrel shows, and literature of a newly urbanized America. We shall focus on melodramaâs voyeuristic appeal and pay special attention to melodramaâs relationship to the body, gender roles, working-class culture, and race. Readings will include Stowe Uncle Tomâs Cabin, Melville Pierre or the Ambiguities, Hawthorne The Blithedale Romance, Smith The Drunkard, Boucicault The Octoroon. Screenings include Uncle Tomâs Cabin, Birth of a Nation, Bamboozled.

title: Representing Revolution: 1689-1789
description: The course will read widely in English and American literature from the 17th to the 19th centuries, focusing on texts in which the tensions of the quest for individual libertyâas against the need for social order--are dramatized and explored. It will range from about 1620 (the Pilgrims seek religious freedom in America) to 1865, the end of the American Civil War. The central focus will move from Englandâs âGlorious Revolutionâ of 1688 to the outbreak of the French revolution in 1789. Thus, three revolutionary eras will be considered: the English, the American and the French. This is a good deal of history and politics, but most of the readings are more strictly âliterary.â We will we see how these large issues of social and political change are reflected in the novels and poems people were reading for entertainmentâand how they in turn affected the larger debates. England is a country that defined itself during this period in opposition to revolutionary change. America, by contrast, came to define itself precisely as the land of revolution. Yet almost all of the authors we will be reading considered themselves to be âEnglishâ for much of this time, and all of course wrote in English. A major goal of the course is to mix together very similar English writings that we usually keep separate because of national boundaries. Major authors and texts: Bradford, Of Plymouth Plantations, Samuel Butler, Hudibras (1663), Milton, Paradise Lost, Book I (1667), Behn, Oroonoko (1688), John Gay, The Beggarsâ Opera (1728), Jonathan Swift, âA Modest Proposalâ (1729), Paine, The Crisis (1776), Jefferson, âA Declaration of Independenceâ (1776), Blake, America: A Prophecy (1795), Wordsworth, The Prelude, Books IX-XI (âResidence in Franceâ), Charles Brockden Brown, Wieland (1798), Anna Barbauld, âEighteen Hundred and Eleven,â Irving, âRip Van Winkleâ (1819), Hawthorne, âMy Kinsman, Major Molineuxâ (1832), Whittier, âMassachusetts to Virginiaâ (1843), Thoreau, âOn the Duty of Civil Disobedienceâ (1849), Dickens, A Tale of Two Cities (1859), Julia Ward Howe, âBattle Hymn of the Republicâ (1862), Whitman, Drum-Taps (1865).

title: Dickens
description: A close reading of three major novels by Charles Dickens: Little Dorrit (1855 â 1857), A Tale of Two Cities (1859), and Great Expectations (1860 â 1861) in which we will consider the writer in the context of his era and the practice of fiction in the mid-nineteenth century. Discussion will dominate class time with students reporting on their analytical, inventive, and library research projects. There will not be any exams as such, but there will be short, factual quizzes on the dayâs reading which will begin each class session. The semester ends with a slightly longer essay which discusses a specific aspect of all three novels.

title: Jane Austen
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR PATRICK O'MALLEY ENGL 183 Jane Austen In this course, we will read all of Austenâs completed novels (Northanger Abbey, Sense and Sensibility, Pride and Prejudice, Mansfield Park, Emma, and Persuasion) as well as such other works as Lady Susan and Sanditon. We will examine such persistent questions in Austenâs work as the relationship between authorship and gender, the class structure, obedience and transgression, national identity, and the development of a literary tradition. In addition, we will read critical appraisals of Austenâs influence from nineteenth-century sources to such early twentieth-century admirers as Virginia Woolf to later analyses of Austenâs literary and political position. Regular participation in class discussions and frequent critical writing projects will be required.

title: Sem: Wordsworth and Coleridge, Lyrical Ballads
description: This course will examine the volume which inaugurated Romanticism in British literature: Wordsworth and Coleridgeâs Lyrical Ballads. This will be a highly demanding course entailing frequent timed essay assignments. Metrical analysis will form an essential part of the course, alongside close examination of semantic and formal elements of poetic practice. For that reason it will be essential for all students to memorise the contents of Philip Hobsbaumâs primer on Metre, Rhythm, and Verse Form on which there will be several tests. The emphasis throughout will be on close examination of the text, and group discussion in which everyone is expected to participate. In addition, students are expected to write occasional response papers which will be analysed and discussed in class. There will be writing assignments which will contribute to overall assessment.

title: Sem: Victorian Fiction
description: Conducted as a seminar with expectations of class participation, this course will examine the range and development of the British novelâboth its techniques and its broad range of subject matterâthroughout the reign of Queen Victoria (1837-1901). We will do close textual readings within the historical context, as well as developing familiarity with a wide variety of critical approaches (including Marxist, feminist, narratological, New Historical, psychoanalytic, queer theoretical, and post-colonial). Students will write critical response papers, conduct class in collaboration with others, and write an original research paper as a culminating project. Required texts: Elizabeth Gaskell, Mary Barton (Oxford World Classics); Charles Dickens, Hard Times (Norton Critical); George Eliot, The Mill on the Floss (Norton Critical); Mary Elizabeth Braddon, Lady Audleyâs Secret (Oxford World Classics); Oscar Wilde, The Picture of Dorian Gray (Norton Critical); Joseph Conrad, Lord Jim (Oxford World Classics). Recommended texts: Carolyn Lesniak, Working Fictions: A Genealogy of the Victorian Novel (Duke U P); Gail Marshall, Victorian Fiction (Bloomsbury Press USA). Please note: because many of the books are long, students will be expected to acquire the specific texts ordered for the class.

title: Seminar: Victorian Sexualities
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR PATRICK O'MALLEY ENGL 192 Seminar: Victorian Sexualities The Victorian era is frequently associated with tremendous prudery and avoidance of all things sexual. Yet recent criticism of the literature and culture of the middle and late nineteenth century has pointed out that the famous Victorian prudery meant not so much that people stopped talking and writing about sex and sexuality but that people talked and wrote about them moreâif only to describe precisely what it was that people werenât supposed to be thinking about. In this course, we will examine the different ways that Victorian literature and theory addressed the many questions of sexual transgression that arose in the period, including divorce, adultery, bigamy, homosexuality, and prostitution. It will focus on the Victorian novel, exploring how central sexual and erotic cross-currents are to the development of the canonical novel itself, but alongside the novels we will also be reading poetry and a wide selection of other genres, from the short story to the newspaper exposÃ© to the diary to the case study. Throughout the entire course, we will devote particular attention to the ways that the literature of the period relates sexual transgression to such other questions as gender roles, national identity, the class structure, religion, and literary history. Authors will include Anne Lister, Mary Elizabeth Braddon, Oscar Wilde, Walter Pater, Henry James, Christina Rossetti, Sheridan Le Fanu, Michael Field, Sarah Grand, Havelock Ellis, and Edward Carpenter. As a four-credit class, this course will have a substantial theoretical component, and theoretical and historical accounts may include works by Michel Foucault, Eve Kosofsky Sedgwick, Sharon Marcus, and James Kincaid. Also, students will be expected to engage in significant archival research in preparation for a final paper that contextualizes representations of sexuality within their cultural and historical context.

title: 19th Century US Lit: Class, Poverty, and the American Dream
description: As scholars routinely note, class remains an under-studied category in 19th- century U. S. literature and culture. This course will examine literary and cultural representations of social class, and will explore how class as literary construction is inseparable from discourses of race, gender, ethnicity, sexuality, nation, and empire. Overall in the course, we will explore the American Dream of unencumbered class mobility as a particular instance of what critic Lauren Berlant terms "national fantasy," a utopian national ideal at once deemed universally accessible and inscribed by a variety of social differences. We will examine the way 19th-century literary texts both reproduce, and expose contradictions within, this governing ideology of âAmericannessâ: we will consider the ways in which these texts measure the distance between utopian ideal and historical ârealityâ and register what Richard Sennett and Jonathan Cobb term the âhidden injuries of classââinvisible psychic and social costs of subscription to this national ideal. Course readings include literary texts by George Foster, Frank Webb, Frederick Douglass, Thomas Skidmore, Sarah Bagley and other New England millworkers who contributed to the Lowell Offering and the Voice of Industry, Nathaniel Hawthorne, Herman Melville, Rebecca Harding Davis, Horatio Alger, Stephen Crane, Edith Wharton, and Anzia Yezierska. We will also read selected theoretical, critical, and historical articles addressing various aspects of class formation and the construction of class subjectivities in the nineteenth-century U. S. We will end the course by considering representations of class in contemporary film and television, to assess how (to quote the title of the influential series of articles (2005) in the New York Times) âclass mattersâ in contemporary U. S. culture, and to situate U. S. class relations and identities in a global context.

title: 19th Century African American Literature
description: The purpose of this course is to study African American literary and cultural production from its beginnings in the 18th century until the publication of W.E.B. Du Boisâ seminal text, The Souls of Black Folk, in 1903. As we examine how African American writers transformed the different literary modes in which they wrote, we will also consider how the literary texts articulate the cultural, political, and national concerns that shaped 18th and 19th century America. Throughout the semester, we will return repeatedly to issues of race, class, gender, sexuality, and religion to consider why these authors are concerned with these particular matters. We will think energetically about the historicity of the texts, and the textuality of history; in other words, we will consider how history enriches our understanding of the texts, and how the texts enrich our understanding of history. In addition to the primary texts and literary and cultural criticism that we will read, we also will engage historical documents. Slavery, emancipation, Reconstruction, and the suffragist movement are just a few examples of the historical moments we must interrogate to make our study most productive. While previous familiarity with African American literature is not a requirement for this course, regular attendance, active reading, and a firm commitment to succeed are. Students enrolling in this course should expect to read the primary works by the following authors: Lucy Terry, Phillis Wheatley, David Walker, Maria Stewart, Frederick Douglass, Harriet Jacobs, Harriet Wilson, Sojourner Truth, Booker T. Washington, Anna Julia Cooper, and W.E.B. Du Bois. Potential Assignments/Grade determination: class participation (15%), two in-class examinations (40%), one paper (6 pages) (15%), 3-4 quizzes (15%), and 1 final exercise (15%).

title: 20th C Afr Amer Literature
description: FALL 2010 PATTERSON, ROBERT J. 20C AFRICAN AMERICAN LITERATURE AND CRITICISM This course will examine African American literature and literary criticism from the mid-twentieth century until the present. To enhance our study of African American literary criticism, we will read novels, poems, plays, and short stories, analyzing the ways in which they influence and are influenced by the various modes of criticism that we are investigating. Additionally, we will read two types of literary criticismâtexts that are about literature in general, and texts that are about the specific literary texts we are reading. Our goal is to consider and understand the key developments and conversations that have defined and transformed the field of African American literary and cultural studies. While we will move chronologically through these periods, we will not consider African American literary criticism or literature as developing linearly. Finally, we will pay close attention to the historical and cultural contexts and activist movements that frame our texts and that our texts frame. Although previous familiarity with African American literature is not a requirement for this course, regular attendance, active and engaged reading, and a firm commitment to succeed are! This course is reading intensive and will have quizzes regularly. Potential authors include: Baldwin, Petry, Hansberry, Gaines, Morrison, Walker, Naylor, Kenan, Jones, Wade, and Whitehead.

title: American Detective Fiction
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR MAUREEN CORRIGAN ENGL 208 American Detective Fiction As the twentieth century began, a new literary form emerged, a kind of urban poetry constructed out of images of gals, guts, guns, and gumshoes with a drinking problem and a fondness for chivalric myth. This course explores the evolution of American hard-boiled detective fiction and the larger mysteries lurking in, (to use Raymond Chandlerâs immortal phrase) âa world gone wrong.â Beginning with the âurban shockâ tales of Edgar Allan Poe and proceeding into the Golden Age British mystery tradition, this course goes on to discuss pulp fiction detective stories of the teens and twenties. Hard boiled detective fiction begins in earnest with the short stories of Dashiell Hammett and his classic novel, The Maltese Falcon. Raymond Chandler and Ross Macdonald--the other two members of The Holy Trinity of Crime Fiction--are represented by The Big Sleep and Black Money, respectively. Other hard-boiled writers whose work will be covered include: James M. Cain, Mickey Spillane, Robert B. Parker, Sara Paretsky, and Richard Stevenson. In addition, we will discuss highlights of the complementary tradition of film noir, including the classic films: âDouble Indemnity,â âThe Maltese Falcon,â âMildred Pierce,â âThe Big Heatâ and âA Touch of Evil.â

title: New York Stories
description: SUMMER 2010 English 209: âNew York Stories: Urban Experience in Twentieth-Century Americaâ Professor Maureen Corrigan Office: New North 405/Phone: 687-9517/ New York City is, arguably, the cultural capital of twentieth- (and, so far), twenty-first century Americaâthe center of literature, music, art, architecture, and fashion as well as the focal point for political debates on the viability of cities, immigration, and the meaning of America itself. This course proposes to study autobiographical, fictional, non-fictional, poetic, and cinematic accounts of twentieth-century life in New York City in order to understand the dreams and nightmares embodied by the city in the popular imagination. Beginning with the first decades of the twentieth century, we will investigate the decline of âold guardâ aristocratic society in the early 1900s and the nature of the immigrant experience in New York. The course will then focus on the Jazz Age when New York artists, writers, and architects shaped a new ethos of modernity that would define âThe American Century.â Attention will also be devoted to the various âcounter-culturalâ movements (e.g. the rise of the American Communist Party, the Beat Movement) that erupted in New York City during the early and middle decades of the last century. The course will conclude with contemporary dystopian and stubbornly utopian predictions about the future of New York City and urban life in America. âThereâs only one city that belongs to the whole country, and thatâs New York.â William Dean Howells âNew York is more full of reflections than of itself.â Zelda Fitzgerald âIt is an ugly city, a dirty city. Its climate is a scandal. Its politics are used to frighten children. Its traffic is madness. Its competition is murderous. But there is one thing about itâonce you have lived in New York and it has become your home, no other place is good enough.â John Steinbeck Texts: Edith Wharton, The House of Mirth Alfred Kazin, A Walker in the City F. Scott Fitzgerald, The Great Gatsby Pete Hamill, A Drinking Life Jan Morris, Manhattan, 1945 Ann Petry, The Street E.B. White, Here is New York Anatole Broyard, Kafka Was the Rage Selections from: Ann Douglas, Terrible Honesty; Joseph Mitchell, McSorleyâs Wonderful Saloon, The Poetry of the Harlem Renaissance, Vivian Gornick, The Romance of American Communism Films: A Tree Grows In Brooklyn The Clock The Out-of-Towners Annie Hall Do the Right Thing Spring 2010 Professor Maureen Corrigan New York City is, arguably the cultural capital of twentieth- (and, so far), twenty-first century America - the center of literature, music, art, architecture, and fashion as well as the focal point for political debates on the viability of cities, immigration, and the meaning of America itself. This course proposes to study autobiographical, fictional, non-fictional, poetic and cinematic accounts of twentieth-century life in New York City in order to understand the dreams and nightmares embodied by the city in the popular imagination. Beginning with the first decades of the twentieth century, we will investigate the decline of the "old guard" aristocratic society in the early 1900s and the nature of the immigrant experience in New York. The course will then focus on the Jazz Age when New York artists, writers, and architects shaped a new ethos of modernity that would define "The American Century." Attention will also be devoted to the various "counter-cultural" movements (e.g. the rise of the American Communist Part, the Beat Movement) that erupted in New York City during the early and middle decades of the last century. The course will conclude with contemporary dystopian and stubbonly utopian predictions about the future of New York City and urban life in America.

But there is one thing about itâonce you have lived in New York and it has become your home, no other place is good enough.â John Steinbeck Texts: Edith Wharton, The House of Mirth Alfred Kazin, A Walker in the City F. Scott Fitzgerald, The Great Gatsby Pete Hamill, A Drinking Life Jan Morris, Manhattan, 1945 Ann Petry, The Street E.B. White, Here is New York Anatole Broyard, Kafka Was the Rage Selections from: Ann Douglas, Terrible Honesty; Joseph Mitchell, McSorleyâs Wonderful Saloon, The Poetry of the Harlem Renaissance, Vivian Gornick, The Romance of American Communism Films: A Tree Grows In Brooklyn The Clock The Out-of-Towners Annie Hall Do the Right Thing FALL 2010 Professor Maureen Corrigan New York City is, arguably the cultural capital of twentieth- (and, so far), twenty-first century America - the center of literature, music, art, architecture, and fashion as well as the focal point for political debates on the viability of cities, immigration, and the meaning of America itself. This course proposes to study autobiographical, fictional, non-fictional, poetic and cinematic accounts of twentieth-century life in New York City in order to understand the dreams and nightmares embodied by the city in the popular imagination. Beginning with the first decades of the twentieth century, we will investigate the decline of the "old guard" aristocratic society in the early 1900s and the nature of the immigrant experience in New York. The course will then focus on the Jazz Age when New York artists, writers, and architects shaped a new ethos of modernity that would define "The American Century." Attention will also be devoted to the various "counter-cultural" movements (e.g. the rise of the American Communist Part, the Beat Movement) that erupted in New York City during the early and middle decades of the last century. The course will conclude with contemporary dystopian and stubbonly utopian predictions about the future of New York City and urban life in America. Its climate is a scandal. Its politics are used to frighten children. Its traffic is madness. Its competition is murderous. title: Harlem and the Renaissance
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR R. SCOTT HEATH ENGL 216 Harlem & the Renaissance In 1925, Alain Locke described the emergence of a generation of black artists and intellectuals who would represent the marginalized âfolkâ in the United States and who would serve as a bridge into the possibility of equitable American citizenship. This âNew Negroâ was characterized as a cultural vanguard responsible for the leadership not only of the African American community but also of oppressed people of color across the planet. In this course we will approach the Harlem Renaissance as a nexus of black cosmopolitanism beginning with an analysis of some of its central literary texts and crossing media to examine some of the visual and popular work of the creative period.
