While unpacking the prose and poetry of Langston Hughes, Nella Larsen, Claude McKay, and other integral figures, we will trace the conceptualization of Harlem itself as a virtual ârace capital,â and we will deal with several more contemporary representations of the aesthetic movement, its political ramifications, and its primary operative space. Considering recent critical disputes about the relative success or failure of this âHarlem Renaissance,â we will explore the limitations of this nomenclature. Through a series of readings, discussions, and essay assignments we will address the recurring themes of the movement with a particular focus on transnational mobility and transcultural exchange. We will investigate the location of black cultural production as it is simultaneously equated with and placed in opposition to conventional notions of Americannessâ-a context in which the New Negro is realized as both producer and product, commodifier and commodified body.

title: Seminar: Transnational Modernisms
description: In what way does the return of modernity in the contemporary discussions of globalization reshape our understanding of modernism? How does the renewed interest in modernity unsettle the opposition between postmodernism and 'classical' modernism, and how is this revived interest in the modernist project implicated in the shared and discrepant experiences of globalization? Are we to accept the argument that the historic avant-garde emerged out of social encounters between writers from the metropole and immigrants from the periphery in the early twentieth century? Did these structured experience and new realities perform the cultural work of engendering and mobilizing dissenting, or alternative transnational cultural formations; or were these para-national formations absorbed into the dominant culture by succeeding generations? How do critical conceptions of 'alternative modernisms,' 'delayed' modernisms, or even 'liquid' modernisms, contribute to a critical understanding of modernism's surprising come back? And finally, what, if anything at all, is at stake in situating modernism as the very aftermath of its successor, postmodernism? Examining the genealogies of modernisms as it traverses national, colonial, and postcolonial cultures, this seminar analyzes how modernist and postcolonial literatures have unsettled the conventional and critical distinctions between modernism and postmodernism. Through our readings of texts by Joseph Conrad, Samuel Beckett, V. S. Naipaul, Al-Tayyib Salih, Kazuo Ishiguro, Elias Khoury, Roberto BolaÃ±o, Zadie Smith, and W.G. Sebald, and among others, we shall examine and question the extent to which the so-called 'return' of transnational modernisms has become a critical element in a decisively new, historical phase of empire, globalization, and cosmopolitanism--in the age of digital reproduction. This seminar requires active particpation, one class presentation, weekly contributions to the seminar's blog or wiki, and several essays.

title: Asian Americans and the Public Sphere
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR CHRISTOPHER SHINN ENGL 222 Asian Americans and the Public Sphere This class will address issues concerning power and visibility in public life for Asian Americans in the 21st century. We will make an informed historical and cultural evaluation of the critical factors that have led to being perennially understood as an âinvisible minorityâ and propose real-world solutions to reverse this trend. The class will center on politics, culture, media and representation with some important critical discussions about government and the public sphere. Using Asian American literature, performance art, multimedia projects, manga, television and film, we will observe the limits and opportunities that accompany entrance into the U.S. cultural mainstream. Readings may include works by Jessica Hagedorn, Adrian Tomine, Catherine Liu, Sesshu Foster, David Henry Hwang, Karen Yamashita, Garrett Hongo and Kaiji Kawaguchi. We will be viewing recently acclaimed films by Asian Americans such as Tze Chunâs Children of Invention, Frances Hsueh and Steven Hahnâs Party, and Greg Pakâs Robot Stories, among others. Requirements will consist of two short essays, a midterm exam, a class presentation and a final research paper or final exam.

title: Survey of Latino Literature
description: English 225 offers students chronologically organized surveys of literary and other cultural products by US-based writers and artists of âLatino/Hispanicâ descent and of non-literary, mostly critical and scholarly writings which position US Latino/a literary and cultural production in larger, more complex, inter-disciplinary contexts. These latter include fields as diverse and as intersecting as history, politics, geography, linguistics and popular- and mass-cultural studies, as well as inter-disciplines as established, inter-active, and evolving as American, Latin American, Cultural, Gender and Queer Studies. As an introduction both to a set of texts and artifacts which in part comprise the object(s) of âUS Latino/a Literary and Cultural Studies,â and to the very field and practice of study which names itself thus, English 225 challenges students to think, and work, outside the familiar terrain explored by more traditional courses in literary history and interpretation. Literary writers may include MarÃ­a Amparo Ruiz de Burton, JosÃ© MartÃ­, Arturo Islas, Piri Thomas, Richard RodrÃ­guez, Esmeralda Santiago, Edwidge Danticat, and Junot DÃ­az. Visual, pop- and mass-cultural work might include musical recordings and performances by Albita RodrÃ­guez, Celia Cruz, Tish Hinojosa and Wyclef Jean; films like The Ballad of Gregorio Cortez, Zoot Suit, Before Night Falls and PiÃ±ero; and television material like Â¿QuÃ© Pasa USA? and Ugly Betty.

title: Asian America: the Local and the Global
description: In this course we will read recently acclaimed works of fiction based in Hawaii, California, India, Sri Lanka, Canada, and Korea. We will discuss how Asian diasporas have endured postcolonial histories, civil and imperialist wars, transcontinental migrations, and global alienation. We will pay particular attention to three modes of claiming citizenshipâthe indigenous, national, and globalâand examine how authors such as Michael Ondaatje, Karen Tei Yamashita, and Kiran Desai reimagine community in the global capitalist era. And finally we will consider how âAsian Americaâ has been redefined in the twenty-first century. The course operates primarily through discussion and close reading; students will be asked to submit short weekly writing analyses, raise questions about the reading assigned for that day, and submit two longer essays. The course particularly privileges engagement, inquiry, and careful analysis.

title: 20th Century Black Women Writers
description: In the twentieth-century, African American women writers participate in the enterprise of self-definition and self-representation while challenging society's constructions of Black subjectivity. By documenting and revealing African American culture and history in their writings, Black women writers also serve as cultural and historical conservators. Beginning with Larsen's modernist novel, we will examine representative texts by contemporary African American women from a variety of critical approaches, employing especially the lens of cultural criticism, critical race theory, Black feminist thought, and new historicism. We will also read critical essays to contextualize our interrogations of the primary texts. This course is reading and writing intensive.

title: 20th Century African Literature
description: This course will offer a survey of later 20th- and early 21st-Century African literature and culture though the lens of urbanization. Through our readings of a range of genres, including fiction, film, photography, poetry, and drama, we will consider how the city centralizes the conflicts of globalization, cultural nationalism, education, gender and sexuality in the texts. How and why do so many different forms focus on the city in various African contexts? How does the representation of metropolitan space engage ongoing questions in contemporary studies of African literature, including the relationships between the rural and the urban, modernity and âtradition,â history and memory, as well as nation and diaspora. Authors will include Cyprian Ewkensi, Chinua Achebe, Zoe Wicomb, Mariama Ba, Ama Ata Aidoo, and Ousmane Sembene, among others, including the 2010 Caine Prize Winner, Olufemi Terry (who will visit our course). Course requirements will include response and critical analysis papers, group presentations, a final project, and significant in-class participation.

title: Italy/Italian: Anglophone Literature
description: This course will be held on Georgetown's Villa la Balze campus. Please see the Office of International Programs for further information.

title: Imagining America in Contemporary New Zealand Literature
description: This course will use contemporary texts from New Zealand to examine whether it is possible to have a distinctively national literature in a global culture and what the dynamics of the literature might be. In the last twenty years New Zealand literature has reflected a changing geopsychology, as the emotional links to Britain fray. Imagining America, along with the Pacific and the indigenous, are strands of the constantly reforming nation we take ourselves to be.

title: Faulkner
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR WAYNE KNOLL ENGL 249 Faulkner This course will investigate one of the greatest American literary achievements of the modern era. We will begin with Go Down, Moses, a collection of interrelated short stories as an entree into the world of Faulkner. Subsequently, we will read and discuss, in chronological order, the major works of Faulknerâs most prolific years: The Sound and the Fury; Sanctuary; As I Lay Dying; Light in August; and Absalom, Absalom. We will discuss these works in terms of predominant themes, psychological and sociological, and Faulknerâs corresponding experimentation in a rich variety of literary techniques. We will also view and discuss The Reivers in movie form. Requirements: a mid-term examination, two 5 page papers and one 10-12 page final paper (or a journal).

title: Eliot's Wasteland
description: We will begin the semester by reading several of Eliot's earlier poems to investigate both his experimentation with poetic technique as well as his thematic concerns. The course will concentrate, however, on The Waste Land. Properly to understand Eliot's opus, however, requires reading its principal allusions: Scripture (Old and New), Dante, Virgil, St. Augustine, Tarot cards, fertility gods and rituals, Grail Knight romances, Shakespeare, Thomas Kyd, Baudelaire, and the Baghavad Gita--virtually a liberal arts curriculum in itself. The final re-reading of The Waste Land, however, with allusions understood and experienced, should represent an extraordinary experience of poetic technique and literature, both East and West.

title: Beckett/Borges/Nabokov
description: In his seminal 1967 article âThe Literature of Exhaustion,â John Barth identifies Samuel Beckett, Jorge Luis Borges, and Vladimir Nabokov as the standard bearers of the new literary tradition. For Barth, Beckett, Borges, and Nabokov are artists who are technically up to date and yet able to speak to the human condition as few others. This course will examine the literary works of these three authors and their respective roles in ushering in the literary tradition we now call postmodernism. We will explore the literary and cultural contexts of their writing, a selection of their major works, and the narrative and dramatic techniques of each. Readings will include Three Novels: Malloy, Malone Dies, and The Unnamable, Waiting for Godot, The Garden of Forking Paths, The Aleph, Lolita, Pale Fire, and Ada, or Ardor.

title: Poetry and Poetics
description: We will be reading a wide range of poetry, from oral poetry and ballads to many of the canonical figures to contemporaries of varying stripes. In addition, we'll read a number of the major statements in poetics, from Plato to Shelley to the contemporary avant-garde. This field, obviously huge, will be focused by continual reference to Modernist through contemporary writing, where histories of primary and long lasting questions continue to surface. Does poetry derive from divine inspiration or personal imagination or collective deliberation? What is the relationship between poetic language and the ârealâ? Is poetry civilized or elemental? Was Plato right in kicking the poets out of his Republic? What sort of work (imaginative, emotional, social, political) can poetry do?

title: Twentieth Century Women Poets
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR LIBBIE RIFKIN ENGL 261 Twentieth Century Women Poets In this class we'll examine the writing lives of selected 20th century women poets who played significant roles in shaping the literary fields in which they worked. Specifically, we'll be studying poets who doubled as editors, critics, publishers, mentors, and activists. We will read closely the careers of two major figures--Gwendolyn Brooks and Adrienne Rich âas well as an array of other writers, from Gertrude Stein to Sylvia Plath, the women of the Harlem Renaissance to contemporary innovative African American poets. This class argues that the âtwentieth centuryâ starts early, with Emily Dickinson. We will have particularly rich connections to explore during the two periods in which women wielded the most power: Modernism (1910-30), and the re-emergence of women's poetic communities in the late 60s and 70s. This class will explore poetry written by men as well. Our broadest questions will have to do with the role gender plays in the creation, dissemination, and reception of poetry, and the role poetry plays in the creation, disruption, and reconstruction of gender ideologies.

title: London and the Modern Novel
description: FALL 2010 JESSE OAK TAYLOR ENGL 262-01 LONDON AND THE MODERN NOVEL London: once capital of the worldâs first industrial society and the center of a vast economic, military, and technological empire, now hub of global finance and cultural hybridity. London also has a special place in the literary imagination. This course will explore the relationship between the city of London and the modern novel in English. We will consider not only the way the city is imagined in fiction, but the sense in which the city itself becomes fictionalââthe product not only of human artifice in bricks and mortar but of the imagination. In the process, we will examine the emergence of the metropolis as a space of cosmopolitanism and global connection. Readings will include novels by Charles Dickens (Our Mutual Friend), Arthur Conan Doyle (The Sign of the Four), Joseph Conrad (The Secret Agent), Virginia Woolf (Mrs. Dalloway), Graham Greene (The End of the Affair), Salman Rushdie (The Satanic Verses), and Zadie Smith (White Teeth). Secondary materials will draw upon literary theory, urban studies, cosmopolitanism and postcolonial studies, and environmental studies.

title: London and the Modern Novel
description: FALL 2010 JESSE OAK TAYLOR ENGL 262-01 LONDON AND THE MODERN NOVEL London: once capital of the worldâs first industrial society and the center of a vast economic, military, and technological empire, now hub of global finance and cultural hybridity. London also has a special place in the literary imagination. This course will explore the relationship between the city of London and the modern novel in English. We will consider not only the way the city is imagined in fiction, but the sense in which the city itself becomes fictionalââthe product not only of human artifice in bricks and mortar but of the imagination. In the process, we will examine the emergence of the metropolis as a space of cosmopolitanism and global connection. Readings will include novels by Charles Dickens (Our Mutual Friend), Arthur Conan Doyle (The Sign of the Four), Joseph Conrad (The Secret Agent), Virginia Woolf (Mrs. Dalloway), Graham Greene (The End of the Affair), Salman Rushdie (The Satanic Verses), and Zadie Smith (White Teeth). Secondary materials will draw upon literary theory, urban studies, cosmopolitanism and postcolonial studies, and environmental studies.

title: American Poetry 1945-Present
description: In this course we will read poets from the United States who might be called âexperimental,â âavant-garde,â or âoppositional.â These terms are flexible and hospitable to a variety of poetic practices, but what they all suggest is an intense interest in the materiality of language and in formal innovation. Poets at work since 1945 make use of techniques such as collage, seriality, intertextual citation, procedural algorithms, typographical manipulations, punning and linguistic error in order to investigate the expressive capabilities of language formed in the American grain. This sort of poetry challenges us to read differently, to become more flexible in our expectations as to the relationships between word and meaning, language and the kinds of sense that it can make. Taking note of the major âschoolsâ of American poetry since 1945--such as Black Mountain, Beat, the San Francisco Renaissance, the New York School, Language--we will explore the ways in which the art of poetry might intersect with social practice, even as poets take up poetic forms and devices that deviate from the ideals of lyric subjectivity. Poets to be considered include Jackson MacLow, Charles Olson, Robert Creeley, Jack Spicer, George Oppen, Amiri Baraka, Susan Howe, Lyn Hejinian, John Ashbery, Barbara Guest, Harryette Mullen, and Alice Notley.

title: British Drama Since 1950
description: The production in 1956 of John Osborne's Look Back in Anger at the Royal Court Theatre in London is said to mark a turning point in the history of British theatre in the twentieth century. The plays that followed Look Back in Anger on the British stage were different in theme and usually in form from those that had been written and produced in the first half of the twentieth century, and they reflected not simply a new kind of theatre in Britain, but a new way of seeing the world outside it. The course will consider some memorable plays by important British playwrights of the last fifty to sixty years, giving attention to theme, form, and possibilities for performance. It will define British loosely, including not only such English playwrights John Osborne, Harold Pinter, Tom Stoppard, and Caryl Churchill, but also such Irish playwrights Samuel Beckett, Brian Friel, Martin McDonough, and Conor McPherson, and the South African playwright Athol Fugard. The course hopes to come to some understanding of the distinct focus and idiom of the British theatre over the last 50 to 60 years and to discover how it might reflect the values and beliefs of the world in which the plays were written and performed. Students will be asked to take part in a number of acting exercises throughout the semester to enhance their appreciation of the theatrical power of these plays. (The course carries four credits in order to provide them an opportunity to do so). They will also be asked to attend at least one performance of a relevant play in Washington.

title: Technologies of Horror
description: Many scholars and filmmakers have asked why people want their movies to scare them, which is an interesting but perhaps unanswerable philosophical question. This course accepts the enduring popularity of frightening films and asks instead how and why movies horrify us. In order to answer that question, we will examine some of the most effective scary movies of the past eighty years from a technologically- and theoretically-informed position that isolates various cultural and mechanical components and study how they contribute to a horror film. By the end of the course, we should have a better understanding of what different film cultures mean by âhorrorâ and how they have attempted to produce this state in their consumers. To that end, this course interprets âtechnologyâ broadly as machinery or equipment developed for practical purposesâin this case for the practice of scaring spectators. Thus the equipment of horror includes everything from gels and squibs to actors and their voices. It also includes social modes of perception that contribute to our spectatorship, such as Renaissance perspective and binary gender. This course wants to know how bodies and sound design, shadows and CGI all contribute to fear, disgust, and mortification at the movies

title: Italian Cinema
description: This course will be held on Georgetown's Villa la Balze campus. Please see the Office of International Programs for further information.

title: News, Story, Film
description: Some stories must be taken up again and carried furtherâalighting first in one medium only to land in another, sparking controversy and debate as they leap about the cultural landscape. In this class, we will examine why and how specific kinds of events become stories, and in turn modern forms of mass entertainment. Why and how, for example, did the secret bombings of Cambodia undergo the double transformation of history into story, story into Hollywood film commodity whose filming itself became the basis of an experimental performance piece by one of the actors? How did a fictionalized adaptation of psychoanalytic theory become a flashy film of femmey spectacle? We shall study a range of written and visual texts considering how in revisiting, reviving, and revising, an array of distinct and often conflicting desires gain saliency. Texts and films include: The Killing Fields, Swimming to Cambodia, Adaptation, Female Perversions, Twilight in Los Angeles, Class Action, North Country, The King and I. All films will be streamed; there are no additional "screening-nights."

title: Sem: Gender and Technology
description: This course will introduce you to an interdisciplinary field known as Gender and Technology Studies that asks how the apparatuses that fill our lives perpetuate and condition extant cultural beliefs about the meaning and limitations of gender, sex, desire, and embodiment. This inquiry requires that we study both industrial design--the shape and functions of the machines themselves--as well as histories and representations of use. Our methodologies will come from media studies, feminist theory, queer theory, cultural studies, and literary analysis, as we seek to answer the following questions: What stories does technology (or do stories about technology) tell me about my body? What role does technology play in our conceptions of sex and gender? How many genders and sexes are there? Can the body be understood as a form of technology? What do our fictional representations of technology suggest we believe about it? Whose interests are served by our current techno-gendered (or it gendered technological) culture? How do race, class, and ethnicity contribute to our understanding of gender or the social uses of technology? How do different racial, sexual, and gender minorities manipulate technological representation or respond to technological representations of themselves? Primary texts covered by this course may include Mary Shelley's Frankenstein, Frankenstein (dir. James Whale, 1931), Shelley Jackson's Patchwork Girl, Gods and Monsters (dir. Bill Condon, 1998), Aliens (dir. James Cameron, 1986), Sadie Plant's Zeros and Ones, Akira (dir. Katsuhiro Otomo, 1988), and Paprika (dir. Satoshi Kon, 2006) as well as theoretical readings by Kate Bornstein, Michel Foucault, Louis Althusser, Lynn Joyrich, Chela Sandoval, and Lisa Nakamura.

title: Class Fictions in the Contemporary US
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR PAMELA FOX Class Fictions in the Contemporary U.S. This interdisciplinary course examines U.S. literary and popular cultural representations of âlowâ classes/cultures (working class/poor/âunderclassâ) from the 1990s to the present. With the aid of recent theory and cultural criticism, we will focus on ways in which categories such as race, gender, and sexuality help to construct our contemporary notions of what âclassâ means in America. We will be studying a wide variety of texts, forms, and genresâfiction, poetry, autobiography, film, musicâand taking up a broad spectrum of issues, including theories of working class identity, social/cultural resistance, and the notion of ârepresentationâ itself. I will also strongly encourage students to add a service learning component to their study. Course work will include 1 short paper (5-7 pp.); 1 class presentation; 1 midterm exam, and one final paper (12 pp.).

title: Pulp Fiction
description: This course will investigate the highly stylized world of popular fiction, including the literary genres of the western, the adventure story, hard-boiled detective fiction, true crime, the historical romance, the suspense thriller, black pulp fiction, sci-fi and fantasy. We will observe the beginnings of pulp fiction from the dime novels and the penny dreadfuls in the 19th century to the literary boom of pulp fiction in the 1920s and 1930s. We will be connecting major themes in popular fiction to the growth of old time radio programs and to the study of rap music, manga, media, television and world cinema and will be watching a series of documentaries and analyzing pulp magazine jackets. Special attention will be given to the popularity of new pulp genres and cult classics in Japan, the U.S., Canada and the Caribbean. Pulp fiction writers may include Edgar Rice Burroughs, Zane Grey, Dashiell Hammett, Mickey Spillane, Laura Joh Rowland, Koushun Takami, Truman Capote, Margaret Atwood, Susan Elizabeth Phillips and J. K. Rowling.

title: Pulp Fiction
description: SUMMER 2010 Professor Christopher Shinn This course will examine the rise of popular fiction, including the literary genres of the detective novel, crime fiction, the western, the adventure story, the historical romance, the suspense thriller, blaxploitation serials, the fantasy novel and science fiction. Pulp fiction imagines the universe through an order of coded signs and popular styles that we will interpret and observe closely in this course. The sign systems speak to broad economic and political realities and collective fears and fantasies, which are deeply embedded within a mythic structure. Popular stories assert beliefs about law and order, vigilantism and revenge, and the roles of men and women. Using a cultural studiesâ approach, we will observe the beginnings of pulp fiction from the dime novels and the penny dreadfuls in the 19th century to the literary boom of pulp fiction in the 1920s and 1930s. We will be connecting the major themes in pulp fiction to the growth of old time radio programs and to the contemporary messages in popular music, media and film and will be watching a series of documentaries and analyzing pulp magazine jackets. Pulp fiction writers may include Edgar Rice Burroughs, Zane Grey, Dashiell Hammett, Mickey Spillane, Philip K. Dick, Margaret Atwood, Susan Elizabeth Phillips, Michael Crichton and J. K. Rowling.

title: Marxism and Culture
description: This course provides neither a comprehensive survey nor historical overview of the âtheoretical practiceâ of Marxist cultural production. Instead, it will offer a highly selective and idiosyncratic probe into some of the key theoretical issues and actions associated with the Marxist cultural tradition around the world. We will explore the realm of culture in its varied relationships with the political and revolutionary demands of socialism in several contexts, including both those of actually-existing socialist states and the capitalist West. We will read representative statements from Marx and Engels from the mid-nineteenth century including the âCommunist Manifesto,â Capital Vol. I and the Grundrisse, but will concentrate on more recent culture and criticism as it relates to postcolonial liberation movements, democracy, civil rights, gender and racial equality post World War II. Texts will include literature, theory, philosophy, political science, economics, film, critical media studies. One short paper, midterm exam, one long collaborative research paper.

title: Sem: Orientalism and After
description: This seminar examines the ongoing critical discussion arising from the publication of Edward Saidâs Orientalism in 1978. A particular emphasis will be placed on the multiple debates and critical discussions it generated. Our focus will be placed on the textâs representation of philology. Among the other topics we shall discuss are the textâs engagement with Postcolonial Studies, Humanism, Cultural Studies, and Aesthetics and Politics. Questions to be considered include: in what way does Orientalismâs critical deployment of Raymond Williams and Michel Foucault incapacitate the textâs critical project to establish non-dominative and non-coercive forms of knowledge? How might Saidâs interpretations and allusions to Joseph Conrad and Erich Auerbach shape the philological concerns of Orientalism? What role does culture play in the transfer of imperial authority from the classical empires of Europe to the United States after World War II? What can we infer from Saidâs interest in music, late style, opera, and his focus on silence? In other words, might he be concerned with not simply what enables a subject to speak, but what allows a subject to sing? Additional critical attention will be placed on the role of the intellectual in the twentieth century, the condition of exile, the discourses of anti-Semitisms, and Saidâs embattled relationship with theories, systems, and orthodoxies. Primary readings include: Conradâs âThe Secret Sharer,â Heart of Darkness, Swiftâs Gulliverâs Travels, Mannâs Doctor Faustus, Genetâs Prisoner of Love. Of Saidâs writings, we shall read selections from Beginnings, all of Orientalism, parts of The World, Text, and the Critic, all of Culture and Imperialism, multiple selections from Reflections on Exile, and all of Humanism and Democratic Criticism. Additional selections from Vico, Nietzsche, LukÃ¡cs, Gramsci, Williams, Foucault, Schwab, Spivak, Bhabha, among others.

title: Human Trafficking
description: How is human trafficking represented in contemporary novels, plays, and poems? The U. S. State Departmentâs Office To Monitor and Combat Trafficking in Persons defines human trafficking as âwhen someone obtains or holds a person in compelled service.â Do contemporary writers reveal the limits of this definition of contemporary slavery? What are the implications of the strategies that writers use to create their plots, characters, settings? What happens when we compare fictionalized accounts of trafficking with the testimonies and memoirs of those actually trafficked? With official reports on trafficking? Where do we, as readers and audiences, position ourselves in response to these disparate representations?

title: Narratives of Violence
description: âSticks and stones may break my bones but words will never hurt me.â In this course we interrogate that adage as we look at the intersections between violent acts and violent words by taking a critical discourse analytical approach to narratives of violence and violation. As well as looking at the presentations of acts of violence by perpetrators, by survivors, and by criminal investigators, we will look at the way that various terminologies position participants as survivors, as victims, as aggressors, as agents, etc. Using spoken, written and visual narratives, we explore disclosures and erasures of violation and violence. Texts include: The Lovely Bones by Alice Sebold; Miguel Pineroâs prison play Short Eyes, Patricia OâConnorâs discourse analysis of narratives of prisoners: Speaking of Crime; Arthur Dongâs Documentary film Licensed to Kill; narrative poems on working class lives by Chris Llewelyn: Fragments from the Fire; Poetry of U Sam Oeur on genocide by Khmer Rouge: Sacred Vows; and Mieke Balâs theory on narratives in A Mieke Bal Reader. As narrative subjects we will explore literature, tapestries, war rugs, paintings, photographs, and stained glass windows. In addition to Balâs theories on written and visual narratives we will read critical pieces by narrative theorists, cognitive scientists, and discourse analysts (for example Gerald Prince, William Labov, Teun van Dijk, Jerome Bruner, Patricia OâConnor, Charles Briggs, Deborah Schiffrin). Students are expected to contribute substantively to BlackBoard postings and discussions, write one small paper on course readings, take one test on theory and methodology, and write one major research paper that examines a group of narratives of violence of their own choosing. Students will periodically report on this project during the course using spoken, visual, as well as written interim reports.

title: Intro to Creative Writing
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR DAVID GEWANTER ENGL 324 Introduction to Creative Writing This course will introduce you to genres of creative writing by studying and practicing four modes: fiction, dialogue, poetry, and personal prose. We will explore and discuss great models of each of these forms; we'll hear craft lectures from the great writers in our Department and elsewhere who produce them; we will write critical prose on selected works; and then we'll try our hand at composing poems, stories, personal essays, and dialogue (for radio). Among the authors studied: D.H. Lawrence, Michael Ondaatje, and Anna Deavere Smith. You should already have experience in studying and writing in one of these genres, and be prepared for intensive research in how each form of writing happens, how it renders human experience, voice, plot, and vision. Be prepared both to experiment with literary forms and to share your work with others. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR DAVID GEWANTER ENGL 324 Introduction to Creative Writing This course will introduce you to genres of creative writing by studying and practicing four modes: fiction, dialogue, poetry, and personal prose. We will explore and discuss great models of each of these forms; we'll hear craft lectures from the great writers in our Department and elsewhere who produce them; we will write critical prose on selected works; and then we'll try our hand at composing poems, stories, personal essays, and dialogue (for radio). Among the authors studied: D.H. Lawrence, Michael Ondaatje, and Anna Deavere Smith. You should already have experience in studying and writing in one of these genres, and be prepared for intensive research in how each form of writing happens, how it renders human experience, voice, plot, and vision. Be prepared both to experiment with literary forms and to share your work with others.

title: Short Story Workshop
description: SHORT STORY WORKSHOP ENGLISH 326 FALL 2010 PROFESSOR JENNIFER NATALYA FINK What makes a story sing? In this workshop, each student will develop his or her own original short stories while examining the elements that make any work of fiction truly brilliant. Questions of form, genre, style, and voice will be central to our investigation as we construct our own pieces and critique our classmates'. While the emphasis will be on the act of writing and the craft of fiction, we will also read selected short masterpieces of the genre as we move from draft to piece to masterpiece. Prerequisites: By permission of instructor ONLY. To apply, send a one page letter explaining who you are and why you're interested in this class, as well as a one page sample of your creative writing. Send to jnf5@georgetown.edu by response by April 11; you will hear back by April 14. Students who have taken ENGL 325 may not enroll in ENGL 326.

title: Introduction to Poetry Writing
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR MARK MCMORRIS ENGL 333 Introduction to Poetry Writing This course will concentrate on poetic practice: first, on the poetry written or composed by members of the workshop during the semester. We will hold weekly discussions and readings of this work, seeking to understand, encourage, and critique the possibilities of each writerâs imaginative project. Of particular interest here will be the construction of the verse line and rhythm; the use of figure; and vocal and acoustic expression. As good writing is inseparable from careful reading, the course will have a second emphasis: on developments and texts in contemporary poetry, especially poetry composed in the experimental mode. Our attention will roam, taking stock of Language poetry, slam and spoken word, âvisualâ and âsoundâ poetry, chance and aleatory techniques, seriality, and the fragment. Readings, film, and audio-visual recordings will therefore supplement the ongoing work of composition.

title: Lannan Poetry Seminar
description: Instructor Permission Only Lannan Poetry Seminar encourages dynamic critical thinking and creativity with a view to examining literary forms and practices; the work of individual authors; the relationship between writing and other arts; and the place of writing in contemporary culture. Fellows study and discuss the work of the yearâs scheduled guests in the Lannan âReadings and Talksâ series, and then produce an independent final project (creative or critical) under the guidance of the instructor. There are also opportunities for students to share their own writing and to participate in a service-learning componentâorganizing and facilitating educational outreach efforts in the D.C. Community. Enrollment in this seminar is limited to the Lannan Fellows.

title: Intro to Script Writing
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR JOHN GLAVIN ENGL 335 Introduction to Writing for the Screen This course is designed to introduce students to the fundamental elements of writing for the large (film) and small (television) screen. It roots in the core precept that plot is character is action. Which means that the first object of study for all dramatic writing must be the examination of character. In workshop exercises, complemented by theoretical models from Keirsey and Greimas, students develop techniques for character analysis and development. At the same time, week by week, they examine character development in iconic films from a wide range of periods and genres. By the end of the term each student will have generated a short (20 minute) film for television and a short (20 minute) film for the wider screen. Students should note that in addition to class meetings during the week, they are also required to attend throughout the term regular film screenings on Sunday afternoons.

title: Tutorial
description: Instructor Permission Only An individual tutorial offered to students who have successfully completed English 335. The student will complete an original, full-length feature script in a genre and with a story line of the student's choice.

title: Experimental Fiction Workshop
description: BY APPLICATION ONLYâinterested students must 1) have successfully completed a college beginning-level creative writing class; and 2) submit a 1-page writing sample and 1-page cover letter . SUBMIT your application by email to jnf5@georgetown.edu by November 7; you will be notified about the status of your application by November 10. In this advanced writing workshop, we will read key texts of 20th and 21st-century experimental fiction. Using these writings as springboards, we will create our own weekly experiments. The class is student-led, and demands enormous commitment from its members. Expect to read sexually transgressive, politically charged, mixed media works. Expect to expand your notion of what constitutes a text. Expect to exceed your own expectations of your writing.

title: Creative Nonfiction Writing
description: By Application Only An intensive writing workshop focused on discovering, researching and crafting creative nonfiction narrative. Readings and writing across the spectrum of this innovative âfourth genre.â Students should expect to write constantly and to contribute, as critical readers and writers, to the common cause of the workshop. Permission: In order to secure a place in the class, please send a one-page letter of application on or before Wednesday, November 17 to Professor Norma Tilden (tildenn@georgetown.edu). Your letter should (1) briefly list any college-level writing course(s) that you have taken, and (2) address the writing prompt below: Drama critic John Lahr has said of the one-person show that âthe most important question is not where to start the story but why to tell it. The actor needs a compelling reason, beyond vanity, to step from the wings onto the stage.â I wonder if his observation might be true of first-person narrative as well. What do you think?

title: Revolution & Respect: Writing for a Cause
description: Using Black protest literature of the Civil Rights era as a model, this course will show students how to craft fiction for a purpose. Do you want to reform schools, expose genocide, stop abortion, end a war, promote or limit immigration? Whatever your political agenda, you can advance itâas writers always haveâthrough thoughtful and intentional storytelling. Readings will include authors such as James Baldwin, Lorraine Hansberry, and John A. Williams, and students will be asked to reflect, in writing, on how these artists identify and attack injustice in their work. The goal of the course, however, will be for each student to produce, through various exercises and extensive drafts, one solid piece of short fiction that advocates a position on a public issue.

title: Organizational Rhetoric & Writing
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR NATHANIEL RIVERS ENGL 345 Organizational Rhetoric and Writing Organizational rhetoric and writing can be defined as communication that motivates and structures values and behaviors within an organizational setting. Communicating within organizations is a crucial component of professional life no matter what shape that life takes or what directions that life moves in. Importantly for students with a range of professional aspirations, this course defines âorganizationsâ broadly, including but not limited to: non-profits, NGOs, businesses, advocacy groups, political action committees, philanthropic organizations, and institutions of higher learning. A central component of the course is group work. Students pitch, select, and organize hypothetical organizations that they then work with during the course of the semester. Working with these groups throughout the semester, students are presented with a series of challenges that they must then respond to with deliberate rhetorical action. That is, students in this course are not assigned documents to compose; they area ssigned problems to solve. This means that students negotiate with one another to determine the shape of their work for the course. While student groups necessarily produce different document sets, all work in the course stresses the importance of teamwork, professional ethics, primary research, document design, effective writing, and audience awareness?considerations that will shape the professional lives of students.

title: Empathy and Activism
description: What is the relationship between the practice of empathy and political activism in the arts? How do performance, poetry, and politics link people together? Is empathy necessary toâor a danger toâthose building a civic space? In the eighteenth century, philosophers thought that sympathetic feeling was central to civic activity. Weâll test that notion by investigating the relationship between empathy and activism in the transatlantic world of nineteenth-century abolitionist literature, both in nonfiction and fiction. Weâll trace the genealogy of abolitionism into contemporary performances involving memory and forgetting. The diverse reading list will include philosophical treatises on sympathy, political theories about citizenship, and anti-slavery and human rights poems, plays, short stories, speeches, letters, and essays. With guidance, students will create their own research projects.

title: Self&Society: American Literature
description: This course will be held on Georgetown's Qatar campus. Please see the Office of International Programs for further information.

title: Lannan Chair Colloquium
description: FALL 2010 LANNAN CHAIR SEMINAR ENGL 391 Lannan Chair Coll: Writing for Children In many of the stories written for younger children (ages 3-8), shadows of the issues that come to plague adult society are already present. The lyrical and wondrous tales that were read aloud to us, or that we first read alone, are the ones we remember for a lifetime because they are also psychologically and socially lasting. Our purpose will be to connect these first-heard stories to the scandals in the contemporary world (Hansel and Gretel, bad parenting, starvation, kidnapping...) and to discern new possibilities for young readers. This class will have three entryways into the poetics of children's literature: one will be by reading it, another will be by writing it, and a third way will be by analyzing it. Some writing will be required every week and often shared in response to the week's readings. This will be a very active, creative and participatory group experience in which the students are urged to contribute their imaginative work and their thoughts freely. Illustrations and animations are welcome. SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR FANNY HOWE ENGL 391 Exchanging Narratives This will be a writing workshop with a focus on personal history. With weekly papers due, and critical thinking expected, as we read each other's and assigned texts, it would be good to have some previous experience in a writing workshop. There will be assigned presentations on the readings to lead us into discussion of other literature and always the opportunity to try out different genres (poetry, photography, etc.) to supplement or replace the prose. The course will be based on the chronology of a life lived from infancy to leaving home, including references to politics, education, religion and environment.

title: Race/Gender in American Lit/Theory
description: This course will be held on Georgetown's Qatar campus. Please see the Office of International Programs for further information.

title: Cultural Constructions of Motherhood
description: Fox, Pamela & Velez, Elizabeth This course will examine constructions of motherhood in the U.S. from the 1950s to the present. We intend to take an interdisciplinary approach to the material, including cultural and social theory; history; popular texts; fiction; poetry; film; television. Possible texts and authors include: Adrienne Rich; Sigmund Freud; Nancy Chodorow; Arlie Hochschild; Susan Douglas (The Mommy Myth); Angela Davis; Audre Lorde; Patricia Hill Collins; Tillie Olsen; Sarah Ruddick; Sharon Olds; Louise Erdrich; Dorothy Allison; Sandra Cisneros; John Updike; I Love Lucy; Roseanne; Julia; Imitation of Life; Far From Heaven; and Rosemaryâs Baby.

title: Introduction to Journalism
description: SUMMER 2011 Barbara Feinman Todd ENGL 470.01 This course will teach students the basics of journalism, beginning with how to assess the news value of an event or situation, how to develop story ideas and how to put together a story. Much attention will be paid to writing clearly and accurately and developing interviewing skills. The ethical issues facing journalists will be also be a focus. Guest speakers will include reporters, critics and editors from Washington and Baltimore. There will be several short writing assignments and a final project. (Barbara Feinman Todd is the Journalism Director at Georgetown University. She is also the co-director of the Pearl Project. Her work has appeared in various media outlets including The Washington Post, NPR, the Daily Beast, Glamour, and Newsweek International.) SPRING 2011 JONATHAN DECKER ENGL 470.01 This course will teach students the basics of journalism, beginning with how to assess the news value of an event or situation, how to develop story ideas and how to put together a story. Much attention will be paid to writing clearly and accurately and developing interviewing skills. The ethical issues facing journalists will be also be a focus. Guest speakers will include reporters, critics and editors from Washington and Baltimore. There will be several short writing assignments and a final project. (Jon Decker is a Washington correspondent for Reuters Television, the USA Radio Network and LBC Radio [London Broadcast Corporation].)

title: Introduction to Journalism
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR ATHELIA KNIGHT ENGL 470.02 Intro to Journalism This course will teach students the basics of newspaper, magazine and online news writing, beginning with how to assess the news value of an event or situation, how to develop story ideas and how to put together a story. Much attention will be paid to improving studentsâ writing styles and developing interviewing techniques, as well as consideration of ethical issues facing journalists today. Several guest speakers from the Washington journalism community will visit. Writing assignments will include several short news assignments and one longer feature story. (Athelia Knight, a 33-year veteran of The Washington Post, has worked as a reporter and a coordinator of a program that helped train and develop young journalists.) SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR ATHELIA KNIGHT ENGL 470.02 Intro to Journalism This course will teach students the basics of newspaper, magazine and online news writing, beginning with how to assess the news value of an event or situation, how to develop story ideas and how to put together a story. Much attention will be paid to improving studentsâ writing styles and developing interviewing techniques, as well as consideration of ethical issues facing journalists today. Several guest speakers from the Washington journalism community will visit. Writing assignments will include several short news assignments and one longer feature story. (Athelia Knight, a 33-year veteran of The Washington Post, has worked as a reporter and a coordinator of a program that helped train and develop young journalists.)

title: Introduction to Journalism
description: FALL 2010 BROWN, DENEEN ENGL-470-03 Intro to Journalism This course will teach students the basics of newspaper, magazine and online news writing, beginning with how to assess the news value of an event or situation, how to develop story ideas and how to write and structure a news story. Much attention will be paid to improving studentsâ writing styles and developing interviewing techniques, as well as consideration of ethical issues facing journalists today. Several guest speakers from the Washington journalism community will visit. Writing assignments will include several short news assignments and one longer feature story. Instructor: (DeNeen L. Brown, a 25-year veteran of The Washington Post, is an award-winning feature writer. Brown is currently a staff writer in The Style Section of The Washington Post.) .

title: Online News Production
description: SPRING 2011 SCOTT ANDERSON ENGL 471 This course gives students a firm grasp of the skills necessary to produce online news content as well as an understanding of the forces that are driving the rapidly changing and diversifying online news industry. Students will receive a mixture of practical instruction in online news writing, multimedia news production, search engine optimization techniques, blog posting and social media management. Students will also be expected to engage in critical analysis of the journalism profession, contemporary online journalism and the economic, social and technological drivers shaping online news consumption. Assignments will include individual news stories and multimedia elements. Students will also be asked to contribute to a class news blog and other group projects. Scott Anderson is Georgetown University's Director of Web Communications and has worked as a producer at CNN.com, washingtonpost.com, and PBS NewsHour. FALL 2010 ELLEN EDWARDS ENGL 471 Journalism: Feature Writing This class will focus on journalistic techniques for feature writing and reporting. Students will work on a variety of feature assignments of varying lengths, with intensive focus on writing and reporting, including some assignments off campus. Weekly readings will be drawn from collections of feature writing as well as from current publications. Vigorous class participation and peer editing are an integral part of the class. Recommended for the serious journalism student. Intro to Journalism is strongly suggested. (Ellen Edwards is a former Washington Post reporter and deputy editor of the Style section.)

title: Media Techniques
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR BARBARA FEINMAN TODD ENGL 472 Media Techniques This elective is for students who will be participating in an internship at a local media outlet (print, broadcast or Internet) during the summer or fall. This workshop-style course will give students an opportunity to advance their reporting and writing skills while sharing their internship experiences with other students engaged in similar journalistic pursuits. Written assignments will include a semester-long journal, two reported articles, and a final project that directly grows out of the internship experience. Students will have the opportunity to post their stories on a GU journalism web site. Required readings will focus on issues common to any journalistic endeavor such as ethics and fairness and the future of the profession in the new media era. Guest speakers will include prominent local journalists. Students must arrange the internship before enrolling in the course. Prerequisites: PERMISSION OF ENGLISH DEPT JOURNALISM DIRECTOR, feinmanb@georgetown.edu SPRING 2011 PROFESSOR BARBARA FEINMAN TODD ENGL 472 This elective is for students who will be participating in an internship at a local media outlet (print, broadcast or Internet) during the spring. An alternative to the traditional one-on-one tutorial, this workshop-style course will give students an opportunity to share their experiences with other students engaged in similar journalistic pursuits. Written assignments will include a semester-long journal and ongoing occasional short pieces. Students will have the opportunity, if they would like, to post their stories on a GU journalism web site. There will also be required readings about issues that are common to any journalistic endeavor such as ethics and fairness. Students must arrange the internship by January 1, 2010 and contact the instructor by then, preferably before the winter break. Students are strongly encouraged to have completed âIntroduction to Journalismâ before taking this class. (Barbara Feinman Todd, the English Departmentâs Journalism Director, has worked as an investigative researcher, a freelance reporter, a ghostwriter and a newsroom writing coach.) Permission of instructor is mandatory. Please contact the instructor at feinmanb@georgetown.edu

title: Media & Social Justice: International Investigative Project
description: FALL 2010 Nomani, A. & Feinman Todd, B. ENGL 473.01 Media & Social Justice: The Pearl Project 2.0 A select group of undergraduate students will embark upon a new iteration of the Pearl Project, a Georgetown University student investigation that focused on the kidnapping and murder of Wall Street Journal reporter Daniel Pearl. This seminar will seek to solve a mystery involving a journalist, either imperiled or slain, whose work remains unfinished and whose personal story has not been fully told. In the spirit of the original Pearl Project, and as a tribute to Daniel Pearl and all journalists who have put their lives on the line to âgetâ the story, this new investigation will carry on the Pearl Project legacy to reveal a truth not yet told, to underscore the importance of a global free press, and to train the next generation of investigative reporters. Led by journalist and author Asra Nomani and GU Journalism Director Barbara Feinman Todd, this class will be run like a newsroom, teaching the investigative reporting tools necessary to do the patient work that an in-depth endeavor such as this requires, including the traditional gumshoe reporting tactics of identifying, developing and maintaining human sources, following paper and electronic trails and filing requests under the Freedom of Information Act. Students will also learn the cutting edge techniques of computer-assisted journalism. Assigned to beats, students will pursue hunches and follow leads that grow out of their own reporting as well as those that come in over the Georgetown Pearl Project tipline (email: pearlproject@georgetown.edu or call 202 687-7136). Permission by instructors only. Interested students should submit a 600-word essay proposing a subject for the fall 2010 Pearl Project class. Student should suggest a journalist whose freedom has been threatened or life has been lost in the line of duty. The essay should make a case for why this journalistâs story would make a good investigation, how this story illuminates or illustrates a larger societal, geo-political, and/or cultural conflict, and what the goals and objectives of the proposed project would be for the class. Please send to essays to feinmanb@georgetown.edu and aqn@georgetown.edu by midnight, April 12, 2010. Credits: 3 Prerequisites: Instructor Permission Required SPRING 2011 Nomani, A. & Feinman Todd, B. ENGL 473.01 A select group of undergraduate students will embark upon a new iteration of the Pearl Project, a Georgetown University student investigation that initially focused on the kidnapping and murder of Wall Street Journal reporter Daniel Pearl. This seminar will seek to solve a mystery involving a journalist, either imperiled or slain, whose work remains unfinished and whose personal story has not been fully told. In the spirit of the original Pearl Project, and as a tribute to Daniel Pearl and all journalists who have put their lives on the line to âgetâ the story, this new investigation will carry on the Pearl Project legacy to reveal a truth not yet told, to underscore the importance of a global free press, and to train the next generation of investigative reporters. Led by journalist and author Asra Nomani and GU Journalism Director Barbara Feinman Todd, this class is run like a newsroom, teaching the investigative reporting tools necessary to do the patient work that an in-depth endeavor such as this requires, including the traditional gumshoe reporting tactics of identifying, developing and maintaining human sources, following paper and electronic trails and filing requests under the Freedom of Information Act. Students will also learn the cutting edge techniques of computer-assisted journalism. Assigned to beats, students will pursue hunches and follow leads that grow out of their own reporting as well as those that come in over the Georgetown Pearl Project tipline (email: pearlproject@georgetown.edu or call 202 687- 7136). Permission by instructors only. Interested students should submit a 600-word essay proposing a subject for the Spring 2011 Pearl Project class. Student should suggest a journalist whose freedom has been threatened or life has been lost in the line of duty. The essay should make a case for why this journalistâs story would make a good investigation, how this story illuminates or illustrates a larger societal, geo-political, and/or cultural conflict, and what the goals and objectives of the proposed project would be for the class. Please send to essays to both feinmanb@georgetown.edu and aqn@georgetown.edu by midnight, Nov. 18, 2010.

title: Journalism: Food Writing
description: The eater, the cook, the traveler, the scientist, the humorist, the investigative journalist, the essayist: all are inspired to write by the simple yet universal topic of food. Such writing requires no media credentials, no press passes, no appointments with a congressman or a movie star. Instead, this hugely accessible topic presents itself to us daily at our table, in our groceries, at the corner cafÃ© and at our holiday feasts. Yet it requires the precision and accuracy of the best journalism, the dedication to inform readers, a curious and adventurous mind and a fine sense of prose that knows when an essay is getting overcooked. This course will be both reading and writing intensive. Students will be required weekly to read diverse examples of food writing and to write stories and essays on a variety of topics and in a variety of forms, from the simple ingredient description, to the straightforward news story, to the restaurant review, to the blog, to the personal essay. (Jeanne McManus was an editor at The Washington Post for 24 years. She worked on the nightly News Desk, the op-ed and editorial pages, the Sunday magazine and the Style section. She was Deputy Assistant Managing Editor for Sports from 1991-1999 and Executive Food Editor from 1999 to 2004, when she retired. She is an occasional contributor to the Post's op-ed page, a regular guest on ESPN980's "The Tony Kornheiser Showâ and a woman who keeps cooking and recipes in perspective.)

title: Crossing Over: Public Intellectuals
description: âSo what?â and âWhy should anyone care?â are the two fundamental (and most irritating) questions that anyone writing for a diverse audience must address: by necessity, public intellectuals must explicitly confront these questions within their writing and throughout their careers. As a matter of professional survival, public intellectuals also must develop a writing style that negotiates between high and low cultures, addressing themselves to audiences composed of educated non-specialists as well as the erudite. âCrossing Overâ explores selected moments in the twentieth-century history of American cultural criticism as well as the practice of writing criticism in an accessible yet intellectually rigorous manner. Students will study critical writing and investigate its influence; they will also practice the art of critical writing in regular assignments. The course will begin by exploring the elusive and changing definitions of âthe public intellectual.â It will then explore twentieth-century culture critics such as H.L. Mencken and the wits of the Round Table. It will then focus on the mid-century with the rise of the New York Intellectuals (Edmund Wilson, Dwight MacDonald), the early stirrings of the Womenâs Movement (Betty Friedan). It will conclude with the emergence of post-1960s public intellectuals out of the New Left and Conservative political movements. (e.g. Tom Hayden, Norman Mailer, Barbara Ehrenreich, Todd Gitlin, Gertrude Himmelfarb, Stanley Crouch).

title: History of the Book / Theory of the Text
description: ENGL 478: "HISTORY OF THE BOOK/THEORY OF THE TEXT" FALL 2010 PROFESSOR MICHAEL MACOVSKI How does the physicality or "thingness" of a book--its material or digital presence--affect our interpretation of it? How does English literature change as we move from oral to scribal to print culture--and from the material to the electronic text? How is literature influenced by, say, Thackerayâs emphasis on his novelsâ illustrations, Joyceâs preoccupation with page numbers, or Emily Dickinsonâs use of stationery and paper scraps? How did the serialization of Dickens's novels in periodicals affect their composition, distribution, and readership? And what do William Blakeâs bizarre engravings tell us about his poetry? Such questions are central to "The History of the Book"âthat is, the understanding of literary texts as both material artifacts and potentially disembodied (electronic) forms. This course offers students of literature an opportunity to explore some of the cultural and historical concepts behind such issues as literary authorship, forgery, piracy, translation, production, dissemination, and audience. It provides the historical foundation underlying several central debates within current literary theory, including the unstable status of the author, the gendered politics of literary publication, the economics of book distribution, the shifting definition of literary textuality, the history of literary censorship, and the legal evolution of copyright and hypertext in the Digital Age. In order to bring these issues into focus, we will read some influential literary works that stand as particular examples and illustrations within the History of the Book. In addition to landmark examples by Austen, Blake, Chaucer, Defoe, Sterne, Pope, Wordsworth, Byron, Hemans, Dickens, and the Brontes, we will also trace some of the more recent and experimental developments within literary textuality and hypertext. Theoretical readings will include essays by Foucault, Benjamin, Derrida, Borges, Habermas, Darnton, Sutherland, Eisenstein, Eaves, McGann, Woodmansee, Negroponte, Landow, Drucker, and Hayles, among others. We will also consider some of the more recent, experimental developments within literary and electronic textuality.

title: Reading, Teaching, Social Reflection
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR JOHN HIRSH ENGL 479 Reading, Teaching, and Social Reflection Reading, Teaching and Social reflection is intended for students interested in teaching, and besides the usual classes, involves working for two hours one morning a week (the morning is chosen by the student) as a teacher's aide in one of three inner-city Catholic schools. These schools include Assumption School in Anacostia, Immaculate Conception School in Shaw, and Sacred Heart Bi-lingual School in Mount Pleasant, all of which have been welcoming Georgetown students for the past 13 years. The focus of the course is on teaching reading in the primary grades, and students from this class have gone on to Teach for America, the Inner-City-Teaching-Corps and to degree programs in Education at Berkeley, Columbia Teachers College and Harvard, among other places.

title: Technologies of the Text
description: Cross-listed as CCTP 681 In this seminar, we consider the term "text" from many perspectivesâincluding digital media, experimental books, art objectsâand even musical scores and dance-notation "texts." We ask how such forms are redefining the terms "author" and "reader"âand how they are changing our definitions of "literature" and "news" and "social networking." Each semester, we develop special topics in the seminar according to the particular research interests of each class member. In past years, for instance, we have read the most popular hypertext ever writtenânamely, "Patchwork Girl," a powerful take-off on the novel Frankenstein (which we also discuss during the course). We have also read graphic novels such as the award-winning Fun Home, Akira, and Persepolis (we saw the movie-version of the latter as well). And we also considered many texts from a cross-cultural perspective, including American versions of Japanese anime and other non-Western genres. During previous terms, we have also looked at the historical origins of today's digital cultureâincluding the history of libraries, museums, and art collections. We have discussed the origins of copyright law, comparing the first amazing trials with today's debates about digital copyright (including the famous Napster and Mickey Mouse cases). And we have investigated notorious internet hoaxes (including the Lonely Girl scandal)âand traced their origins to the most intriguing art and literary forgeries (such as those by Thomas Chatterton). Students with specifically literary interests might also consider how various textual forms affect novels, plays, and poetry. How is literature influenced by, say, Thackeray's emphasis on his novels' illustrations, Joyce's preoccupation with pagination, or Emily Dickinson's use of stationery and paper scraps? How did the serialization of Dickens's novels in periodicals affect their composition, distribution, and readership? What do William Blake's bizarre paintings tell us about his poetry? In the past, we have also read influential literary by Chaucer, Defoe, Sterne, Pope, Wordsworth, Byron, Hemans, Austen, the Brontes, Joyceâas well as about some of the more recent, experimental developments within literary and electronic textuality. If you would like more information about the course, please email Michael Macovski at msm44@georgetown.edu.

title: Issues in Education
description: By Instructor Permission Only This course is intended primarily for students in the Georgetown University Catholic Schools Project who wish to continue in their placement during the second semester, though other students who have worked actively in education can add in with the Instructor's permission. It assumes, however, a good knowledge of teaching strategies and a reasonable amount of practice in executing them, and considers such topics as the role of standardized tests, the place of whole language and of phonics, and the impact and meanings of inner-city Catholic primary education, among other topics. It requires, among other things, an extended research paper on an educational topic agreed to in consultation with the instructor, and researched in Washington libraries other than our own.

title: Comm. Tutoring: Sursum Corda
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR JOHN HIRSH ENGL 484 Community Tutoring: Sursum Corda This course is designed for students wishing to tutor, on Tuesdays and Thursdays evenings, K-6 children at the Sursum Corda community. Founded in 1970 by Rev. Horace McKenna, S.J. and attached to the Sursum Corda Tenants Association, the Sursum Corda-Georgetown University Tutoring Program is the oldest such program in the country established between an American university and a specific urban community. Students learn in a weekly seminar what is involved in teaching a young child to read, and work, twice a week, in the Sursum Corda Community Center, teaching young learners the language arts, and learning themselves about the community of which they are a part. SPRING 2010 PROFESSOR JOHN HIRSH ENGL 484 Community Tutoring: Sursum Corda This course is designed for students wishing to tutor, on Tuesdays and Thursdays evenings, K-6 children at the Sursum Corda community. Founded in 1970 by Rev. Horace McKenna, S.J. and attached to the Sursum Corda Tenants Association, the Sursum Corda-Georgetown University Tutoring Program is the oldest such program in the country established between an American university and a specific urban community. Students learn in a weekly seminar what is involved in teaching a young child to read, and work, twice a week, in the Sursum Corda Community Center, teaching young learners the language arts, and learning themselves about the community of which they are a part.

title: Those Who Teach, Lead
description: Public figures and education reformers often assert that great teachers possess the qualities of great leaders. That said, there are many forms of leadership, and many ways that people teach. Which styles and practices of leadership help create and sustain high-impact teaching? Which approaches to teaching empower us to be effective leaders in society? Can certain habits or mindsets make us both better teachers and better leaders? âThose Who Teach, Leadâ addresses these questions. Designed for undergraduates in any tutoring, teaching, or mentoring program in the community, the course will be conducted as a workshop, with students evaluating one book and one essay per week. Weâll explore methods for results-based teaching; strategies for drawing upon our studentsâ communities and cultures; approaches that emphasize critical social awareness and student empowerment; ways of growing personally as educators; and the value of learning the larger context of education innovation, debate and reform. Students will develop their own philosophies of leadership in education. Students will submit one short essay (750 words) and one reflection essay (1 page) each week, along with a ten-page final paper. The class will meet with successful teachers and education leaders -- many of them Georgetown alums -- hold two evening meetings, and attend the new documentary on education, Waiting for Superman. Also, on Sunday, November 7th and Monday, November 8th the class will travel to New York City to visit a group of emerging players in urban education including Cristo Rey-NY High School and KIPP Infinity School. All expenses will be covered and the group will return to campus the evening of November 8th. Course readings include Paule Marshallâs Brown Girl, Brownstones; Rodolfo Anayaâs Bless Me, Ultima; Lan Caoâs Monkey Bridge; Gregory Boyle, S.J.âs Boundless Compassion; Jay Mathewsâ book on the KIPP Network Work Hard, Be Nice; and Steven Farrâs account of Teach For Americaâs approach to teaching, Teaching as Leadership: The Highly Effective Teacherâs Guide to Closing the Achievement Gap. Students may apply for the seminar by filling out the form below and emailing it to Dr. Porterfield at porterfd@georgetown.edu by Friday, August 27th. Students will be notified if they are in the class by Monday, August 30th. Class size is limited to 20 students. APPLICATION FORM NAME _________________________________ YEAR____________ SCHOOL AND MAJOR_____________________________ Why do you want to take the seminar? What education program will you take part in? Have you had any experiences at Georgetown or before attending college that inform your interest in this class? (Please limit your answer to 250 words.)

title: Humanities Research Seminar
description: This course assumes that while many students may not seek a humanities-based career, the skills one acquires through research, thinking and writing are valuable to all fields. The course will ask students to develop a research topic BEFORE the semester begins. The course itself will include theoretical and practical readings, but will mostly focus on studentsâ ideas and the process of developing a topic into a research paper. We will have an intensive introduction to advanced research skills offered by a research librarian and will focus our class discussions on student topics, research challenges, evaluating and using evidence, drafting, revision, developing a powerful thesis and presenting ideas in a convincing, sustained and effective manner. The goal of the course is for each participant to produce an original argumentative research paper.

title: Proseminar
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR KATHRYN TEMPLE ENGL 495 Proseminar This course is for students accepted into the Honors Program. Students will be expected to read and comment on critical and theoretical texts related to the honors projects, to read and comment on each other's work, and to present their own work for critique. Attendance and engagement as well as periodic written responses are required. Student should expect to complete about 25 pages of the honors thesis by the end of the course.

title: Worlds of Beowulf
description: The recent translation of Beowulf by Nobel laureate Seamus Heaney has reawakened interest in the Anglo-Saxons, yet stereotypes and misinformation persist in popular culture to shape our views. This seminar will cover the intellectual and physical cultures of the Anglo-Saxons through the texts, archaeology, language, and art of the sixth to eleventh centuries in Britain to test our assumptions and develop new ways of seeing this ancient society in an interdisciplinary way. You will learn to read the physical culture alongside textual culture, creating a dialogue between them and using the different perspectives of each to test your perceptions of the period and its peoples. Is the term "Anglo-Saxon" useful at all, given the hybridity of the populations of the British Isles? How do we read early burials: do they reveal something about Anglo-Saxon beliefs or more about those of later scholars? How do the secular and spiritual worlds interact and influence each other? What do we make of a cultural representation so overwhelmingly homosocial? Do site excavations and architectural space reveal attitudes towards power, privacy, gender, access? ! Where were the women in this heroic world, and can we find their voices? We look at such issues as cultural interaction and opposition; the marginal vs. the normative; mythmaking and identity; gender constructions and representations; and the social, religious and political impacts of Christianity. We will read selections from Old English poems, riddles, sermons, histories, saints' lives and legal documents against the physical cultures of manuscripts, sites, and artifacts, always with the enriched understanding of the text of Beowulf as the core. All texts will be read in translation, but there will also be some original language translation. (Language study will be required to pass, but not graded. This is not a language course.) Varied assignments throughout the term will develop a longer final paper at the end of the course.

title: Milton
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR JASON ROSENBLATT ENGL 536 Milton By starting with the early literary productions in poetry and prose and concluding with /Samson Agonistes/, written in old age, when Milton, like his champion, was blind and disillusioned, we will give attention to the development of his literary powers. But we will spend most of our time on /Paradise Lost./ With all due reverence for Chaucer's /Canterbury Tales/ and Shakespeare's /Hamlet/ and /King Lear/, Milton's epic is the greatest single work ever written in English. We will try to understand it mostly on our own but also with help from some of the best contemporary essays. Besides a longer final paper, everyone will turn in a short paper (500-750 words, no more than two to three double-spaced typed pages) every two weeks. Since the class will be split in two, and the assignments staggered, one half of the class will be turning in an assignment every time we meet. Papers should concentrate on interpretive problems or questions raised by the work to be discussed in class that week.

title: The Theatrical Transformation of the Public Sphere
description: Habermas describes the public sphere as a space of public discourse outside official culture. He locates it in the newsletters and coffee houses of eighteenth-century Europe, but historians have since attempted to make claims for the earlier development of a public sphere in seventeenth-century England, especially in connection with the Civil War. This seminar will test the idea of taking the concept yet another step back, to the commercial theater of Tudor and Stuart London as a site of public culture. Building also on such classic work as Jonathan Dollimoreâs argument about âradical tragedy,â we will search out plays that engaged actively with contemporary political, social, and economic issues and events. These may include the collaborative Gorboduc (1561), the anonymous Arden of Feversham (1592), Shakespeareâs Richard II (1595), Thomas Heywoodâs If You Know Not Me You Know Nobody (1605), the collaborative Witch of Edmonton (1621), Thomas Middletonâs Game at Chess (1624), and others. We will discuss ways in which these works address political thought, social theory, and international relationsâas well as the citations and censorship that give evidence of their impact on public discourse.
