title: Literature of the Atlantic Empire
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR DENNIS TODD ENGL 552 Literature of the Atlantic Empire In this course, we will examine some British, Native American, Anglo-American, African-British, and American literature that grew out of the collision of cultures in the Americas in the late seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, particularly, though not exclusively, in the Chesapeake and the Caribbean. In these fictional and non-fictional narratives and poems, we will be interested in several related concerns: the ideas of civilization and savagery; the dynamics of slavery and freedom (historically, economically, and politically, and also as figures for psychological and spiritual processes); and the articulation of selfhood in a colonial context. We will pay attention to whether there are significant and growing differences between Old and New World identities, and we will chart some of the anxieties of dealing with a new American identity in the literature of the early Republic. We will study the following authors and works: Behn, Oroonoko; Defoe, Robinson Crusoe; the captivity narrative of Mary Rowlandson; narratives of indentured servitude by Moraley and Ashbridge; Sarah Knightâs Journal; Cookeâs Sot-Weed Factor; Byrdâs Secret History of the Dividing Line; Olaudah Equianoâs Interesting Narrative; Franklinâs Autobiography; Fosterâs The Coquette; Irvingâs âLegend of Sleepy Hollowâ; Brownâs Wieland; and Cooperâs The Prairie.

title: 19C Comedy
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR JOHN PFORDRESHER ENGL 580 Nineteenth Century British Comedy A study of the way in which various writers took up the traditions of the comic and comedic and experimented with them, stressing the interplay between writers and the rapidly changing world of nineteenth-century Britain. We will read some theoretical work by writers like Freud and Bakhtin but the focus of the seminar will be on primary texts including: Austenâs Pride and Prejudice, a hefty portion of Byronâs Don Juan, Thackerayâs Vanity Fair, dramatic monologues by Robert Browning, the Alice books of Lewis Carroll, examples of mid-nineteenth century cartooning from Punch and other illustrated magazines, Gilbert and Sullivanâs operetta Patience, and Oscar Wildeâs The Importance of Being Earnest. Students will take responsibility for leading seminar discussion and will report on recent scholarship and criticism dealing with the course topic and its texts

title: 19th Century US Lit: Class, Poverty, and the American Dream
description: As scholars routinely note, class remains an under-studied category in 19th- century U. S. literature and culture. This course will examine literary and cultural representations of social class, and will explore how class as literary construction is inseparable from discourses of race, gender, ethnicity, sexuality, nation, and empire. Overall in the course, we will explore the American Dream of unencumbered class mobility as a particular instance of what critic Lauren Berlant terms "national fantasy," a utopian national ideal at once deemed universally accessible and inscribed by a variety of social differences. We will examine the way 19th-century literary texts both reproduce, and expose contradictions within, this governing ideology of âAmericannessâ: we will consider the ways in which these texts measure the distance between utopian ideal and historical ârealityâ and register what Richard Sennett and Jonathan Cobb term the âhidden injuries of classââinvisible psychic and social costs of subscription to this national ideal. Course readings include literary texts by George Foster, Frank Webb, Frederick Douglass, Thomas Skidmore, Sarah Bagley and other New England millworkers who contributed to the Lowell Offering and the Voice of Industry, Nathaniel Hawthorne, Herman Melville, Rebecca Harding Davis, Horatio Alger, Stephen Crane, Edith Wharton, and Anzia Yezierska. We will also read selected theoretical, critical, and historical articles addressing various aspects of class formation and the construction of class subjectivities in the nineteenth-century U. S. We will end the course by considering representations of class in contemporary film and television, to assess how (to quote the title of the influential series of articles (2005) in the New York Times) âclass mattersâ in contemporary U. S. culture, and to situate U. S. class relations and identities in a global context. Short (1-2 page) weekly reflection papers, a 5-page mid-term essay, and a final 15-20 page seminar paper are the major written requirements for the course.

title: George Bernard Shaw
description: This course emphasizes the spoken (as distinguished from the written or merely read) word, both words spoken by characters in Shaw's plays and also words spoken by us as professionals speaking about the plays. We will concentrate intensively on how language functions as dramatic action, and we will also work throughout the term on strengthening and extending the individual student's voice, particularly her or his ability to speak effectively in professional situations. We will read a selection of Shaw's plays that will probably include Cesar and Cleopatra, Heartbreak House, Major Barbara, Man and Superman, Misalliance, Mrs. Warren's Profession, and Pygmalion. There are two required texts: Uta Hagen's "Respect for Acting" and Cecily Berry's "The Actor and His Text."

title: Harlem & the Renaissance
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR R. SCOTT HEATH ENGL 616 Harlem & the Renaissance In 1925, Alain Locke described the emergence of a generation of black artists and intellectuals who would represent the marginalized âfolkâ in the United States and who would serve as a bridge into the possibility of equitable American citizenship. This âNew Negroâ was characterized as a cultural vanguard responsible for the leadership not only of the African American community but also of oppressed people of color across the planet. In this course we will approach the Harlem Renaissance as a nexus of black cosmopolitanism beginning with an analysis of some of its central literary texts and crossing media to examine some of the visual and popular work of the creative period. While unpacking the prose and poetry of Langston Hughes, Nella Larsen, Claude McKay, and other integral figures, we will trace the conceptualization of Harlem itself as a virtual ârace capital,â and we will deal with several more contemporary representations of the aesthetic movement, its political ramifications, and its primary operative space. Considering recent critical disputes about the relative success or failure of this âHarlem Renaissance,â we will explore the limitations of this nomenclature. Through a series of readings, discussions, and essay assignments we will address the recurring themes of the movement with a particular focus on transnational mobility and transcultural exchange. We will investigate the location of black cultural production as it is simultaneously equated with and placed in opposition to conventional notions of Americannessâ-a context in which the New Negro is realized as both producer and product, commodifier and commodified body.

title: 20th Century Black Women Writers
description: In the twentieth-century, African American women writers participate in the enterprise of self-definition and self-representation while challenging society's constructions of Black subjectivity. By documenting and revealing African American culture and history in their writings, Black women writers also serve as cultural and historical conservators. Beginning with Larsen's modernist novel, we will examine representative texts by contemporary African American women from a variety of critical approaches, employing especially the lens of cultural criticism, critical race theory, Black feminist thought, and new historicism. We will also read critical essays to contextualize our interrogations of the primary texts. This course is reading and writing intensive.

title: 20C Poetry
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR CAROLYN FORCHE ENGL 655 The Poetics of Lyric Space in the Twentieth and Twenty-first Centuries In this seminar we will read post-war and recent poetry, in English and translation, as well as short lyric prose, and excerpts from works on the philosophy of lyric poetics. Poets will include Paul Celan, Tomaz Salamun, Michael Ondaatje, Fanny Howe and others; prose writers in philosophy and poetics will include, among others, Gaston Bachelard, Emmanuel Levinas, Walter Benjamin, and Giorgio Agamben. We will address the poetics of witness, the construction of lyric selfhood, lyric temporality and lyric space.

title: Modern and Contemporary Poems and Practice
description: This course will provide a close study of significant Modern and contemporary poems, and of the prose arguments by poets that (seemingly) advocate for such poems. Some changes in aesthetic theory have been triggered by groundbreaking poems, and by the essays and letters of poets. But groundbreaking poems may also undermine the aesthetic theory proposed by their makers, and others. This course will test theory against practice, that is, against our own, developed readings of poems. We will begin by taking a brief tour of 'pre-historical' arts movements such Romanticism, then look closely at the major Modern poets, and at some of the outwardly spiraling clusters of significant poets producing notable work since then. The course assumes that you have a developed literary background; it does not assume that you are very experienced in reading poems; and it may even recognize that you find poems difficult, murky, or bewildering. We will read fro m Ramazani & Ellmann's Norton Anthology of Modern & Contemporary Poetry, and several single volumes. We'll write short papers; give presentations; produce a longer research essay on an area of your interest; meet poets; and try our hand at writing some poems (ungraded).

title: Class Fictions in the Contemporary US
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR PAMELA FOX ENGL 675 Class Fictions in the Contemporary U.S. This interdisciplinary course examines U.S. literary and popular cultural representations of âlowâ classes/cultures (working class/poor/âunderclassâ) from the 1990s to the present. With the aid of recent theory and cultural criticism, we will focus on ways in which categories such as race, gender, and sexuality help to construct our contemporary notions of what âclassâ means in America. We will be studying a wide variety of texts, forms, and genresâfiction, poetry, autobiography, film, musicâand taking up a broad spectrum of issues, including theories of working class identity, social/cultural resistance, and the notion of ârepresentationâ itself. Course work will include 1 short paper (5-7 pp.); 1 class presentation; 1 seminar paper (15-20 pp.). Some likely texts include: Fiction: Allisonâs Bastard Out of Carolina, Viramontesâ Under the Feet of Jesus; Meyerâs American Rust; Autobiography/Memoir: hooksâ Bone Black and Marâs Paper Daughter; Film: Hustle and Flow, SherryBaby, Frozen River, Real Women Have Curves, Precious (a selection from this possible mix!); Music: recent country and hip hop.

title: Orientalism and the Human
description: This seminar examines the ongoing critical discussion arising from the publication of Edward Saidâs Orientalism in 1978. A particular emphasis will be placed on the textâs distinction between secular philology and theological philology, in order to question how that division operates as an expression of the relation between the critical study of languages and the discourse of the human, humanism, and human rights. Primary readings in Saidâs Orientalism, The World, the Text and the Critic, Culture and Imperialism, and Humanism and Democratic Criticism. Secondary readings in Vico, Nietzsche, LukÃ¡cs, Williams, Foucault, Spivak, Bhabha, Butler, Adorno, Agambenâs Homo Sacer, and Cary Wolfeâs Post Humanism.

title: Literary Theory
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR RICARDO ORTIZ ENGL 712 Intro to Critical Theory While the phrase âcritical theoryâ was initially coined in the mid-20th century to name the work done by mostly Marxist intellectuals to make more practical sense of how both high- and mass-cultural production participated in either the legitimization or subversion of the operations of industrialized capital, it has since that moment come to mean more generally the history (initially Western, more recently post-colonial, and even global) of intellectual attempts to make sense of, and even to intervene into, the operations of culture in all its most salient contexts (political, economic, social, historical, technological). This course will survey the most influential figures and statements in that latter construction of âcritical theory,â but with a keen interest in how, and why, that latter construction came to subsume the former. While no one problem, question, or theme will govern the selection of topics or readings, or even guide most discussions, the course will devote significant attention to the fate of critical theory, and of intellectual labor more generally, in what many have been calling our post-historical, post-cultural and certainly post-theoretical moment. Course readings will consist primarily of essay-length selections collected in at least one anthology (most likely Rivkin and Ryanâs Literary Theory: an Anthology) and across a short list of representative monographs of fairly recent vintage (candidates include Fredric Jamesonâs The Cultural Turn, Judith Butlerâs Excitable Speech, and George YÃºdiceâs The Expediency of Culture). Course assignments will include one oral presentation, a short paper related to the presentation, and a longer end-of-term paper/project.

title: Postcolonial Studies
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR HENRY SCHWARZ ENGL 715 Postcolonial Studies An introduction to the rapidly growing field of Postcolonial Studies in literature, film, social and cultural theory, other media. The modern world has been profoundly shaped by colonial processes. This course will survey outstanding examples of the literature and theory representing colonialism around the world, concentrating on the various national resistance movements that have struggled for decolonization since WWII. Issues of class, race, gender and sexuality will be foregrounded in the movements for national liberation, and we will view literary and cultural expression in the context of wider efforts to achieve justice and equality. Thematic and regional emphases will vary from year to year. Writers may include Frantz Fanon, Jean Rhys, Edward Said, Mahasweta Devi, C. L. R. James, Gayatri Spivak, Michael Ondaatje, Homi Bhabha, Leila Khaled. Class presentation, three papers, collaborative research project.

title: Approaches to Teaching Writing
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR NORMA TILDEN ENGL 722 Approaches to Teaching Writing This course offers a practical introduction to the history, theories, and issues that shape the field of Writing Studies. The systematic study of the production of texts â rhetoric and composition --began as a âschoolâ discipline, and these origins are preserved in the title of our course. Written assignments and class discussion will focus on teaching and course design, with particular attention to the integration of reading and writing. Weekly readings will assist you to design writing assignments and to theorize your own practice in composing assignments for your courses. But the teachable parts of writing need not be reduced to techniques, whether of close reading or mechanisms of process, skill, and craft. In the words of theorist Walter Ong, âWriting is a technology that restructures thought.â We will explore issues of cultural and critical literacy as these concepts inform the teaching of writing. By definition, the graduate seminar offers the opportunity to enter an ongoing conversation among scholars in a particular field as well as a forum for intellectual exchange among peers. Students will be expected to take an active role in the course as evidenced by close, critical attention to the readings; contributions to discussion; organized seminar presentations of their research; and engaged, informed writing. Bulletin: A practical introduction to the history, theories, and issues that shape the field of Writing Studies and inform the teaching of writing.

title: Transnational Feminist Theory
description: The boom in the academic production of and attention to âtransnational feminismâ has been well under way since the nineteen-nineties. But what does the term mean, and why and how has it gained such prominence in the academy and beyond? We will read some new work in the field while we investigate some of its foundational theories, anthologies, and cultural texts in an attempt to map the work that transnational feminism hopes to do and undo in an era of globalization. Looking particularly at the intersections of gender and aesthetic practices, we will think about how feminism might move, and move differently, across various national and international frameworks, and discourses of global economics, human rights, and queer desire. We will read the critical work of Inderpal Grewal, Anne McClintock, Chandra Mohanty, and M. Jacqui Alexander alongside of literature by Miriama Ba, Mahesweta Devi, Assia Djebar, and Jamaica Kincaid, and films by Deepa Mehta and others. Assignments will include a presentation, an abstract, an annotated bibliography, and a final project/paper.

title: Rhetoric, Technology, and Culture
description: The ubiquity of electronic communication and the evolution of new media technologies (e.g., Facebook, Wikipedia, and online games) need no introduction. Our public and private lives are increasingly lived across electronic networks. Wireless technologies and portable smart devices have only accelerated this migration. The move toward networks of new media, which has been scrutinized from a number of critical perspectives, has been deeply felt in the areas of rhetoric and writing. Both fields have devoted significant intellectual energy exploring the implications and promises of technology: what is its impact on the composition and distribution of texts, on the work of persuasion and identification, and on the cultivation of ethos and identity? New technologies provide fresh ways of understanding the ancient art of rhetoric, which this course, borrowing from George Kennedy, defines as âthe energy inherent in emotion and thought, transmitted through a system of signs, including language, to others to influence their decisions or actions.â This definition will likely evolve over the course of the semester. Merging form and content, students in this course use (or perform) new media technologies to theorize rhetoric, technology, and culture. For instance, students collaborate online with graduate students at another university, and they are encouraged to incorporate their own wireless and smart device technologies (e.g., laptops, iPhones, and iPads) into the classroom. Students also produce weekly reading responses as audio (or video) podcasts. Class time is devoted to traditional discussions but also to engagements with or performances of new media technologies (e.g. workshops, demonstrations, and tutorials). Students likewise develop a research program, which includes a book review of a relevant text, a conference proposal, and a conference paper/presentation. In addition to developing a theoretically robust response to rhetoric, technology, and culture, it is hoped that students leave the course prepared to integrate technology into their research and teaching. Students with a variety of academic and research interests are encouraged to enroll, as are students with varying comfort levels with new media technology. This course hopes to generate a diverse set of critical rhetorical practices and responses to technology: a diverse student population is crucial.

title: Contemporary Queer Theory
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR DANA LUCIANO ENGL 840 This seminar explores a number of the directions in which âqueer theoryâ has extended itself over the past decade or so, locating these within a larger history of critical, theoretical and political debates on gender, sexuality and pleasure. Topics of particular interest will include: theoretical and political remappings of the body and of sexuality; diasporic, global and transnational queer studies; the relationship between queer theory and (trans)gender studies; queer pasts and queer futures. Students will be expected to engage actively in both in-class and online discussion, to give one in-class presentation and to write two short papers and a longer seminar essay. No prior exposure to queer theory or critical sexuality studies is required.

title: Literacy and the Law
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR JOHN HIRSH ENGL 861 Literature and the Law This seminar explores the relationships between literacy and law. The course will explore learning theories and practices regarding emergent literacy and examine their legal and social implications. Readings will be drawn from three areas: educational theory and practice, law, and childrenâs literature. The course will focus upon whole language and other approaches which now inform literacy instruction in America. Legal implications to be considered may include restrictions on literacy due to slavery and educational disadvantage, statutory interpretation, equal protection and diversity, freedom of speech, school finance, discipline, and English as a second language. In order to provide practical experience in emergent literacy, students are required to participate in a practicum of at least one hour per week in which they work with a student in emergent literacy. The practicum may be a tutorial or literacy program sponsored by the Law Center (normally, the beginning reading program at Sursum Corda community near the Law Center) or some other arrangement approved by the instructors. Writing requirements include both a journal and short paper. The seminar is also open to graduate students in the Department of English, with permission of the instructor and Director of Graduate Studies. Cross-listed with LAWJ-315-05

title: Folger Institute Seminar
description: Seminar courses offered through the Folger Institute.

title: Thesis Research I
description: Graduate students enrolled in a master's thesis program or a doctoral program who have completed all coursework must register for Thesis Research - 999, section 1, in each Fall and Spring semester; the registration fee is currently $2,750 per semester. This includes students who have completed all course work and plan to take language proficiency examinations or comprehensive examinations before beginning research and writing of the thesis or dissertation.

title: Thesis Research III
description: Students enrolled in a master's thesis program or a doctoral program who are near the end of their coursework, and for whom registration for the remaining required course credits would result in less than full-time enrollment, may register for Thesis Research - 999, section 3. Those who do not need to be certified as enrolled full-time are not required to do so. No additional registration fee will be assessed for this enrollment (i.e., the student will be charged only the tuition rate for the enrolled course credits).

title: Intensive EFL Basic
description: Students establish a firm foundation of English sounds, forms, and functions in order to communicate with English speakers in everyday settings and to function in an English-speaking environment. Reading and writing activities reinforce the oral communications work and help students develop a balanced language skill profile.

title: Intensive EFL Basic
description: Students establish a firm foundation of English sounds, forms, and functions in order to communicate with English speakers in everyday settings and to function in an English-speaking environment. Reading and writing activities reinforce the oral communications work and help students develop a balanced language skill profile.

title: Intensive EFL Low Intermediate
description: Integrated learning activities involving all skill areas help students practice and apply the fundamentals of English in more varied settings and develop more accuracy and control of their spoken and written English. Reading activities promote vocabulary expansion while writing activities teach the basics of paragraph development and essay organization.

title: Intensive EFL Low Intermediate
description: Integrated learning activities involving all skill areas help students practice and apply the fundamentals of English in more varied settings and develop more accuracy and control of their spoken and written English. Reading activities promote vocabulary expansion while writing activities teach the basics of paragraph development and essay organization.

title: Intensive EFL Intermediate
description: Students learn the more complex forms and functions of English to express their ideas more clearly and accurately. Classroom and independent reading texts include both adapted and authentic material. Student writing activities include journals, short compositions, and longer essays. Students select two elective classes that focus on either pronunciation or vocabulary in different content areas.

title: Intensive EFL Intermediate
description: Students learn the more complex forms and functions of English to express their ideas more clearly and accurately. Classroom and independent reading texts include both adapted and authentic material. Student writing activities include journals, short compositions, and longer essays. Students select two elective classes that focus on either pronunciation or vocabulary in different content areas.

title: Intensive EFL High Intermediate
description: Students target their English skill development toward more specialized language learning goals. Core classes help students develop accuracy and fluency in speaking and writing, understand the main and supporting ideas of authentic listening and reading materials, and apply their language skills to more complex communicative and guided research tasks. Students choose four elective classes that focus on refining specific language areas including grammar, vocabulary, and pronunciation.

title: Intensive EFL High Intermediate
description: Students target their English skill development toward more specialized language learning goals. Core classes help students develop accuracy and fluency in speaking and writing, understand the main and supporting ideas of authentic listening and reading materials, and apply their language skills to more complex communicative and guided research tasks. Students choose four elective classes that focus on refining specific language areas including grammar, vocabulary, and pronunciation.

title: Advanced English
description: Advanced English courses are offered with content and skill focus in three areas: academic, professional, and cultural. Advanced Academic is designed to prepare students for study in a university setting. Advanced Professional is designed for students who expect to work in areas that demand a high level of English proficiency. Advanced Cultural English is designed for students who are interested in increasing their use of English for cross cultural communication. Courses include instruction in advanced reading, writing and research skills as well as aural/oral communication skills. Students also enroll in two advanced elective classes that address communication skills in specific content areas.

title: Academic Bridge
description: The Academic Bridge course is designed for students who have been or expect to be accepted into a U.S. university and want to polish their research, academic writing, and classroom presentation and discussion skills. Academic Bridge students also audit one unversity course during the first half of the semester in order to prepare for the academic demands of university courses.

title: Academic Bridge
description: The Academic Bridge course is designed for students who have been or expect to be accepted into a U.S. university and want to polish their research, academic writing, and classroom presentation and discussion skills. Academic Bridge students also audit one unversity course during the first half of the semester in order to prepare for the academic demands of university courses.

title: Expository Writing for Foreign Students
description: The primary aim of this course is to assist foreign students in preparing themselves for the type of writing required for themes, essay examinations, term papers, and lengthy reports. The principles of rhetorical organization and development are thoroughly presented within the context of each student's language and cultural background. Frequent individual conferences with the instructor are scheduled. Four class hours weekly. Pass/fail grade only.

title: Expository Writing for Foreign Students
description: The primary aim of this course is to assist foreign students in preparing themselves for the type of writing required for themes, essay examinations, term papers, and lengthy reports. The principles of rhetorical organization and development are thoroughly presented within the context of each student's language and cultural background. Frequent individual conferences with the instructor are scheduled. Four class hours weekly. Pass/fail grade only.

title: Critical Writing for International Affairs
description: This course is designed to help you to improve your reading and writing skills so that you can communicate clearly and effectively in your academic writing assignments, including essay assignments, essay exams, and research papers. You will read articles on political issues, government policies, and international affairs in order to identify how good writers communicate effectively in English. We will focus on reading materials both from your textbook and from newspaper, professional journals, and other sources in the field of international affairs. These texts will serve as models of good writing, and you will analyze them and apply what you learn to your own writing. This course is for SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Literature and Writing for Foreign Students
description: Through the use of selected American writings, supplemented by other media, this course provides an introduction to American literature, life, and culture which will help foreign students understand and give written expression to their personal experiences here. The course requires extensive writing and discussion. Fall semester focuses on twentieth century American fiction. Spring semester covers American literature to 1900 in survey format. (Foreign students who are not native speakers of English may elect this course to satisfy the requirement in literature.)

title: Literature and Writing for Foreign Students
description: Through the use of selected American writings, supplemented by other media, this course provides an introduction to American literature, life, and culture which will help foreign students understand and give written expression to their personal experiences here. The course requires extensive writing and discussion. Fall semester focuses on twentieth century American fiction. Spring semester covers American literature to 1900 in survey format. (Foreign students who are not native speakers of English may elect this course to satisfy the requirement in literature.)

title: Gateway to Film and Media Studies
description: This course will introduce students to the major elements of the Film & Media Studies Minor: the fundamentals of film composition and theory; media histories; practical experience of producing visual images and sound; and awareness of issues of social justice. We will also discuss spectatorship, audiences, and industrial structures of film, in addition to examining newer media and distribution platforms, ranging from radio and television to videotape and the internet. A portfolio of assignments will give students an opportunity to demonstrate their proficiency in analysis and technology as well as to reflect on aspects of production, post-production, and reception. Pre-requisites: Admission to the Film & Media Studies minor

title: Fundamentals of Finance
description: This course is a survey course, designed to explore the major concepts in corporate finance. Topics include Time Value of Money; Net Present Value/Internal Rate of Return; Valuation of Stocks and Bonds; Financial Statements/Ratios; Managing Growth; Discounted Cash Flow; Risk, Return and the Cost of Capital; Capital Structure; Dividends; Raising Capital/Going Public; Mergers and Acquisitions; and Corporate Restructuring. The course focuses on applications of finance to assist students outside the Business School to understand financial statements, apply financial analytical techniques, and learn the basics of the capital markets. Note: students will not be expected to have prior knowledge of course concepts.

title: Business Financial Management
description: This basic course in finance covers the functions of financial managers, financial markets, and financial analysis tools, and corporate financial decision making. It provides an introduction to cost of capital, investment analysis, capital budgeting and the valuation of securities.

title: Advanced Financial Management
description: This course provides an extensive coverage of corporate finance theory and the applicability of this theory for the financial manager. Topics include: capital budgeting under uncertainty; the relevance of capital structure decisions on security valuation and riskiness; the theory and practice of dividend policy; implications of financial market efficiency for management; risks and rewards of international financial markets; security valuation including the use of option pricing models; the market for corporate control; risk management through the use of options and future contracts; financial engineering; corporate restructuring and bankruptcy. Cases are used. Prerequisite: Business Financial Management.

title: Real Estate Finance
description: The course is structured to give the student an introduction to the principles of real estate with an emphasis in finance. Topics include real estate and the economy, financing residential properties, sales contracts, leases, appraisal, land use controls, and the real estate development process.

title: Investment Banking
description: The primary goal of the course is to provide a broad overview of the investment banking business and mergers and acquisitions. The first half of the class will deal with general investment banking topics, including the structure and direction of the securities industry and its participants, the securities offering process (focusing on initial public offerings), convertible and high yield debt securities, spin offs and carveouts. The second half of the course will examine mergers and acquisitions. Topics to be covered include deal design and structure, valuation of synergies, deal tactics, hostile takeovers, and private equity. The course will have a case orientation.

title: Principles of Investment
description: Course Objective: The objective of this course is to teach finance students about investment theory and practice. The course will utilize a combination of theoretical studies, case applications and a portfolio project to teach students about: 1) the structure of the capital markets; 2) theories and practice in portfolio management; 3) asset pricing theories used to analyze securities; 4) equity and debt securities; and 5) derivative instruments. The course will focus primarily on the U.S. markets, although we will touch briefly on international investments. This course will provide students with information and skills needed to succeed in careers in asset management, corporate finance, research, sales or trading.

title: Management of Financial Institutions
description: The course will be organized into interconnected modules. Each module will draw heavily on concepts of modern financial theory and risk management as practiced by leading firms. The first module will provide a contextual framework for assessing current and future industry structure, competitiveness and risk. This module will provide students with perspective on the management issues facing commercial and investment banks. With this industry perspective as background, the second module will concentrate on industry and company financial statement analysis, performance analysis and company valuation techniques. The levers for managing risk, return and growth to enhance enterprise value will be examined. The techniques learned and conclusions reached will be used in assessing the creation of value in FIs and in assessing corporate strategy development and financial management policies. Armed with this knowledge of profit performance and strategy development, the third module will deal with a critical dimension of FI management: the management of interest rate risk and overall firm asset/liability management as a key driver of profitability and market value. Techniques for spread management and hedging consistent with a firmâs strategies and objectives will be examined in detail. The fourth module will be a more specific assessment of risk, return trade-offs of portfolio allocation decisions as well as those of funding, liquidity and capital management. This module will also deal extensively with FIâs drive for additional diversification and new sources of income. The emphasis here will be on financial engineered products such as securitization of assets, corporate finance, secondary market making, and trading and arbitrage activities. This module will take the perspective of the ânew wholesale bankingâ, which encompasses Investment banking. The fifth module will concentrate on modern retail banking and brokerage including the commercial loan aspects of major consumer oriented banks. The evolution of commercial and consumer loan practices and pricing will be examined from a risk, return standpoint and also from the perspective of developing different product market segments. The final module will look ahead toward the next Century. The module will concentrate on new product market development, technology and distribution innovation as well as strategies for geographic consolidation, corporate restructuring and expansion of global reach. The future outlook for the FI industry will also be discussed.

title: Fixed Income Securities
description: The objective of this course is to provide an on overview of fixed income markets and securities. The course will be useful to students who are interested in pursuing careers in corporate finance, banking or investment management. The course will provide highly practical information as well as theoretical information regarding US Government and foreign government securities, corporate bonds, municipal bonds, asset backed securities, hybrid securities, bond funds, and strategies related to investing in and issuing fixed income securities. During each class, in addition to discussing the theory critical to understanding the fixed income markets, we will discuss the current market environment and real-life decisions that finance professionals must make in this environment.

title: Global Financial Markets and Institutions
description: A study of the flow of funds through the domestic and international markets and institutions. Emphasis will be on the roles of money and capital markets and financial institutions. Topics will include interest rate determination, financial intermediation, regulation of financial services, and the role of the Federal Reserve and other central banks.

title: International Finance
description: The course addresses key issues of international financial management. It studies the international financial environment as well as corporations' strategic alternatives and operational practices. The following topics are among those covered: foreign exchange markets and the management of foreign exchange exposure; capital budgeting in multinational corporations; cross-border lending by banks; trade finance; and foreign direct investment. The reading materials and class discussions include both theoretical analyses and cases.

title: Corporate Governance & Valuation
description: Recent corporate scandals have put the spotlight on the importance of effective corporate governance. This course will examine the financial aspects of corporate governance and its effect on value creation for shareholders. Various readings and case studies will be used to provide students with real-life examples of the key elements of corporate governance. The course is geared toward undergraduate business students who are majoring in Finance. This course is ideal for students who are considering careers in corporate finance, investment banking, auditing, or law. To be successful in this course a student should possess strong analytical and communication skills. The emphasis of the course will be on the impact of corporate governance decisions on the value of the firm and value creation for shareholders of publicly traded firms. The course will apply valuation techniques to case studies involving corporate governance decisions within firms.

title: Derivatives and Financial Markets
description: This course program is designed to expand participants' understanding of derivative-related financial instruments (forwards, futures and options) and their use in investment and corporate financial management.

title: Entreprenuerial Finance
description: The course would seek to examine entrepreneurial firms and challenges in financing such firms. The course would be heavily based on cases, many of which would be based on young firms although some would be about more established firms. An entrepreneur needs to understand how to identify a business opportunity as well as how to marshal resources (especially capital) to and exploit the business opportunity. The course would reinforce skills needed to analyze a business (with a strong emphasis on valuation techniques). The course would then focus on issues relevant for financing of entrepreneurial ventures. This would include understanding financial institutions (Angel investors, Venture Capitalists, and Banks) that provide risk capital for such firms. The course would conclude by examining the harvesting of entrepreneurial ventures where by the entrepreneur and the financiers realize the returns for their effort.

title: Fundamentals of Finance
description: This course is a survey course, designed to explore the major concepts in corporate finance. Topics include Time Value of Money; Net Present Value/Internal Rate of Return; Valuation of Stocks and Bonds; Financial Statements/Ratios; Managing Growth; Discounted Cash Flow; Risk, Return and the Cost of Capital; Capital Structure; Dividends; Raising Capital/Going Public; Mergers and Acquisitions; and Corporate Restructuring. The course focuses on applications of finance to assist students outside the Business School to understand financial statements, apply financial analytical techniques, and learn the basics of the capital markets. Note: students will not be expected to have prior knowledge of course concepts.

title: International Finance
description: This course extends the principles of financial management and investments to the international context. The course will be concerned with understanding the international finance environment and the implication of exchange rates on firm value and investments. This includes the understanding of the determinants of exchange rates and the international currency markets, including institutional structures and underlying economics. This section includes hedging practices of international firms. This course is designed from the viewpoint of someone that participates in the international financial environment, whether this person is a CFO, General Manager or Treasurer of an international firm, an investment banker, an investor, or a consultant.

title: Financial Markets
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. MBA Core In FINC 551, and FINC 557 we shall take the perspective of a high-level executive (CEO, CFO) and we shall focus extensively on a specific scarce resource which economists call CAPITAL and you and I call cash or money. We know cash is scarce - after all we would all like to have more cash/capital. Since there is not enough capital to go around - how is it (and how should it) be allocated is the central issue underlying this course. This capital allocation takes place at two levels - First, capital is allocated by capital markets across the wider economy (e.g. corporations such as Starbucks, IBM, Gap etc., as well as Federal, State, and Local governments). At the second level, capital is allocated to different projects within a corporation (e.g. Ford's decision to invest in a Chinese plant). Thus as a business manager you would be faced with two types of decisions where knowledge of Finance is critical. â¢How to gain access to capital? (This decision is the focus of FINANCE I) â¢How to invest available capital? (This decision is the focus of FINANCE II)

title: Investment Analysis
description: *This course focuses on the application of investment theory and contemporary research to the practice of equity securities analysis and portfolio management. Topics include valuation of equity securities, investment management for institutional and individual investors, and portfolio performance evaluation. *

title: Investment Banking
description: The primary goal of the course is to provide a broad overview of the investment banking business. Topics will include a current industry issues and trends, the basics of valuation, the securities offering process (focusing on initial public offerings), a review of various types of debt securities, and an overview of leveraged buyouts and private equity. The course will be a mixture of case studies and lecture.

title: Derivatives and Financial Markets
description: Description: This course develops understanding of the basic derivative-related financial instruments (forwards, swaps, futures and at-the-money options), and their use in transforming and managing risky investments and projects. Objectives: To provide a basic understanding of derivatives practice and use in financial markets. To provide practical and simple investment and corporate financial management strategies using derivatives. To allow students to apply these concepts and skills to meet investment and corporate finance objectives, using a series of examples.

title: Corporate Finance
description: This course focuses on aspects of corporate financing decisions including cost of capital, capital structure, dividend policy, and an introduction to the use of derivatives for risk management. Emphasis is placed on the application of finance principles to business cases. Prerequisite: FINC 551. MBA 1st years students only.

title: Real Estate Finance
description: This course covers the financing of residential and commercial real estate from the standpoint of both equity and debt investors. Major topics include valuation of income-producing real estate, analysis of markets & risk factors unique to real estate & development, mortgage financing, secondary mortgage markets, land development and construction loans, legal and regulatory issues.

title: Fixed Income Securities
description: The objective of this course is to provide an on overview of fixed income markets and securities. The course will be useful to students who are interested in pursuing careers in corporate finance, banking or investment management. The course will provide highly practical information as well as theoretical information regarding US Treasury securities, corporate bonds, municipal bonds, asset backed securities, hybrid securities, bond funds, and strategies related to investing in and issuing fixed income securities. During each class, in addition to discussing the theory critical to understanding the fixed income markets, we will discuss the current market environment and real-life decisions that finance professionals must make in this environment.

title: International Finance
description: This course extends the principles of financial management and investments to the international context. The course will be concerned with understanding the international finance environment and the implication of exchange rates on firm value and investments. This includes the understanding of the determinants of exchange rates and the international currency markets, including institutional structures and underlying economics. This section includes hedging practices of international firms. This course is designed from the viewpoint of someone that participates in the international financial environment, whether this person is a CFO, General Manager or Treasurer of an international firm, an investment banker, an investor, or a consultant.

title: Applied Project Finance
description: This course focuses on the application of project finance techniques to the financing of large-scale infrastructure projects. We will begin by defining project financing as a technique distinct from other types of financing, such as corporate finance, and identifying the key participants in a complex infrastructure transaction and thoroughly discussing the structure of a large scale project. We will then turn to an analysis of the key components of a successful project finance transaction, using actual transactional experience as our guide. A substantial portion of the course will focus on the identification and allocation of project risks between various participants. Special attention will be given to the perspectives of project developers on such risks. We will demonstrate that the project finance model, including related sensitivity analysis, is a critical quantitative tool for project evaluation. Building on all of these concepts, we will turn our study to new markets. The course will be taught by Rusty Brewer and Laura Hegedus. Rusty and Laura are attorneys at the law firm of Cooley LLP. Biographical information of Rusty and Laura can be found at //www.cooley.com.

title: Corporate Risk Management
description: Corporate Risk Management is the sequel to Advanced Corporate Finance. Topics include: extensions of capital budgeting, mergers and acquisition, and valuation into an international context; foundations of option pricing and application of option pricing to capital budgeting and security valuation including real options; measuring and managing a firmâs risk exposures. Topics in international financial markets are studied in depth from a managerial perspective with an emphasis on hedging exchange rate risk.

title: Real Estate Financial Markets
description: â¢ In real value terms, Real Estate is the single largest, and arguably most important, asset class in the world. Ownership and leasing of real estate assets is critical to operation of businesses of all types and sizes, is the largest component of most consumerâs monthly expenditures (in the form of rent or mortgage payments), literally provides the âplaceâ in retail marketplaces worldwide and is the most significant collateral asset for bank loans and asset-backed securities markets. â¢ Capital markets for real estate are similar to other asset classes, in the sense that equity and debt interests are valued using similar methods of cash flow discounting and multiples. The underlying risk factors of owning real estate (or taking real estate as loan collateral) involve some similar factors such as market, interest rate, term and credit risk, but also require nuanced understanding of risks somewhat unique real estate - particularly cyclical supply/demand dynamics which may vary widely in terms of capital markets (cyclicality of financing and liquidity) as well as geography (cyclicality of supply/demand for real estate assets). â¢ The course will examine the process of financing commercial real estate from the standpoint of both debt and equity investors. Consideration will be given to asset valuation methodologies, debt and equity structures, examination of special risks and risk management techniques. Public market instruments, such as Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) and commercial mortgage backed securities (CMBS), will be examined as well as private equity instruments such as limited partnership interests. â¢ Each week will mix class lecture with analysis of case studies, with students leading class discussion on group case study assignments. Note: you are responsible for reading ALL case studies, not just those assigned to your group. I reserve the right to deduct points from your grade if you are unprepared for the case discussion in any given week.

title: Alternative Investments
description: Alternative Investments have grown tremendously as an asset class in recent years and yet are not well-understood. This course will focus mainly on two classes of Alternative Investments: Hedge Funds and Private Equity. It will cover hedge fund operations and strategies, investment and valuation, risk management, performance measurement, and regulatory issues. The course will provide an understanding of the private equity market, market participants, financial strategies, value creation, deal structuring, and exit strategies. We will also discuss the current state of the industry and its role in global capital markets including the entrance of new participants, such as, sovereign funds. Lectures will be supplemented by case studies and a number of guest speakers from industry and regulatory organizations.

title: Special Topics: Venture Capital
description: Venture Capital" is unique form of capital and the impact of this source of capital is considerably larger than its size. It is frequently cited as one of the main reasons why entrepreneurial activity is much higher in the United States compared to the other parts of the world. On the other hand popular press has demonized venture capitalist as âVulture Capitalistâ seeking to hoodwink hard working entrepreneurs in giving away their companies for cheap. This course aims to increase studentsâ understanding of how these organizations are structured and operate, why they take the form they do, and what special challenges and opportunities this industry faces.

title: Investment Management for Endowments and Pensions
description: Spring 2008 description: This seminar course examines the investment process used by the investment offices at endowments and pensions. Students will study the tools and the investment challenges faced by investment managers at such institutions. These include evaluating the role of the endowment and pension, portfolio choice, manager choice, asset allocation, risk management and alternative asset class investing.

title: Measuring & Creating Value
description: This course focuses on core principles of value creation for businesses. Students will be placed in the role of senior corporate managers and be tasked with understanding how and why shareholder value is created by managerial decisions. Students will first learn why value is a relevant metric for managers to examine as well as different valuation methods. The bulk of the course will involve the detailed mechanics of how to measure firm value through multiple hands-on exercises involving a live case with a publicly traded company. The course will conclude with a broad overview of the types of decisions which managers can implement to create and sustain value for their shareholders.

title: Corporate Restructuring
description: A Seminar in Corporate Restructuring is led by Senior Managing Directors of FTI Consulting and supplemented with the experience of various guest lecturers from the business community. The class explores the strategic, financial and operational skills critical to successfully improving performance during periods of significant transition including business turnarounds, distressed company sales and bankruptcy. The course combines lectures, case studies and readings.

title: Financial Markets:  Institutions, Innovations and Regulations
description: This course aims to develop a deep understanding about how real-life financial markets function and how banks, hedge funds and financial institutions profit from a changing technological, competitive and regulatory environment. The emphasis of the course is on how actual financial markets function. The course will integrate and strengthen your knowledge about financial markets. It will do so in three ways, byâ¦ - Interacting with lots of experienced practitioners that will be coming to class as guest speakers. - Discussing how real financial markets operate, though the use of case studies (rather than solely by abstract theoretical models). - Delivering hands-on expertise and analyzing the latest developments in the market. Previous students have reported that this approach has many synergies with the job market during the fall. The course will analyze in a recent technological, institutional, legal and regulatory topics, including: - What are the different business models of financial institutions such as banks, exchanges, electronic communications networks, savings and loans, and âagenciesâ? - How do hedge funds actually work? What are the implications for how markets function? - The evolving structure of stock and bond markets. - The 1990s âdot comâ bubble: causes and practical consequences. - Research analyst conflicts within banks: emerging business models. - Functions of different financial regulators, policy goals and unintended consequences. - The integration between commercial and investment banks. - The possible existence of a housing bubble.

title: Financial Services, Management and Regulations
description: The course focuses on financial analysis and regulation of insured American depository institutions, the Federal Reserve, the European Central Bank, the Bank of Japan, the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund. Key balance sheet and income ratios will be employed to analyze bank assets, liabilities, capital, and risk management. Financial institutionsâ cost structures, revenue models, and competitive markets are employed to examine the implications of regulation and optimal decision making.

title: Financial Markets and Public Policy in a Time of Crisis
description: This course examines the connections between financial markets, regulation, and monetary policy, and the impact of recent developments on the economy and the business environment facing firms. The course is aimed at students who enjoyed their introduction to macroeconomics and would like more depth, or students who will be working in financial markets and want a deeper understanding of how the economy and financial markets fit together. The ongoing financial crisis will provide a natural prism through which to relate the analytical material to the business environment

title: Financial Engineering in a Time of Crisis
description: In this course students will learn how to apply the tools of corporate finance to analyze a series of market interventions that were made by the Government during the recent financial crisis. We will review the impact of the crisis on specific markets; the various alternatives considered by policy makers in response and then assess the actual programs. There will be an emphasis on developing an understanding of the objectives, the detailed financial design, and the outcome of each program. Key to understanding whether a policy approach results in âsuccessâ will be developing an ability to recognize how to balance the often competing interests of the marketplace, politics, and public policy. While this class has been designed to complement Phil Swagelâs class on the macro-economic causes of the crises, Professor Swagelâs class is not a pre-requisite. We will be digging in at the micro level to understand specific market dynamics, financial engineering choices faced by policy makers and the pro and cons of the numerous programs they designed.

title: Introductory French I
description: This sequence is intended for students with little or no previous background in French. Through an audiovisual introduction to French culture, emphasis is placed on the active use of the spoken language with diversified oral and written exercises, dictation, and conversational practice. Required laboratory.

title: Introductory French I
description: This sequence is intended for students with little or no previous background in French. Through an audiovisual introduction to French culture, emphasis is placed on the active use of the spoken language with diversified oral and written exercises, dictation, and conversational practice. Required laboratory. This course is open to SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Introductory French II
description: This sequence is intended for students with little or no previous background in French. Through an audiovisual introduction to French culture, emphasis is placed on the active use of the spoken language with diversified oral and written exercises, dictation, and conversational practice. Required laboratory.

title: Introductory French II
description: This sequence is intended for students with little or no previous background in French. Through an audiovisual introduction to French culture, emphasis is placed on the active use of the spoken language with diversified oral and written exercises, dictation, and conversational practice. Required laboratory. This course is open to SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Intensive Basic French
description: This sequence is designed for beginners who desire intensive instruction in oral communication as well as in basic grammar and writing skills. Pronunciation drills, grammatical patterns, aural comprehension exercises, acquisition of common vocabulary, and conversational practice are integrated within an audio-visual introduction to French culture.

title: Intermediate French I
description: Intermediate French I (FREN 021) is the continuation of Introductory French II (FREN 002). Students who enroll in this course have typically taken Introductory French II (FREN 002) or have been placed in this course by means of the French Department Placement Test. This course is based on the Intermediate French method âEnsembleâ (John Wiley & Sons, Inc.), which consists of a grammar text (with an optional workbook) and a reader. Additional audio-visual material will be provided by the instructor. This course will help you develop and strengthen all four language skills (speaking, listening, reading, and writing) and expand on your every-day vocabulary.

title: Intermediate French I
description: In developing their speaking, listening, reading, and writing skills, students have the option of either continuing and diversifying work with the audiovisual program introduced in the Basic French program, or embarking on a more traditionally text-based program of intermediate study. This course is open to SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Intermediate French II
description: Intermediate French II (FREN 022) is the continuation of Intermediate French I (FREN 021). Students who enroll in this course have typically taken Intermediate French I (FREN 021) or have been placed in this course by means of the French Department Placement Test. This course is based on the Intermediate French method âEnsembleâ (John Wiley & Sons, Inc.), which consists of a grammar text (with an optional workbook) and a reader. Additional audio-visual material will be provided by the instructor. This course will help you develop and strengthen all four language skills (speaking, listening, reading, and writing) and expand on your every-day vocabulary.

title: Intermediate French II
description: In developing their speaking, listening, reading, and writing skills, students have the option of either continuing and diversifying work with the audiovisual program introduced in the Basic French program, or embarking on a more traditionally text-based program of intermediate study. This course is open to SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Intensive Intermediate French
description: This sequence is designed for intermediate language learners who desire intensive instruction in phonology and oral communication as well as in basic grammar and writing skills. Pronunciation drills, grammatical patterns, aural comprehension exercises, acquisition of common vocabulary, and conversational practice are integrated within an audio-visual introduction to French culture. Prerequisite: Basic French (FREN-011 or FREN- 002) or placement.

title: Knights of Old & Harry Potter
description: The objective of this course is to explore the medievalism of J.K. Rowlingâs Harry Potter novels. To do this we need to go back to their medieval antecedents in the 12th-15th centuries, which will allow us to contrast and compare the old and the new. We will read masterpieces of imaginative storytelling from French, German, and English medieval literature in addition to selected Harry Potter volumes, but we will also consult Plato and Joseph Campbell. The old and the new are linked thematically in that they are all narratives about growing up and finding oneâs identity: a complex, mysterious, and sometimes arduous process that the hero/heroine experiences as a magical world where the natural laws governing human existence are suspended, the unexpected is bound to occur, and marvels are reserved for the chosen few. The readings and discussion are in English. Counts as English Department Gateway equivalent.

title: Advanced French I
description: A general survey of French civilization is integrated with continued work on mastery of all requisite language skills. Readings and compositions focus on material from the French media. The three hours of class instruction are supplemented by a required one-hour conversation section each week.

title: Advanced French II
description: A general survey of French civilization is integrated with continued work on mastery of all requisite language skills. Readings and compositions focus on material from the French media. The three hours of class instruction are supplemented by a required one-hour conversation section each week.

title: Advanced French II: Contemporary Civilization
description: This course is a continuation of Advanced I but qualified students who have not taken Advanced I at SFS/Q may be enrolled with permission of the instructor. This course prepares students for gateway and upper-division courses in French. Students will work on improving their oral and writing skills in French through a review of grammar, intensive writing, a study of vocabulary and idioms through magazines and TV news program, as well as the close reading of an integralnovel. They will also gain insights into contemporary issues concerning the French speaking world through a regular reading of the Francophone weekly LâExpress, Le Point, Le Nouvel Observateur and Jeune Afrique, watching the boroadcated news on France 2, and reading Amin Maaloufâs novel LÃ©on lâAfricain in its integrality. Classes will emphasize oral communication by means of oral presentations, debates and discussions. Written grammatical exercises and essays will also contribute to develop a wider and a more precise ability to write in French. This course is for SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Intensive Advanced French I
description: Designed for French majors and other students seeking intensive advanced training in the language, this course provides rigorous and comprehensive work on French speaking, writing, reading, and listening skills as preparation for upper-division offerings. Required laboratory. Prerequisite: Intermediate French (FREN-032 or FREN-022) or placement.

title: Intensive Advanced French II
description: For French majors and other students seeking intensive advanced training in the language, this course provides rigorous and comprehensive work on French speaking, writing, reading, and listening skills as preparation for upper-division offerings or direct matriculation studies in francophone programs. Prerequisite: Advanced I French (FREN-111 or FREN-101) or placement.

title: Advanced Grammar and Writing
description: Through the grammatical, structural, and stylistic study of a variety of literary and non-literary models, this course explores techniques of expression that are practiced in a number of writing forms: descriptions, summaries, critical essays, narrations, and textual analysis.

title: Topics for Oral Proficiency
description: Guided conversations, discussions, and student presentations based on contemporary material from the French media.

title: Social History of the French People
description: Targeted for non-majors, this course presents a chronological survey of French civilization in its historical and social context, and prepares students for upper-division culture electives.

title: Rdg Txts/Fr-Speak World: Cultures
description: General Description of the Sequence: French 250 and 251, required of all French majors and open to other qualified undergraduates, is a post-advanced level sequence that can be taken in any order and is designed to meet two goals. First, the sequence prepares students for upper-division French courses and for direct matriculation overseas study programs by focusing on the development of critical thinking and advanced writing abilities. Second, it enhances studentsâ capacities to perform close reading and analysis by introducing them to a variety of texts and documents selected to increase their content knowledge of the culture, geography, history, literature, and artistic media of the French-speaking world. Individual sections of these courses will emphasize specific approaches to French and Francophone cultures and literatures, such as the anthropological, the historical, the thematic, the formal (including notions of genre) and/or the rhetorical, depending on instructor expertise. Study and analysis will be carried out in class discussion, as well as through specific writing formats (such as explication de texte, dissertation, research paper ) for which students will receive structured training, including systematic procedures for organization, drafting, and rewriting, as well as an introduction to research materials and methodology in the discipline. The final paper for each course will be designed to mobilize the reading, analytical and research competencies developed over the course of the semester. Prerequisites for these courses are Intensive Advanced French II (112) or Advanced Grammar & Writing (151) or direct placement through AP, SAT II and/or formal placement test results. FREN 250 Reading Texts in the French-Speaking World: Cultures French 250 offers a broadly-defined introduction to the complex, multi-ethnic cultures and social practices of France and the French-speaking world. This course focuses on texts, from all historical periods but especially since the French Revolution, that might be categorized as âdocumentsâ: among them, essays, ethnographies, political and historical tracts, life writing and testimonials, journalism, studies, literature, documentary images, and films. In what context were these texts created, how were they crafted, and what impact have they had? What issues and problems do they illuminate within and beyond their communities of origin, and how do we appreciate their âdocumentaryâ value or âauthorityâ? In order to address such questions, students will be given methodological tools and techniques that will allow them to approach texts in a systematic, cogent, and critical manner, both for their content and their form. Key notions such as genre, ideology, autobiography and autofiction, (self-)censorship, and frame will be explored and applied to multiple readings of works selected.

title: Rdg Txts/Fr-Speak World: Lits
description: General Description of the Sequence: French 250 and 251, required of all French majors and open to other qualified undergraduates, is a post-advanced level sequence that can be taken in any order and is designed to meet two goals. First, the sequence prepares students for upper-division French courses and for direct matriculation overseas study programs by focusing on the development of critical thinking and advanced writing abilities. Second, it enhances studentsâ capacities to perform close reading and analysis by introducing them to a variety of texts and documents selected to increase their content knowledge of the culture, geography, history, literature, and artistic media of the French-speaking world. Individual sections of these courses will emphasize specific approaches to French and Francophone cultures and literatures, such as the anthropological, the historical, the thematic, the formal (including notions of genre) and/or the rhetorical, depending on instructor expertise. Study and analysis will be carried out in class discussion, as well as through specific writing formats (such as explication de texte, dissertation, research paper) for which students will receive structured training, including systematic procedures for organization, drafting, and rewriting, as well as an introduction to research materials and methodology in the discipline. The final paper for each course will be designed to mobilize the reading, analytical and research competencies developed over the course of the semester. Prerequisites for these courses are Intensive Advanced French II (112) or Advanced Grammar & Writing (151) or direct placement through AP, SAT II and/or formal placement test results. FREN 251 Reading Texts in the French-Speaking World: Literatures French 251 offers a broadly-defined introduction to the rich, varied literatures and other art forms of France and the French-speaking world. This course focuses on texts, from all historical periods and French-speaking regions, that might be categorized as âfictionâ: among them, works of literature (novels, short stories, poems, plays, essays), âpopularâ narrative forms (comics, detective stories), films, music, and art. In what context were these texts created, how were they crafted, and what impact have they had? What issues and problems do they illuminate within and beyond their communities of origin, and how do we appreciate their aesthetic value? Where do the boundaries of âfictionâ lie and what formal characteristics and vocabulary help us describe different genres? In order to address such questions, students will be given methodological tools and techniques that will allow them to approach texts in a systematic, cogent, and critical manner, both for their content and their form. Key notions such as genre, narrative structures and strategies, rhetorical techniques, reader response, and âhorizon of expectationsâ will be explored and applied to multiple readings of works selected.

title: French Theater Workshop: Moliere
description: MOLIERE, also known as " le grand Poquelin ", is to the French what Shakespeare is to the English, ie. the founding -- but ever young -- reference for theatric accomplishment and endless source of dramatic interpretation. This semester, in keeping with a tradition here at Georgetown going back nearly twenty years, we are going to do scene study from three famous plays by this 17th century master : L'ECOLE DES FEMMES, LE MISANTHROPE and TARTUFFE. This course is resolutely performance-based, meaning : " up-on-your-feet " acting, under the guidance of a very experienced teacher and director. You will be expected to participate on a regular basis in scene work with other actors, maintain a " journal de bord " ( personal theater diary ) and perform in a small-scale production at the conclusion of the semester. For each of the above theater performance workshops, students will present at least two live interpretations and write a paper or theater diary (journal de bord) in French based on their scene interpretation. Both majors and qualified non-majors are invited to enroll. Both French 263 and 270 are cognate courses with the Department of Art, Music, and Theater, and also fulfill the upper-division post-advanced language requirement for the French major.

title: French Theater Workshop: Sartre and Claudel
description: Ever since 1990, Dr. Roger Bensky has been offering French Theater Workshops which combine academic inquiry with investigation of actual performance of stage scripts by students. This unique approach is based on the understanding that the text of a play is but the DNA of what is ultimately shared with the audience through the work of the Actor, which can be thought of as painting with words on a 3-D canvas through your voice and your body. In the present class, we will be juxtaposing two 20th century giants of French thought and literature who also produced major works for the stage : the fervently Catholic poet, diplomat and essayist Paul Claudel and the champion of atheistic existentialism and political activism Jean-Paul Sartre. Of the former we will study the epic drama Le Soulier de Satin and the famous play of love and death Partage de Midi. Of the latter, we will plunge into the political and metaphysical vertigo of Le Diable et le Bon Dieu, which is Sartreâs answer to the Claudelian epic, and Les SÃ©questrÃ©s dâAltona, which explores pathological denial of responsibility for war-time crimes of humanity among the rich and powerful. Apart from developing the capacity to actively perform scenes from these challenging works, students will be required to maintain and submit a Journal de Bord, a theater diary of their own progress as well as their evolving understanding of the class process as a whole. Prior acting experience is not required. However, students should possess a very good working knowledge of the French language and some prior exposure to French literature to gain maximum benefit. This course counts toward the upper division Post-advanced language requirement for the French major.

title: French Theater Workshop: Plays by Women
description: Students will present at least two live interpretations and write a paper or theater diary (journal de bord) in French based on their scene interpretation. Both majors and qualified non-majors are invited to enroll. Cross-listed with Womenâs Studies and Comp. Lit. Cognate course with Performing Arts. All theater workshops fulfill the upper-division post-advanced language requirement for the French major.

title: FR Stages of Love/Sex: Acting Workshop
description: Continuing the tradition of performance-based theater courses in French combining thematic and dramaturgic analysis with selective scene-study for actors, we will, this time around, be exploring a handful of plays, from the 17th century to the present, to see how they treat the infinite variations on the crucial matters of love and sex. In addition to performance work, students will maintain a Journal de Bord and participate in a final public montage before a campus audience. Works studied: MoliÃ¨reâs LâEcole des Maris, Marivauxâs La Dispute, Mussetâs On ne badine pas avec lâamour, Claudelâs Partage de Midi, Cixousâ LâHistoire (quâon ne connaÃ®tra jamais), Lagarceâs Le Pays Lointain.
