We will begin by examining the roots of Jewish and Arab nationalisms, rival claims to Palestine, and growing conflict during the period of the British Mandate, including the growing involvement of Arab states. The second and main part of this course will cover the years 1947-1985, analyzing the causes and effects of six wars between Israel and the Arab states; those of 1948, 1956, 1967, 1969-70, 1973 and 1982, and the circumstances that made possible the Israeli-Egyptian peace treaty of 1979. This stage of the course will also examine the impact of regional and global factors, such as inter-Arab rivalry and the Cold War. The third part of the course, time allowing, will begin with Israelâs withdrawal from most of the Lebanese territories (1985) that was followed by the Palestinian intifada of 1987-1993 and then deal with the Israeli-Palestinian Declaration of Principles of September 1993 (the Oslo Agreement) and the Israeli-Jordanian peace agreement of 1994. The course concludes with an examination of the evolution of the conflict since the mid-1990s; topics to be included in the last few class meetings are the 2000 Camp David Summit, the Israeli separation fence, the main issues that need to be resolved (Jerusalem, territorial borders, Arab and Jewish refugees, security arrangements), the involvement of Hizballah and war of 2006, and the rise of Hamas in the Gaza Strip. Not every unit (one â sixteen) represents a class session. Some units represent two, or even three class sessions.

title: Politics of Finance: West & East
description: This course explores the dynamics financial change in the modern world from a political perspective. Global imbalances, the 2008 US housing crisis, the rise of China and India, and future prospects for the US financial system, among other issues, will be set in historical and analytical context. The focus is on industrial finance and the overall character of countriesâ âfinancial structureâ. The course emphasizes comparative political analysis and the international context. The balance of state, market and social forces that determines the character of national financial systems varies across countries; that balance also varies across time within countries. How and why do these variations occur? Do ideas also play a role? How do powerful political, social and ideational forces shape finance? How do financial forces, in turn, shape ideas, institutions or political and social actors? This is not a course in financial economics; power â not price or efficiency â is the primary focus. Growth and efficiency are considered along with inequality, representation, ideas and institutions (both domestic and international). Having first surveyed the experience of modern Anglo-American, Continental and Japanese finance, as well as the key assumptions of modern finance theory and the foundational events of modern finance (such as the Great Depression and the development of the global monetary system), we move on to a detailed study of financial change in developing countries. The financial system, according to theories presented in this course, may play a crucial role in helping backward economies to âcatch upâ with advanced countries. The shift from bank-dominated finance to greater use of securities â such as stocks and bonds â will be a key theme throughout. Accordingly, explanations of variation in corporate governance will also be considered. The readings draw from various political economy and political sociology approaches to present a range of explanations for outcomes in the government-finance relationship and financial organization. Along the way we will consider the impact of capital mobility and international financial institutions on developing countries. Finally we examine closely the political economy of Chinese and Indian finance.

title: Democratic Consolidation: In Latin America
description: The central drama of Latin America and the Caribbean is the dual transition toward market economies and liberal democracy. The course focuses on themes of political culture, civil society, institutions and policy-making processes, and policy issues as these relate to the consolidation and quality of democracy in that region. Some emphasis will be placed on the larger countries, but we go where the good literature takes us. We shall use a seminar-discussion format in a collective search and evaluation of the relevant literature. The course requirements will remain fixed, but the topics and readings will be revised and expanded during the semester to reflect the findings of our collective search.

title: Dept Sem: Crime, Corruption & Democracy
description: The course explores the dynamics of crime and corruption and their relationships with the creation and maintenance of democracy. Our focus is less on law enforcement and justice administration than on assessing the significance of crime and corruption with respect to how both new and established democracies operate. We consider political institutions and processes as well as civic culture and civil society as determinants of criminal-political dynamics. Students write a mid-term and take-home final exam and a term paper.

title: Roman Catholic Political Philosophy
description: This is a course in political philosophy. It inquires about the relation of classical, medieval, and modern political philosophy to the corpus of revelation as found in Catholicism. The course is intended for undergraduates or graduates interested in this understanding.

title: Constitutional Law of U.S. Foreign Policy
description: In establishing the legal basis for U.S. foreign relations, we will study a number of pressing issues at the intersection of constitutional and international law. Topics we will cover include the balance of power between the President and Congress, constitutional authority to enter a variety of international agreements, the ability of international law to preempt state law, war powers under the Constitution, authority to detain enemy combatants, and jurisdictional limitations on U.S. courts. The course materials will consist primarily of legal cases, supplemented with relevant articles and commentary. The course will require a midterm exam and a final paper.

title: Dept Sem: Asian Political Economy: From the Tigers to China and India
description: This course attempts to explain the origins of, the reasons for, and the policy implications of economic change in Asia. Along the way we will explore some political economy theories that may explain patterns of development, and critically examine political economy. Emphasis will be on the rapid rise of East Asia and recent change in South Asia (focusing on India and Pakistan). We will explore differences between development in East Asia, South Asia and Europe, drawing analytic and conceptual lessons from various country, industry, and sector cases. We will read historical analysis and socio-anthropological accounts of the emergence of state and market institutions in Japan, Korea, India, and âGreater Chinaâ (The PRC, Taiwan, Hong Kong). Among the questions to be examined in this course will be: (1) Are there cultural, intellectual, institutional factors that favor some countries rather than others? (2) How have social, economic and political institutions shaped economic development outcomes in Asia? (3) What explains variation between the patterns and rates of development in East Asia, India, and Western Europe? (4) What may be the reasons for the relative lateness of industrialization of India, China and Korea compared with Japan? (5) Is there such a thing as an East Asian model of development? How might that model differ from South Asian (or other regional) models? (6) What were the causes and consequences of the 1990s Asian Financial Crisis, and how did countries respond to that crisis? (7) How do globalization, international relations and the global economy affect political economy processes and outcomes in Asia?

title: Dept Sem: Terrorism & Proliferation
description: The theme of Terrorism and Proliferation concerns psychological restraints on rational decision-makingâhow leaders seek to simplify complexity and reduce uncertainty but cognitive constraints interfere. Prospect theory from cognitive psychology is a source of explanation for behavior that rational choice theory is unable to explain. The plot involves international terrorism and proliferation of weapons of mass destruction. Regarding terrorism, the focus is on the Americanâled war on global terrorism, with special focus on the Taliban as well as al Qaeda in Afghanistan, Pakistan, and Yemen. Regarding proliferation, the course looks back on regime change in Iraq for lessons learned in potential regime change scenarios for Iran. Participants shall have an opportunity to conduct case studies on al Qaeda, Iraq, and Iran, as well as to meet in a computer lab to access resources of the Web while in class. [International Relations]

title: Comparative Democratization
description: This course will serve as an introduction to the vast literature on democratization in comparative politics. Democratization is studied here from the perspective of the transition and consolidation processes of the late 20th century. The principal goal of the course is to understand the social, economic, cultural, institutional and political conditions, both domestic and international, that help explain democratic development and, in particular, the multifaceted problems of transition and consolidation of democracy. We shall also consider the reasons for the more recent resurgence of various forms of authoritarianism.

title: Politics in North Africa
description: This course explores the English language literature on politics in the western part of the Arab World: the Maghreb or North Africa. We cover Mauritania, Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, and Libya. To familiarize students with the region we will utilize case studies and the analytical tools adopted in the books assigned for this course. Beyond that, the course explores a number of important themes for the region in depth. These include Islamist politics, the political economy of development, regime transitions, women and minority rights, the politics of âBerberâ identity, regional issues including the Western Sahara, and strategic and security relations.

title: Dept. Sem.: Contemperary Liberalism
description: This course is designed for upper-level undergraduates and graduate students. It presupposes some knowledge of the liberal tradition (Locke, Kant, Mill, etc.), especially as it has developed in the English-speaking world. The readings are designed to acquaint students with themes that have been important in the recent development of liberal political thought. The themes to be emphasized this year are: 1) the possibility of state neutrality concerning âthe goodâ; 2) the role of equality in the pursuit of democratic ideals; 3) the possibility of a "post-modern" liberalism; and 3) the liberal response to post-national aspirations. The readings will be drawn primarily from the works of four authors: John Rawls, Michael Walzer, Richard Rorty and Pierre Manent. The course is conducted as a seminar, and each student enrolled in the course for credit is expected to write several papers.

title: Dept Sem: 20th c. Christian Soc/Pol Thought
description: This course will survey the development of 20th century Protestant and Catholic social and political theory. We will begin with Rerum Novarum, explore realism (e.g. R. Niebuhr) and liberalism (E. Troeltsch and P. Tillich) and the Barthian rejection of liberalism, then later Catholic Social Teaching (Quadragesimo Anno, Populorum Progressio, and Laborem Exercens), a bit of Liberation Theology, some of the recent work of Metz and Moltmann, and finish with some of the new natural law theory (J. Finnis, R. George, etc.). We will focus on the ways that classic Protestant and Catholic political theologies construct a vision of power politics, the demands of justice, and the appropriate configuration of the religious grounds of conceptions of justice and right with the realities and demands of power politics. Particular topics include: â¢ How do Christian thinkers legitimate their political involvement and activity? â¢ How do Christian thinkersâconscious of history, time, and material realityâshape the political activity around them and their own political activity? â¢ How do Christian thinkers grapple with modern demands of reason and the principles of liberal democracy in formulating their political agenda? â¢ How can liberal democratic political communities, shaped by enlightenment ideals, accommodateâor cabinâpolitical theologies? â¢ What do Christian thinkers make of o the secularization of political and legal authority, the rise of liberalism (political and theological) o the role and limits of revelation and tradition as a source of meaning and authority o the impact of historical consciousness on the claims of religious authority, particularly for political messianism and the enduring religious sourcesâif anyâof legitimating liberal democratic politics

title: Dept Sem: Problems in Democratic Theory
description: Leading works dealing with theories of democratic government will be examined with care. Attention will focus on both the theoretical and practical difficulties identified in these works.[Political Theory]

title: Dept Sem: Catholicism and the American Political Order
description: In Democracy in America, Alexis de Tocqueville, makes the following provocative (and seemingly strange for the time) statement regarding Catholicism and American democracy: âIf Catholicism could ultimately escape from the political animosities to which it has given rise, I am almost certain that same[democratic] spirit of the age which now seems to be so contrary to it would turn into a powerful ally and that it would suddenly make great conquests.â This claim serves as a catalyst for this political theory seminar which will explore the foundational relationship between Catholicism and the American democratic political order and culture from a theoretical, practical, and historical perspective by using representative thinkers, texts, and historical movements and events. We first will attempt to understand a normative Catholic framework for assessing this relationship with America by reading many of the social teachings of various popes and other documents of the Church. Then we will attempt to understand the American political order and culture â its implicit and explicit principles, assumptions, and tendencies - as expressed through the founding documents, select American political thinkers, and historical movements and events. The course will include both an examination of the differing views among some Catholics regarding the relationship between Catholicism and the American political order, and an examination of how Catholics, and the Church in general, have practically and historically fared in this relationship â especially with respect to current political issues. Among the questions to be addressed are: How are the principles of the American political order as found in the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution to be understood, and how should a Catholic approach them? What is âthe separation of Church and Stateâ and how can a Catholic understand this separation? What is the Catholic understanding of the relation between faith/revelation and reason, and what role should they play in American politics? Are Catholics divided in their loyalties to the Church and America as they have been historically accused? How does a Catholic approach a liberal and pluralistic political order and culture? How ought a Catholic to participate in American politics, voting, and the culture? How can Catholicism contribute to the success of the American political order and culture?

title: Poverty & Inequity: Development Changes
description: This seminar combines grounding in systems and institutions and analytic exploration of major global policy issues for international development. It takes the year 2000 United Nations formulation of Millennium Development Goals as a framework, and uses a practical introduction to the work of the key development institutions, notably the World Bank, to illustrate how the policy choices that are relevant for the global effort to end poverty are framed and acted upon in practice. The seminar will explore different perspectives on why the challenges of poverty and social justice are so significant today, ranging from security to political to moral.

title: Dept Sem:  Senior Honors Writing Seminar
description: The aim of this course is twofold. Practically, it is focused on the planning of your entire Senior Honors Thesis and the writing of one good draft chapter. We will also spend time on the nuts and bolts of researching and writing a thesis of this length. Logistical discussions about the Honors program requirements, procedures and deadlines will also be included. Intellectually, it is a forum for you to experiment, test and exchange various ideas with your colleagues in the Honors program. Moreover, you are also expected to constructively criticize and assess your colleaguesâ work-in-progress. We will pursue these goals in various ways, beginning with some sessions on research, writing and citing, followed by in-depth assessments and discussions of previous Honorsâ theses and culminating in student presentations of their current research.

title: Tocqueville: Democracy in America
description: This course will offer students an opportunity to carefully consider Tocquevilleâs Democracy in America. Our analysis will consider, among other things: the relationshipâhistorical and logicalâbetween aristocracy and democracy; the instability of democracy; the institutional mechanisms that serve as antidotes to these instabilities; the significance of habit in Tocquevilleâs thought; the case for American Exceptionalism; the importance of religion for democracy; whether Tocqueville himself recognized the limits of his âinstitutionalâ political science; and finally whether the problem of democracy is, in the final analysis, a problem of boundaries. The intention is less to defend what Tocqueville says than to begin to comprehend the way in which he thought through democracy and its problems.

title: Dept Sem: Faith, Ethics, and America's Role in the World
description: The course will address the intersection of religion, philosophy, politics and foreign policy in the United States from a variety of disciplinary perspectives. Topics to be addressed will include just war, humanitarian intervention, and human rights. Frequent guest presentations will expose students to a variety of different analytical perspectives on these and other issues. The course will be videotaped, edited, and presented to a wider national and international audience on the web. Admitted students will be required to sign a release form. Jean Bethke Elshtain is the Thomas and Dorothy Leavey Chair in the Foundations of American Freedom at Georgetown University, a three year visiting appointment in the Department of Government, in addition to her permanent position as the Laura Spelman Rockefeller Professor of Social and Political Ethics at The University of Chicago.

title: The Psychotic Edge of Politics
description: We will be examining the destructive and nihilistic edge of political life: cultures, societies, states falling into a state of mind where mass murder, annihilation become the ends of public and state policy. Racism, in modern political times, stands behind a politics that elevates rigid ideological positions and violence against groups into normal, everyday positions. That kind of politics possesses public characteristics or qualities that can be called "psychotic" or crazy. We will look at the value assumptions behind such a politics and some of its major mechanisms of operation. [Political Theory]

title: Analysis of Political Data
description: The best political analysts have strong analytical powers, deep substantive knowledge, and sharp methodological skills. In this course we will sharpen one methodological skill, quantitative analysis. We will learn to speak the language of numbers, and use descriptive and inferential statistics to do so. Learning descriptive statistics will help us accurately portray the various dimensions of individuals and groups of interest to political actors. Inferential statistics allow us to test hypotheses about these individuals and groups and the populations they represent. Together, these statistics can give us the ability to describe political data and answer questions about political phenomenon. Our main goal is to learn how to use quantitative methods in political analysis. To achieve this goal, we will develop three main skills. The first skill is statistical theory. A second skill involves social science research methods more broadly defined. A final skill involves using computer software programs for creating and analyzing statistics. By the end of the semester, you should be able to read quantitative articles about politics, begin to judge the quality of these articles, and initiate your own analysis of political data.

title: Current Issues in NonProliferation & International Relations
description: This course is designed to be a primer on/overview of issues relating to the development, acquisition, use and threat of use of Weapons of Mass Destruction (WMD). Course will begin by defining what constitutes a WMD and will focus on issues such as Nonproliferation, Counterproliferation, Arms Control, Cooperative Threat Reduction, Sanctions, Law of War, Decision Making Authorities, Nuclear Bureaucracy, Hedging Strategies and Treaties. The course will be practically oriented and tied to current events with and eye towardnwhere International Law and norms come into play.

title: Nonviolence
description: In recent history, nonviolent protest movements have succeeded in asserting collective rights of self-determination, establishing civil liberties, transforming social conflicts and toppling repressive regimes. This course will provide an opportunity to examine the theory, practice and historical record of nonviolent political campaigns in the modern era. The course will include critical evaluation of the ethical and strategic merit of nonviolent approaches, elaborate distinctions between principled and strategic paradigms of nonviolence, and examine the roles of culture, gender, identity, leadership, psychology and religion in nonviolent campaigns. A series of contemporary and historical case studies, illustrated by film and guest lectures, will provide opportunities for grounded examination of the discourses, methods, ethical and tactical dilemmas of nonviolent struggle.

title: Palestinian/Israeli Peacebuilding
description: This course provides a historical and theoretical overview of peace and conflict resolution efforts in the Palestinian/Israeli context. Drawing on analytical frameworks from conflict analysis and resolution, the course will examine a range of cross-conflict initiatives at state, civil society and grassroots levels, including official negotiations, political campaigns, social movements, interfaith and intergroup dialogue, peace education, media, human rights advocacy and nonviolent direct action. Students will be challenged to understand peace and conflict resolution initiatives in their complex historical, political, social and theoretical contexts, and to assess the contributions of these initiatives to any potential resolution of the intractable Israeli/Palestinian conflict. Course materials will include film, literature, media, online resources as well as conversations with leading practitioners and scholars of the field.

title: Sport and Peacebuilding
description: Sport has been recognized as a useful tool for promoting peace when used in a safe and supportive environment in which people can meet together around a common interest and goal. In this class, we will explore the history, current trends, and future direction of sport & peacebuilding in the international community. We will also gain experience in designing, implementing, and evaluating a Virtual Youth Sports Exchange (VYSE) Pilot Project. VYSE will integrate a systematic and coherent youth sport and peace-building curriculum with an interactive videoconference program to promote peace (The eventual goal for VYSE will be to connect Iraqi and American youth through the use of sport and technology).

title: Approaches/Topics in American Politics
description: To be added

title: MA Capstone: Leadership & Process
description: This is the first semester of a sequence required for and limited to American Government Accelerated M.A. Students. The seminar compares theoretical models of American government and politics with lessons learned from the experience of practitioners charged with making the system work. A practicum experience of twenty hours per week based on the student's personal interest and professional objectives accompanies the seminar. The format is intensive discussion of the readings and the case studies and of the student's practicum experience. [American Government]

title: Leadership
description: This course will examine leadership in three ways: 1) by studying the definitions and descriptions of leadership through case studies and other readings; 2) by reviewing comparative analyses of leaders in public, profit, and not-for-profit sectors; and 3) by teaching through self assessments and a group project the essential competencies leaders need to be effective and hence what will be required of the students to be leaders. Specifically, the course is designed to assist the students in answering a number of key questions about leadership, including: What is the definition of âleadershipâ? Has the definition of âleadershipâ changed over time? If so, how? What are the major leadership styles? How do people become leaders? How much is innate and how much taught? What are the ethical responsibilities of leaders? How important are communication skills (written and oral) for leaders? What is the difference between leadership and management? The class requirements will be as follows: 1.) Class discussion of cases and background material (35%); 2) Self assessment of leadership qualities (15%); Final paper on leadership (20%); and 4) Group project on leadership goals, strategies, tactics and outcomes (30%). The group project topic will be: âThe United Statesâ Policy Regarding China and Human Rights.â The class will break up into four sectors. The following sectors should be represented by a student group: the Executive Branch (the U.S. Department of State); the Legislative Branch (e.g. either the House Committee on Foreign Affairs or the Senate Committee on Foreign Relations); the human rights community (e.g. Human Rights Watch); and the multi-national organizations (e.g. the United Nations). The product of the group project should be a written description by each sector of their leaders - their goals, responsibilities, their strategies, tactics, and the desired outcomes.

title: Executive Branch Politics & Policymaking
description: This course is designed to be a seminar, where students examine the nature and history of bureaucracy as an institution of government, the different models and processes of government decisions vis-a-vis public policy-making, the context in which the executive branch operates, the executive branch's relationship to other institutions of government, and the unique policy execution and management issues the executive branch faces. Several specific policy arenas are also examined in an effort to apply theories learned throughout the semester. Students are expected to share their insights from the readings and from their personal experiences. Field: AG

title: Research Methods in Democracy & Development
description: This is a course on research design for social sciences. The course will cover how to create a useful research question, how to develop, refine, and test hypotheses, and how to select the appropriate research methodology. Each student will identify a research question, develop a research design for testing it, and present the research design to the rest of the class.

title: Peace Process and Post-Conflict Reconstruction in Latin America
description: This seminar will examine internal armed conflicts, attempts at negotiated settlements, and experiences of post-conflict reconstruction, national reconciliation and political processes in Latin America, primarily in Central America and the Andean region during the past three decades. It will approach these issues from three related perspectives: 1) theoretically, using some of the best recent literature derived from the growing experience of international involvement in internal conflicts across the globe; 2) through an in-depth examination of specific Latin American cases -- principally Colombia, Mexico, El Salvador, Guatemala and Peru âand the special case of Haiti; and 3) through an analysis of specific issues incorporating experiences from Latin America and other international cases, such as applying justice in post-conflict setting, and the involvement of outside actors such as the United Nations and World Bank, or individual states and âGroups of Friends.â During the final third of the semester, students will simulate actual negotiations in one of the countries, playing the role of a government representative, guerrilla leader, international mediator, civil society leader or other actor in one of the peace processes being studied.

title: Democracy  Promotion
description: to be provided

title: Governance and the Rule of Law
description: This course is an advanced seminar that brings together the conceptual foundations of development paradigms in the context of rule of law. It seeks to accomplish the following goals: 1. To provide students with a background in the theory and practice of governance and institution building with a specific focus on challenges in the 21st century. 2. To review the conceptual foundations of development paradigms in order to examine the extent to which law is key to the management of public institutions and their response to shifting political, economic and judicial trends. 3. To sharpen students' ability to think and write analytically about current trends and potential policies to improve institutional incentives and reforms to promote good governance and the rule of law. The class is structured as a seminar in which students are expected to complete each week's assigned readings and to be prepared to rigorously discuss the readings with fellow classmates. Each student will serve as a discussion leader for one week's reading assignment in the course of the semester. Students should be prepared to make original critical analyses of reading assignments in their capacity as a discussion leader. The purpose of this requirement is to prepare students for speaking in public forums, delivering presentations in a professional environment and serving on conference panels.

title: International Electoral Policy and Practice
description: The objective of this course is to provide the student with knowledge of the history of electoral policy and practice and the analytical frameworks for electoral policy-making, design and evaluation. With the completion of this course, the student should be capable of engaging in elections and political process policy design with international electoral assistance programs, electoral reform initiatives, or state building activities for international organizations, diplomatic missions, bilateral development agencies, or national election authorities.. Approach to Subject Matter Each class session will focus on a different topical element of electoral process as shown below: â¢ Introduction to elections as instruments of governance; â¢ History of electoral process development; â¢ Elections and the international community; â¢ Election Management Bodies and other electoral regulation institutions; â¢ Non-governmental elections; â¢ Elections and technology; â¢ Electoral systems and delimitation; â¢ Political party systems and resources; â¢ Enfranchising marginalized electorates; â¢ Electoral conflict and security; â¢ Electoral fraud and manipulation; and â¢ Electoral observation and validation. Each class session will involve a lecture and discussion on the topics listed above. A preparatory readings list will be developed on each topic. Where possible, guest lecturers such as visiting election officials, will be invited to address the class.

title: Post-Communist Politics
description: This course traces the processes of economic, social and political change in Russia and Eastern Europe beginning with the dramatic period of reform and revolution in the 1980s. Students will investigate the break with communism and simultaneous efforts at market building and democratization. For the contemporary period, we will inquire into the causes of the divergent political development evident in the current regimes that have emerged from the ruins of the old Soviet bloc.

title: International Courts
description: In the United Nations era, international courts have been a significant engine in the promotion and restoration of international peace and security, and have also been created to ease conflict between nations in regards to borders and territory, international trade, and the environment. In this course, we will undertake an exploration of the major international courts of the UN era, including the International Criminal Court, the ad hoc Criminal Tribunals in Rwanda and the Former Yugoslavia, the Special Court for Sierra Leone, the Extraordinary Chambers of the Courts of Cambodia, the International Court of Justice, the International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea, and the WTO. The course will conclude with a multi-week mock trial seated in one of these courts.

title: The Science and Policy of National Cybersecurity
description: With the growing world dependence on information technology, cyberspace is emerging as a new battlefield for 21st war where the combatants may range from dominant nation states to high school pranksters. Protecting and defending cyberspace is fraught with extreme technical and political challenges. This course explores the diverse range of science and policy issues associated with these challenges, along with opportunities for unique solutions, new capabilities, and alternative approaches. To organize this discussion, the course begins with the establishment of a conceptual framework for analyzing cyberspace and associated defense system. The resulting reference model decomposes cyberspace into seven distinct layers of abstraction: physical, signal, data, information, knowledge, intelligence, and wisdom. The course then systematically progresses through each layer, carefully examining the science and policy aspects at each level. A diverse collection of reading materials, experiences, demonstrations, and discussions are leveraged to illustrate the complexity of comprehensive cyber defense.

title: Transnational Justice: Truth Commissions, Prosecutions &  Peacebuilding
description: The course considers how countries seeking to extricate themselves from massive human rights violations and undemocratic rule can best deal with their past, in pursuit of the rule of law and participatory democracy. Central to this concern is the need to ensure both peace and justice in what are often fragile states living at the interregnum between the past and the future. Which comes first â justice or peace? Is it possible to have both? Those who put justice first argue that the promotion of international law, through mechanisms such as the International Criminal Court (ICC) and related structures, is imperative to prevent impunity for major crimes (genocide, crimes against humanity and war crimes), as a deterrent to further perpetration, and as a basis for the establishment of the rule of law in an emerging new society. Those who prioritize peace-initiatives stress the need for societies in transition to deal with the political and other trade-offs in a manner that may require those bearing major responsibility for past violations being exempted from retributive justice. The course considers mechanisms of transitional justice in different historical and political contexts, ranging from the Nuremberg and Tokyo trials in the latter part of the 1940s, through the international criminal tribunals of Yugoslavia and Rwanda, to the South African and similar Truth and Reconciliation Commissions. Required reading Jeffrie G. Murphy, Getting Even: Forgiveness and Its Limits (New York: Oxford University Press, 2003); Gerry Simpson, Law, War and Crime: War Crimes, Trials and the Reinvention of International Law (Malden, MA: Polity Press 2008); Charles Villa-Vicencio, Walk with Us and Listen: Political Reconciliation in Africa (Washington, D.C.: Georgetown University Press, 2009). Additional material will be made available on blackboard.

title: African Union: Conflict Transformation and Accountability
description: The African Union (AU) is both a vulnerable and a potentially significant vehicle for African partnership and good governance. Central to its endeavours are security, the rule of law, democracy, institution building, economic growth and human development in each of its member states and across the continent. This course focuses on the vision of the AU, which is critically assessed. Consideration is given to realistic options for African renewal through its different structures, including the Pan-African Parliament, Peace and Security Council, the New Partnership for Africaâs Development (NEPAD), the African Peer Review Mechanism (APRM) and the African Court of Justice. Attention is further given to the relationship between the United Nations (UN) and the AU. Three specific case studies that reflect political tensions in Africa are addressed. These are developed around the Acholiland conflict in Uganda, the Comprehensive Peace Agreement (CPA) in Sudan and the current conflict in Kenya. Students will be given the opportunity to write their final (third) paper on an African country of their choice, other than these particular countries. An underlying question within the course is on the extent to which external or international interventions are able to address local and regional African problems. The course culminates in a structured debate on âInternationalism and Localism in the Promotion of Peace and Justice in Africa.â Required Reading: John Akokpari, Angela Ndinga-Muvumba and Tim Murithi (eds.), The African Union and its Institutions (Johannesburg: Fanel, 2008) Charles Villa-Vicencio, Walk With Us and Listen: Building Sustainable Reconciliation in Africa (Georgetown: GUP, 2009) Additional readings will be made available on Blackboard.

title: Conflict Resolution Theory
description: This course provides an overview of the historical roots, philosophical debates and theoretical foundations of the interdisciplinary field of conflict analysis and resolution. The course will examine classical and contemporary analyses of the sources and dynamic processes of social conflict, and leading practical approaches to the conduct and evaluation of peace-building and conflict intervention. Our study will encompass interpersonal, intergroup and international levels of analysis, and highlight the roles of collective identity, historical narrative, and political power in the constitution and resolution of social conflict.

title: Conflict Resolution Skills
description: In order to pursue a career in conflict resolution, whether as a researcher, scholar or practitioner, a strong foundation in conflict resolution skills and processes such as conflict analysis and assessment, communication, cross-cultural, facilitation, and dialogue skills are necessary. The course focuses on practical and experiential learning through exercises, simulations, case studies, guest lectures and discussions. Students will also develop their skills as reflective practitioners. Since many individuals pursuing careers in conflict resolution will find employment in related sectors such as development, human resources, community organizing, this course explores how these skills can be integrated into other sectors and conflict mainstreaming. In addition, throughout the course strategies for how to market conflict resolution expertise within todayâs competitive job market will be covered. [IR]

title: Managing Organizational Conflict
description: Effectively managed organizational conflict is something to which managers often aspire, yet often fail to accomplish. Why is this so? What makes managing organizational conflict such a difficult task? Today, in a globalized economy, the consequences of poorly managed organizational conflict are many. Poorly managed conflict gives competition an advantage where one may not have existed previously. Misunderstanding, enmity and conflict have derailed many projects, people, and ideas. In the short run a poorly managed employee may result only in slightly lowered morale, in the long run, however, the poorly managed employee transmogrifies into a lawsuit, bad publicity and sometimes even violence. The unit Managing Organizational Conflict is designed to canvass a variety of issues that impact upon the effective management of organizational conflict. In this unit we will examine the dynamics of poorly managed organizational conflict, to better determine what goes wrong. We will also examine effectively managed conflict, so that we may ascertain the many ways in which conflict can be used constructively. Also canvassed with a range of subjects addressing directly methods for encouraging effective conflict management.

title: Islam and Peacebuilding
description: There is a comfortable acceptance of maligning the tradition of Islam as the sole source for global conflict and transnational terrorism. The Western view of Islam is rooted in centuries of mistrust, ignorance, and stereotypical attitudes that contribute to inherited biases of the pre-modern clash of civilizations. Moderate and conservative western religious scholars still argue that Islam is a fierce and fanatical faith that demands of its followers blind obedience to a system of belief rooted in hatred. In the same light, fundamentalist Muslim theologians assert vitriolic conspiracy theories of western hegemony to undermine the religion of Islam. These attitudes are no longer viable in a globalized world where there is immense interdependence shared between the West and Islam, moreover, the very real presence of millions of law-abiding Western Muslim citizens have blurred the lines of these two categories. This course explores the concept of peace, peacebuilding, and conflict management within the Islamic tradition. There are several practical and theoretical peace paradigms that are used in Muslim communities to work towards peaceful relations. We will examine traditional, reformist, modern, progressive, orthodox, and mystical interpretations to understand the divergent views of Islamic peacebuilding. This course will examine theoretical frameworks and specific case studies to address issues in the field of peace and conflict resolution and the multi dimensional aspects of the relationship between reconciliation and justice in a post conflict context. We will compare specific processes of conflict management used by Muslim religious and non-religious leaders in order to analyze commonalities and differences in peacebuilding efforts. (IR)

title: Peacebuilding
description: Since the publication of an Agenda for Peace in 1992, peacebuilding has increasingly become a central area of focus within the field of conflict resolution. This course will provide an overview of the theory and practice of peacebuilding and explore how the field of has evolved over the past two decades. Given that peacebuilding activities increasingly are integrated into other sectors including development, business, human rights, this course will focus on cross-sectoral approaches to peacebuilding. Current challenges in the field such as challenges of evaluating impact, ethics of practice, power relations between Northern and Southern NGOs and measuring impact will also be highlighted. Through taking this course students will be better prepared to undertake research, practice and policy careers within peacebuilding and related fields. The course will cover a combination of theory and real-world cases, helping to contextualize many of key concepts covered in the course. Several guest experts will also contribute to the course.

title: Criminal Law and Conflict Resolution
description: In a globalizing world, humankind continues to make rapid advancements in many areas such as medicine and science. However, history has repeatedly shown that humans have yet to learn how to control their aggression. A solution to this dilemma may lie in greater worldwide adherence to the notion of the rule of law, combined with implementation of the study and practice of conflict resolution through the means of the rule of law. In this course, we will embark on an exploration of the meaning and significance of the term rule of law, and its interplay with the field of conflict resolution. The overarching goal of this course is to use the combined strength of the rule of law and conflict resolution to create a global culture of human rights that better controls, or even changes, the current paradigm of constant aggressive behavior by governments, groups, sub-groups, and individuals. The first portion of the course will explore the theory and main concepts of the rule of law, and will demystify the discipline of law for the non-lawyer. The discipline of law is quite misunderstood by those outside the legal profession, and lawyers have a tendency to silo themselves in law and not engage with non-lawyers committed to peace, practice, and ethics. In addition, law is often taught by law professors as an end to itself, instead of as a means to achieve the resolution of conflict. This lack of understanding and lack of cooperation between law and conflict resolution has contributed to the aforementioned lack of progress in controlling human aggression. The goal of the first portion of the course is to allow the students to fully comprehend the meaning of the rule of law, its potential use in the field of conflict resolution, and to better understand, and communicate with, lawyers engaged in the field, so as to better be able to exercise leadership in the comm! on search for international, regional, and national peace and reconciliation. The second portion of the course will consist of explorations of core concepts vital to the issues endemic to the rule of law and conflict resolution, and the implementation of such concepts to real life situations that have caused and might cause aggressive human behavior. In these scenarios, the students will explore lessons learned from the conflicts and the implementation of the rule of law, and consider whether greater cooperation between law, conflict resolution, and other disciplines could produce or could have produced a greater impact upon the problem. The third and final portion of the course will consist of the practical implementation of the theory of the rule of law and conflict resolution as they pertain to disaffected groups of people in modern society. In a series of exercises and simulations, the students will implement their new understanding of, and appreciation for, the discipline of law, and its interplay with conflict resolution, allowing the students to communicate and work effectively with lawyers committed to the search for an international culture of human rights.

title: The European Union
description: This course examines the European Union from a variety of disciplinary perspectives - political science, history, and cultural studies. It is divided into three parts. The first part addresses the history of postwar European integration in its political, social, and cultural dimensions. The course then moves on to a detailed examination of the contemporary politics of the EU - its institutional architecture, party politics, contested legitimacy, and ambiguous identity. The final part of the course explores an array of policy challenges now facing Europe, including the introduction of monetary union, the coordination of social and cultural policy, the development of a common foreign and security policy, and expansion to the East. [Comparative Politics]register as GEST 523

title: Evaluation of Conflict Resolution
description: Impact evaluation is an essential element of the contemporary conflict resolution field. Yet assessing the effects of peacebuilding interventions in volatile contexts can prove as challenging an endeavor as resolving conflict itself. This course will provide an overview of the leading organizational, practical and theoretical approaches to the evaluation of conflict resolution initiatives. The course will focus on applied research; students will meet practitioners, review evaluation frameworks from development, humanitarian and peacebuilding agencies, and submit case studies of evaluation methodology and practice in the organization or project of their choice. This seminar is designed to complement courses in Conflict Resolution Theory (GOVT 580) and Conflict Resolution Skills (GOVT 581), but these are not prerequisites.

title: Evaluation and Conflict Resolution
description: This class will teach students the principles of evaluation. Students will learn how to create a logic model, establish variables and indicators, construct standards, and synthesize and integrate outcomes to obtain a judgment of worth. Students will also learn the basics of writing a questionnaire and conducting an interview, as evaluators often rely on interviews for data gathering. We will then survey how evaluation strategies have been applied to the study of conflict, including to balance of power, conflict termination, and post-conflict issues. Guest speakers for this class include a former insurgent from Rwanda, a former counterinsurgent from Colombia, and a former negotiator in many African conflicts.

title: Capstone Course in Conflict Resolution
description: One of the challenges in the field of conflict resolution, given the diversity of theories and practices, is integrating a broad range of ideas, concepts and practices. In addition, as students near the end of the degree they often see things in a new light, with new insights, questions or interests. Finally, the challenge of integrating âtheory and practiceâ often remains elusive. Professionals often grapple with the nexus between theory and practice. The Capstone Course provides students with an opportunity to reflect upon the theory-practice nexus, fill in gaps in previous learning, and develop new ideas in conflict resolution great! Selecting an area for further work students will collaborate with other students and the course instructor to 1) reflect upon their own areas of interest in the conflict resolution field, 2) to examine the link between theory and practice, 3) further investigate areas relevant to conflict resolution, and to 4) develop a project that assists in their own understanding of the theory-practice link, expands their knowledge base, and makes a contribution to the wider field. Much of the course will be built around student projects. These projects can help students integrate ideas, flesh out new areas of interest, develop new ideas and develop career paths.

title: Environment and Conflict Resolution
description: Environmental conflict occurs at all levels of society, from local or regional disagreements about watershed management to national debates about managing nuclear waste to international disputes about how to address climate change. Understanding the sources of environmental conflict and adopting effective resolution techniques are critical to achieving an appropriate balance between the natural world and humanity in the 21st century. This course offers students the opportunity to explore the multifaceted relationship between the environment and social conflict from both theoretical and practical perspectives, with an emphasis on the scholarly literature and real-world cases. Topics we will cover include transboundary water conflict, climate change, environment and war, environment and peace, and international environmental negotiations. Approximately half of the course will be devoted to contemporary environmental conflict resolution (ECR) practice, including situation assessments, public involvement, negotiation, and environmental mediation, and how ECR practitioners assist stakeholders in navigating challenges posed by organizational dynamics, the policy setting, and scientific and technical complexity.

title: Conflict Resolution/Terrorism
description: Over the past decade, there has been an increasing focus on the threat of domestic and international terrorism around the world. Groups and individuals who carry out acts of terrorism often fall outside the scope of traditional state-sponsored armed actors. Therefore, responding, preventing and stopping terrorism requires a host of traditional (policing, military, economic sanctions, intelligence) and non-traditional (cultural, social and development initiatives) initiatives. This course will cover alternative means of understanding, preventing and responding to terrorism. Topic will include: * Explore the current and potential contributions of the conflict resolution field to developing effective policies related to ending terrorism. * An overview of sources of terrorism and the motivations of individuals who engage in such acts. * Discussion of existing tools to motivate armed actors to transition from terrorist actions to joining the political process. * A review of how governments transition from a refusal to engage with armed actors to political engagement will be covered. * Review of how to integrate a conflict sensitive approach with traditional security responses. Through taking this course students will be better prepared to undertake research, practice, and policy careers within the security and conflict resolution fields. The course will cover a combination of theory and real-world cases, helping to contextualize many of key concepts covered in the course. In addition, the course will include several simulations, the screening of several short films on the topic and presentations by experts in the field.

title: Welfare States in Transition
description: This course examines competing political and economic theories for social welfare policy design and reform in recent decades. Concentrating on the main areas of social policy - health, labor regulation and unemployment insurance, and old-age pensions - it examines divergent trends in policy scope, coverage, and spending both between countries and programs within the same country. The contrasting outcomes between the advanced industrial economies of Western Europe and the United States, as well as between the developing economies of Latin America and East Asia, illustrate the importance of domestic political and economic institutions in shaping diverse responses to the forces of globalization.

title: Congressional Politics
description: This course is designed to prepare students for understanding how Congress actually operates, and what motivates its members. The reading materials, written assignments, and final exam will cover both practical, real world applications and a graduate-level, scholarly examination of the institution. Topics will include political development of the institution, the committee system, voting behavior, elections, women and minority representation,policymaking, and the role of Congressional staff.

title: The American Presidency
description: [American Government]

title: Presidential Electoral Politics
description: This course examines some of the major research on the presidential nominating and election process in the United States. Each student will be required to write a research paper âpredicting presidential elections.â The paper will be an original piece of research using either political or economic variables to explain election outcomes. Class sessions will be devoted to discussing the required readings and individual research projects. Students will present their papers and findings in class at the end of the semester. [American Government]

title: Political Parties
description: This course explores the role of political parties in American democracy. We will engage the leading literature on how and why political parties form, their consequences for policy, politics and democracy. We will consider the role of parties in a variety of settings, as well as consider how parties unite activity between settings. We will use a variety of methods and perspectives to get this leverage. We will focus on open questions in the literature, with an eye toward contributing to that debate. This course assumes no particular background in American politics, political science or research methods. Students with such a background may get more out of this course, but since political parties permeate so many aspects of politics, this course provides a good background for future courses as well. We will, when appropriate, review some background material as it arises. Students will write a research paper on an original question that contributes to the literature on political parties.

title: Psychology & Politics
description: to be added

title: Comparative Political Economy
description: This course studies the political dynamics of economic relations, examining cross-national variation in the role of the state and private actors, relationships between labor and capital, and exposure to the international system. We will focus on the interaction of political institutions and systems of economic organization, including production regimes, financial markets, and social welfare policy. We will give special attention to recent reforms and restructuring in the face of globalization and economic crises. Examples will be drawn from Western and Eastern Europe, Latin America, and East Asia. As a seminar, this course will require active preparation and participation by students. Requirements include: â¢ Roughly 150-200 pages a week of reading. This will generally include one extended work or related set of papers by a single author. â¢ Regular participation in the discussion in class each week. â¢ Three short response papers (3 pages maximum) in response to a particular weekâs reading. These papers should be posted on the Blackboard discussion board no later than midnight the night before our class session, and all students should read them prior to coming to class. â¢ One five-page paper prospectus due before the middle of the term. â¢ One research paper, examining a specific labor-related policy or political outcome, in the region or country of the studentâs choosing. In consultation with the instructor, this paper should be tailored to fit with the studentâs academic program and educational goals

title: The Politics of Human Rights: Latin America in Comp Perspective
description: This advanced seminar will focus on the politics of human rights and the challenges of applying international humanitarian law in countries confronting major human rights violations. The primary regional focus of the course will be on Latin America, however the seminar will encourage cross-regional and comparative analysis with key cases in Africa, Asia, Middle East and Europe. It will examine such issues as transitional justice, the jurisprudence and protective measures of the Inter-American Court of Human Rights, methodologies and typologies of data collection and truth telling, early warning systems for massive violations, balancing demands for amnesty and national reconciliation with the demands for justice, and the role of human rights in consolidating democratic governance. The seminar will provide an opportunity to undertake original research, as well as to discuss, debate, and refine that research through constant dialogue and debate with other seminar members over the course of the semester. (Comparative Government)

title: Nation, State and Sovereignty in Asia
description: This course will consider how the historical development of sovereignty and nationalism shaped state formation in modern Asia. We will also evaluate sovereignty and nationalism as analytic categories â as causes and consequences â in the Asian context. Case material will emphasize South Asia, Greater China, and South East Asia. The written requirement is series of eight three to four page short synthetic essays on the weeks' readings.

title: Theories of Political Development
description: Theories of Political Development provides a comprehensive and critical overview of the major conceptual paradigms, theories and approaches that have guided Western studies of Third World societies over the last half a century. We are concerned first and foremost in understanding how analytical paradigms have guided, shaped and sometimes limited our analysis of development politics. We will also explore how these paradigms have helped shape the perceptions of US policy makers, particularly as regard questions of political and economic reform. Readings are drawn from a wide range of scholars working in fields of political economy, comparative politics, anthropology and sociology. Topics include regime transitions, the persistence of autocracy, ethno-religious conflict (and conflict resolution), and the political economy of reform. These topics will be explored comparatively, both within and across many regions, including the Middle East, Sub-Saharan Africa, South and Southeast Asia, Eastern Europe and Latin America. All students must have taken a basic introductory course in comparative politics. Further coursework in related fields is highly recommended.

title: Graduate Research Seminar on Russian Politics
description: Welcome to Government 648. This is a graduate research seminar on Russian politics. The focus of this seminar will be on the continuing politics of the presidential succession. For the past four years, this issue has been at the center of Russian politics, providing a rich source of insights into the workings of power in the Russian political system. Now the succession is a factâor is it?. Dmitri Medvedevâs election as president and his appointment of Vladimir Putin as prime minister have been widely dismissed, both in Russia and in the West, as a change without a difference. Vladimir Putin is expected to remain the most powerful figure in the Kremlin, the president in all but name, while Medvedev is viewed essentially as a loyal figurehead. This is indeed how things may be for the first year of the new administration. Putin and Medvedev enjoy an unusually close relationship, based on long-established trust and common views. Both men are determined to preserve the political stability of the last eight years. Indeed, it cannot be ruled out that Dmitri Medvedev will remain smoothly obedient to Putin throughout his four-year term, following which Putin may run again, as the Russian constitution allows him to do. Yet there are three reasons to suspect that this simple picture will not prevail much beyond the first year: â¢The institutional power of the Russian presidency: The position of the Russian president is far more powerful than that of prime minister. The president commands a vast apparatus of power and patronage. The so-called âpower ministriesâ all report to him, as does the diplomatic machinery. The prime minister, in contrast, is mainly responsible for social and economic policy, yet even in these domains the key ministries look to the Presidential Administration. To be sure, the position of prime minister can be strengthened and that of the president weakened. Reporting lines can be changed, budgets can be shifted, and staffs can be moved. But short of removing the entire presidential apparatus from the Kremlin to the White House and rewriting the Constitution, the presidency is likely to remain the seat of power. Over time, the person who holds it will become stronger. â¢A destabilizing economic and social environment: The domestic economic and social environment is likely to be less benign than it has been in the last eight years. The government is likely to have fewer resources at its disposal to support its rapidly growing welfare and investment programs. This will force painful reappraisals and catalyze political conflict. Even now, the âreformâ wing of the government is issuing public warnings of what could lie ahead, and is calling on the president and his prime minister to re-think some of the more conspicuous prestige programs, such as the vast spending planned for the Winter Olympics in Sochi and the Vladivostok ASEAN conference. The reform wing is tending to line up behind Medvedev, in the belief (which may turn out to be mistaken) that he is more attuned to economic realities than Putin may be. â¢The battle of clans will sharpen, but Putinâs power to moderate it may decline: Vladimir Putin is widely viewed as the only man who can hold the middle against the competing clans in the Russian political and business elite. Yet it is noteworthy that even at the height of his power he was barely able to keep the peace among themâwitness the failure of his attempt to merge Rosneft and Gazprom, his mixed success in moderating jousting among competing FSB factions, and his reluctance to intervene to defend finance minister Kudrinâs team against attacks by prosecutors. The successful management of the succession issue, which had divided the Russian elites for much of Putinâs second term, has not resolved the increasingly bitter rivalries among them. Yet if Putin is perceived as having to spend his political capital maintaining order, that will weaken both him and the president. The âbattle of the clans,â if not successfully contained, periodically breaks out in the form of raids, media attacks, exchanges of kompromat (âcompromising materialâ), arrests, indictments, and a general politicization of the prosecutorial and judicial system. The misuse of state coercion as an instrument of clan warfare weakens property rights and saps investor confidence, at a time when Russian needs more investment than ever. In particular, the clans will continue to do battle for ownership and control of oil and gas properties and other commodities. Indeed, such battles never stopped during Putinâs eight-year term, and there is little reason to believe that they will lessen in the period ahead. These will be the central research questions in Government 848. The aim is to examine the interplay of power, personalities, and policies shaping the new government under the âOdd Coupleâ in the Kremlin, and thereby to understand better the dynamic of power in Russian politics. The seminarâs in English, but the research materials must all be in Russian. That is because one of the main aims of the seminar is to encourage students to use their Russian skills for active research, mainly using the Internet to explore the many kinds of resources available there. Together we will explore useful websites, share lore and data, and expand our knowledge of the range of sources available for covering opinion poll data, legislative politics, policy initiation and debate, the law and the courts, economic policy, and the rough-and-tumble of todayâs Russian politics. To be in the course, you must have a minimum of three years of Russian. Advanced Internet skills are not necessary. The instructorâs permission is required. The course requirements are straightforward: 1) three short reports (4 pages each) on weekly class topics; 2) a 30-page seminar paper based exclusively on Russian-language sources; and 3) active participation in class discussion.

title: Politics of Economic Reform
description: Why are some countries rich and other countries poor? This has been a subject of heated debate among policymakers and scholars stretching as far back as Ancient Greece. Until recently, modern thought on why some countries are wealthy and others are not has tended to focus primarily on narrow economic issues, such as openness to trade and macroeconomic policy. However, a consensus is growing that politics, not economic policy, is the most important factor that determines countries growth trajectories. Macroeconomic policies may be proximate causes of economic growth (or lack thereof) yet these policies derive from political incentives. This raises the fundamental paradox of the political economy of development: why do leaders choose policies that discourage economic growth? Understanding why countries choose the economic policies they do is the main objective of this course.

title: Politics of Economic Reform
description: Why are some countries rich and other countries poor? This has been a subject of heated debate among policymakers and scholars stretching as far back as Ancient Greece. Until recently, modern thought on why some countries are wealthy and others are not has tended to focus primarily on narrow economic issues, such as openness to trade and macroeconomic policy. However, a consensus is growing that politics, not economic policy, is the most important factor that determines countries growth trajectories. Macroeconomic policies may be proximate causes of economic growth (or lack thereof) yet these policies derive from political incentives. This raises the fundamental paradox of the political economy of development: why do leaders choose policies that discourage economic growth? Understanding why countries choose the economic policies they do is the main objective of this course.

title: Political Institutions
description: The electoral institutions that link voters to the government they elect profoundly affect the incentives that politicians encounter. For example, some systems encourage elected officials to appeal to broad constituencies while others motivate them to satisfy the interests of only narrow segments of their population. Moreover, political systems that work well in relatively homogeneous societies can have catastrophic effects in countries that have deep social cleavages. The purpose of this course is for you to learn how political institutions connect voters to the governments they elect and the incentives these systems create for citizens and politicians. Understanding these links is central for comprehending how democracies function, and why variations exist among democracies. In the course we will examine what these institutions are, the differences between these institutions in democracies and non-democracies, institutional variation within democracies, and why countries choose different types of democratic institutions.

title: Democratization: Sub-Saharan Africa
description: While most of the world is growing economically, sub-Saharan Africa continues to stagnate. Moreover, whereas political stability and state consolidation characterize most regions of the world, political volatility and failed states remain ubiquitous in Africa south of the Sahara. Why does Africa seem to encounter greater obstacles to economic development and political stability than any other region of the world? The purpose of this course is to consider these questions by analyzing contemporary politics and political economy in sub-Saharan Africa. In the first part of the course, we examine the regionâs pre-colonial and colonial political structures. Subsequently, we will survey the political continuities and changes that have occurred since independence. The main topics we will cover are state consolidation, democratization, patronage politics, economic stagnation, civil war, ethnicity, failed states, and HIV/AIDS. The course provides an overview for why the region remains economically depressed and politically volatile.

title: Future of International Legal Order
description: Contact Instructor.

title: Sources of Nationalism
description: This course examines what nationalism is, where it comes from, and what effects it has on societies and international systems. We will cover material that presents contrasting historical and theoretical interpretations of nationalism. In class, we will carefully examine the main arguments contained in the readings and the historical evidence used to support these arguments. Students will be responsible for developing expertise in at least one historical or contemporary case of nationalism. Students will be expected to draw on their area of expertise in class discussions and in their research paper.

title: Scope & Methods of Political Science
description: This course is a non-credit required course for first year Ph.D. students in the Department of Government. Students will meet to discuss the norms and goals of the discipline and to hear from a cross-section of political scientists at Georgetown. Students will be required to attend academic talks and write very brief response pieces.

title: Analysis of Political Data
description: This course provides an introduction to statistical research methods. The course has two important goals: 1) to understand work that uses empirical research methods; and 2) to provide the skills necessary to analyze data. No previous experience in statistics is assumed. [American Government]

title: Advanced Political Analysis
description: Advanced Political Analysis continues the study of quantitative methods begun in 701 Analysis of Political Data. Topics covered include violations of Gauss-Markov assumptions, GLS and other extensions of the linear model, causal inference, and maximum likelihood estimation. Students will complete a quantitative paper on a subject and with a dataset of their own choosing.

title: Qualitative Methods
description: This course is a seminar for PhD students in political science and related fields on the design of case study and qualitative research projects. It covers the philosophy of science of qualitative methods as well as specific methods such as process tracing, comparison, typological theorizing, and multimethod research. Students will present their own research designs for constructive critique at the end of the course. Depending on the space available, Georgetown MA students and PhD students from area universities are welcome to join the course. Permission needed from instructor.

title: Game Theory
description: In the last few decades game theory has become one of the most powerful analytical tools in the study of politics. In this approach, human interactions are analyzed in situations in which individual decisions depend on expectations as to what others will do. Game theoretical models have been applied to the analysis of international relations, collective action, leadership, voting and elections, negotiations and conflict settlements, coalition formation and other relevant political dilemmas. This course will: 1) provide a set of tools of the theory of games of strategy with analytical value, 2) develop some relevant applications in the field of political science and international relations, 3) introduce a number of normative solutions to conflict, competition and negotiation problems. Numerous examples, exercises and applications of the models to the analysis of relevant problems will be presented, required and promoted.

title: Approaches/Topics in American Politics
description: This course serves as the field seminar in American politics. It will introduce you to the basic approaches and research on some key questions in American politics. The course entails a significant amount of reading, mostly journal articles. The course will feature at least one journal article or book chapter by every member of the American politics faculty. In addition, there will be some readings in comparative politics and political theory that help students put readings in context. Students are expected to complete the readings before class and come prepared to discuss them.

title: Research in American Politics
description: to be added

title: Methodology of Comparative Politics
description: The purpose of methods is to help us generate persuasive answers to questions that matter about the world. This course will provide an overview of research methods used in one subfield of the discipline: comparative politics. The intent of the course is to explore why methods matter, to familiarize students with a range of comparative methods, and to explore how they are used in the design and practice of comparative research. We also intend to review current debates about the use of methods in comparative politics, and to discuss the limits of comparison.

title: Authoritarianism in Comparative Perspective
description: In the low tide after the third wave of democracy, scholars are returning to the study of authoritarianism that was an important part of post-World War II research in comparative politics. This course seeks to expose students to both the older and more recent literature on authoritarianism. The course emphasizes political economy, institutional studies, and cultural approaches to understanding the forms and dynamics of authoritarian rule. Within these approaches a number of important themes in the literature are highlighted including typological studies, regime transitions, democratic breakdown, corporatism, authoritarianism and economic development, and domination and resistance. The course spans all developing regions, though the Middle East and North Africa receives somewhat more attention.

title: Field Methods
description: This course introduces Ph.D. students to a variety of field methods potentially applicable to their doctoral dissertations. Students will apply the different methods to their dissertation topics and choose the most appropriate method(s) for testing their arguments and for testing alternative explanations for their findings in the field. Students will complete the course with a defensible research design for their field work.

title: Fundamentals of International Relations
description: to be added

title: IR Theory & Approaches
description: Government 761 is an advanced class in the understanding and production of international-relations theory. This course has two primary objectives. First, to enhance studentsâ understanding of aspects of international-relations theory and contemporary debates in the academic study of international relations. Second, to help prepare students to be producers of international-relations scholarship by focusing their attention on issues in epistemology, methodology, and research design beyond those addressed in their standard allotment of methods and introductory courses.

title: PhD Seminar in Political Economy
description: The study of International Political Economy has come to include many questions surrounding international relations and political economy. Perhaps the most fundamental question is: How do domestic and international politics influence the economic relations between states?
