title: Soviet Dissident Movements
description: This seminar addresses the rise, activities, and achievements of anti-Soviet opposition movements during the last decades of the USSR (1960sâ1991) and their subsequent transformation in the post-Soviet space. It examines these movementsâ wide variety of ideological currents and political strategies, their contribution to the emergence of an alternative human rights culture, and their impact on the fall of the Soviet Union. It addresses activistsâ debates over the defense of human rights, the right to national self-determination, the right to emigrate, religious and cultural freedom, and environmental issues. Special emphasis is placed on how dissidents were perceived by Soviet authorities and in the international arena and how those perceptions shaped East-West diplomatic relations during the Cold War. This seminar also surveys the instrumental role dissidents played in the aftermath of the collapse of the Communist rule before being isolated from post-Soviet politics.

title: U.S.-Southeast Asian Relations in the Post 9/11 World
description: This seminar course will examine the relationship between the United States and Southeast Asia in the aftermath of the September 11, 2001 terrorist bombings. The United States is paying more attention to this region due to the links between several militant Islamic groups in Southeast Asia and the Al Qaeda network of Osama bin Laden. The course will cover U.S. relations on the political, economic, and socio-cultural levels with the eleven countries of Southeast Asia: Brunei, Burma (Myanmar), Cambodia, East Timor, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, the Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, and Vietnam. The United States is especially concerned about Indonesia, the largest Muslim nation in the world.

title: Social Development in South Asia
description: This undergraduate seminar presents an interdisciplinary survey of work of scholars, facilitating experts, and activists who are concerned with social development in South Asia. While we always are guided by theoretical-conceptual knowledge, studying work of facilitating experts and activists offers added benefits of practical understanding. Hence, we focus on landscapes comprised of interrelated contexts (e.g., post-colonial, democratizing, globalizing), sectors (e.g., education, health care, governance, micro-finance, environmental and cultural preservation), spaces (e.g., urban, rural, gendered, sacred), and actors (e.g., facilitating experts and activists, women, youth, minorities). Crucial with landscape studies, then, are discussions of ways in which development may improve access to resources, capital, and services through social mobilization, income generation, and capacity building of various kinds, and of unintended consequences of development as well. Throughout the seminar we also consider how South Asian development addresses disparities of wealth and power, conflicts between tradition and modernity, and demands for human rights and sustainability.

title: US Pol/Isr-Pal Conf: 9/11-2011
description: This seminar will examine US policy toward the Israeli-Palestinian conflict in the Bush and Obama Administrations. We will quickly review the negotiations before the Clinton years and then examine why Clinton's efforts failed. We will then turn to Bush policy before 9/11, and how that event changed his views and policies. Considerable time will be spent examining the policy moves that followed: endorsement of Palestinian statehood, the Quartet, the Roadmap, support for Gaza Disengagement, and the Annapolis conference. We will then turn to the first half of the Obama Administration, including the tension in US-Israel relations and the contrast between Bush and Obama approaches. We will conclude with discussions of "Fayyadism" and the debate over constructing a Palestinian state, and of the way in which US policy is formulated as a reflection of both bureaucratic power struggles and American domestic politics.

title: Islam and Politics
description: The course will discuss Islamic notions of politics, government, sovereignty and legitimacy and their varying interpretations throughout history. It will also discuss the more recent emergence of Islam as a political force and the reasons behind it. It will address how Islam has been used both as a factor of legitimating power by governments and as tool to challenge the legitimacy of existing systems. It will use case studies to illustrate these points.

title: Images of Eve, Mary, And Fatima
description: Eve, Mary, and Fatima are the central women of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. This course explores their definitions, roles, and presentations in visual culture. Feminist scholars argue that the fundamental purpose for the imaging of women is to teach both theological doctrine and appropriate societal roles for women. Following that mode of analysis, images of women in the modes of Eve, Mary, and Fatima will be witnessed through the arts from painting and sculpture to film and television. Lectures and in-class discussions will focus on modules devoted to an in-depth examination of the scriptural texts on the role of the visual modality, a chronological overview of the role of women in each tradition, and an analysis of the core relationship between the image of woman and the religious understanding of woman. Special questions arising from the images and theologies of Eve, Mary, and Fatima will be considered.

title: Egypt 1952-Present: Pol/Society
description: This seminar is an introduction to the politics, culture, society and economy of Egypt covering roughly the period from 1952 to the present. As such, the course takes up an interesting and tumultuous period in Egyptâs recent history; an era of tremendous political, social and economic transformation. In addition to conveying a basic understanding of Egyptian politics under Gamal Abdel Nasser (1952-1970), Anwar Al-Sadat (1970-1981) and Hosni Mubarak (1981-present), the seminar will approach Egyptâs recent history from an interdisciplinary perspective. Along with political history and analysis, we will examine Egyptian literature, film, and music in order to understand Egyptian society more broadly. An attempt will be made to introduce students to some of the major questions, debates, and recurring themes in the study of contemporary Egyptian politics and society.

title: Political Legitimacy & Identity in Southeast/East Asia
description: The purpose of this interdisciplinary course is to attempt to explain the complex forces that affect the ability of individuals and institutions in Southeast and East Asia to guide the internal operations of states and interaction with other states in accordance with internal or international norms and expectations. It will analyze the present drawing upon history in the region, and attempt to delineate forces that will affect the future. The course will examine some relevant theoretical literature on legitimacy, varying historical patterns of classical and modern legitimacy in the area, nationalism and legitimacy, questions of identity based on ethnicity/ language/religion related to legitimacy, and then explore how states/individuals have used these concepts for their acceptance and governance. The course will be open to juniors and seniors (sophomores and graduate students with the approval of the professor), and will meet once weekly for approximately two hours. The course will be limited to twenty-five students, and, depending on the number of students, be conducted in the manner of a seminar. Students will be expected to write a term paper on one country or topic approved by the instructor, and make a class presentation on the progress toward that paper. There will be a take-home final examination.

title: The United States and Latin America in the Modern Era
description: Latin America was a major battleground for competing political, economic and ideological interests of the United States and the Soviet Union in the post WWII era (1945-1991). Both Washington and Latin Ameria capitals had strategies for dealing with this bipolar competition. This course, divided into three parts, will begin with a series of readings on the background and objectives that North America and the Southern Hemisphere pursued dealing with the Cold War in the Americas. Second, the course will require an essay on how a Latin American leader or a country proposed a strategy while confronting the U.S. or the Soviet Union as the two global bipolar states sought allies in Latin America. Last, an essay will provide a case study on how a Washington initiative responded to a real or perceived Russian prescence in the Western Hemisphere in the time period (1945-1991).

title: Islam and Global Terrorism
description: The course will attempt to put September 11 and the current war against global terrorism in perspective. It will survey the rise of militant Islamic groups from North Africa to Southeast Asia, their ideologies, strategies, tactics and track records. Special attention will be given to Osama Bin Laden and the origins and development of al-Qaeda, America's relationship with Islam and Muslim extremist groups and its prosecution of the war against global terrorism, internationally and domestically.

title: Religion and Peace
description: The course examines the tension between the expressed purposes of religious traditions that call for peace, compassion, and service, and persistent frictions and the occasional break out of hostility and conflict across religious boundaries. The course also examines recent efforts across religious lines to find common grounds for peacebuilding. Theoretical approaches to this paradox will be drawn mainly from the Jewish, Christian, and Islamic traditions, and special emphasis will be given to ongoing initiatives and movements that aim at bring more understanding and cooperation across the religious divides.

title: China and India as Emerging Global Technological Powers
description: The Emergence of China and India as Technological and Economic Powers looks at the history, and recent performance of these two giant countries. They account for 38% of the world's population. If measured in terms of purchasing power parity, they already are the second and fourth largest economies in the world, although they are still poor developing countries. There are many similarities and differences in their development strategies. Both turned inward and went left in the middle of last century; and adopted Soviet style five year industrialization plans. Both have been opening up to more interaction with the rest of the world, albeit at a different pace and to a different extent. China is becoming the worldâs manufacturing hub; India is becoming its information technology enabled service exporter. China is a one party state, India is a tumultuous democracy. Furthermore they are growing at more than three times the world average, and have critical mass in terms of highly trained people, scientists and engineers, and R&D expenditures. Where are they coming from? Where are they going? What can we learn about development strategy from their experience? How sustainable are their strategies? The course looks on the impact they are already having on other developing countries, as well as on developed countries. It also explores what further impact they may have under different scenarios.

title: Taiwan in the 21st Century
description: The rise of China, US power and influence in Asia and the Pacific, and Taiwan assertiveness have made Taiwan a focal point of US and international policy concern as the main international hot spot that could lead to great power military conflict involving the United States. This course explains the background, status, and outlook of the China-Taiwan-US triangular relationship. It gives special focus to political, economic, social, and intellectual trends on Taiwan, how they affect Taiwan's standing in the triangular relationship, and what this means for both China and the United States. Class procedures are a combination of lectures, student reports, and class discussion.

title: Anthropology of Arab World
description: Coming Soon

title: Religion and Modern Politics of Iran
description: In 1979, a broad-based revolution led by the Ayatullah Rouhullah Khomeini toppled the regime of Muhammad Reza Shah Pahlavi thus ending a 2,500-year tradition of monarchy in Iran. The forces that contributed to this momentous event adhered to different political ideologies and belonged to different segments of the Iranian society. They also had widely divergent visions for a post-revolutionary Iran. However, in the end, Islamic forces prevailed and established an Islamic republic, marking the beginning of a fundamental alteration of Iranâs social, political and cultural make-up in the hopes of creating a utopian Islamic society. They also took it upon themselves to fill the role of champions of the rights of Muslims and all other âopppressedâ peoples, at a great cost to Iran. The remnants of this mindset continue to influence Iranâs external behavior and the character of its relations with other major powers. In the nearly three decades since the revolution, the Islamic republic has had to abandon many of its earlier objectives because of internal opposition and external events, notably the eight year war with Iraq in the eighties and pressures exerted by the great powers. Iranian nationalism and culture have proven stronger than the regime had anticipated after the revolution, while the disappointment with the performance of the Islamic regime has undermined the regimeâs ideology and to some extent the position of Islam. In a more positive vein, it has given rise to a new Islamic reformist discourse with a potential for reconciling Iraniansâ desire for modernity with their attachment to their religious and cultural values. Yet, Iran is not the absolute failure and the cultural wasteland at times portrayed by the international press and media. Rather, it is a country full of contradictions and conflicting intellectual activity of all sorts takes place. This course will focus on the following themes: (1) the factors that contributed to the Islamic Revolution in 1979, including the role of Islam; (2) Iranâs evolution in the post-revolutionary era and the factors that influenced it; and (3) an assessment of the likely trends for Iranâs future direction and factors that are likely to affect various alternative scenarios.

title: American Diplomacy, Religion, and the Politics of International Religious Freedom
description: Note: This class will be held at the Berkley Center, 3307 M Street, NW, Suite 200 Religion is assuming an increasingly critical role in international affairs. Although the secularization theory holds that faith will diminish with the advance of modernity, the 21st century is literally brimming with religious ideas, actors and communitiesâfor good and for ill. Religion influences societies and politics from China to Saudi Arabia to Cuba, and transnational trends from terrorism to the spread of democracy. Given the religious nature of the American people, it is perhaps surprising that U.S. diplomacy has had considerable difficulty engaging this new world of faith. At the same time, the United States is the only country in the world with an official policy of advancing international religious freedom (IRF). Could that policy provide a vehicle for U.S. diplomacy to engage religion in the world, or is advancing religious freedom too disruptive? Using IRF policy as the starting point, this course will explore the contradictions and continuities in U.S. foreign policy in a world of faith. It will examine the origins and performance of IRF policy and its prospects for development. Along the way it will explore the attitudes about religion and religious freedom which have helped to mold diplomatic actions. It will ask whether U.S. strategies have reduced religious persecution, or advanced religious freedom, and what impact they have had on terrorism and the spread of democracy in the greater Middle East.

title: Migration Pol: Hist/Pol US/Eur
description: This course examines the history as well as contemporary debates on migration and migration policies in Germany and the United States. The readings will reflect not only competing points of view but also highlight the transatlantic differences. The fact that even though the same categories are used in current arguments, they have vastly different analytical meaning and political implications on either side of the Atlantic, will serve as point of departure for class discussions. The course will explore the conceptual issues that lie behind contemporary debates as well as the policy implications of these debates.

title: Turkey In World Politics
description: Coming SoonThis course will examine the nature, goals and directions of Turkish World Politics, especially with an eye on Turkish Foreign Policy today. For many, this may seem an interesting novelty but, in fact, Turkish diplomacy and foreign policy represent one of the longest and most skillfully managed institutions in the Western world. In order to better understand and appreciate this tradition and the institution we know today as the Turkish Foreign Ministry (D????leri Bakanl???), this course will first turn to the late Ottoman Empire and the creation of the Ministry and the role it has played in shaping modern Turkey. In the second section, the discussion and examination will turn to the environmental and other constraints placed on the early Republics policy goals in light of defeat in World War I and the Global Depression. Section three will deal with Turkeyâs post-WW II efforts to counter Soviet encroachment and, at the same time maintaining regional stability. Lastly, we will study the new, multilateral foreign policy of the Justice and Development Party (AKP) since it came to power in 2002.

title: Coporate Finance
description: This course is a âdeeper diveâ into the fundamentals of corporate finance as provided by the Business Financial Management course. The course is intended for students that are looking to extend their understanding of the corporate finance world and provides insight to the finance concepts, accounting and analytical tools that are comparable to what the typical MBA student has upon graduation. The course includes discussions of critical finance analysis, valuation techniques, financial analysis, and includes working with/building actual modeling tools, similar to those used by Corporate Finance professionals. Further, we will cover critical corporate risk management policies/processes and discuss why these are important in the Corporate Finance world. We will utilize several case studies â some that I will select and others that you will select to demonstrate âin real life situationsâ the âunderpinningsâ of why the analytical approach is so important to this area. The ultimate goal of this course is for the students to develop skills in recognizing business problems, understanding the analytical tools to define such problems and being able to see how financial tools actually are deployed in corporate decision-making. This course will focus more on analytical thinking and assessment techniques and less on âmemorizing the formulas of various financial analyticsâ. Finally, we will utilize a number of teaming arrangements, especially for case studies, in order to increase your team building skills (a critical element to the business world).

title: Senior Capstone Seminar:International Development
description: The multidisciplinary seminar encourages senior students to think critically about development as an urgent global challenge and as a contested theoretical field. The course addresses major themes in all five subfields of the Certificate in International Development curriculum and allows students to integrate and synthesize what they have learned in international development during heir undergraduate careers. Specifically, it revisits theoretical approaches taught in the classroom and encourages students to test the strength and weakness of these approaches by drawing upon the practical experience they have acquired through internships in the field and through development weekend workshops hosted on campus by the certificate. The seminar begins with a review of international development in the social sciences and the history and politics of development knowledge. We will then look at the complex factors that have influenced change in the developing world: the relationship between political order and economic growth; the importance of technology, health, energy and environmental sustainability; the role of ethics, religion, culture. Finally, in our analysis of the opportunities and challenges of international aid we will look at both successes and failures to help us focus on the road ahead. The format of the seminar allows students to concentrate on themes or regions of special interest in class room discussions, oral presentations and in interactive workshops for the research paper and helps to prepare them for their careers after graduation. --------------------------------------------------------------------------------

title: Problems in Multilateral Diplomacy
description: A series of case studies illustrative of the most serious problems in modern multilateral diplomacy. Cases will include intervention in intrastate conflict; UN Security Council reorganization; arms control; trade and environmental policy; and human rights.

title: International Financial Markets
description: This course provides an introduction to modern finance theory and its application to international financial markets. The course examines the foreign exchange market and related markets in derivatives such as foreign exchange futures, currency and interest rate swaps, and currency options, the eurocurrency market, international bond markets, and cross-border flow of equity investment. The course, which is open both to undergraduates and masters students, emphasizes computer and quantitative skills that are essential to understanding the operations of modern financial markets.

title: Muslim-Christian Relations in World History
description: This course examines the nature of Muslim-Christian relations in the different contexts and eras of world history. Particular case studies ranging from medieval debates and the Crusades to modern experiences in Bosnia and in globalization will be discussed. The course concentrates on historical and societal dimensions rather than theology.

title: Islam, Pol & Sec in Mod C Asia
description: The course will examine the role of Islam in politics and society in Central Asia, the Caucasus, and the Muslim-inhabited parts of the Russian Federation. It will provide an historical background of Islam in these regions especially under Russian and Soviet rule, and in the post-independence era. The course will cover topics such as the rise of extremist groups, the response of the state to Islam, the relationships among the Muslims of Central Asia, the Caucasus, the Russian Federation, and the rest of the Muslim world, Islam and security related issues, the political uses of Islam and the impact of these interactions on the evolution of Islam in Central Asia and the Caucasus especially in the post-Soviet Era.

title: Jewish Civilization Senior Colloquium
description: Individual directed research with a faculty member working in an area relevent to student's research interests in Jewish Civilization. The semester's work will culminate in a spring colloquium where students will present condensed oral versions of their 30-40 page senior thesis. PJC certificate/minor students only.

title: European Political Systems
description: This course will study the political regimes of all major countries in Western Europe, covering political parties, elections and electoral rules, the relationships between parliaments and governments, the role of local and regional governments, and other relevant institutions and processes. Also the European Union will be analyzed as a political regime in a comparative perspective with the EU member-states and with the USA.

title: Investng in Afr: Hlth Ed Refrm
description: People and their ability to achieve and maintain good health and acquire basic skills are central to anti-poverty efforts and sustainable development world-wide. Health and education programs account for approximately 30% of U.S. Foreign Assistance and are reflected in all eight of the United Nations Millennium Development Goals. This course will explore the status of health and education in Africa, challenges surrounding improving health and increasing educational attainment, and interventions and efforts to reform these crucial social services. This course will help students identify key issues informing policy and programs and help them to think about the importance of health and education to all development efforts and ways in which health and education promoting activities can be integrated into all aspects of development in Africa, relative to their own professional endeavors, future areas of study or areas of specialization. An emphasis will be put on promoting evidence based decision making. Students will be encouraged to think about how policy, funding and research can best respond to the diverse cultural and economic realities of African human capacity issues.

title: Poverty and Inequality: Development Challenges
description: This seminar combines grounding in systems and institutions and analytic exploration of major global policy issues for international development. It takes the year 2000 United Nations formulation of Millennium Development Goals as a framework, and uses a practical introduction to the work of the key development institutions, notably the World Bank, to illustrate how the policy choices that are relevant for the global effort to end poverty are framed and acted upon in practice. The seminar will explore different perspectives on why the challenges of poverty and social justice are so significant today, ranging from security to political to moral.

title: China's Evolving Role in Africa
description: China and Africaâs economic and diplomatic relations have developed rapidly in the last twenty years. Media stories depict Chinaâs engagement as a new âscramble for Africaâ reminiscent of the Westâs colonial engagement with Africa in 20th century. What is the real story? What is China getting from its engagement with Africa? Even more important, what are the Africans getting from the relationship? Will it be transformative or a repeat of the Western colonial extractive experience? This course will provide students with a balanced and nuanced understanding of the China-in-Africa story, as outlined in the syllabus posted on blackboard.

title: African Development
description: The course will survey the development of the Sub-Saharan African economies since independence. The focus will be on economic development, but aims to place the subject in its broader political, social and cultural context. The approach will be historical: starting with a brief review of the colonial inheritance, then covering the largely optimistic period to the early 1970s, moving on to the problems and stagnation of the 1980s, followed by the slow recovery of the 1990s which has accelerated in the first half of this decade. The survey will conclude by looking at where Africa is today and the prospects going forward. The course will explore key issues and themes that marked each period and try to piece together an overall story concerning where African economies have been and where they are going.

title: America's National Security Tool Box
description: The primary objective of this seminar is to investigate the tools available to todayâs foreign-policy practitioner. Students will evaluate the strengths and limitations of individual policy instruments in the areas of traditional diplomacy, economic measures, military force, and public diplomacy by looking at them in the context of recent cases. Role-playing scenarios will challenge students to design U.S. policy responses to foreign affairs crises, thereby testing the application of their policy âtoolboxâ in a complex, dynamic, inter-agency environment.

title: European Studies Colloquium
description: This interdisciplinary reading and research colloquium is intended to serve as the capstone course for senior candidates for the Certificate in European Studies. Particular topics of inquiry will vary according to the interests of both students and instructors; course requirements will typically include a substantial research project(s).

title: Religion and International Affairs
description: One of the key issues in the study of international affairs, notably the question of what factors most help determine the direction of states external relations, has been the relative role of idea/ideals versus more tangible security, economic and political interest, and desire for power and prestige. For the greater part of the Cold War era, this issue was raised in connection with the assessment of the role of ideology and ideological aspirations in determining the direction of statesâ external relations and, more fundamentally, in affecting their perceptions of what constitutes their vital interests. Since the collapse of the Soviet Union in 1991 and the end of the ideological Cold War, religion has increasingly acquired the role of ideology in this context. Thus the question has increasingly been raised about the relative role that countriesâ religious beliefs and values play in determining their external behavior and shaping their perception of their vital interests. Within this broad context, Muslim countries for two reasons occupy an especial place. First, the majority of Muslim peoples are religious and religion plays an important role in their social and cultural lives. Second, Muslims view themselves as a single community (Ummah) and have always aspired for greater unity. Given these factors, logically, religion should have played a more significant role in determining the pattern of Muslim countriesâ external relations as compared to those of other less religious societies. Yet, this has not been the case, and external relations of Muslim countries have been primarily determined by other factors than religion. This course will examine: the reasons why this has not been the case by studying the pattern of external behavior of key Muslim countries in the last fifty years, and the principal factors that have shaped them; assess the relative role of religion in determining the direction of these relations; assess Muslim efforts at cooperation and greater unity, and identify factors behind the failure or success of such efforts. In addition to putting the role of religion as a determinant of Muslim countriesâ external behavior this course will help in a better understanding of the relative role of ideas and ideals, including religion, in shaping all statesâ external relations.

title: Miracles in Islam and the West
description: Many people today would respond to news of a miracle with skepticism. Others would embrace such a report as evidence of divine favor or presence. The cultural and intellectual circumstances that govern our reactions to reports of miracles have fluctuated over the centuries, and different faith traditions have addressed miracles and their credibility in contrasting ways. This course will explore how the Western intellectual tradition has dealt with religious miracles, starting with Greco-Roman historians, proceeding through early Christian writers like St. Augustine, exploring the work of Renaissance humanists and finally studying Enlightenment stances on religion and science. Islamic civilization shared a great deal with the Western tradition, including a Greco-Roman heritage and medieval scholasticism. This course will compare the Western tradition with medieval and modern Islamic scholarly discussion of miracles and when they should be believed.

title: European Economic Integration
description: The course will study the origins, historical development and current situation of European economic integration. Detailed attention will be paid to its historical roots and development to provide a full understanding of its dynamics and rationale. The course will be organized and assessed according to three major arguments: a) the historical development of the economic integration process; b) the European statesâ reactions to integration challenges and opportunities, and c) the design and deployment of European common economic policies. The course will be a discussion-based seminar, with a number of student paper presentations, but for the very first weeks that will be devoted to an overview of the major issues at stake. The fundamental reading material will include Alan Milward, The Reconstruction of western Europe, and The European Rescue of the Nation State; Nick Crafts and Gianni Toniolo, eds., Economic Growth in Europe since 1945, and Larry Neal, The Economics of Europe and the European Union.

title: Turkey in World Cinema
description: Contemporary Turkey is a nation of 72 million people located at the crossroads of the modern world. Largely Muslim but offically secular, Turkey and Turkish culture has a rich and dynamic history. It also has one of the most creative cinemas. Turkish films, during the past decade have consistantly taken top prizes in Europeean, Asian and american film festivals. This course examines both Turkey and Turkish cinema. I examines both the art and what we can come to understand of Turkey and its culture through the constructed lens of film.

title: Islamic Theological Texts
description: Unlike its counterpart in Christianity, Islamic theology did not emerge as the most ornate and well developed genre of thought in the faith tradition. Islamic theology did, however, address core concerns of understanding God, metaphysics and the defense of Islamic doctrines against outside attacks. Islamic theologyâs chief influence was on the epistemological foundations of Islamic thought, which informed law, legal theory, communal identification and mysticism. Islamic theology has taken on new importance in the modern period, when pluralism and ecumenicalism have raised anew the questions of who is a Muslim and who can expect salvation. This course provides an introduction to Islamic theology, focusing on Sunni Islam for the sake of brevity. In addition to reading representative Islamic theological texts, we will focus on signal debates such as the permissibility of seeking the intercession of saints and grave visitation. This class will center on reading Arabic texts, with supplemental secondary-source readings in English. Students must have an effective working knowledge of Arabic.

title: Burma/Myanmar as Nexus: Regional Dilemmas
description: This course will examine the questions of regional strategic concerns and foreign policy responses toward a multi-ethnic state, riven by internal ethnic, political, and economic tensions, while pivotal in China, India, and ASEAN relations. International and internal conceptions of legitimacy and problems of human rights, often highly polarized, will be considered within the context of contemporary Burmese history and its relations within the China, South Asia, and Southeast Asia regions. United States policies will be analyzed. Too often ignored, Burma/Myanmar is an important case study illustrative of the effects of colonial policy, ethnic diversity, periods of incipient pluralism, political repression, economic autarchy, and militarism. Although unique, its dilemmas represent case studies more broadly applicable. Guest speakers of varying views will be invited.

title: Corruption in Developing Countries
description: Corruption has become the litmus test for the viability of democratic institutions, the ability for countries to develop economically, and the effective influence of society in policy making. In the past, some strands of âfunctionalistâ policy science justified political corruption as necessary for economic development and tolerated its "natural" place in third world societies. In recent years, however, the scholarly and policy communities have assigned a high priority to controlling corruption by linking better understanding of causes and consequences of corruption to more effective anti-corruption policies and practices. This seminar examines (1) scholarly and policy perspectives on corruption; (2) issues of corruption in partisan politics and policy formulation; (3) varieties of corruption in program administration at the national, subnational and independent agency levels in various world regions; and (4) agreed-upon tools and remedies employed in anti-corruption efforts by international, national and civil society actors, with special emphasis on transparency and accountability mechanisms. Emphasis will be placed on developing countries, although we will include coverage of post-industrial and post-Communist cases as well. Students will write two short papers and a substantial research paper or a policy brief.

title: Religion and Global Development
description: This graduate seminar will explore both theoretical and practical aspects of links among religious and development institutions in approaching policies and programs aimed at social and economic development. It will begin with practical exploration of current debates and partnerships for a list of priority issues, notably health, education, HIV/AIDS, conflict resolution, and environment. This will reflect both exploration of "state of the art" thinking and experience and work with specific case studies. The institutional picture of how institutions operate, approach issues, and interact will be explored. The seminar will also explore the evolution of thinking within both faith and development institutions and in the major global ecumenical and interfaith institutions about poverty and equity and their links with changes brought by globalization. The seminar will involve both active discussion and team projects and an indepth individual research paper.

title: The Chinese Economy
description: The Chinese Economy: Transition to the Market, Economic Development, and Integration into the Global Economy. The course analyzes Chinaâs economic transformation and growth with particular emphasis on Chinaâs economic reforms since 1978, including the restructuring of its agricultural, industrial, and services sectors; the evolution of its financial, fiscal, and legal systems; and the roles of trade, foreign investment, and technology in driving Chinaâs economic advance.

title: Islam & Study/Mslm-Chrstn Rel
description: The course will provide an overview of the study of the history of the encounter between Christians and Muslims over fourteen centuries, a history that has been marked by conflict and cooperation, diatribe and dialogue, hatred and tolerance, hostility and personal friendships. It will focus on particular periods that are perceived as having particular impact in shaping the relationship.

title: American Entrepreneurship in China
description: This course looks at China's relative might in the global economy through the perspective of business on the ground: How stuff actually gets made in China -- from oil rigs to nuclear power plants to medicines -- and why that stuff is so systemically risky. What threats does this systemic risk pose to America's national interest and security? What opportunities? China's true competitive capability will be assessed as we seek to answer the question, "Can America rebuild its economy in a world with 'a rising China?'"

title: German Economy 21st Century
description: The course covers the structure and performance of the German economy as well as current challenges, policies and reform options. It is structured into several parts. We will begin with a brief look backwards at economic history to gain familiarity with the historical roots of current institutions and debates. We next explore key aspects of the German economy including core sectors, regional differences and the impact of globalization. We will then look at labor markets and policies, including a comparative European perspective. We next turn to macroeconomic aspects, including fiscal, monetary and financial policies and challenges. In the fifth part, we explore the economic growth performance and outlook, again including a comparative European perspective. The final part of the course explores the challenges facing the German welfare system.

title: Religious Sources of American Foreign Policy
description: U.S. foreign policy has always been influenced by domestic religious traditions and religiously informed purposes. Since the early days of the Republic faith-based groups have lobbied for or against particular policies, and American exceptionalism has been energized by a religious sense of mission. During the late 20th century the âfourth great awakeningâ intensified the involvement of some religious groups in foreign affairs, especially in advocacy for human rights and religious freedom. More broadly, evangelical Protestants, Catholics, Jewish supporters of Israel and, particularly after 9/11, American Muslims, have had a significant impact on our foreign policy. Abroad, religious actors and communities have become major players on the international stage, with enormous implications for American national interests. This course will examine the impact of religion on U.S. foreign policy and its salience in the contemporary international order. It will begin with an historical survey of religion in U.S. foreign affairs. It will then examine contemporary religious movements and their influence on foreign policy, and conclude by exploring the effects of religion on the major international issues of the day, including terrorism, war, economic development and the spread of democracy.

title: Global Health, Disease, and History in Asia
description: Utilizing a case study approach focused on societies in East and South, this seminar provides a critical historical perspective on many contemporary global health issues. Global health challenges now clearly transcend national borders: in the age of the jumbo-jet, infectious and vector-borne diseases can spread easily from one country to another. Certain health problems such as smoking and obesity are of such magnitude that they have global impact. Food and drug safety are of increasing concern in our interconnected world of globalized trade. At the same time, previously distinct medical traditions and practices such as Ayurveda and Chinese acupuncture now circulate globally even as biomedicine prevails as the dominant form of health care around the world. These and many other issues of global health are deeply rooted in the past. Historical forces and events, from colonialism to the global spread of HIV/AIDS, have shaped the current state of global health. Understanding the history of global health in local Asian contexts can thus help us to respond to the many health challenges of today. Through focused case studies drawn primarily from the historical experiences of China and India since 1800, we will examine how social, economic, and political forcesâincreasingly global in natureâhave influenced medical processes and disease outcomes in Asia. We will also investigate how developments in Asia have affected global health since the nineteenth century. Topics include strategies of public health in colonial and post-colonial contexts; changing ideas about hygiene, sanitation, and contagion; state and social responses to specific disease outbreaks; the emergence of international health institutions; the inequitable burden of disease; vertical and horizontal approaches to primary health care; the individual and collective experience of illness due to consumer culture and changing life-styles; and the persistent presence and global diffusion of Asian healing cultures. This course is open to all graduate students and advanced undergraduates including those interested in Asian Studies; International Affairs; International Development; Regional and Comparative Studies; Global Disease; International Health; and Global, International, and Comparative History. This course is cross-listed in the History Department. M.A.G.I.C. students and advanced History majors are welcome to enroll along with other graduate and advanced undergraduates.

title: Dvmt Challenges of the BRICs
description: Although Brazil, Russia, India and China are often lumped together since a report in 2001 by Goldman Sachs famously grouped them as the BRICs and projected that they would be larger economically than the developed countries by 2050, they are very different economically, politically and structurally. The growth and development of these emerging economies raises many challenges and opportunities, not just to their own development strategy, but also for the rest of the world. In the aftermath of the current global financial and economic crisis they are on track to become larger than the developed countries faster than earlier projected. Their rise also raises issues of competition for markets, and natural resources; the role of population, education, and technology in global competition and relative wages between developed and developing economies; the most effective development models in terms of the mix of the state vs. the market, and authoritarian vs. democratic governance systems; the appropriate structure of global governance institutions and mechanism and their representation within them; security and the stability of the global system; and not least, global warming and sustainability in light of their large contributions to CO2 emissions and different approaches to what they consider appropriate burden sharing between developed and developing countries in dealing with this challenge. This course will begin by reviewing the history and development of these countries and the challenges to their development. It will then explore the issues highlighted above. By the end of the course students will have a good grasp of how the BRICs are impacting and being impacted by the world economy. There will be a midterm and a 25 page research paper. Open to Seniors and Juniors and Graduate Students.

title: Technology, Globalization and Growth
description: The acceleration in the creation and dissemination of technology in the last five decades, combined with the information revolution, has a major impact on the growth prospects of advanced and developing countries. These revolutions in information and technology are also increasing trade integration and other interdependences across countries and regions. The course starts with a brief overview of the role of technology in growth and development, as well as the basics of invention, innovation, and commercialization of technology. It explores the interplay between four critical elements of a country's readiness to take advantage of these revolutions: a demanding economic incentive and institutional regime; an educated and skilled population; a dynamic information infrastructure; and an effective innovation system. It uses measures of these elements to assess the strengths and weaknesses of the US, the main European countries, Japan, China, India, Russia, Brazil, Mexico, and major developing regions. It also explores how the technology and information revolutions affect four key issues of global concern: inequality, security, sustainability, and quality of life. With these building blocks and some basic assumptions about key political and economic trends, students will be asked to project how the world may look in 2020, and to propose actions to improve these prospective futures. The course is open to upperclassmen and graduate students with some background in economics and international relations.

title: Economic Devt in SE Asia
description: The goal of the course is to create and enhance a capacity for understanding contemporary development issues and debates in Southeast Asia. Long-run and current economic issues in Cambodia, Indonesia, Lao PDR, Malaysia, the Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam will be examined; Brunei, East Timor, Myanmar, and Singapore will receive secondary attention. Lectures will cover the economic, political, and institutional context, draw on theoretic essentials in the areas of economic growth, international trade, and human capital, as well as confront national statistics and firm-level data. Students will acquire comprehensive knowledge of the economic experience in Southeast Asia, and develop an analytical framework to understand the determinants of competitiveness and growth in the region. Current debates on growth, distribution, and globalization will be addressed by utilizing the tools of economics and interpreting data in order to understand and make judgments about alternative development paths in Southeast Asia. To do this well requires a degree of formal economic analysis as well as carefulscrutiny of the empirical evidence. These are the kinds of exercises the course aims to make possible for students.

title: Economic Devt in SE Asia

title: Business Politics in Africa
description: Many scholars and practitioners in the developing world once held that the private sector was peripheral, if not antithetical, to development goals, preferring instead statist or nationalist approaches. Today, a more nuanced approach to development accounts for the states need to rely on resources and information provided by a viable private sector in order to formulate and implement effective economic policies. While hardly a paragon of âmature capitalism,â the increased relevance of African private sectors â spurred in part by marked changes in democratic and economic institutions beginning in the 1990s â is today undeniable. We can now at least begin to talk about African private sectors on their own merits, rather than in terms of their parasitism vis-Ã -vis the public sector. Private sector activity in Africa is also deeply influenced by external actors and the culture of business is both more diverse and dynamic than appreciated. How have business politics and the politics of business changed in the last two decades? This course aims to address these essential questions by investigating how business in Africa has been historically misconstrued, as well as through examination of business prospects. This course will examine the changing role and perception of the private sector in African development, and the interactions of businesspeople and business organizations with the African state. Certainly, the private sector role in Africa has been both positive and negative. We will explore the various tradeoffs and the implications for development and democracy, drawing on numerous country and firm cases from around the continent.

title: War, Peace and Violence in Global Visual Culture
description: To demonstrate the central importance of visual culture in globally defining the reality of war, peace, and violence in modern culture, this course will introduce students to key works of cinema, art, and literature that have shaped our definitions of war, peace, and violence. An initial chronological survey of the visual culture of war and peace will be complemented by discussions distinguishing types or degrees of war: Classical Greece and Rome; The Crusades; World Wars; Civil Wars (especially Spain), genocide(Armenia, European Jewry), struggles of national liberation (Algeria, Vietnam), and prospects of a global holocaust-with this last creating new imagery in art, cinema, monumental sculptures, photography, poetry, and fiction.

title: International Business Ethics
description: International business helps drive globalization trends that are reshaping political, economic and socio-cultural values. Increasingly, many governments and civil society groups urge corporations to go beyond traditional business roles to help improve human rights, labor conditions, and environmental impacts in countries around the world. Such actions can pose serious policy dilemmas, involving difficult value choices. Should foreign companies try to influence a countryâs domestic political process on issues such as democratic elections, religious freedoms, the treatment of indigenous peoples, or the domestic allocation of tax revenue? Should large international retailers seek to impose âliving wageâ standards on their foreign suppliers? Do the marketing practices of international companies impose inappropriate choices on vulnerable consumers in developing countries, or enhance their free choice of products? Should corporations decide how a developing country balances trade-offs between current economic growth and longer-term environmental goals? Who should decide, What should be done, When and Where? Why? This course studies the application of ethics to contemporary issues of international business operating in different economic, political and cultural settings. Drawing on established ethical theory, the course uses normative criteria to evaluate âbest choiceâ options for real-world decisions. Consideration of global economic justice and corporate social responsibility are examined, along with mandatory and voluntary methods of influencing business behavior. Students will examine these issues from the perspective of corporate employees and managers, home and host government officials, and civil society activists.

title: Dvmt & Conflict/the Arab World
description: Coming Soon

title: US Strat in Asia:Hist & Pract
description: This course examines the evolution and practice of U.S. grand strategy towards Asia. The course opens with a consideration of the American tradition of grand strategy and then focuses on key periods of innovation in U.S. strategic thinking towards Asia, from the early drive for control of the Pacific Northwest to the contemporary debate about how to manage the rise of China. Students will read authors from John Quincy Adams through Henry Kissinger and the 2010 Quadrennial Defense Review and will use those texts to reconstruct the strategic thinking of the period and to trace the enduring elements --and failures -- of America's Asia strategy from the Republic's founding to the present.

title: Assessing U.S.-Japan Relations
description: From its earliest associations, economics has been a central feature of Japanâs relationship with the United States. Of course, other issues between the two nations are important and these have become more salient in the past several years, especially in the years after the 9/11 terrorist attacks. This course focuses on the economic relationship.

title: Outsourcing, Offshoring & Services in Global Economy
description: Popular media and political rhetoric often reference outsourcing, both as a potential source of growth and a potential source of job destruction. This course goes beyond the buzz words to examine what we really know about global production networks. What is meant by the terms âoffshoringâ and âoutsourcingâ? Are traditional models of trade sufficient to handle increasing complex global production strategies? How do we measure trade in intangibles such as intellectual property and business services? What are the impacts of offshoring on both the countries doing the investing and the countries receiving the investment? How worried should you be about losing your job to someone in India? Is the world getting âflatterâ? What policy responses are appropriate? These and other related questions will be addressed using both theory and empirical evidence.

title: Outsourcing, Offshoring & Services in Global Economy
description: Popular media and political rhetoric often reference outsourcing, both as a potential source of growth and a potential source of job destruction. This course goes beyond the buzz words to examine what we really know about global production networks. What is meant by the terms âoffshoringâ and âoutsourcingâ? Are traditional models of trade sufficient to handle increasing complex global production strategies? How do we measure trade in intangibles such as intellectual property and business services? What are the impacts of offshoring on both the countries doing the investing and the countries receiving the investment? How worried should you be about losing your job to someone in India? Is the world getting âflatterâ? What policy responses are appropriate? These and other related questions will be addressed using both theory and empirical evidence.

title: WTO Dispute Settlement
description: The multilateral trading system is widely argued to be more ârules-basedâ than ever before. Dispute settlement under the World Trade Organization (WTO), in particular, is increasingly being called upon to adjudicate rights and obligations in international commerce. These decisions bear directly on business opportunities, both nationally and internationally. Indeed, not only do these decisions influence specific industries and trade-related measures, but the breadth and depth of âglobalizationâ more generally. This course is about WTO dispute settlement. The course begins by examining theories of political economy, setting the stage for an in-depth look at international trade law and WTO dispute settlement. Readingsâof which there are manyâare drawn from these disciplines.

title: Topics in IBD: International  Business and Governments
description: Popular media and political rhetoric often reference outsourcing, both as a potential source of growth and a potential source of job destruction. This course goes beyond the buzz words to examine what we really know about global production networks. What is meant by the terms âoffshoringâ and âoutsourcingâ? Are traditional models of trade sufficient to handle increasing complex global production strategies? How do we measure trade in intangibles such as intellectual property and business services? What are the impacts of offshoring on both the countries doing the investing and the countries receiving the investment? How worried should you be about losing your job to someone in India? Is the world getting âflatterâ? What policy responses are appropriate? These and other related questions will be addressed using both theory and empirical evidence.

title: Politics & The Media: An American-European Perspective
description: In the World of Politics and Policy the 24-hour the news cycle defines success as "how you effectively understand and use the media to achieve your goals." During this course you will learn to analyze, compare and utilize all forms of media in the 24-hour news cycle across the Globe. Together we will explore the use of media by examining its historical effects on the events that shaped our common history from "Remember The Maine" to Twitter in Iran. We will use current case studies to discover how the media is used and manipulated by political campaigns as well as governments in highly developed and developing countries, including the US, UK, EU, Iran, Iraq and Afghanistan. This course will offer you an opportunity as well as the responsibility to improve your skills in understanding and managing US and International media by: examining political, advocacy and marketing campaigns; exploring the interplay between the media and the political processes; creating student led seminar discussions and debates and participating in hands-on media training. You will be required to provide individual case studies and oral presentations as part of your final grade. Additionally, the course will include discussions with prominent Washington insiders who will impart their personal views and experiences on the use of media in the public policy and political arena. Materials for the course will include books, selected readings, as well as visual and sound recordings shown in class.

title: International Business Operations and Risk in Emerging Markets
description: This course will examine the background and operations of relevant international political actors; special attention will be paid to the incentives influencing the behavior of these actors and the process by which they generate policies that can adversely or favorably influence investor operations.

title: Topics: IBD, Business, Government and the Global Economy
description: This course offers an introduction to the political economy of international business. In todayâs global economy, firms are as much in need of non-market strategies for dealing with governments and non-governmental organizations as they are in need of market strategies for competing with other firms. The aim of this course is to examine how these strategies interact in a marketplace that is increasingly shaped by the âvisible handâ of governments and international institutions. In particular, the course surveys issues from the prevalence of so-called âborder effectsâ (i.e., the way political boundaries impede the flow of commerce) to obligations under the World Trade Organization and the growing number of regional trade agreements (i.e., the North American Free Trade Agreement), and from labor and environmental standards to the strength of intellectual property rights.

title: Business and the Political Economy of East Asia
description: This seminar examines the international political economy of East Asia, and implications for business and the private sector. The region is undoubtedly of critical importance: it is home to half the worldâs population, and rapid economic development has fueled global economic growth. Yet it is also a region experiencing political and social changes that have profound implications for regional and global stability, as well as private enterprise. This seminar analyzes trends in the region by examining the political nature of the most salient economic issues, and their implications for the policy and business communities. Students are not expected to have an advanced proficiency in economics; however, it is highly recommended that students have some background in basic macroeconomic concepts and a certain familiarity with the international monetary and trading system.

title: US/European Economic Relations
description: The United States and the European Union are the two largest economic areas of the world and each otherâs main commercial partner: they account for more than 40% of world GDP and for one third of world trade. However, the changing geography of growth and challenges arising from present globalization trends, as well as the post-Cold war ridefinition of international political and security issues, raise a series of new questions on the structural foundations of the âTranstlantic economyâ and on its future prospects. The Course aims to reconstruct the historical evolution of US-European economic relations in the post-1945 period, with particular reference to their institutional dimension and to the relationship between multilateralism and regionalism. Topics will include: - the legacy of the 1930s and of wartime economic planning - multilateral economic institutions and postwar US foreign policy - European regional integration and trade liberalisation, from the Marshall Plan to the EEC - was the Cold war a globalizing force? - the changing nature of economic growth in the 1970s and 1980s - Transatlantic monetary issues and the European path towards monetary unification

title: Redefining Geopolitical Relationships
description: Redefining Geo-political Relationships Chuck Hagel The 21st Century has ushered in a global transformation that is redefining the world order. This transformation is shifting geo-political centers of gravity and is re-casting geo-political influences as the world experiences an unprecedented diffusion of power. This course will examine the above thesisâ¦ is this in fact happening? Are the six and a half billion people in the world â soon to be eight billion â and the nations they reside in, redefining geo-political relationships governed by the new realities of global challenges? Global challenges such as an interconnected global economy, proliferation of weapons of mass destruction, terrorism, environmental and energy issues, pandemic health threats, poverty, despair, and new technologies. Are we entering a new era of global engagement and accommodation? Is all of this reshaping and redefining relationships? This course will focus on a wide-lens optic of global relationships and its impact on American foreign policy and Americaâs future.

title: National Interests and Global Business
description: This course examines major trends that shape a growing divergence between traditional national interests and evolving global business. While incorporating international trade concepts, the course focuses on cross-national investment and establishment patterns that raise questions of corporate national identity and citizenship, including how transnational business strategies affect national policy objectives. Interrelationships between global corporate operations and the mechanisms of national political sovereignty and control are analyzed in both developed and developing countries across a range of manufacturing, advanced technology, natural resource and service sector activities. Assigned readings and lectures will provide an historical and comparative context while class discussion will focus on applying past experience to an analysis of current events, drawing on recent case studies and new issues emerging from media reports.

title: Islamists/Elections/Politics:ME
description: This course is about Islamists, elections, and politics in the contemporary Middle East. It will address at least three, sometimes related, topics: Islamist politics; Islamists and Elections; and Elections in the Middle East. Although these topics sometimes overlap, this course will take a broader approach to these subjects and will not be limited to only those instances in which Islamists participate in elections. We will also be concerned with mass-based Islamist politics more generally as well as elections in the Middle East. And when examining elections, the course will attempt to integrate some of the theoretical literature on elections in non-democratic contexts (e.g., âauthoritarian elections,â âcontrolled elections,â âlimited electionsâ). From Morocco to Iraq (and beyond), Islamist groups and movements are the most popular and dynamic political forces in the region. Moreover, contrary to the focus in the United States and Europe, mainstream, non-violent and/or âmoderateâ Islamist groups are much more popular and influential than the extremist, radical, and violent groups that often receive a disproportionate share of media attention. It also appears that Islamists have increasingly chosen to participate in formal politics, including legislative elections and parliamentary politics. Elections in the Middle East are also receiving more international and domestic attention than in the past and they have possibly become more meaningful and competitive as a result of both domestic pressures for reform and international attention to democratization. And there is reason to expect continued Islamist success at the ballot box. This course, therefore, seeks to focus our attention on the intersection of these two interesting and increasingly important political phenomenon: Islamists and Elections in the Middle East. Some of the Islamist groups we will focus on include the Egyptian Muslim Brotherhood, Moroccoâs Justice and Development Party (PJD), HAMAS, Jordanâs Islamic Action Front, and Turkeyâs AK Party. (We might also study Kuwaitâs Islamic Constitutional Movement and the Lebanese Hizbullah). We will attempt to explore a number of questions surrounding Islamists, Elections, and Politics in the Middle East including: what explains the success (electoral and otherwise) of these movements? To what extent does ideology, organization, and/or social service provision explain Islamist popularity? How important is service provision (e.g., the provision of social welfare services to citizens and constituents, such as schooling, healthcare, pensions, orphanages, recreational activities, etc.) in the success and popularity of these groups? What is the extent of variation in ideology, organization, strategy, etc. of these groups? How important is religion for these movements? Does âinclusionâ in the political process lead to âmoderationâ? How have different regimes (at different times) dealt with these movements (e.g., regime-opposition dynamics)? How have these dynamics affected the development and trajectory of Islamist movements in different parts of the Middle East? Are these movements on the ascendancy or on the decline? These are only some of the many questions we hope to explore during the course of this seminar. The course will be conducted as a seminar and âresearch workshop.â The seminar will be based on the intense discussion of selected readings which all participants will complete before class meetings. Moreover, all seminar participants will be engaged in their own research projects throughout the course of the semester. At different point in the semester, participants will (informally) present their ongoing research projects, in addition to being responsible for leading class discussion at least once during the term. Your ongoing research project, which will form the basis of your final paper, need not be limited to the countries covered in the seminar. You will also be encouraged to conduct primary research (in addition, of course, to the use of secondary sources), including analyzing the documents, web sites, electoral platforms, newspapers and other materials produced by Islamist political movements and parties.

title: Medicine, Disease, and History in Asia
description: Utilizing a case study approach focused on societies in East and South Asia, this seminar provides a critical historical perspective on many contemporary issues in global health. Global health challenges now clearly transcend national borders: in the age of the jumbo-jet, infectious and vector-borne diseases can spread easily from one country to another. Certain health problems such as smoking and obesity are of such magnitude that they have global impact. Food and drug safety are of increasing concern in our interconnected world of globalized trade. At the same time, previously distinct medical traditions and practices such as Ayurveda and Chinese acupuncture now circulate globally even as biomedicine prevails as the dominant form of health care around the world. These and many other issues of global health are deeply rooted in the past. Historical forces and events, from colonialism to the global spread of HIV/AIDS, have shaped the current state of global health. Understanding the history of global health in local Asian contexts can thus help us to respond to the health challenges of today. Through focused case studies drawn primarily from the historical experiences of China and India since 1800, we will examine how social, economic, and political forcesâincreasingly global in natureâhave influenced medical processes and disease outcomes in Asia. We will also investigate how developments in Asia have affected global health since the nineteenth century. Topics include strategies of public health in colonial and post-colonial contexts, changing ideas about contamination, hygiene, sanitation, and contagion, specific disease outbreaks, the individual and collective experience of illness due to consumer culture and changing life-styles, the emergence of international health institutions, the inequitable burden of disease, the tensions of bio-security, and the persistent presence and global diffusion of Asian healing cultures. This course is open to all graduate students including those interested in Asian Studies; International Affairs; International Development; Regional and Comparative Studies; Global Disease; International Health; and Global, International, and Comparative History. Advanced undergraduates may enroll only with permission of the instructor.

title: Political Institutions
description: The âcoreâ of political science consists in Comparative Politics and Political Institutions (as identified, for instance, by the editor of the encompassing /Oxford Handbook of Political Science/ (2009) at observing âthe state of the disciplineâ). This course will provide theoretical and comparative analyses of political institutions in democratic regimes. Political institutions are conceived as âthe rules of the gameâ; basically, they establish the domains of public activity and the procedures to select leaders, which induce political actors to develop diverse strategies producing different outcomes. We will examine the consequences of the different institutional formulas for policy-making, governmental performance and political stability. Major topics include the foundations of political communities, the diffusion of democracy in the world, the role of political parties in elections and collective decision-making, the advantages and disadvantages of two-party and multiparty systems, and the different forms of division of powers between the executive and the legislative. We will also address the evolution, change and reforms of democratic institutions in the world in a long-term perspective.

title: Globalization: Challenges for Developed Countries
description: This course develops the theoretical and practical tools needed to participate in the design of public policy, conduct business-government relations, and formulate some aspects of corporate strategy in the industrial economies of Europe, Asia and North America. The course focuses on policies to deal with the dilemmas of globalization, and to strengthen the competitiveness of firms, workers, and nations; strategic trade theory and contemporary trade policy; forward pricing, fair pricing, and dumping; outward investment and the A Great Sucking Sound; inward investment and the Who-Is-Us? Debate; critical technology development; foreign acquisitions and national security; and policies to enhance the returns to workers and slow the growing disparity in income. While concentrating on contemporary problems of public policy, business-government relations, and corporate strategy in the United States, the course contrasts dilemmas in North America with the corresponding experience in Europe, Japan, and to a certain extent China. (This course replaces Political Economy of Competitiveness, and cannot be taken by any student who has enrolled in that prior course.)

title: Balkan Instability: Intl Peacebuilding
description: Serving for centuries as a volatile boundary between east and west, the Balkans region of Southeast Europe sparked a world war at the beginning of the 20th The collapse of Yugoslavia in the 1990s drew global powers into an unprecedented stabilization effort, involving some of the most dramatic successes and tragic failures of the international community. Issues such as Bosnia and Herzegovinaâs continued instability, Kosovoâs recent independence, and Russiaâs strategic interest in the region continue to challenge policymakers and world leaders today. century and introduced the world to the term âethnic cleansingâ near the end. Taught by two Foreign Service officers with 12 years combined experience in the region, most recently at the U.S. Embassy in Croatia, this course will review the contemporary history of this complex area and examine the foreign policy tools the U.S. and international community have employed in the struggle for stability. Readings and discussion will consider both how governments and multilateral organizations such as the United Nations and the European Union have influenced developments in this volatile region and how events there have shaped diplomatic approaches to nations in turmoil. Exploring the countries that emerged from the former Yugoslavia, we will investigate the relative success and failure of the diplomatic, economic and military initiatives we have used to address conflict and post-conflict issues. Classes will highlight EU and NATO integration as a driving force behind these stabilization efforts while also exposing the unique challenges of multilateral diplomacy. Students will benefit from the perspective of practitioners and their experience implementing policies and programs in the Balkan region. With written assignments and classroom simulations that give students a window on the life of an American diplomat in the field, this course will provide insight into U.S. foreign policy formulation and identify challenges that remain ahead.

title: International Political Risk Analysis
description: This course has three major purposes: first, to introduce the student to the political assessment and management needs of private firms; second, to allow the student to become familiar with a range of conceptual approaches and methods which are used by corporate political analysts to assess political factors and their impact on the firm; and third, to expose the student to the full range of political risk management techniques.

title: Games & Strategies in Politics
description: In the last few decades game theory has become one of the most powerful analytical tools in the study of politics. In this approach, human interactions are analyzed in situations in which individual decisions depend on expectations as to what others will do. Game theoretical models have been applied to the analysis of international relations, collective action, leadership, voting and elections, negotiations and conflict settlements, coalition formation and other relevant political dilemmas. This course will: 1) provide a set of tools of the theory of games of strategy with analytical value, 2) develop some relevant applications in the field of political science and international relations, 3) introduce a number of normative solutions to conflict, competition and negotiation problems. Numerous examples, exercises and applications of the models to the analysis of relevant problems will be presented, required and promoted.

title: Debate EU Access
description: This course studies and debates Turkeyâs EU accession process in the present. In this regard, the course analyzes both the European and Turkish debates on Turkeyâs EU accession. Students will also learn about U.S. and Russian views on Turkeyâs membership, and discuss the perspectives of Muslim countries. The course aims to finds answers to the following questions: Is there room for Turkey in Europe? Is Turkey European? Is the EU ready for Turkey?

title: Economy of Energy and Oil
description: This course studies the links between the economy and politics of the major oil producers/exporters with a special focus on the main exporters of the Arab-Persian Gulf: Iraq, Iran, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, UAE, Qatar. Each of these states is attempting to diversify away for straight production of crude oil and expand in value adding energy-based products like petrochemicals. This transition will be reviewed in some detail, especially the internal and external political consequences of this evolution.

title: Makers of Modern Asia
description: This course will examine the role of national leaders in East Asia. The semester is divided into three sections.
