The course learning and assessment tools will be allocated as follows: Class participation (20%); Semester Writing Assignment (30%) Mid-Term Exam - Problem Set (20%) Final Examination (30%)

title: How HUD Works
description: This course offers the perspective of a very senior government official upon the operations of the United States Department of Housing and Urban Development, an agency with vast impact upon the housing market and the wider real estate economy. The impact of the mortgage financial crisis upon HUD naturally is singled out for special attention, including the powers of the Federal Housing Administration (FHA) to issue foreclosure moratoriums, as well as the operations of the Government National Mortgage Administration (GNMA). The course describes and analyzes programs like the Community Development Block Grant Program (CDBG), HOPE VI, and the Gulf Coast Disaster Response Program. The course also discusses HUDâs role in enforcing real estate consumer protection statutes such as the Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act (RESPA) and the Interstate Land Sales Act. Finally, the course gives special attention to the management systems integration issues of real estate organizations and government agencies.

title: Community Real Estate Development and Finance
description: MPRE 603-01: Community Real Estate Development and Finance Joseph Firschein and Jonathan Kivell This course will explore developing and financing real estate in low- and moderate-income communities from a practitionerâs perspective. The course will be taught by two adjunct professors, a senior staff member in the Division of Consumer and Community Affairs at the Federal Reserve Board of Governors and a member of the Community Development Finance staff of United Bank in DC. The main focus of the class will be to understand the roles of the public and private sectors in the development and financing of affordable housing, charter schools, community facilities, and other community development activities. The class will give students practical experience in how to develop and finance real estate projects in low- and moderate-income communities from the perspective of lenders, developers, and investors. It will also describe the work of Community Development Financial Institutions (CDFIs) around the country, with a focus on the DC metropolitan area. In addition, the course will give students an understanding of how to use tax-advantaged investing (via programs such as the Low Income Housing Tax Credit and the New Markets Tax Credit) to support community development real estate projects. Although the course includes a discussion of financial analysis and underwriting techniques used by public and private sector practitioners to structure and implement community development projects, no previous community development finance or public policy experience is required.

title: Foundations of RE Finance
description: Foundations of Real Estate Finance provides a graduate level introduction to real estate finance. The class will focus on financial metrics commonly used to value real estate transactions and proforma financial statement development. Topics include cap rates, discounted cash flows, pro forma analysis, IRR and strategies. This is an introductory level graduate finance class. The objective of this course is to provide you with a sound understanding of the current finance concepts and finance elements. You will be able financially analyze a real estate property transaction with an understanding of financial elements and metrics by the end of the semester. This class will also be enhanced by a number of practical experiences and examples.

title: Foundations of Real Estate Finance
description: This course provides you with a critical foundation in real estate finance. Mastering the basic and advanced concepts of real estate finance is essential to your understanding of other courses in the MPS curriculum, and to your success in the real estate industry. Topics covered will include: the basics of the time value of money and discounted cash flow analysis, commercial lease structures, property-level pro forma analysis, financial modeling, capitalization rates, the use of debt and mortgages, bankruptcy basics and CMBS basics, among others. The primary text is Real Estate Finance & Investments: Risks and Opportunities by Peter Linneman; additional readings will supplement the book. Students will perform individual and group case study assignments, and guest speakers will complement traditional lectures.

title: Foundations of RE Finance
description: This course provides you with a critical foundation in real estate finance. Mastering the basic and advanced concepts of real estate finance is essential to your understanding of other courses in the MPS curriculum, and to your success in the real estate industry. Topics covered will include: the basics of the time value of money and discounted cash flow analysis, commercial lease structures, property-level pro forma analysis, financial modeling, capitalization rates, the use of debt and mortgages, bankruptcy basics and CMBS basics, among others. The primary text is Real Estate Finance & Investments: Risks and Opportunities by Peter Linneman; additional readings will supplement the book. Students will perform individual and group case study assignments, and guest speakers will complement traditional lectures.

title: Acquisitions and Dispositions
description: The Office Acquisitions & Dispositions course, MPRE-630, presents a high-level survey of transactions engaged in by commercial real estate professionals with a particular focus on the office sector. The course primarily focuses on the acquisition and disposition process as well as discussions regarding the debt and equity markets for real estate investing today. The course begins with an in-depth look at the capital markets and a historical perspective on the commercial real estate industry. A thorough discussion of the acquisition and disposition process, along with the corresponding analytics involved will follow. A class project involves the underwriting and valuation of real properties from the buyerâs and sellerâs perspective. The course then considers lending transactions in some depth. It devotes special attention to the basic mortgage loan transaction between the borrower and lender and the equity return requirements and structures for investors. A panel discussion featuring top real estate executives representing real estate investment vehicles including REITs, Pension Funds, Opportunity Funds and Private Investors gives the class real time feedback and perspectives on the transactional market place. Office Acquisitions and Dispositions builds upon Foundations of Real Estate Finance and Foundations of Real Estate Accounting. In addition to building upon these Foundational courses, aspects of the course are related to such other MPS/RE courses as Structured Finance and Real Estate Software. Proficiency with Argus software and access to CoStar a plus.

title: Foundations of RE Markets
description: This course will focus on how real estate practitioners analyze information to make the informed market analysis decisions that drive what they do. The class will cover a broad range of real estate disciplines from single-family and multifamily residential, retail, office, hospitality, and industrial and introduce the perspectives of developers/builders, investors, lenders, landlords, tenants, planners, public officials, and end users/consumers. The class will offer a mix of theory, analytic tools, and practical experiences/real-life examples. Student Work: The goal is a highly interactive class environment in which the students play an essential role in the education process. Active participation in class discussions, including interaction with guest speakers and reference to reading, is expected of every student and a key component of participant performance. Readings will be assigned in advance of class sessions The instructor will incorporate assignments into the learning experience as we go along and will give them as appropriate. The mid-term will consist of a take-home examination. The final case study project will consist of a case study report and presentation that will build upon the work completed throughout the semester; the final presentation and report will be due on the final exam date (not the last day of class). Working in a team environment is a critical aspect of future success in the real estate environment. Midway through the semester the class will be broken into smaller groups of 4-6 participants, depending on class size, which will act as your final exam teams. Project teams will be expected to work with their project team outside of class time.

title: Foundations of RE Accounting
description: Foundations of Real Estate Accounting provides a graduate level introduction to real estate accounting, taxation concepts and equity incentives utilizing various tax credit and incentive programs. Topics include GAAP financial statements for real estate companies, financial analysis, fair value and impairment analysis, consolidation, joint venture accounting, Low Income Housing Tax Credits, Historic Tax Credits, New Markets Tax Credits, energy tax credits, internal controls for real estate companies and understanding operating and partnership agreements. Taxation topics include partnership tax concepts and tax free exchanges. The objective of this course is to provide you with an understanding of the current financial accounting and taxation issues likely to be encountered in the real estate sector. You will be able to analyze GAAP prepared accounting statements and identify potential tax issues. At the end of the course, students should: Understand the principles and rules of real estate accounting Be familiar with the accounting standards and pronouncements related to real estate accounting Understand the principles for the capitalization of preacquisition costs Be familiar with the costs incurred to sell or rent a real estate project and the accounting rules associated with those costs Be familiar with the valuation process of real estate and how to account for acquisitions of real estate Understand the principles of non-monetary exchanges of real estate, particularly the basic principles of section 1031: like-kind exchanges Understand the process underlying a sale of real estate and the different accounting methods associated with profit recognition Be familiar with other applicable methods for recording a sale of real estate Understand the principles of a lease and be able to identify the key characteristics of and differences between a capital lease and an operating lease Understand the accounting methods used by both the lessee and lessor Understand the differences between the four methods of accounting for interests in real estate ventures: consolidation, equity method of accounting, pro rata consolidation, and the cost method Understand what a variable and non-variable interest entity are and how to account for them Be familiar with time-sharing transactions and how to account for them Be familiar with the special accounting rules related to retail land sales.

title: Foundations of RE Accounting
description: Foundations of Real Estate Accounting provides a graduate level introduction to real estate accounting, taxation concepts and equity incentives utilizing various tax credit and incentive programs. Topics include GAAP financial statements for real estate companies, financial analysis, fair value and impairment analysis, consolidation, joint venture accounting, Low Income Housing Tax Credits, Historic Tax Credits, New Markets Tax Credits, energy tax credits, internal controls for real estate companies and understanding operating and partnership agreements. Taxation topics include partnership tax concepts and tax free exchanges. The objective of this course is to provide you with an understanding of the current financial accounting and taxation issues likely to be encountered in the real estate sector. You will be able to analyze GAAP prepared accounting statements and identify potential tax issues. At the end of the course, students should: Understand the principles and rules of real estate accounting Be familiar with the accounting standards and pronouncements related to real estate accounting Understand the principles for the capitalization of preacquisition costs Be familiar with the costs incurred to sell or rent a real estate project and the accounting rules associated with those costs Be familiar with the valuation process of real estate and how to account for acquisitions of real estate Understand the principles of non-monetary exchanges of real estate, particularly the basic principles of section 1031: like-kind exchanges Understand the process underlying a sale of real estate and the different accounting methods associated with profit recognition Be familiar with other applicable methods for recording a sale of real estate Understand the principles of a lease and be able to identify the key characteristics of and differences between a capital lease and an operating lease Understand the accounting methods used by both the lessee and lessor Understand the differences between the four methods of accounting for interests in real estate ventures: consolidation, equity method of accounting, pro rata consolidation, and the cost method Understand what a variable and non-variable interest entity are and how to account for them Be familiar with time-sharing transactions and how to account for them Be familiar with the special accounting rules related to retail land sales Required Reading Accounting for Real Estate Transactions: A Guide for Public Accountants and Corporate Financial Professionals by Maria K. Davis ISBN 978-0-470-19852-0 published by Wiley

title: Real Estate Development: Art and Science
description: A successful, real estate developer, running a sustainable development company, is neither a pure analytical number cruncher or a force of personality âcowboyâ entrepreneur. Technical and analytical skills allow âinformed intuitionâ in the value creation process. The course will analyze revenue generation and the impact of fixed and variable cost on profitability. This course, as an introduction to the fundamentals of real estate development, will explore the art and the science of the process of value creation in real estate. The course will utilize both case studies and guest panels. Building upon Foundations of Real Estate Markets and Foundations of Real Estate Finance, this course will examine the information needed to generate accurate proformas at the project level, and use that information to create cash flow analysis necessary at the enterprise level to operate a profitable development company. Proforma assumptions include pursuit cost, land acquisition strategies, feasibility, market analysis, and debt and equity alternatives including construction debt, permanent debt and joint venture structures. The due diligence process will be reviewed in depth including title, land use, permits, environmental issues and site engineering as they impact the use and viability of a project. Enterprise cost analysis will look at the cash flow impact of development revenue versus the burn rate associated with fixed and variable costs of operating a real estate development company. Case studies will look specifically at the process of building an effective development team and the collaboration of development, architectural and construction professionals including acquisition and retention of talent, and the leadership skills needed to operate a development organization efficiently. Specialist panels will discuss the skills needed to anticipate and overcome problems in the development process with municipalities, lenders, contractors, and tenants, as well as litigation, arbitration, and other means of resolving development-related disputes.

title: Washington By Design
description: Optimism and an entrepreneurial spirit bred by economic expansion and the exuberance of a country coming into its own as a world power marked Washington at the beginning of the 20th century. The remaking of Washington occurred in the context of a particular set of social and cultural preoccupations prevalent in the years leading up to and following the turn of the century. Unlike today, in the past architects were at the forefront of the cityâs urban form and development. The precise manner in which the ânew United Statesâ was to be realized in Washington over the 100 years of the 20th century is a story of the coalescence of private and public vision, of architecture and real estate, of ideologies and practices expressed through building designs (and rhetoric about them) coupled with means for achieving their construction through expert commissions, committees, and regulating bodies, through private and public will. With a view to understanding these forces, this course will highlight the role of design in the nationâs most significant symbolic city.

title: Green Building and Asset Management
description: COURSE DESCRIPTION: Green and sustainable development generally includes planning, design, site and material selection, and construction. One area which has received comparatively less attention is green building operations and asset management. The number of existing non-green buildings vastly exceeds new green building development. Given the significant negative impact buildings can on the environment, their exists a tremendous opportunity to improve the environment by implementing green and sustainable operating practices. This course examines commercial real estate operations with a focus on improving the environmental impact and performance of existing non-green buildings. Topics will include property management, asset management and commercial leasing. In addition to commercial properties, we will examine multi-family apartment management, operations, marketing and leasing. Students will learn to prepare management plans and operating budgets, commercial lease negotiations, and project marketing using green operations as a differentiator. Additional areas of focus include the specific roles of leasing agents, brokers, property managers, asset managers and tenant coordinators, along with risk management, insurance, green vendor selection, service contracts and green purchasing policies. LEARNING OBJECTIVES: After successful completion of this course, students will be able to fully discuss green and sustainable asset management. This includes how to write and use a management plan and prepare a property level operating budget. Students will be able to clearly describe the roles the following positions: property manager, leasing agent, real estate broker, tenant coordinator and asset manager. Students will be able to discuss contracts, risk management, insurance, and discuss how to effectively work with third party vendors. Students will also be able to negotiate a commercial lease letter of intent, broker commission agreement, vendor contract and property management agreement. Students will also be able to discuss how to use green and sustainable practices as a differentiator in marketing property, in addition to green leasing techniques.

title: Green Buildings
description: The issue of sustainability is surrounded by controversy and uncertainty, but offers the opportunity for an awakening, a chance to improve our environment, built or otherwise. Buildings represent 39% of the of energy consumption, 18% of greenhouse gas emissions, 71% of electricity consumption. This course covers the breadth of sustainability, from a real estate design associated perspective. This course will discuss sustainability, its past and future, the jurisdictional issues associated with building design, systems used in measurement, cost/benefits, process, and major issues as impediments/opportunities to creative future sustainable development.

title: Hotels and Resorts
description: Hotels and Resorts provides a graduate level introduction to hotel and resort development, financing and operation. The class will focus on the overall development process from the initial planning stages, to design, construction, legal issues, financing and operational considerations as well as an introduction to some of the various players in the hospitality industry. This class will also be enhanced by a number of practical experiences and examples. We will also have a number of guest lecturers from the industry to provide differing views and perspectives. Required Material: Walter A. Rutes, FAIA; Richard A. Penner; Lawrence Adams, Hotel Design: Planning and Development, Norton, 2001, ISBN 13: 978-0-393-73055-7 and ISBN 10: 0-393-73055-7 Joseph Ransley & Hayden Ingram, Developing Hospitality Properties & Facilities, Second Edition, Elsevier Butterworth Heinemann, 2004, ISBN 978-0-7506-5982-6 James J. Eyster and Jan De Roos, The Negotiation and Administration of Hotel Management Contracts, 4th ed., Pearson, 2009, ISBN 10: 0-558-07968-7 and ISBN 13: 978-0-558-07968-0 In addition, you will also be asked to read a variety of articles on current hotel and resort industry issues. These will be provided in class.

title: Hotels II:Valuations/Transactions
description: Class One - Review of overall Basics from Fall Semester and a preview of the spring curriculum including discussions of the semester project Class Two â Introduction to Asset Management Learning the fiduciary responsibilities of managing hospitality assets Reviewing management contracts from the view of an asset manager, benchmarking performance hurdles, capital improvement decisions. Class Three â Tour of Property through the eyes of an asset Manager. Possible hotels to tour would be St. Regis or W. Class Four - Detailed analysis of Hospitality financing. Blending together all of the hurdles needed to satisfy debt such as Debt Coverage, Loan to Value, Loan to Cost, For new construction (when the market returns) how do develop profomas that will coincide with appraisals of future value. Class Five â Hospitality finance continued. Detailed analysis of the capital stack. Particular emphasis on the brave new world of new IRRâs for equity and higher spreads on different stacks of debt. Possible guest speaker showing an actual case study from concept through construction. Class Six â Acquiring assets through debt. CMBS, Special Servicers, Receivers, FDIC etc. Class Seven (could be moved up to Class 2) -Valuations past and future trends. Guest Speaker Dan Lesser, head of Valuation for CBRE Hotels. Class Eight â Flags and Brands comparing and contrasting and how do you decide which is best for your property. Possible guest speaker Todd Repport of Morgans Hotels Class Nine â Resort and Golf course valuation and feasibility analysis. Class Ten â Negotiations in the Hospitality Industry, Part I Class Eleven â Negotiations in the Hospitality Industry, Part II possibly including role playing. Class Twelve â Class Presentations of Group Projects I Class Thirteen â Class Presentations of Group Projects II Please note that I am not sure if Negotiations will take two classes since there is a separate negotiations class. We might just keep this as one class and add another topic. Class reading materials will be a combination of current readings of articles and papers along with text book/s which I would be open to suggestions that would follow the texts of the fall semester class.

title: Leasing
description: Course Description: This course will give students a broad overview of leases and their importance to commercial real estate. We will discuss the various roles that leases play in commercial real estate, and the differences and similarities between different types of leases. Students will learn to parse out the true and complete economic terms of a lease transaction in a manner that permits meaningful comparisons. We will review the various elements of commercial leases, placing particular emphasis on construction, pass-through of expenses, flexibility and exit strategies, options, and the effect of the current economic situation. Throughout, we will discuss the roles and motivations of the various players, and how competing interests reach agreement. Finally, through one or more guest speakers, we will hear how leases are interpreted in the real world on a day-to-day basis. Course Objectives: â¢ Provide an understanding of leases as the economic underpinning of commercial real estate â¢ Impart a working knowledge of a broad array of lease types â¢ Teach students to analyze the full economic impact of a lease in a manner that facilitates comparisons across leases â¢ Give students a full working knowledge of the key components of commercial leases and how they fit together â¢ Explore the motivations, similar and dissimilar, of the parties (landlord, tenant, lender) and the usual compromise positions â¢ Students will leave the course with the ability to read a commercial lease and understand and extract its key terms, and with an understanding of how those terms are put into practice Course Requirements: 1. Participation: Students are expected to attend class for all sessions, having read the required material and done the assigned work. Students are expected to participate in class discussions and exercises. 2. Economic Analysis of Competing Leases: Students will be provided with two competing lease proposals and will be required to complete a memo analyzing the economic terms of the two proposals, making a recommendation to a hypothetical client as to which proposal to select, and justifying that recommendation. This will be an individual assignment. 3. Negotiation of Letter of Intent: Students will be divided into small groups, representing tenant or landlord. The tenant group will be given a lease proposal from landlordâs broker, together with certain background information, and will be responsible for crafting a response indicating which items are acceptable and making modifications to those that are not. Similarly, the landlord group will be given a lease proposal from tenantâs broker, together with certain background information, and will be responsible for crafting a response indicating which items are acceptable and making modifications to those that are not. This will be a group project, and class time will be spent discussing the results reached by the groups and their reasons for reaching them. 4. Lease Abstract: Students will be given a lease and will be required to abstract its key terms. This will be an individual assignment. Class time will be spent reviewing the answers to ensure that students have full comprehension of the relevant lease terms and how they interact with each other.

title: Real Estate Investment Trusts
description: From the standpoint of a REIT economist and topmost trade association leader, the Real Estate Investment Trusts course presents a comprehensive survey of this crucial area of real estate investment. The course describes the origins of REITs in the 1960s, their behavior over the business cycles since that time, and their performance in comparison with common stocks, bonds, and other real estate investments. It emphasizes that REITs are the easiest way for individuals to own commercial real estate, that they offer the liquidity of being publicly traded, and that their relatively low correlation with other assets makes them advantageous for diversification purposes. It addresses distinctive aspects of REITs that invest in specific sectors of the real estate industry: office REITs like Boston Properties, industrial REITâs like Pro-Logis, retail REITs like Kimco and Simon, apartment REITs like Equity Residential, diversified REITs like Vornado, hospitality REITs like Host, medical REITs like Health Care Property Investors, and mortgage REITS like Friedman, Billings, Ramsey. It explains the differences between equity REITs, mortgage REITS, and hybrid REITs, between public and private REITs, between UPREITs and DownREITs, and between lending REITs and ownership REITs. The course discusses and analyzes Marylandâs favorable REIT law, which leads many REITs to be incorporated in Maryland and often located there, and which makes REITs disproportionately important in Greater Washington. In addition, it discusses the 1999 REIT Modernization Act. It also considers the difference between corporate profits and REIT funds from operation and the tax treatment of REITs, including the favorable impact of the 1986 Tax Reform Act. The course invites frequent guest speakers from such major area REITs as Avalon Bay, Host Hotels & Resorts, Federal Realty Investment Trust, Washington Realty Investment Trust, Saul Centers, LaSalle Hotel Properties, Diamond Rock Hospitality Company, First Potomac Realty Trust, American Capital Agency Corp., JER Investors Trust Inc., and Gladstone Commercial Corp.

title: Leadership and Organizational Management
description: The primary purpose of this course is to learn and observe characteristics of great leaders in real estate, leadership styles, and how to effectively manage organizations. In addition, you will learn about different organization structures and organizational management theories including their applications, risks and effectiveness, plus and more. The course will include a blend lecture, case study review, class discussion, outside speakers and project assignments.

title: Public Sector Real Estate
description: Since the issuance of Executive Order 13327on Real Property Asset Management, the executive branches of two Presidential administrations have been seeking innovative means of financing and recapitalizing the transformation of Federal governmentâs aging infrastructure and assets. The Nationâs largest landlord has a growing $1+ Trillion federal deficit constricting public Sustainment Restoration & Modernization funding for public buildings. Federal departments, including the DoD, will need to rely on more innovative Public Private Partnerships (P3) to make up the funding gap or face more drastic disposition measures. This course is designed to teach practical approaches to Federal Public Sector Real Estate. It will begin with providing distinctions between public sector and private sector perspectives and paradigms of Asset and Portfolio Management both in theory and in practice. It will provide an understanding of how the current public sector realty crisis came to be and continues to escalate. Private sector speakers and public sector real property officers will provide an array of case studies demonstrating the best and worst program/project cases. The myriad of methodologies, obstacles, and opportunities will be discussed to provide a lessons-learned approach in an effort to discover new prototypical means of creating successful public projects in challenging economic times. Specific detail will be dedicated to the impacts of Executive Order 13327 over the past decade and the more recent Presidential Memorandum on June 10, 2010 imploring federal agencies to step up their efforts. The class will also cover the current FASB debate on whether to distinguish short and long-term realty leases into capital and operating categories.

title: RE Entrepreneurship
description: Real Estate Entrepreneurship is a course designed to explain and explore entrepreneurship in the context of the business of real estate. Utilizing the dynamic Washington, DC market as a backdrop, the course will use case studies, share experiences from accomplished guest speakers, and draw from the instructorsâ first-hand understanding to immerse students in the when, where, how, and why of undertaking entrepreneurial endeavors. It discusses the theory and practice of entrepreneurship generally, the motives of entrepreneurs as compared with other businesspeople, and the specific application of entrepreneurship to the real estate sector. The coursework will focus on the mechanics of putting together deals, creating real estate businesses, the constant need to âsellâ as an entrepreneur, and paths to becoming an entrepreneur, with emphasis on the constant balancing act required in a start-up operation. At the conclusion of the course, students will have enhanced insight and skills to undertake an entrepreneurial real estate venture either now or at some future point in their careers.

title: Taxation
description: COURSE OBJECTIVES The goal of the course is to provide an introduction to the fundamentals of federal income tax rules as they relate to real estate transactions and operations. We will refer to the Internal Revenue Code, Regulations, Revenue Rulings, Private Letter Rulings and Court Cases, all of which comprise the body of law that establishes, explains and interprets federal income tax rules. The textbook and other reading material will provide a good background for each weekâs discussions. Homework assignments will be drawn from the examples in the text so that students work through applying real estate tax rules to determine the after tax result of transactions. Discussion of the assignments in class will expand on practical tax planning possibilities. By the end of the course, students will gain a basic understanding of real estate tax including: â¢ How to evaluate and choose the type of entity to own real estate â¢ What expenditures are deductible and what must be capitalized â¢ When are losses incurred in the ownership of real estate deductible â¢ What is so special about qualified nonrecourse mortgage indebtedness â¢ Identify when and how the gain on the disposition of real estate can be deferred â¢ The difference between a passive activity (being a couch potato?) and a non-passive activity Evaluation and Grading â¢ Assessments during the semester (homework, quizzes and presentations) (60%) â¢ Classroom participation (15%) â¢ Final Exam (25%)

title: Distressed Properties and Real Estate Workouts
description: Distressed Real Estate and Workout Strategies Real estate companies, banks and investment firms face strategic challenges during rapidly changing economic cycles. This course will focus on strategies in analyzing markets, repositioning troubled assets/debt, cost control techniques and managing internal change and conflict during uncertain times. The course will incorporate lectures, class discussion, case studies and guest lecturers. The guest lecturers will include; real estate transaction workout/bankruptcy attorneyâs, bankers, finance executives, real estate developers, market analysts and strategic planners. Topics include: Â· Evaluating financing sources and structures -- Monetary and non-monetary loan default resolution --Developing an asset or portfolio plan --Purchasing and selling real estate in a credit constrained market --Bankruptcy and consensual workouts --Developing and implementing workout plans and alternative strategies -- Evaluating your overall position within the market -- Strategic planning in a rapidly changing economic environment

title: Real Estate Private Equity
description: The courseâs objective is to introduce the world of private real estate investment to the student by reviewing the role of private real estate in a portfolio as well as reviewing various strategies for gaining exposure. We will look at the alternatives from the perspective of both a portfolio manager/investor as well as a fund operator. At completion, the student should be conversant in different types of offerings, risks and rewards, and how they may fit together as part of an overall real estate investment strategy. First, we will examine the rationale for private versus public real estate investment in the context of an overall real estate portfolio strategy. Once the theoretical justification is made, we will examine various strategies for gaining private real estate exposure, including reviewing private funds, fund of funds, property and/or geographic specific funds as well as private REITâs. As part of this course, we will examine different fund structures, review fund governance, how the different vehicles are marketed as well as tax issues affecting each of the different investment structures. Furthermore, we will review best in practice due diligence procedures on the various offerings. Then we will review the implementation of the investment strategy including a review of the investment process. Given the current state of the markets, we will examine the investment process in the context of evaluating distressed property opportunities. In addition, we will cover negotiating joint ventures, execution of venture business plans, and troubleshooting when plans go awry. Course Outline Private v Public real estate Rationale Arbitrage Private equity structures Private Funds/Private (unlisted)REITâs Fund of funds Direct Investment Sponsor funds (property or geography specific) Fund Strategies and Risk/Return Profiles Core, Core-plus, Value-Add, Opportunistic Niche strategies â Property/Geographic specific, Tax liens, LP liquidity, Fund structures â Commingled, Separate accounts, Private REITs, Fund governance â control rights, limitations on the general partner, administration and compensation Real Estate Tax considerations â UBIT, offshore investors, Firpta, 1031, fractions rules, leverage Marketing Private Equity funds â SEC Compliance, consultants Fund level due diligence Investment process â focus on current distressed environment including bidding on distressed debt, loan to own strategies, and gap equity, back of the napkin analyses, detailed investment model, internal discussions, investment committee process, and closing/asset management. Additionally, will review negotiating joint ventures, execution of business plans, and troubleshooting when things go wrong.

title: Investment Banking
description: TBD

title: Corporate Real Estate
description: Real Estate enthusiasm and education often default to the supply side. When focus shifts to the demand side it is usually from a macro or aggregated sub-market perspective. This course concentrates on the demand side in granular detail, but with the dynamics of a dual viewpoint: A. The Corporate Real Estate Professional (CRE) and his/her objectives and the constraints. B. And the Owner, Developer, Investor who negotiates with the CRE to develop or lease his project and must understand these issues for successful design, marketing and deal making. Corporate Real Estate is one of the top two or three cost centers in all major corporations, but it is also the source of stabilized net operating income for any successful commercial property, and ultimately, the basis of all capitalized value. Hence the dual viewpoints. But corporate real estate is not always well managed strategically; matching operational planning with market opportunities. This presents an opportunity to expand upon current practices within the corporation or, conversely, for developers to leverage these inefficiencies to their benefit. A broader understanding of the CRE function, essentially the in-house developer, should also open up a career avenue with an alternate risk/return profile. Coursework will include case study and group project work, with an emphasis on interactive participation. We will consider a broad range of property types and transaction mechanisms both in the US and abroad, with an underlying theme of design, location (urban design) and configuration. â¢ Site Selection and Competition at different scales and across different markets â¢ Headcount driven Government Incentives and the Public/Private Partnership â¢ Structured Finance and alternative delivery mechanisms â¢ Headquarters Campus development drivers & constraints â¢ Build-to-Suite, Sale/Leaseback and Development Partnerships â¢ Alternative Workplace Design and the impact on demand and configuration â¢ Corporate Environmental demands and the Green Lease â¢ Corporate Agility, product & business cycle impact of lease term and flexibility â¢ Portfolio Management and Decision Metrics, including M&A activity â¢ Disposition, Consolidation, Reorganization and Lease Restructuring

title: Real Estate Economics
description: Course Description This course applies economic analysis to the study of urban real estate markets. The goal of the course is for students to understand and apply economic principles to the analysis of real estate. The course provides an overview of real estate in the U.S. relative to other asset categories, and examines unique aspects of both residential and nonresidential property in urban markets. Among topics covered include growth of the urban core, determination of rent and vacancy, loan-product choice, property price indexes, and economics of the real estate cycle. Previous coursework in economics is expected, preferably through intermediate economics, and familiarity with basic terms and concepts in economics. Calculus is not required, but a good understanding of algebra and comfort in interpreting graphs is important. Format is lecture with occasional guest speaker. Texts Urban Economics and Real Estate Markets by Denise DiPasquale and William C. Wheaton (Prentice-Hall); A Primer on U.S. Housing Markets and Housing Policy by Richard K. Green and Stephen Malpezzi (Urban Institute Press).

title: Urban Economics
description: This course examines macro economic, social, and political forces that shape the built environment and real estate markets. It provides the framework for understanding more detailed, project specific market analysis and feasibility analysis, which while discussed in class, are not its primary focus. The course explores: Economic rationales for cities and metropolitan areas. Economic and social processes that spawn land use patterns. Economic development concepts, growth management, and planning issues. The use of data collection methods and trend analysis. Present characteristics and future prospects for real estate development. The course is structured around active class participation, drawing upon student day-to-day experiences, preparation of two written papers, and a final exam. Standard textbook and periodic readings are required, supported by the instructorâs 30 years of experience in real estate research and consulting practice.

title: Structured Finance
description: The Structured Finance Course examines how large commercial real estate financings are put together. The course will explore the evolution of the real estate finance transaction. Who the players were and who they are now. How have structures changed with the influence of Wall Street as a major player having entered the market in the early 90âs and the issues they faced during the credit crisis of late 2007. We will examine the options available and the problems currently facing both Borrowers and Lenders. The course will give a practical overview of how we finance the entire capital structure from senior loans to mezzanine tranches to equity. Guest lecturers, who are major forces in the business, will tell you their stories and impart their wisdom in this challenging time for structured finance. Upon completion of this course, students should have a full grasp of how deals are financed. As a result, the student should be an asset to their employer in this field. The course will include some lecturing, discussion and a significant amount of team projects designed to teach the student the anatomy of âthe deal.â Class participation (20%), significant team projects including the creation of a finance memorandum, as well as specific research assignments to help train the real estate mind (30%), how the professor feels you grasp the knowledge (20%), and a final examination (30%) will serve as the learning and assessment tools.

title: Real Estate Statistics and Modeling
description: Course Description: Develop a firm foundation of statistical analysis techniques to include linear and multiple regression analysis. Students will be able to construct computer models to predict and understand relationships between variables with specific emphasis on real estate. Students will understand two methods for modeling and forecasting, stochastic and multivariate regression. The strengths and weaknesses of statistics will be understood along with the ability to recognize pitfalls in existing statistical models. Most importantly, students will be able to discuss and understand statistics in greater depth than would result from an introductory course and how to best use statistics in real estate forecasting. In addition, multiple types of pro forma models will be constructed. Students will construct a multifamily model and add functionality to the model throughout the semester. At the end of the class, student will have a scalable multi-family model with stochastic modeling incorporated and a waterfall distribution of equity returns on the back-end. Further, students will be exposed to portfolio management and understand the difference in project and portfolio variances as well as how to construct a simple 4-asset portfolio model. Prerequisites: None! General knowledge of basic mathematics, algebra, finance, and MSExcel. Objective: Gain a very solid understanding of the statistics and how they are used in real estate analyses. An understanding of distribution types and confidence intervals and how they are used and must be used in all fundamental analyses. Students will have a solid understanding of how to construct forecasting models including multivariate regression models and medium-complexity pro formas with stochastic modeling, i.e. monte carlo analysis, to analyze future risk expectations.

title: Bankruptcy
description: This course is a comprehensive examination of the impact of federal bankruptcy and state insolvency laws on the real estate industry. The course begins with a brief study of various state creditors' rights laws that relate to or are incorporated into the United States Bankruptcy Code. The course continues with a review of the constitutional basis, historical origins and statutory framework of federal bankruptcy law. The primary focus of the course is on the bankruptcy issues that affect real estate properties and projects when a party to a real estate transaction becomes a debtor in a chapter 11 bankruptcy case, including a landlord, a tenant, a ground lessor or lessee, a mortgagor and a mortgagee. In particular, the course will emphasize the effect of a bankruptcy filing by a borrower on the remedies available to a mortgage lender. Topics covered by the course include: Â· Initiation of a bankruptcy case Â· The bankruptcy automatic stay Â· Property of the bankruptcy estate Â· Adequate protection of a mortgagee's interests Â· Use of cash collateral Â· Single asset real estate case considerations Â· Treatment of unexpired nonresidential real property leases Â· Voidable transactions, including fraudulent conveyances and preferences Â· Content of a plan of reorganization and the plan confirmation process Â· Cramdown Â· Substantive consolidation

title: Construction Management
description: The core business of real estate development is the design, construction and delivery of buildings and improvements to real property. This course presents the fundamentals of (1) project delivery methods, (2) preconstruction services and contractor procurement, (3) field and project management processes, (4) construction materials, equipment and systems, (5) design and construction contracts, (6) technology tools, sustainability and trends in construction, and (7) ethical considerations.

title: Transit-Oriented Development
description: Transit Oriented Development (TOD) â Gain insiderâs knowledge on this emerging concept, which is part real estate product and part public policy. Learn about the theory and practice of TOD, one of the few types of development that has strong support in the environmental community, and responds to an emerging demographic market. Students will develop a thorough understanding of the essentials needed by transit agencies and local officials, the principles for developing successful projects, and the basics of transit, traffic, and parking issues. A range of guest lecturers will add unique perspectives of practitioners - transit officials, environmentalists, planners, and developers, as well as in depth knowledge of market demand, niche housing, and financing. Key distinctions between urban and suburban projects will be clarified. Examples of some of the better projects, and some of the lesson learned, will be drawn from US and Washington area developments.

title: Green/Sustainable Construction
description: In todayâs real estate environment owners, tenants, and jurisdictions are all asking how sustainability can be incorporated into new and existing buildings. Sustainable Construction and the impact on building operation is a graduate level course which builds on the foundation of the Green Building and Construction Management courses, and allows students to understand the link between design, construction and ultimately building operations. The course will present how the design impacts the construction and operation of the building as they relate to sustainable practices. The course will cover, the impact of design decisions upon construction and operations, including cost benefit analysis of design decisions. We will examine sustainable construction practice and how it impacts on owners and tenants and leases. What state and local legislation is driving sustainable building construction and operations. Ethical considerations of sustainability practice in construction and operations.

title: Construction Estimating & Procurement
description: TBD

title: Inward Investments in International Real Estate
description: The primary purpose of this course is to learn how to participate in international real estate transactions. This translates into working on investments involving foreign investors in the United States, as well getting a clear understanding of the motivations and subsequently the cultural differences encountered of US investors investing overseas, taking a close look at development, real estate brokerage, architecture and corporate users. The course will bring in speakers with experiences of working with different nationalities to relate case studies and work in their sectors. The schedule of the speakers, especially those from out of town, will determine which class they speak at and this schedule will be constantly updated. Readings preparing for the next class will follow the sessions, topic and speakers, as revised. Class participants will be given $100 million to invest in the US, and on the last day of the class present orally for no more than 12 minutes as well as providing a written report on how, why and where they invested the monies, which will represent 70% of their grade. They will be asked to also present investing $100 million in their chosen country from the US which will contribute 15% to their grade. Participants will be asked to provide an outline by March 14th, 2011 of their presentations. They will also be asked to write 500 words on their favorite course speaker. Other contributors to their grade will be their class participation and attendance.

title: Selected Regions in International RE: China
description: TBD

title: Selected Regions in International Real Estate: Central & Eastern Europe
description: Overview For more than 50 years, real estate in Central and Eastern Europe (CEE) remained cut off from private market forces. The fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989 and the formation and expansion of the European Union in the 1990s created a burst of reintegration for the economies of this region that generated tremendous demand for modern office, retail, residential, industrial and hotel facilities. Anticipating cap rate compression, developers rushed to complete projects that could be sold once these countries either joined the European Union, or simply became less exotic and more acceptable to institutional lenders and investors. Today, with a 20-year track record of development, investment, leasing and sales â through several cycles and more than one âcrisisâ â the region provides a wealth of development case studies that offer lessons for making predictions about real estate trends in CEE and other Emerging Markets across the globe. This course will start with a brief historical context and study the nuances of individual country markets and the evolution of office, retail, hotel and residential markets across the CEE region. We will look at specific deals and market trends to examine the differences and similarities between EU, Central European and Russian markets, large and small nations and dynamic verses incremental approaches. The goal will be to build a framework for the measurement and management of risks basic using development and investment criteria such as cap rates, loan rates and terms, types of investors, etc. Instructor Glenn Williamson Glenn Williamson is the owner and managing director of Amber Real Estate LLC, an international real estate advisory firm serving institutional investors and property owners in Washington DC and across Central and Eastern Europe (CEE). Williamson first experienced living in Russia in 1981 while a student at Georgetownâs School of Foreign Service. After graduation in 1983, he served as a volunteer teacher in Mexico and went on to earn an MBA at the University of Chicago in 1986. He began a career in real estate developing a medical office building to catalyze revitalization efforts in a south side Chicago neighborhood. A downturn in US real estate in the early 1990s, coupled with dynamic growth in CEE markets following the fall of the Berlin Wall, resulted in 10 years living and working overseas in Bulgaria, Russia and Poland. Williamson led the development team for Chicago-based Golub & Co. for The Atrium at Nevsky 25, St. Petersburgâs premier retail/office center, which was the first non-recourse project financing executed in Russia and won the FIABCI Prix dâExcellence in 2000. In Warsaw, he ran the CEE real estate advisory services group for PricewaterhouseCoopers and carried out engagements with major corporations and investment funds in Poland, Romania, Bulgaria and the Czech Republic. Since forming Amber in 2003, Williamson has continued to travel back and forth from the US to close hotel, office and mixed-use redevelopments, acquisitions and dispositions in Poland, Bulgaria and Hungary. Williamson is a member of the Urban Land Institute (ULI) and the DC Building Industry Association (DCBIA). Here in Washington, he acts as a real estate advisor to numerous DC government agencies including the Deputy Mayor for Planning and Economic Development, the Department of Health and the Office of Planning. Contact Information: glenn.williamson@amberreal.com, (202) 256.3922 (cell)

title: Real Estate Investments
description: Course Description: The Real Estate Investments course will prepare students for all aspects of making a leveraged income-producing real estate acquisition. Students will learn how to evaluate a projectâs viability by learning how to perform a market analysis, determine economic feasibility, structure debt, raise and structure equity, and perform tax analyses. Topics will include discounted cash flows, net present values, internal rate of return analyses, sensitivity analyses and before- and after-tax cash flow analyses. Distressed debt and equity restructures will be introduced. The class structure will include lectures, class discussion, readings, guest speakers, preparation & discussion of case studies, and a team project. Students will engage in actual, current investment and development projects. Methodology: Lectures, class discussion, readings, ARGUS, guest speakers and preparation and discussion of case studies and term project. Instructors will discuss actual investment and development projects as well as stressed debt workouts.

title: Negotiations
description: This course is designed to provide the student with an understanding of the negotiatorâs role in disputes and to enhance the studentâs understanding of negotiation for managing differences between individuals and groups. The students will study the nature of conflict, learn how to handle two and multiparty conflicts, exerting leadership where there is no leadership, and explore the impact of facilitators and mediators on the negotiating process. A substantial amount of personal learning is accomplished through participation in and critique of class negotiation exercises. The value of the exercise is often dependent on the participation of your fellow students. Therefore, students should make every effort to attend all classes.

title: Practicum: Building the DHS Headquarters
description: In a challenging, exciting, fast-paced, ever-changing environment illustrative of a typical real estate development project, but on the largest imaginable scale, the Practicum course gives students the chance to participate meaningfully in a wide range of DHS HQ activities requiring real estate expertise. There are opportunities for students to apply their specific real estate skills, to work together in teams, and for significant leadership experience. While the DHS HQ project constitutes a veritable laboratory of real estate development, perhaps the most appealing aspect of the project is its combination of both major public sector and major private sector development components. On the public sector side, the DHS HQ project enables students to support the work of the DC Office of the Deputy Mayor for Planning and Economic Development, the DC Department of Planning, the Department of Homeland Security, the General Services Administration, and other local and Federal agencies. And because the St. Elizabeths Hospital site on which the DHS HQ is being built is located in the most economically disadvantaged neighborhood in the District, the project has a significant community economic development and social welfare dimension as well. On the private sector side, students will collaborate with Chesapeake Crescent, a business group focused on economic development in the homeland security field in the Metro DC region broadly conceived, to determine the nature of the private sector spinoff development from the DHS HQ project. Proposed spinoff projects include (1) a homeland security university, (2) a homeland security conference center, (3) a homeland security product testing center, (4) a homeland security business incubator, (5) a coalition village, and (6) substantial retail real estate development. The Chesapeake Crescent group includes a consortium of real estate developers who contemplate involvement in these spinoff projects, and is led by noted business leader and telecommunications executive George Vradenberg, and by famed retail developer Herb Miller, creator of major DC retail projects including Washington Harbor, Georgetown Park, Potomac Mills, and (with The John Akridge Companies) Gallery Place. MPS/RE students already involved in the DHS HQ project to date have divided themselves into 3 student-led work groups: (1) a group that performs real estate market analysis upon research and development clusters, in order to learn the principles that will produce the optimal research cluster at the DHS HQ, (2) a group that analyzes a number of options for the DC Office of the Deputy Mayor for Planning and Economic Development and the DC Department of Planning, and (3) a group that analyzes a number of best practices related to the private sector spinoff from the DHS HQ. The work of MPS/RE students on the DHS HQ project has real impact: workshops of high-level national experts making major recommendations to Federal and local agencies on various aspects of the DHS HQ project utilize the work produced by Georgetown real estate students as the basis for their discussions and decisions. The DHS HQ Practicum is particularly recommended for Development Track students, but is valuable experience for students on all Tracks. Students who have taken most or all of the Foundational courses are likely to perform most effectively in the Practicum. Given the scale and duration of the DHS HQ project, we anticipate that the Practicum course will be offered on an ongoing basis every semester for several years, perhaps even through its scheduled completion in 2017. The Practicum may be taken for credit only once. However, students who have taken the Practicum and wish to remain involved in the DHS HQ project, and to continue to gain the exceptional development experience the project affords, are welcome to continue participating on a non-credit extracurricular basis in subsequent semesters.

title: Independent Study
description: The Independent Study course, MPRE 901, is designed to accommodate students who wish to pursue real estate-related academic endeavors which in some manner cannot readily covered by conventional MPS/RE courses. In appropriate circumstances, students doing internships may receive Independent Study credit for deliverables created in the course of their internship. Independent Study also provides flexibility for students who have been detailed to another city, are pursuing international real estate research, have a prolonged illness, and in similar situations. A student who wishes to take MPRE 901 should approach the MPS/RE Associate Dean as early as possible prior to the semester in which Independent Study is contemplated. The student must submit a brief proposal for the studentâs Independent Study project, which the MPS/RE Associate Dean must approve. In conjunction with other MPS/RE Faculty members as appropriate, the MPS/RE Dean will determine the appropriate deliverables from the student, and will assign the most appropriate MPS/RE Faculty member for the project that the student proposes. A student may only take MPRE 901 once.

title: Case Competition
description: The NAIOP Independent Study: Real Estate Case Competition, NAIOP Maryland/DC Capital Challenge course offers MPS/RE students the opportunity to participate in a real estate case competition generously sponsored by the local chapter of one of the leading commercial real estate trade associations. In addition to the Georgetown MPS/RE program, teams from the graduate real estate programs of the University of Maryland and George Washington University are participating. The subject of the 2010 Capital Challenge is the utilization of a substantial land assemblage located in the North of Massachusetts Avenue (NoMa) submarket (the âSiteâ) which has been amassed by the JBG Companies, one of the top real estate development and investment organizations based in the Greater Washington region. Working in teams, students on the Georgetown MPS/RE Capital Challenge Team visit the Site, analyze its current utilization, assess competitive sites in the NoMa submarket, and develop alternative plans for putting the Site to its highest and best commercial real estate use. Students conduct substantial research, write reports on their research findings, and apply that research to developing such plans. A group of top industry advisors who are provided by NAIOP Maryland/DC advises and assists the MPS/RE Capital Challenge Team in developing its Case. Industry advisors include architects, developers, financiers, engineers, and land use attorneys. This is an incredible opportunity for students to work closely with some of the top real estate professionals in Greater Washington. The MPS/RE Capital Challenge Team presents its ultimate findings to the panel of NAIOP Capital Challenge judges, hopefully emerging victorious. Go Hoya Capital Challenge Team!!!! The subject of the 2011 Case Competition is forthcoming.

title: Capstone
description: The required Capstone Course marks the culmination of a studentâs career in the Master of Professional Studies in Real Estate program, and the studentâs transition from journeyman to advanced real estate professional. The Capstone Course offers the student the opportunity to explore a major real estate topic in significant depth and make a substantial contribution to knowledge in a selected sector of the real estate profession. Based upon previous courses and professional experience, the student formulates a Capstone Project topic. Under the tutelage of a senior real estate professional from Metropolitan Washington or beyond, the student researches the chosen topic, participates in any appropriate activities related to the topic, and produces a substantial work which constitutes a significant contribution to real estate knowledge. Because MPS/RE encompasses institutional, financial, legal, marketing, accounting, environmental, planning, design, engineering, and other disciplines, it is impossible to specify a single genre of Capstone Project, and the Capstone Project is thus defined chiefly by quality. With the fullest support of the entire MPS/RE program, the student has maximum freedom to pursue a real estate topic that embodies the very essence of the studentâs professionalism, and should be one of the best pieces of work the student ever does.

title: Applied Ethics in Sports
description: A course that allows students to explore their own values and challenges them to codify and commit to their own code of ethics in relation to professional codes of conduct and best practices.

title: Sports Leadership and Management
description: An examination of management issues and organizational behavior in the industry, relating mostly to staffing, motivation, and communication. In application, it introduces theories on leadership and the tools and techniques of how to effectively lead a sports organization on a daily basis.

title: Diversity and Social Responsibliity in Sports
description: A thorough introduction to the history and socioeconomic underpinnings of sports. This course examines the issues of ethnicity, gender, and international scope in relation to the modern sports world. Through readings, discussion, and role-playing, this course explores the stereotypes, misconceptions, and social elements of a changing sports landscape.

title: Sports Marketing Strategy
description: An examination of marketing issues specific to the sports industry, based on the application of basic marketing principles to a range of sports organizations. This course will analyze the field of sports and lifestyle marketing with the practices, applications, and strategy of mainstream marketing. Course content will include a historical overview of sports marketing, delving into areas such as the team-side versus agency-side of the industry, including sponsors and corporations, sporting goods, special events, and sports enterprises in general.

title: Sports Communications and Public Relations
description: An analysis of the role of public relations and mass media in sports communication. Hands-on training in the tools and technology of sports PR is combined with an examination of the publicâs relationship with athletes and sports. This course explores the progressive techniques and activities used to promote sporting events. Discussion topics include brand awareness, media management, message recall, and the examination of four news mediums: television, internet, print, and radio.

title: Sales Promotion, Licensing and Sponsorship
description: An exploration of techniques used to sell and generate revenue in the business cycle. Topics include sales proposal development, sponsor solicitation, licensing rights, new business development, endorsements and merchandising, commercialization of technology, and corporate partnerships.

title: Sports Digital Media and Consumer Engagement
description: A review of how to develop and apply essential data in marketing decision-making in the sports industry, including database design, website development and management, privacy issues, and social media. This course considers the application of marketing principles, research methodologies, design implementation, and the analysis and interpretation of data. Incorporating horizontal and vertical distribution systems that integrate with the digital marketplace, the role of technology is explored in relationship to providing management the necessary support and maintenance of customer profiles. Students will gain an understanding of distribution channels, segmentation, demographic characteristics, and e-marketing methods and their applications.

title: Sports Business and Finance
description: This course provides a broad overview of the sports business marketplace, including the financial and accounting acumen necessary for executives to succeed in sports organizations. The goal is to provide hands-on and practical experience to real-time business challenges. Topics as they relate to these sectors include fiscal and budgetary control, ownership, and the skills used in the day-to-day operations of professional and amateur sports organizations.

title: Sports Law, Contracts, and Negotiation
description: This course provides an in-depth overview and analysis of legal principles, ethical issues, and salary cap negotiations in professional sports. Issues that are identified, analyzed, and explored include athlete representation, labor law, antitrust exemption, arbitration, collective bargaining, salary cap administration, free agency and the application of ethics as a method in the decision making and problem solving process.

title: Sports Event Planning and Facility Management
description: This course provides students with an understanding of the complexity involved in major sports events and facilities in relation to their social, cultural, and global environment. Featured topics include the analysis of goals and resources, the development of the revenue streams, design and planning, scheduling and operations, staff management, and the implementation of action deadlines.

title: Economics, Brand Management and Under Armour
description: An economic analysis of a variety of issues affecting the sports industry, including ticket pricing, luxury taxes, competitive balance, apparel and retail strategies, revenue sharing, the impacts and rationale of government subsidies, and the global currency marketplace.

title: Internship I
description: Practical learning opportunities will also take place outside the classroom. The SIM program will contact organizations of interest with individual students to successfully undertake, implement, and complete a timely industry-specific internship under the supervision of an internship advisor. These organizations encompass the professional, amateur, corporate/agency, municipal, and non-profit sectors of the sports industry. Action Items: * The course will include an internship handbook and will follow a monthly meeting schedule with the internship advisor and program director. * Students will keep a weekly journal that will detail their objectives, activities, benchmarks and assessment tools. * At terms end, student will submit a project portfolio to the internship advisor and program director.

title: Internship I
description: Practical learning opportunities will also take place outside the classroom. The SIM program will contact organizations of interest with individual students to successfully undertake, implement, and complete a timely industry-specific internship under the supervision of an internship advisor. These organizations encompass the professional, amateur, corporate/agency, municipal, and non-profit sectors of the sports industry. Action Items: * The course will include an internship handbook and will follow a monthly meeting schedule with the internship advisor and program director. * Students will keep a weekly journal that will detail their objectives, activities, benchmarks and assessment tools. * At terms end, student will submit a project portfolio to the internship advisor and program director.

title: Internship II
description: Practical learning opportunities will also take place outside the classroom. The SIM program will contact organizations of interest with individual students to successfully undertake, implement, and complete a timely industry-specific internship under the supervision of an internship advisor. These organizations encompass the professional, amateur, corporate/agency, municipal, and non-profit sectors of the sports industry. Action Items: * The course will include an internship handbook and will follow a monthly meeting schedule with the internship advisor and program director. * Students will keep a weekly journal that will detail their objectives, activities, benchmarks and assessment tools. * At terms end, student will submit a project portfolio to the internship advisor and program director.

title: Capstone
description: A semester-long opportunity for students to apply what they have learned in the program to produce one substantial piece of work under the tutelage of an industry sponsor and the program faculty. The subject will be appropriate to the students chosen interest. Board members and industry guest lecturers will visit and students will have the opportunity to present their work for the purpose of review and advice. Each student will be assisted in devising a strategy to support the topic of interest, appropriate to the course goals, by semesters end.

title: Ethics in Technology Management
description: Provides a firm foundation of essential concepts, skills, and strategies to allow for informed decision-making and effective leadership. The course emphasizes ethical responsibilities and core values endemic to the professional world, cutting across several disciplines but focusing particularly on the connections between applied ethics and areas such as technology, business management, and law. You will explore real-world dilemmas and the framework for reaching ethical decisions. Topics could include intellectual property rights, government regulations, privacy, and others. Professional guests from various fieldsâsuch as business, law, and governmentâwill present case studies representative of their experiences.

title: Management of Technology
description: The Management of Technology course provides theoretical and practical experience in using information technology to support organizational decision-making processes. You will examine the requirements of an organization to optimize its competitive strategy and core competencies. You will learn different tactical, strategic, and organizational factors relative to various information systems, as well as how to gauge the effectiveness of an organizationâs information system. The course addresses some of the unique issues in managing different types of IT professionals, from senior-level architects to junior-level infrastructure support personnel. You will explore tools and techniques related to hiring, developing, assessing, and retaining staffâas well as theories/methods of managing remote workers and virtual teams.

title: Project Mgmt for IT Professionals
description: Project Management for IT Professionals will master both the practical and theoretical sides of project management for IT professionals. In keeping with the ANSI standard for project management, this comprehensive course covers such core concepts as triple-constant theory, critical path method, and tracking earned value. Youâll follow the project lifecycleâfrom planning to monitoring to controlâand learn how to avoid the most common challenges of successful project management.

title: Technology Entrepreneurship
description: This course examines the startup process from both sidesâexploring the perspective of the entrepreneur as well as the corporation pursuing a model of open innovation. Based largely on case study discussions, the course focuses on intellectual property, high-tech product development, venture finance, high-tech market strategy, strategic alliances, and entrepreneurial leadership skills.

title: e-Business/Social Media Technology Trends
description: Effective corporate e-business strategies demand on an in-depth understanding of the various technologies that enable electronic business. In this course, youâll examine how emerging trendsâin telecommunications, search engines, security, portals, website and user interface design, electronic payment systems, e-publishing and digital download features, mobile commerce, and pervasive computingâimpact industry, corporate strategy, and competitive dynamics. Specific topics covered include: Web 2.0, Service Oriented Architecture, On-Demand Computing, and Open Source Software.

title: Enterprise Modernization & Technology Assertion
description: Enterprise modernization changes every dimension of an organizationâhow it views itself, responds to its needs, and conducts its business. In this course, youâll focus on the management challenges posed by emerging technologies and learn how to overcome these obstacles. Youâll explore how these technologies affect (and are affected by) political, social, economic, and cultural factors. Specific topics include emerging standards, nanotechnologies, artificial intelligence, artificial life, and expansion of the digital world.

title: R & D Management
description: What challenges do managers face as they seek to make research and development (R&D) serve the needs of the corporation in an increasingly competitive global marketplace? This course explores managing R&D in a corporate environment from both strategic and tactical perspectivesâ providing a deep understanding of technology as a powerful tool for competitive advantage and growth. Topics include key R&D business processes, issues in global R&D management,and methods of measuring and optimizing the return on R&D.

title: Database Analysis,Design & Mgmt
description: In the Database Analysis, Design, and Management course you will master the fundamentals of databasesâfrom structure to securityâin this comprehensive course. Youâll begin with the core principles of database design, analysis, deployment, and use: data models, entity-relationship diagrams, data normalization, data dictionaries, database security and access rules, and common database applications. Move on to advanced topics, including connectivity technologies, scripting languages, API interface designs, warehousing, and data mining methods. Youâll also explore how to implement a relational database management system.

title: Managing Information Security
description: The Managing Information Security course is designed for managers charged with protecting IT systems, this course prepares you to anticipate the threat of Internet attacksâand develop effective strategies to defend against them. Learn the core concepts of data security, signature algorithms, key distribution, hash functions, and identification schemes. Youâll even explore public/private key cryptography, secret key schemes (DES and IDEA), public key schemes (RSA and ElGamal), and systems based on elliptic curves. The pertinent implementation aspects and current security estimations will be covered for all schemes.

title: Service-Oriented Architecture
description: In the Service Oriented Architecture course you will learn the standards of SOA implementation and gain a thorough understanding of the value, benefit, cost, effectiveness, and challenges of moving to an SOA. In this course, youâll master the basics of modeling, designing, and implementing an SOAâand discover the theories and best practices behind interoperability.
