description: Services account for more than 50% of the worldâs GDP, 65% of the US GDP, and 80% of jobs in the US (Business Roundtable). This course is designed to provide the participant with the concepts and principals needed to design, manage, and improve service delivery processes. Utilizing the Socratic method, we explore business models, conceptual frameworks, and the use of qualitative and quantitative analyses for enhancing the effectiveness of service operations. An important aspect of the course focuses on the development of participants' skills to learn, analyze, synthesize, and apply new developments in service management across a broad range of service industries. In addition, we consider the evolving effects of technology and globalization on services. We learn how to apply these concepts and principles to specific service settings through class discussion, class members' experiences, and a group project involving an actual service organization.

title: Applied Business Statistics
description: MBA Core Topics Covered in this Class: 0) Brief review of Essential Statistical Concepts 1) Statistical Inference: Estimation and Testing Hypotheses Statistical Inference is the art of making statements about the general (the Population) based on the specific (the Sample). Any such attempt has two sobering properties: â¢ Our answer is almost certainly incorrect â¢ We never know by how much we are wrong. Nevertheless, most human endeavor proceeds by making such inferences and it is therefore, important to conduct the process as effectively as possible. The two general categories of statistical inference, estimation and hypothesis testing, can be distinguished by different purposes: estimation is concerned with estimating the value of an unknown population parameter; hypothesis testing is concerned with making a decision about a hypothesized value of an unknown population parameter. In chapter 5 âInferences Based on a Single Sample: Estimation with Confidence Intervalsâ, we estimate population means and proportions based on a single sample selected from the population of interest and, more importantly, show how to attach a measure of uncertainty to that point estimate. In chapter 6 âInferences Based on a Single Sample: Tests of Hypothesis,â we show how to utilize the sample information to test whether a population parameter conforms to a pre-specified value. 2) Applied Regression Analysis: Regression analysis is a quantitative tool for modeling the relationship between a set of explanatory or independent variables and a dependent variable. The explanatory variables may be controlled (e.g. advertising expenditures) or observational (e.g. responses to a survey). The value of such models lies both in direct prediction and in âwhat ifâ analyses. Chapter 10 âSimple Linear Regression,â covers the simplest situation, the linear relationship between a single explanatory variable and a dependent variable. Chapter 11 âMultiple Regression and Model Buildingâ extends the basic concepts developed in chapter 10, modeling the mean value of dependent variable as a function of two or more explanatory variables. The emphasis in the course is the development of model-building skills and the ability to interpret the results of a regression analysis.

title: Management Science
description: Management Science (MS) is a quantitative approach to managerial decision making. It is a body of principles, techniques and methods concerned with providing solutions to managerial problems. Management Science is not statistics. However, as in many other fields of study it may employ statistics to obtain model inputs, analysis and outputs. In our approach to Management Science we will draw from topics that immediately link to and have an interface with your courses in the program. In particular, our Management Science course is designed to offer a well integrated sequence of quantitative topics in support of select concepts encountered in your Operations Management, Finance and Marketing courses. This approach is consistent with our objective of teaching an integrated course where the topics covered serve to support, reinforce and assimilate the conceptual teachings of your first year core courses. Related material, assignments and cases illustrate topical concepts and their integration. The material covered include: Break-even Analysis; Inventory Models; Statistical Process Control; Decision Analysis; Black & Scholes and Binomial Option Pricing Models; Linear Optimization; Non-Linear Optimization portfolio risk minimization (time permitting).

title: Computer-Based Decision Support Systems
description: This course explores advanced topics in management support systems with a focus on decision theory. The course will present insights into key issues and problems in decision support information systems. The Visual Basic Applications (VBA) programming language within Microsoft Excel will be used as the primary tool to build basic decision support systems. Class deliverables will be systems built with VBA code.

title: Advanced Regression Analysis
description: This course provides the advanced yet extremely applied perspective on business statistics. Two specific topics account for the majority of its content: regression analysis and analysis of variance. Additional topics include deviating from model assumptions, multicollinearity, transformations, residuals and lack of fit, heteroscedasticity, detecting outliers/influential outliers: Jack Knife, detecting residual correlation: Durbin-Watson test, piece-wise linear regression, inverse prediction, and weighted least squares. MBA second year students only.

title: Business Forecasting
description: Our objective is to introduce various approaches to forecasting, to understand their strengths and weaknesses, and to develop a framework for identifying an appropriate method for a particular forecasting problem. Along the way, we will also see that some methods, although widely used, may be flawed and/or inefficient. Topics to be covered will include: â¢ Judgmental forecasting â¢ How to measure forecasting performance â¢ Exponential smoothing for regular and seasonal series â¢ Time series regression â¢ Autoregressive models â¢ Intervention analysis

title: Special Topics: Real Options
description: âReal Optionsâ is a new valuation standard for evaluating and analyzing capital investment decisions in the face of uncertainty. The real options approach is an extension of option pricing theory for managing real assets. This course is about the real options approach to strategic investments. It aims to develop a real options thinking rather than focusing only on the technical aspects of modeling in a real options framework. In that vein, our objective is to introduce real options as a viable means to evaluation of investment opportunities. Selected case studies and material will ensure an in-depth understanding of various concepts in a realistic setting. MBA second year students only.

title: Operations Management
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. MBA Core This course is designed to introduce you to concepts, tools and techniques for managing this function. Moreover, since this is a new field for many of you, we also want to give you a new perspective to look at organizations. If we are successful, many of you after this course will change the way you look at a restaurant, a hospital, a bank, a factory, a repair shop, a daily newspaper, an airline, a warehouse, a government agency, an airport, or even a consulting company! Youâll observe more closely and critically how they are producing their product (goods or services), and will notice that some of their production processes are smart and some not-so-smart (sometimes irritatingly inefficient). We believe this course will give you a better appreciation of the real drivers behind success or failure of many of these organizations. To use our usual business school jargons, youâll learn about the strategic importance of the firmâs supply chain and production systems.

title: Project Management
description: The course is open to all MBA students. No pre-requisite. This course provides an overview of project management. The course addresses (1) the culture, (2) the principles, and (3) the basic techniques of project management. The course reviews the general stages of a project in rough chronological order and describes how the stages interrelate. Basic tools of project management, such as work breakdown structure, scheduling, earned value analysis, and risk management, are introduced. The elements of project management critical to the success of a project also are identified and explained. Specific Objectives: 1. To describe, demystify, and formalize project management so that students are aware of the Project Management discipline and can utilize new knowledge and skills in their professions. 2. To enable the immediate practice of project management in any workplace, independent of discipline. 3. To lay a foundation for those students interested in the PMP Certification. 4. To enjoy the class and have spirited, knowledgeable debates with classmates and instructor.

title: Corporate Cybersecurity
description: This course introduces the student to the key concepts, issues, risks and impacts associated with corporate information security. Cybercriminals, corporate adversaries, state-actors and other hackers are increasingly agile and sophisticated in their malicious activities. And with the advance of social networking, mobile computing, cloud computing and other prevailing technology trends, security practitioners are ever more challenged to protect enterprise, client and employee information. It is critical that todayâs manager understand his/her companyâs security risks, vulnerabilities, threats and related consequences and be able to make strategic and tactical decisions about responding to security challenges. Although the course is non-technical in nature the student will learn complex technology concepts and frameworks and be required to apply existing operational, financial and strategic expertise in solving executive level, enterprise problems. Current, real-world intrusions and compromisesâsuch as the recent attack on Google and other companiesâwill be covered and the student will gain a detailed understanding of the threats organizations face as well as the challenges inherent to effectively managing cyber-related risks to both the enterprise and its employees.

title: Global Logistics
description: The emphasis of the course is the logistics or Supply Chain function in firms that source, produce, distribute and market in multiple nations. The management of the logistics in such firms differs from its domestic counterpart along several key dimensions. First, there is the need to be able to identify and analyze factors that differ across nations, which influence the effectiveness of this function. These include worker productivity, process adaptability, governmental concerns, transportation availability, and culture and so on. In addition, because of the distances involved, transportation and distribution are of greater significance. Finally, these geographically dispersed set of facilities and markets must be integrated and manage to enhance the strategy of the business unit. Therefore, some of the sessions of the course will focus on cross-national decisions and others will focus on managing across nations. Throughout the course we will emphasize the role that emerging technologies are playing within the supply chain and e-commerce. For the purposes of the course, the perspective will be that of the director of logistics or Supply Chain, vice-president of logistics or other, senior level general managers with primary responsibility for the production and distribution of manufactured products. A major theme throughout the course is that sourcing, manufacturing and distribution are of primary importance in the formulation of business and corporate strategy. We want to look at the strategic decisions within logistics as they impact the ability of the firm to achieve its selected overall strategies.

title: Operations Strategy
description: A firm derives a competitive advantage either by achieving a cost advantage, or through product differentiation (Michael Porter, Competitive Advantage, Free Press, NY, NY, 1985). That is, either the firm must be able to produce its goods at a lower cost (through process innovation), or it must offer features that customers desire and competitors lack (through product innovation). We explore specific operational strategies that the firm might pursue to achieve this superior process or product innovation. Within each topic, we develop a framework, or theory, that the firm can use as an aid in its decision-making process, and also tackle a real-life problem through a case study. The topics are as follows: Introduction: The Dynamics of Innovation; Competing Through Operations; Strategic Implications of the Learning Curve; Encroachment Strategies; Outsourcing; New Product Development; Strategic Quality Management; Capacity Management; Capacity, Flexibility and Timing; Path to Profitability; Value Creation in Private Equity.

title: Intensive First Level Persian I
description: This course introduces students to the basic structures of the Persian language. All four language skills: speaking, listening comprehension, reading comprehension, and writing will be taught equally using the immersion method. This method will help students achieve confidence communicating in the Persian language. Aspects of Persian culture will be introduced on a regular basis. At the end of this course, students will be able to: 1. Have basic level conversation in Persian: 1st level 5 minutes, 2nd level 10 minutes. 2. Read texts of elementary level: 1st level 50- 100 2nd level 100-200 words. 3. Write short paragraphs with reasonable accuracy: 1st level 50- 100 2nd level 100-200 words. 4. Develop cultural awareness through readings, films, music, etc.

title: Intensive First Level Persian II
description: This course introduces students to the basic structures of the Persian language. All four language skills: speaking, listening comprehension, reading comprehension, and writing will be taught equally using the immersion method. This method will help students achieve confidence communicating in the Persian language. Aspects of Persian culture will be introduced on a regular basis. At the end of this course, students will be able to: 1. Have basic level conversation in Persian: 1st level 5 minutes, 2nd level 10 minutes. 2. Read texts of elementary level: 1st level 50- 100 2nd level 100-200 words. 3. Write short paragraphs with reasonable accuracy: 1st level 50- 100 2nd level 100-200 words. 4. Develop cultural awareness through readings, films, music, etc.

title: Intensive Second Level Persian I
description: Permission needed from Instructor. This intensive intermediate level I course is designed to continue developing the student's communicative skills (reading, writing, listening, and speaking). This course is conducted in the target language. Initially, it affords students a view of basic Persian, then an introduction to more complex syntactic grammatical forms. Aspects of Persian history, culture, and contemporary life are also introduced through readings, listening materials, videos and films, and through the use of language technologies (web tools). The students will also be given practice in reading aloud and giving oral presentations of materials taken from the web, media, and television broadcast. As a final project, students will write a composition of 200-350 words (Intermed. I), 400-500 words (Intermed.II).

title: Intensive Second Level Persian II
description: Permission needed from Instructor. This intensive intermediate level I course is designed to continue developing the student's communicative skills (reading, writing, listening, and speaking). This course is conducted in the target language. Initially, it affords students a view of basic Persian, then an introduction to more complex syntactic grammatical forms. Aspects of Persian history, culture, and contemporary life are also introduced through readings, listening materials, videos and films, and through the use of language technologies (web tools). The students will also be given practice in reading aloud and giving oral presentations of materials taken from the web, media, and television broadcast. As a final project, students will write a composition of 200-350 words (Intermed. I), 400-500 words (Intermed.II).

title: Advanced Persian I
description: Two years of Persian and permission of the instructor. This course is designed to enable the student to reach higher levels in the various language skills a stage where they use Persian in wider arrays of cultural, professional and social contexts. Students will prepare newspaper and journal articles, short stories, reports and presentations. Students will be expected to argue and debate extensively, paraphrase and summarize texts, and to express points of view in both speaking and writing. Emphasis will be placed on understanding nuances, idiomatic expressions, and rhetorical devices. By the end of the course, it is expected that students will be able to converse in a clear and concise participatory fashion and to carry out a wide variety of communicative tasks requiring diverse discourse strategies.

title: Advanced Persian II
description: Two years of Persian and permission of the instructor. This course is designed to enable the student to reach higher levels in the various language skills a stage where they use Persian in wider arrays of cultural, professional and social contexts. Students will prepare newspaper and journal articles, short stories, reports and presentations. Students will be expected to argue and debate extensively, paraphrase and summarize texts, and to express points of view in both speaking and writing. Emphasis will be placed on understanding nuances, idiomatic expressions, and rhetorical devices. By the end of the course, it is expected that students will be able to converse in a clear and concise participatory fashion and to carry out a wide variety of communicative tasks requiring diverse discourse strategies.

title: Graduate Pharmacology
description: This course includes both a broad survey of pharmacology and detailed discussions of experimental approaches used in pharmacological research. Discussions of primary literature articles related to the major lecture topics are included. PBIO501 is a prerequisite.

title: Advanced General Pharmacology
description: This course consists of an in-depth analysis of selected major units presented in PHAR-504. PHAR504 is a prerequisite.

title: Modern Methods in Molecular Biology
description: Introduction and evaluation of most of the commonly used methods in molecular biology.

title: Applied Statistical Principles in Pharmacology
description: To provide students with a working knowledge of how to design and analyze pharmacological experiments. Course Structure: Each week, students will be given a take-home problem set with a brief (30-min.) introduction to the new topic area. This problem set will be discussed in a âroundtableâ format during the first 90-min. of the following session, and then a new problem set will be introduced for the following week. Students will be evaluated based on their level of participation, homework, and quizzes.

title: Ethical Issues in Scientific Research
description: Discussions of ethical questions and dilemmas facing scientists today.

title: Introduction to Pharmacology
description: This minicourse presents an overview of some aspects of pharmacology. It begins with basic principles of pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics. Neuropharmacology, including the autonomic nervous system and the central nervous system are then covered. Cardiovascular pharmacology and its central control is discussed. Finally, there is a specialized discussion of the role of voltage and ligand-gated channels in controlling cellular function. The course meets from mid-semester to December. Check with bwolfe01@georgetown.edu for exact start date.

title: Drugs, the Brain, and Behavior I
description: This course uses a interdisciplinary approach to study function and dysfunction of the brain. We emphasize basic concepts in neuroscience, examine the cellular and molecular underpinnings of various abnormalities of the nervous system, and explore neuropharmacology as therapy and as a tool to study brain function. Special emphasis is placed on 1) bridging basic neural mechanisms (neurotransmitters, circuits, systems) and higher brain processes (emotion, cognition, memory) and 2) the utility and limits of animals models of human behavior and disease. The course will involve lectures, student presentations, an discussion of primary literature. A term paper focusing on the pharmacology of a disorder covered in class will be a requirement of the course. Fall: stress and homeostasis, reward and addiction (drugs, eating disorders), mood regulation (anxiety and depression) and attention, social behavior, and cognitive control (schizophrenia, autism, and ADHD).

title: Drugs, the Brain, and Behavior II
description: This course uses a interdisciplinary approach to study function and dysfunction of the brain. We emphasize basic concepts in neuroscience, examine the cellular and molecular underpinnings of various abnormalities of the nervous system, and explore neuropharmacology as therapy and as a tool to study brain function. Special emphasis is placed on 1) bridging basic neural mechanisms (neurotransmitters, circuits, systems) and higher brain processes (emotion, cognition, memory) and 2) the utility and limits of animals models of human behavior and disease. The course will involve lectures, student presentations, an discussion of primary literature. A term paper focusing on the pharmacology of a disorder covered in class will be a requirement of the course. Spring: memory systems (Alzheimer's), motor systems (Parkinson's, Huntington's, Multiple Sclerosis, Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis), Neural injury, prion disease, HIV-dementia, and epilepsy. Fall is *not *a prerequisite for spring.

title: Seminar / Journal club
description: Research progress reports by staff, students, and distinguished visitors. Analyses of recent articles and current concepts in research.

title: Seminar / Journal club
description: Research progress reports by staff, students, and distinguished visitors. Analyses of recent articles and current concepts in research.

title: Advanced Topics in Nutrition
description: Description: This course examines a range of topics on human nutrition, including macronutrients, micronutrients, the effects of diet on health, and claims and evidence for use of megadoses of vitamins, minerals and amino acids for treating specific health conditions. The emphasis is on evidence-based nutritional information, utilizing the medical and epidemiological literature to analyze the impact of food, diets and nutritional supplements on human health. The class will also address food preparation and cooking to facilitate the adoption of nutritional principles into your diet. Special topics include breastfeeding, exercise nutrition and sports supplements, the nutrition of beverages, genetically modified foods, prebiotics & probiotics, , the science of dieting, the evolutionary origins of the human diet, and the endocrinology of adipose tissue and obesity. Grading is based on the performance of several assignments, a paper and a final exam.

title: Pharmacology I - basic principles
description: Fundamental concepts in pharmacology such as drug-receptor interactions via radioligand binding, functional agonisim, and antagonists as well as drug metablolism and pharmacokinetics will be covered. Meets M-F 10-12 in January. Quizzes every day. Final at end.

title: Pharmacology II - Drug targets
description: Ion channels, GPCR, S/T kinases and phosphatases, growth factors and receptors, transcription factors will be covered. Additionally cell death by necrosis and apoptosis as well as death and dependence receptors will be presented. Meets 10-12 Jan-Feb. Quizzes every day. Final at end.

title: Pharmacology III - Neuropharmacology I
description: The autonomic nervous system will be covered. Excitatory neurotransmission in the CNS will be presented including receptors and signal transduction systems for glutamate. LTP, LTD and retrograde signaling will be covered. Meets M-F 10-12 in February. Quizzes every day. Final at end.

title: Pharmacology IV - Neuropharmacology II
description: Inhibitory neurotransmission by GABA and the many pharmacological tools used to treat anxiety, epilepsy, insomnia, etc. will be presented. Drugs used in the treatment of pain will be covered. Cholinergic and adrenergic neurotransmission and systems in the CNS will be covered. Drugs used to treat scizophrenia and depression will be presented. Meets M-F 10-12 in March. Quizzes every day. Final at end.

title: Pharmacology V - General Pharmacology
description: Treatment of cancer, viral and bacterial diseases will be covered. Obesity and diabetes as well as lipid metabolism will be presented. Inflammatory diseases and asthma as well as anticoagulants will be covered. Meets M-F 10-12 in April. Quizzes every day. Final at end.

title: Pharmacology VI - Cardiovascular and GI Pharm
description: Treatment of arrhythmias, heart failure, angina, and hypertension are covered. The enteric nervous system and GI disorders as well as reproduction are presented. Meets M-F 10-12 in April. Quizzes every day. Final at end.

title: Fundamentals of Basic and Clinical Pharmacology
description: Summer 2005 only

title: DRUG DISCOVERY AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
description: This course is comprised of a series of seminars by specialists in drug discovery and development areas concerning both therapy and diagnostics. It will focus on the scientific, clinical, economic and regulatory processes as well as stewardship involved bringing a drug from the laboratory to the marketplace.

title: Pharmacology Tutorial
description: Individualized tutorials between students and faculty to address topics not covered by other courses.

title: Pharmacology Tutorial
description: Individualized tutorials between students and faculty to address topics not covered by other courses.

title: Research
description: Individualized research tutorial with laboratory component. Open only to MS students in the Pharmacology program.

title: Research II
description: Individualized research tutorial with laboratory component. Open only to MS students in the Pharmacology program. Requires permission of instructor.

title: Continuing Registration in Pharmacology
description: Continuing Registration is for students who have completed all requirements for the Ph.D. degree but who have not yet graduated. M.S. students can use this mechanism to maintain their student status while finishing needed requirements.

title: Continuing Registration in Pharmacology
description: Continuing Registration is for students who have completed all requirements for the Ph.D. degree but who have not yet graduated. M.S. students can use this mechanism to maintain their student status while finishing needed requirements.

title: Thesis Research--Pharmacology
description: Thesis research leading to a PhD dissertation.

title: Thesis Research--Pharmacology
description: Research for Doctoral Dissertation

title: Thesis Research--Pharmacology
description: Thesis research leading to a PhD dissertation. This section 03 is for the student who is still in coursework, but also in thesis research.

title: Intro to Ethics
description: This course is an introduction to the study of ethics, understood as both a subject for theoretical reflection and a practical guide for how to live. We will explore the views of the Westâs greatest ethical thinkers, both past and present, and we will use narratives from literature and film to illuminate the ethical issues those thinkers raise. The course begins with an exploration of individual moral character, including virtue and vice, moral education, conscience, moral luck, and integrity. From there, we will move to a discussion of ethical issues that arise out of interactions with others, with a special focus on the immediate Georgetown community. We will consider the moral ramifications of academic honesty, alcohol use, sexual relationships, and Facebook, using philosophical readings to help us think reflectively about how these issues influence life at Georgetown. Finally, we will broaden our outlook to consider global ethical issues, including our relationship to the worldâs poor. Requirements include three papers, a take-home essay final, and participation in discussion sections. REQUIRED TEXTBOOKS Moral Philosophy: A Reader, edited by Louis Pojman and Peter Tramel, 4th edition Hackett, 2009). ISBN-13: 978-0-87220-962-6. A Rulebook for Arguments, Anthony Weston, 4th edition (Hackett, 2008). ISBN-13: 978-0-87220-954-1

title: Intro to Ethics
description: SUMMER 2011 Everywhere we go we make moral decisions. Even seemingly mundane activities like cutting in line at the movie theater, telling a friend you love his/her outfit when you really think it unflattering, or buying that daily cup oâ joe can be morally charged. Most of the time, our everyday lives present us with situations in which questions of right and wrong are even more evidently weighty. Sometimes we agree in our determinations of right and wrong/ good and evil; other times, our beliefs diverge. Consider the following questions, for example: Is it wrong to spend money on expensive cars and homes when people in other parts of the world are dying from malnutrition? Is physician-assisted suicide moral? Do non-human animals have rights? Ethics is a branch of philosophy concerned with such questions of right and wrong. Normative ethics attempts to arrive at practical procedures or moral standards that can tell us what we ought to do in a given situation. This course will provide an introduction to three of the most historically prominent normative ethical theories â Aristotleâs virtue ethics, the deontology of Immanuel Kant, and the version of utilitarianism propounded by John Stuart Mill. Our discussion of these theories will also lead us into more theoretical questions that are the province of meta-ethics: questions about what it means to say that an act is morally right (or morally wrong); about whether there is an objective difference between right and wrong acts; about how we might define âgoodâ; about what criteria determine whether a being (e.g. an embryo, or cow, or laboratory mouse) has rights. The course aims to enable you to critically evaluate your own beliefs about concrete issues such as the ones mentioned above, and also, to equip you with tools to assess the success of an ethical argument.

title: Intro to Ethics
description: SUMMER 2011 Is it morally permissible to have an abortion? May we take peopleâs moneyââin the form of taxes, for exampleââto provide universal health care? Is it right for us to execute those convicted of serious crimes like murder? Different people answer these questions differently. Those with a devout Catholic upbringing are likely to condemn abortion; people who grew up in politically conservative circles probably oppose universal health care; and odds are that the children of social progressives will oppose capital punishment. What we believe about morality isââunsurprisinglyââprofoundly impacted by our own personal history. Given that weâre biased by our respective upbringings, how can we make moral judgments that are anything but a blind repetition of the ideas weâve been raised with? One promisingââor at least interestingââanswer is that we can use ethical theories to call our own moral views into question. By studying ethical theory with an open mind, we can evaluate moral issues from a new angleââwe can, so to speak, see things from the perspective of a plausible theory rather than the perspective of our upbringing. In this way, we can hope to recognize our biases and make better moral judgments. In this course, weâll study the most prominent ethical theories that philosophers have devised: Consequentialism, Deontology, and Virtue Ethics. Readings will include both classic and contemporary selections, including pieces from Mill, Kant, Aristotle, and others.

title: Intro to Ethics
description: It is not long into an individualâs life before the specifically moral dimension of human existence emerges as salient. No longer content simply to live but to live well or as one ought, we become attuned to questions concerning the proper treatment of our fellows and how to lead a life that is meaningful and fulfilling. In this course we will look at some of what the longstanding ethical tradition of western philosophy has had to say about these questions. To begin, we will focus on metaethical questions concerning the nature of morality as such. These questions, which have gripped most of us at one time or another, will center on 1) the relation between morality and self-interest, 2) whether morality is in some sense culturally relative, and 3) the relation between morality and religion. From there, we will move on to consider two of the most historically prominent ethical theories, the deontology of Immanuel Kant and the version of utilitarianism defended by John Stuart Mill. Finally, we will conclude by reflecting on how the seemingly impersonal demands of morality can be balanced with the deeply felt modern need to establish a unique, authentic self. TEXTS: Tolstoy, The Death Of Ivan Ilych Plato, Five Dialogues Immanuel Kant, Groundwork for the Metaphysics of Morals David Hume, A Treatise of Human Nature John Stuart Mill, Utilitarianism Charles Guignon, On Being Authentic Charles Taylor, The Ethics of Authenticity

title: Intro to Ethics
description: In this course, we will explore the history of Western ethical thought through close readings of influential texts. Our central questions will be: What is it to organize your life around the good? What kind of commitment, and courage, does this take? What if attaining the good is impossible for human beings, or for us specifically? To address these questions, we will read texts by Aristotle, Plato, Kant, Kierkegaard and Sartre. The course will conclude with Jonathan Learâs Radical Hope: Ethics in the Face of Cultural Devastation. Topics covered include: virtue, courage, love, commitment, wisdom, duty, self-creation and hope. Our goals are: to appreciate the history of Western ethical thought; to understand some of the different frameworks that underlie ethical thinking and intuitions, and to consider how these play out in practice; to understand how conceptions of human nature are built into ethical views; and to critically question different ways of thinking about the good and human life. Texts: Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics. tr. Terence Irwin, Hackett, Second Edition. 0-87220-464-2 Plato, Symposium. tr. Alexander Nehemas and Paul Woodruff, Hackett. 0-87220-076-0 SÃ¸ren Kierkegaard, Fear and Trembling, tr. Alastair Hannay, Penguin â Great Ideas. 978-0-14-303757-6 Jonathan Lear, Radical Hope, Harvard. 0674027469 978-0674027466

title: Intro to Ethics
description: This course will introduce students to the study of ethics by both surveying ethical theories and investigating contemporary ethical issues. We will explore topics such as moral relativism, global justice, the importance of integrity, and how living in a diverse society should affect our ethical decision-making. Our investigation will be guided by the works of significant Western philosophers, both historical and contemporary. This will be taught during the First Summer 2010 Session.

title: Intro to Philosophy
description: This course will examine the question of what it is to be a person. Aristotle famously noted that humans are featherless bipeds, social animals, and rational animals - characterizations that we can, these 2000+ years later still recognize as biological, social, and psychological characterizations. But what is the relation between these very different ways of characterizing what we are? Is one primary? Do they come to the same thing? (That is, could a non-social creature have thoughts of the sort that we do? Could a non-biological being form the kinds of social bonds characteristic of people?) If they are not equivalent, how do they relate? How do we understand things like moral value in these terms? We will consider a wide range views of historical and contemporary philosophers on these issues, views that in many ways shape our current self-conception. In addition to attending class and participating in discussion, students will take a mid-term, a final, and write two short papers. This description applies to PHIL-020-01 through PHIL-020-09.

title: Intro to Philosophy
description: This course is a general introduction to philosophy through film. The first half of the course will be about moral and general skepticism. Moral skeptics maintain that there are no objective moral truths--no moral facts; there are only feelings and culturally induced habits that prompt you to behave non-selfishly. Or perhaps the only moral fact is that you should behave selfishly? General skeptics question whether you have sufficient grounds for thinking there is even a physical world. You think, you reason, you have plenty of experiences, but are they experiences of anything? Is there any reason to believe reality is as you suppose? The second half of the course is metaphysics. Here we will assume you do have a body, and are not just a disembodied mind. You will be asked to explain what the relation is between your mind and your body, and to figure out whether you act freely sometimes or you are merely a cog in the relentless causal machine that is the universe. In addition to watching some useful films you will read excerpts from the writings of great philosophers, extract key concepts and arguments and then analyze those arguments. Four 3-page papers. Participation in class and Blackboard discussions.

title: Intro to Philosophy
description: What is philosophy? Why study philosophy at a university? Is there such a thing as objective truth? What does it mean to be rational? What is an argument? How do we assess arguments? What is a human being? What does it mean to be a person? What are the criteria for personal identity? Can my soul survive the death of my body? If so, will I still be the same person? What is the relationship between mind and body? Are we free or determined? These and other questions will be the main concern of this course. The course will introduce you to some important ideas in philosophy. It will improve your ability to identify and evaluate arguments, to think and write clearly and critically, to gain an understanding of the variety of answers that have been given to questions concerning the nature of persons and to gain an appreciation for some classic and contemporary philosophical texts. Assignments will include: four exams (non-cumulative), regular weblog posts throughout the semester, and several unannounced quizzes on the assigned readings. The texts for this course are: Philosophy: The Quest for Truth Edited by Louis P. Pojman and Lewis Vaughn. Oxford University Press. Seventh Edition (2008). ISBN-10: 0195311329; ISBN-13: 978-0195311327 The Life of the Mind. On the Joys and Travails of Thinking. James V. Schall. ISI Books (2006). Wilmington, Delaware. ISBN:978-1-933859-61-3

title: Intro to Philosophy
description: This course will address philosophically the relation of a human being to other human beings. In a first chapter, we will read Plato as an example of philosophical thinking. In a second chapter we will study the interdependence of single humans beings by means of a classic passage in Hegel's philosophy: the master-slave dialectic. We will use Frederick Douglassâ Memoirs as an illustration of Hegelâs ideas. We will discover the paradoxical consequences of dominance/submission and idealization and the positive function of work and thinking. In chapter three we will study the relation of one human being with privileged others. We will do that by first studying the complexity of the human subject (jokes, slips of the tongue, denials) and then by studying the function of privileged others (parents) in the development of the human subject. We will make use of philosophy of psychoanalysis which will introduce us to the meaning of both mental illness (schizophrenia and depression) and sexual difference. A member of the counseling center will address the class as part of the Engelhardt program. He will concentrate on depression. In a fourth chapter we will analyze the relationship of individuals in the capitalist system. We will, first, reflect on the relationship between economic actors in economic theory using the concepts of private, public and merit goods. We will argue that individuals in the free market must compete (private goods), co-operate (public goods) and must submit to the will of the community (merit goods). Second we will study the consequences for a human person of being a member of a special class. Here we will use Marx' analysis of class conflict and class exploitation. In a fifth and last chapter we will analyze the characteristics of philosophy. We will pay special attention to multi-culturalism as a threat to philosophy in as much as it may lead to skepticism about truth. We will use a book by Jaspers and two videos to provide a summary view of six major civilizations. Readings: Jaspers. Socrates, Buddha, Confucius, Jesus.Harvest. ISBN 0-15-683580-0. Plato. Great Dialogues of Plato. Mentor. ISBN 0-451-62827-6. Douglass, Frederick. Narrative of the life of Frederick Douglass. On the web: //docsouth.unc.edu/douglass/douglass.html Ver Eecke. Denial, Negation and the Forces of the Negative. SUNY Press.ISBN 0-7914-6599-3 Marx. Communist Manifesto. International Publishers. ISBN 0-7178-0241-8 Some articles will be on reserve and on blackboard

title: Intro to Philosophy
description: SUMMER 2011 We will examine the relation between the theory of knowledge and speculative theology in Aristotle/Aquinas and Kant. Specifically, we will try to determine to what extent important positions in their theologies were made possible by positions they had taken in their theories of knowledge so that doubts about the latter would compromise the credibility of the former. There will be an exam worth 30 points on Aristotle/Aquinas each and an exam worth 40 points on Kant. We will try to set a date for the exam on Aristotle/Aquinas that best suits the needs of the majority of the students in the class. Both tests will target the texts covered in the lectures. These texts define the limits of your responsibility Any certified violation of the Universityâs Honor Code will result in an F for the course regardless of any other penalties imposed by the University. This is a Zero Tolerance policy. Office Hours: 12:30-1:15 and by appointment. Required texts: Introduction to Aristotle. McGraw-Hill (0-07-553652-5) Introduction to St. Thomas Aquinas. Modern Library (0-07-553653-6) Critique of Pure Reason. St. Martinâs Press (1-40391-195-9)

title: Philosophy of Sport
description: Course Goals & Description PHIL-098 is an introduction to the discipline of philosophy by way of questions that arise in the context of sports. That is, we will work our way towards philosophical questions by working through more practical questions that we ask ourselves in the context of sports. For example, what (if anything) is wrong with using performance enhancing drugs? Is it cheating? What is it to cheat? And what's wrong with cheating anyhow? Is it dangerous? Many sports are dangerous, some can even be life-threatening. So, what's wrong with something's being dangerous? And even if it is dangerous, does that give anyone aside from the athlete him- or herself the right to stop the athlete from using PED's? Is it unnatural? What does "natural" mean in the context of human beings? Lots of things we do are "unnatural" in some sense. Hi-tech sports equipment is "unnatural" in some sense. Another example: what's the difference between an amateur and a professional, and why does this distinction matter? Do amateurs play for "love of the game," while professionals earn money? Can't someone who earns money, even lots of money, playing a professional sport also or even primarily play for love of the game? Should collegiate athletes be paid? Are big time collegiate athletes exploited by their institutions or by the television networks? Do the pressures of professional or collegiate athletics place an alienating barrier between the athlete and his or her sport? Between the athlete and his or her education? We all debate questions like these, and when we do we often hit barriers to our understanding. Those barriers are philosophical: What are the ethics of competition and games? May anyone dictate to an adult what he or she does to or with his or her own body? What is the distinction between the natural and the artificial? What is exploitation? What is alienation? These are all philosophical questions, and we will examine them through a range of literature, including contemporary contributions to the growing discussion of the philosophy sport and classical contributions by the great philosophers of the past (incl. Kant, Mill, Marx, and others). Texts & Readings A mix of contemporary and classical literature, but the detailed reading list is not entirely settled yet. I will update this page when it is. Assignments & Expectations of Students Students will write some short reflection papers based on their own experiences, some short (5 pp. or so) formal philosophy papers, and probably take a test/quiz or two. What Students Should Know This course fulfills either the General Philosophy or the Ethics component of the Main Campus General Education Requirements in philosophy. Students may complete their requirements by completing any other philosophy course numbered 001-199. This is course is not intended only for university athletes, but it is hoped that it with appeal esp. to students with some serious connection to sports, whether as a collegiate competitor, a high school athlete who has hung up his or her cleats, a recreational athlete, a fan, or anyone who is curious about the questions above.

title: Political and Social Thought
description: Required text: Steven M. Cahn, ed., Classics of Political and Moral Philosophy Other readings will be mad available on E-reserves or Blackboard. This is an introductory course in political and social thought. The focus is on two concepts, freedom and equality, and the presumed tensions between them. We will examine a number of classical theorists, including Aristotle, Locke, Rousseau, Marx, and Mill, as well as some contemporary theorists who Course requirements: 4 papers, each approximately 2500 words: only the 3 papers with the highest grades will count, with each representing 25% of your overall course grade. The remaining 25% of your grade will be based on the oral and written participation requirements set for your weekly discussion section meetings. This descriptions applies to PHIL-099-01 through PHIL-099-06.

title: Ethics: Individual Liberty
description: Can the State legitimately interfere with individual liberty? If yes, then why, when and by what means? Many responses have been given to these questions, and political and moral philosophers, like everyone else, have not reached a consensus on this topic. While there are strong disagreements about the correct response, several general reasons (or principles) have emerged as possible candidates for legitimizing State interference with individual liberty (besides the overall goal of promoting freedom itself), including: - to avoid harming others; - to avoid offending others; - to secure collective benefits; - to avoid harming oneself; - to promote morality (even in the absence of harm to others); - to promote well-being; - to satisfy the requirements of justice. The objective of this course is to critically examine the justification for, and the scope of these âliberty-limitingâ or interference-legitimizing principles. In order to formulate a response to the question, âCan the State legitimately interfere with individual liberty?,â we need to examine three interrelated questions: 1.What is the nature and value of the cluster of the interests referred to as individual âlibertyâ, âfreedomâ, and âautonomyâ, as compared to other interests? 2.What are the proper obligations and responsibilities of the State toward its citizens? 3.Should the state regulate the powerful and pervasive impact on individual liberty of non-State collective actors and institutions (organized religions, associations, families, the economic system, etc.)? We will begin the course by reading Mill (1806-1873) who set the stage for the contemporary debate on individual liberty in On Liberty. We will next discuss how two more recent major philosophers, John Rawls (1921-2002) and Joseph Raz (1939-), have responded to the same questions. We will approach this topic in a variety of ways, including reading philosophical texts, watching documentaries and feature films, and through discussions and debates. Required text: Mill, On Liberty & Other Essays. New York: Oxford University Press, 1991. Assignments (1)Class participation: 5% (2)Very short assignments: 15% (3)One debate: 20% (4)Paper 1 (3 pages): 15% (5)Paper 2 (4 pages): 20% (6)Paper 3 (7 pages): 25%

title: Ethics: Bioethics & the Law
description: BRIDGE COURSE âBIOETHICS, THE END OF LIFE, AND THE LAWâ Course outline This course provides an introduction to some of the most interesting ethical questions surrounding the end(ing) of life in the medical context. After a short introduction to different theories of bioethics, the course examines specific controversial questions, not least whether and if so when the law should permit euthanasia and/or physician-assisted suicide and the withdrawal of life-saving treatment and tube-feeding. Discussion revolves around real-life questions, particularly as raised by case law and legislation. No previous study of ethics or law is, however, required. Students get the opportunity to research, write and present their own paper on a topic chosen after discussion with the professor. To encourage class discussion, the course follows a seminar rather than a lecture format. Assessment is by way of a 5000 word end-of-semester paper (80%) and class participation (20%). Syllabus 1 Introduction to bioethics 2 Introduction to bioethics (and tour of bioethics library) 3 Introduction to bioethics 4 âEuthanasiaâ: definitions, distinctions and history 5 Arguments for legalising euthanasia and physician-assisted suicide 6 Arguments against legalising euthanasia and physician-assisted suicide 7 Discussion of student paper topics 8 Euthanasia in practice: the Netherlands 9 Physician-assisted suicide in practice: Oregon 10 Withholding/withdrawing treatment 11 Withholding/withdrawing tube-feeding 12 Defining death, taking organs & âliving willsâ 13 Individual student presentations 14 Individual student presentations 15 Individual student presentations Course text: John Keown, Euthanasia, Ethics and Public Policy (CUP, 2002) Required initial reading: Alfonso Gomez-Lobo, Morality and the Human Goods (GUP, 2002) Peter Singer, Rethinking Life and Death (OUP,1995) Raanan Gillon, Philosophical Medical Ethics (Wiley, 1986)

title: Ethics: Bioethics
description: We will use key medical and legal cases as our frame for surveying the main issues in contemporary bioethics. Bioethics is a rapidly growing interdisciplinary field that draws (at least!) upon perspectives and expertise from medicine, nursing, public health, law, anthropology, religious studies, literature, psychology, political science, and philosophy. Although we will engage with all of these disciplines to some extent, our focus will be on philosophical methods and perspectives. Topics will include assisted reproduction, genetic engineering, abortion, heath care systems and health resource allocation, health and social justice, physiciansâ duties to their patients, the ethics of medical research, end of life care, social determinants of health, the influence of pharmaceutical companies on health care, and a variety of other topics.

title: Ethics: Bioethics
description: SUMMER 2011 Bioethics: The Beginning and End of Life This course will focus on the ethical implications that advances in medicine and biotechnology have on the beginning and end of life. Beginning-of-life issues discussed will include abortion, stem cell research, in vitro fertilization, and pre-implantation genetic diagnosis. End-of-life issues discussed will include advance directives, assisted suicide, euthanasia, organ donation and transplantation, and the allocation of scarce healthcare resources. This format of the course will, for the most part, combine lecture presentation and class discussion. Films and case studies will also serve as aids to learning. The primary aims of the course are: (1) to introduce students to systematic philosophical approaches to ethics and bioethics; (2) to introduce students to contemporary beginning- and end-of-life bioethical problems; and (3) to assist students in the development of their philosophical thinking and writing skills.

title: Ethics: Aristotleâs Ethics
description: This course will focus on Aristotle's ethics, and also on important contemporary ethical thought influenced by Aristotle's work. One of the ways we will bring Aristotelian ethics into relief is by comparing and contrasting it with alternative ethical theories and examining what other philosophers have had to say -- and what we might want to say -- about some of the topics that come up in his ethics: the nature of virtue, the problem of moral "luck," moral education, and many more. That is, we will spend the semester becoming familiar with and thinking critically about the ethical writings of Aristotle, of those we might call his followers and competitors, and the issues that are raised along the way.

title: Ethics: Utility/Rivals
description: Utilitarianism, the view that our moral duty is to make the world better, is the most well-developed and carefully examined of all ethical theories. In the first half of the semester, we will examine various formulations of the theory and various objections to it. In the second half of the course we will consider alternative theories of moral duty. Readings will include classical philosophical texts as well as more contemporary writings. One goal is to see and appreciate how philosophers have thought about problems of identifying what is right and wrong, good and bad, virtuous and vicious. A second, equally important, is to get students thinking about these problems for themselves. The course emphasizes ethical theory, rather than its application to controversial aspects of contemporary life.

title: Ethics: Just War Theory
description: When is it right for a nation to go to war? Once at war, what sort of behavior becomes permissible for soldiers and commanders? And does the answer to the first question influence the answer to the second? Throughout the semester, we will tackle these questions (and many tangential and subsidiary ones) through the lens of contemporary just wary theory. We'll begin with an overview unit on relevant moral theory, which will include readings in utilitarianism, virtue theory, deontology, and moral nihilism. We will subsequently turn our attention to moral issues concerning the initiation of war (aggression, preemptions, preventive wars, interventions, secessions, reprisals). We shall then turn our focus to conduct within war, and take a close look at the Doctrine of Double Effect, noncombatant immunity, the moral responsibility of soldiers fighting unjust wars, and conditions of "supreme emergency". We'll discuss recently popular trends in just war theory that focus on justice in the conclusion of war (jus post bellum). Finally, we will conclude with a brief unit on alternatives to the just war tradition (realism, pacifism, militarism). While our readings will come primarily from contemporary western just war theory, we will also examine the historical roots of the western just war tradition, as well as just war traditions in other cultures.

title: Ethics: Philosophy & Privacy
description: This course will examine the philosophical dimension of privacy. It will start by getting our philosophical bearings in the various distinctions between the public and the private through readings of John Locke, John Stuart Mill and Hannah Arendt. We will then move on to the different policy perspectives. Warren Brandeis and Warren Samuels in their famous law review article defined privacy as the right to be left alone. Further commentary raised the question of the whether privacy was a single value or a combination of other values. Alan Westin defined privacy as the right to control information about oneself. Since research showed that people differed in their preferences about information collection and use, these ideas naturally led to a focus on informed consent â information collection and use could be made legitimate through a process of appropriate notice and choice. The fair information practices approach to privacy built on that idea and added a number of other protections such as restrictions on further use of information collected for a specified purpose. This approach to protecting the privacy interests of data subjects treats privacy as a fundamental aspect of human dignity and can be traced back to the human rights tradition in political philosophy. An alternative policy approach focuses less on the rights of data subjects and more on the harms that people might suffer as a result of the misuse of information. This outcomes-based framework can also include the idea of granting to data subjects a property right in information that concerns them. It approaches privacy through the lens of economic cost-benefit analysis and can be traced back to the utilitarian perspective in political philosophy. For both approaches the question of how to balance privacy against other values has to be addressed. The final section applies the insights gained from this philosophical assessment of privacy to two contemporary policy issues: government access to personal information for national security purposes and the collection and use of personal information by businesses for purposes such as online behavioral advertising

title: Ethics: Paradoxes/Dilemmas
description: This class is about being trapped. Paradoxes occur when we find ourselves in places that are somehow absurd, and at which we could not have expected to arrive, departing as we did from un?absurd quarters and traveling as we did only on the wheels of eminently reasonable assumptions. The shocking (and sometimes amusing) character of the result usually prompts backtracking, a search for an innocent wrong turn. But in the case of real paradoxes, we can reach no other destination. We are trapped. Dilemmas are another way of being without a way out â insurmountable obstacles or unyielding obligations block both possible exits. While in these cases we will usually either search for a third route by which to escape or try to show that the barriers on one exit are illusory, in genuine dilemmas no such option will ultimately work. We are trapped. In the case of moral paradoxes and moral dilemmas, being trapped is more than just theoretically uncomfortable, for in these cases we find our ability to come to practical conclusions and justify our actions is stunted. We will spend considerable time in this class struggling to find a way out of a number of moral paradoxes and dilemmas, perhaps some of which we will even succeed in escaping. But we will also consider the significance of being trapped, and whether there is any genuine need to escape in all cases. This discussion will involve us in questions about the aims and adequacy of moral theory as such. This class thus focuses on a peculiar intersection of applied and theoretical ethics, for on the one hand we will engage in some popular and gripping applied issues â bioethical questions, ethics in war and obligations to future generations â but on the other we will also be taking up questions about the desiderata and limits of moral theory.

title: Ethics: Law & Morality
description: Not everything that is immoral is illegal, and not everything that is illegal is immoral. But there are important connections between law and morality. We explore them in this course. To what extent may the law limit peopleâs liberty? We examine J.S. Mill's "harm principle" and its application to issues such as paternalistic legislation (seatbelt, helmet, and drug laws), privacy (sexual activity), and freedom of expression (libel, hate speech). These issues raise questions about legal and constitutional interpretation. For example, how should we understand and interpret the First Amendment ("Congress shall make no law abridging the freedom of speechâ¦or of the press") or the Eighth Amendmentâs prohibition of âcruel and unusual punishmentâ? We examine fundamental ideas in criminal and civil law, such as intent, causation, responsibility, and punishment. Topics include the rationales for legal punishment; self-defense as a justification for killing; legal excuses for criminal acts; negligence and strict liability in tort law; and "Good Samaritan" laws requiring people to come to the aid of others. In the last part of the course we investigate questions about the nature of law is and its authority. Does one have a moral obligation to obey the law? Does the legitimacy of law depend on a correspondence with moral or natural law?

title: Ethics: Queer Theory
description: In this course, we will analyze conceptions of gender and sexuality, with a special focus on the efforts societies make in creating and enforcing conditions that are supposed by those societies to be ânatural.â We will frame the issue by looking at Michel Foucaultâs landmark Discipline and Punish, which highlights the extent to which we are the product of disciplinary institutions as well the way those institutions attempt to pass themselves off as natural and inevitable rather than as the historical accidents that they are. We will then make a first go of analyzing the gendering projectâexamining the ways in which both men and women are the product of robustly normative systems. Subsequently, weâll examine the specific role that sexuality plays in the gendering process, highlighting the normative privilege that heterosexual identity brings with it. In the final sections of the class, we will consider how the lives of trans and intersex persons complicate theories of gender and sexuality as well as what might constitute the proper moral and political responses to deeply ingrained practices based upon compulsory heterosexuality. Due to the subject matter of the course, we will be consideringâin addition to philosophical textsâwork in biology, sociology, history, and psychology. Additionally, we will be drawing on the resources of mass culture, including television and film, as source materials for inquiry and discussion. [N.B.: This course will not be a forum for discussing questions about whether non-heterosexual sexualities are morally problematic; students interested in that sort of question are encouraged to take one of the Philosophy Departmentâs courses that address the issue of sexual ethics.]

title: Ethics: Forgiveness
description: Forgiveness is a central concept in our lives; we seek and are petitioned for forgiveness of transgressions quite often. However, there are a number of difficult questions that arise when we examine this concept closely. Depending on how we understand forgiveness and its relationship with such concepts as virtue, justice, punishment, blame, reconciliation, etc., we may arrive at very different moral implications for both the party who has committed the wrong and the party who has been wronged. What conditions must a wrongdoer meet to earn forgiveness? Is unconditional forgiveness permissible, or might there be cases in which the victim of a wrong must (or at least morally ought to) withhold forgiveness? Are there certain actions/agents that may appropriately be deemed unforgivable, and what does such a claim amount to? Is forgiveness only interpersonal, or is there a kind of social or political forgiveness, as well? The first section of this course will cover some classical texts which bear on this topic (mainly philosophical, but likely some religious perspectives as well), and will provide us with a background in different broad approaches to thinking about morality. The second â and most substantial â part will focus primarily on more contemporary discussions of forgiveness and its related concepts. In the final section of the course, we will apply our discussion to some current ethical and political issues (to be determined during the semester).

title: Ethics: Global Justice
description: Over a billion people in the world live in extreme poverty. Millions die from poverty-related causes each year. Much of the worldâs wealth is concentrated in the hands of relatively few. Most people would agree that these conditions are unfortunate; however, many philosophers have argued that they are more than unfortunateâthey are unjust. Are they? If so, why? In this course, we will explore a number of questions related to global justice, including: -Is there a universal standard of justice that we can apply in different cultures? -How much does justice require of us? -What human rights do people have and why do they have them? -Are we responsible for global poverty in any way? -Are we justified in showing partiality to those near and dear to us when we decide whom to help? We will approach these questions by both exploring the resources of major ethical theories in Western philosophy and focusing on more specific issues related to global justice, including fair trade, the ethics of medical research in the developing world, and whether we have obligations to stem global climate change.

title: Ethics: Global Justice
description: Over a billion people in the world live in extreme poverty. Millions die from poverty-related causes each year. Much of the worldâs wealth is concentrated in the hands of relatively few. Most people would agree that these conditions are unfortunate; however, many philosophers have argued that they are more than unfortunateâthey are unjust. Are they? If so, why? In this course, we will explore a number of questions related to global justice, including: -Does justice require us to work to alleviate global poverty? Or is it a matter of charity? -How much does justice require of us? -Are we responsible for global poverty in any way? -Are we justified in showing partiality to those near and dear to us when we decide whom to help? -Is there a universal standard of justice that we can apply in different cultures? We will explore these questions by reading ethical and political theorists as well as discussing practical issues related to global justice, such as the ethics of medical research in the developing world.

title: Ethics: Aquinas
description: TBA

title: Ethics: Pol./Legal Authority
description: Do we have a moral duty to obey the government? Sometimes the answer is an obvious âYes.â The law prohibits murder; we are morally obliged to refrain from murder. But in this case, we must obey because the law instructs us to do what morality already requiresââmurder is independently wrong, no matter what the government says about it. So can the government make something thatâs otherwise unproblematic (like skateboarding) wrong by forbidding it? To put the question more generally: Does a reasonably just governmentâs say-so always carry moral weight? This practical questionâwhether the governmentâs say-so carries moral weightâwill serve as our entry point for philosophical theorizing. In an attempt to answer it, weâll have to consider a wide range of political and moral theories. By way of quick preview, weâll study the nature of genuine authority, the nature of government and law, and look at what kinds of moral theories our most promising defenses of political authority presuppose. Readings will include selections from Aristotle, Locke, Kant, Rawls, Mill, and others. âScheduleâ of Topics: Weâll start by spending a week (or so) distinguishing coercive force, de facto authority (coercive force that citizens believe is legitimate), and de jure authority (the real, full-blooded, normatively potent item). With these distinctions in hand, weâll turn to a few arguments against the existence of political authority. Weâll read Robert Paul Wolffâs famous argument, and whatever else catches my eye as appropriate for undergrads. The aim here is to problematize political authority; I suspect that most students will take its legitimacy for granted. Weâll then examine a series of defenses of political authority. Since consent theory is so prominent in American thought, weâll probably begin with it. Locke is a good figure to read here. The problems with consent theory will be obvious: Most of us have never consented; tacit consent is a sham. The failure of consent theory will inspire a move to Rawlsian hypothetical consent theory. I donât have a definite order in mind for the remaining defenses of political authority. But weâll look at theories that invoke a natural duty of fair play, natural law theories (possibly along with other theories that try to derive political authority from (alleged) divine authority), and instrumental theories. If all goes to plan, the students will at this point understand what real authority is, recognize that it requires a justification, and see that our best attempts to justify it are not obviously successfulâthough, of course, they will be encouraged to rehabilitate them in their papers! Next, weâll shift gears and to bring issues that have been in the background to the foreground. The idea is to motivate foundational philosophical issues by grappling with the practical problem of political authority. These background issues include the nature of government and law (Are these institutions the sort of things that can be authoritative?); what kind of moral theory can accommodate true authority (Arguably not, for example, Utilitarianism.). Weâll conclude with a week dedicated to thinking about what it would meanââpractically speakingââif anarchism is true.

title: Ethics: Moral Disagreements
description: We find ourselves inclined to a variety of reactions to deep moral disagreement. Sometimes we find ourselves thinking that our side is right and those on the other side must be evil or crazy. At other times it seems that the persistent disagreement indicates that our different moral beliefs are "just our opinions" and neither side is right or wrong. Similarly, our reactions to disagreement vary from hostility and contempt toward those with whom we disagree to the feeling that the disagreement isn't really about anything important in the first place. Part of this course will be devoted to considering the various things we can say and do about moral disagreement. We will ask questions such as these: When should we conclude that the disagreement wasn't important to begin with? Can we maintain that a persistent disagreement is more than just a difference of opinion without concluding that the proponents of one side or the other are either irrational or bad? When should we stand up to those we think are wrong and when should we tolerate their behavior and beliefs? What would it look like to treat those we genuinely think are wrong about something important with respect and dignity? Of course the details will depend on the particular disagreement in question. Is there anything to be said for a rival side of an issue or would a person have to be bad or irrational to believe it? Is there in fact any good reason to believe one answer to some question rather than another? If so, is there more reason to stick with my old opinion and behavior, or might I have good reason change? Since the way we answer these questions will depend on what the parties to the dispute have to say in favor of their particular claims, we will need to look at some particular disputes and the rival claims advanced. Some of the most contentious contemporary moral questions revolve around economic and sexual ethics. What's more, both money and sex are relevant to each of our lives. So, in part of the course, we will look at some of those contentious questions and the arguments in favor of the various answers proposed.

title: Ethics: Debates About Reason
description: The use of reason has long been taken to be one of the defining aspects of a distinctively human existence. Human beings, we are all familiar with being told, are rational animals, thinking things, etc. Just as distinctive, however, is the fact that human beings are moral agents, creatures motivated to live in a way that reflects deeply held values concerning what is right and good. The aim of this course is to explore the relation between these two crucial dimensions of human existence. In the first part of the course we will examine the philosophical tradition inaugurated by Plato and carried on by Kant and modern day theorists such as John Rawls and JÃ¼rgen Habermas that understands moral agency as a particular form of rational agency. For this tradition, made canonical by Kohlbergâs theory of moral development, the mature moral agent is one who regulates her conduct according to the impartial application of universal principles. In the second half of the course we will take a critical eye toward this tradition, using Carol Gilliganâs feminist critique of Kohlberg, as well as the work of Aristotle and Hume, as a point of departure. One of our main objectives here will be to decide whether the rationalist tradition can adequately capture the lived texture of our moral experience. For example, we will ask whether there are aspects that may seem central to the moral dimension of our livesâemotional responses, intuition, our attachments to family and friendsâthat are left unaccounted for, neglected, or even outright disparaged by rationalist approaches to moral agency. In the end, all of our authors will help us get a better sense of the richness and complexity of moral experience, and in the process, perhaps, come to a deeper understanding of ourselves. Plato, Republic Immanuel Kant, Groundwork for the Metaphysics of Morals David Hume, A Treatise of Human Nature Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics

title: Ethics: Kantian Constructivism
description: One of the most influential strands of theory in contemporary ethics traces its source to the moral philosophy of Immanuel Kant. Theorists of this stripe seek to disentangle Kantâs ethical theory from some of his more troubling metaphysical presuppositions while retaining his emphasis on the free and rational human will as the ultimate source of value and obligation. Constructivists draw on Kantâs theory to answer fundamental questions about the nature of value, sources of moral normativity, forms of moral judgment, and structure of the human will. This course is organized around the three formulations of the ethical directive (the âCategorical Imperativeâ) at the core of Kantâs moral philosophy, with selected readings from constructivists whose theories are most firmly moored in each of these. The final portion of the course takes up the application of Kantian constructivism to contemporary moral issues in political philosophy, global justice, and environmental ethics. Readings from Immanuel Kant, John Rawls, Thomas Hill, Onora OâNeill, Barbara Herman, T.M. Scanlon, Christine Korsgaard, and Stephen Darwall, among others.
