title: Ethics: Is Morality Possible?
description: What do we mean when we utter sentences like âmurder is morally wrongâ or âyou should not cheat on that examâ? Do moral judgments state facts about the world, or are they relative to particular cultures, maybe even particular communities? Do they offer opinions and feelings rather than describing actual moral properties? Is our tendency to make ethical judgments simply an evolutionary byproduct? Or is moral language a relic of an outmoded, anti-scientific world-view? Questions like these cast doubt on the very possibility of morality. They challenge not the truth of particular moral claims--such as the idea that abortion is wrong or that suicide is ethically permissible--but the concept of moral truth itself. Because they are questions about rather than within ethics, they are âmeta-ethical.â This course provides an introduction to the subject matter of ethics, with a particular focus on meta-ethical concerns like the ones sketched above. It includes an overview of various normative ethical theories as well as a discussion of moral realism.

title: Ethics: The Untold War
description: In this course, we examine issues in the moral psychology and moral philosophy of war. While just war theory has long been a central focus in understanding war from a moral and legal point of view, and traumatic illness (posttraumatic stress disorder) a focus in the clinical treatment of soldiers, little attention has been given to the moral psychology of soldiering itself. How do civilians take up the role of soldier? How do they leave that role behind? What sort of role duties are involved in being a citizen soldier? What moral quandaries and emotional conflicts do individual soldiers face as they rev up for war, deploy, and return home? Are feelings of revenge morally justified in war? Is survivor guilt or luck guilt a rational response to loss? In short, how do we conceive the interior moral landscape of soldiering? By listening to soldiers own narratives and exploring a wide range of readings in the moral philosophy and psychology of war, we shall try to understand the untold internal war that soldiers often fight.

title: Ethics: Public Policy
description: This course is an introduction to the study of ethics, from a theoretical and practical perspective, as it relates to public policy. The course will begin with a close reading of classic texts including works of Aristotle, Kant, and Mill, which try to make sense of what makes human lives and actions good, happy, and meaningful. We will subject these views to contemporary reflections on what it means to be a moral actor, considering pointed critiques of traditional western thinking about ethics. Throughout, we will be asking ourselves about the constraints that ethical considerations might ask us to impose on our own freedom, and whether there are elements of the ethical life that are not within our control. Finally, we will apply these ethical frameworks to public policy asking how ethical considerations inform (or fail to inform) our public policy through close study of specific issues of contemporary national, political and global significance such as international migration, poverty, and corporate globalization. Throughout the course we will explore the question of how and when morally evaluative judgments are possible concerning the practices of individuals, communities, corporations, and nations. Requirements of the course will include two papers, a mid-term and final examination, and class participation.

title: Ethics: Research Ethics
description: The goal of human health related research is to produce generalizable knowledge and advance our understanding of important medical conditions and other factors affecting our health. However, as several past (and present) research scandals have made clear, conducting this research raises a number of ethical concerns. This course will examine issues that pose challenges for efforts to ensure that research is ethically and scientifically sound, including subject recruitment, informed consent, risk-benefit assessments, clinical trial design, vulnerable populations, and social justice. The objective of this course is to introduce students to basic ethical theories and methods that can be used to critically analyze and respond to ethical challenges arising in the context of clinical, public health, and international research.

title: Introduction to Logic
description: In this course we will examine the formal structure of arguments; we will critically examine the relationship between an argument's premises and its conclusion. Initially we will expose common fallacies of reasoning that you are sure to come across in many contexts of public discourse. Then we will develop a system of formalizing the basic propositional structure of arguments, which you will use to prove that the conclusions of some arguments necessarily follow from their premises. In addition, we will utilize modern methods of semantics to uncover the deeper conceptual relationships that commonly hold among sentences of convincing arguments. You will be able to demonstrate why such arguments convince, and, perhaps, why some initially appealing arguments are nonetheless illogical. In addition to satisfying the philosophy major's requirement in logic, successful completion of this course will lead to better skills in reasoning on any topic.

Assignments will include: four exams (non-cumulative) and regular homework exercises throughout the semester. The text for this course is: Gary Hardegree: Symbolic Logic: a First Course, McGraw-Hill, 1999 (3rd ed.) ISBN 0-07-239027-1

title: Intro to Logic
description: SUMMER 2011 We gain much of the knowledge we possess through reasoning, and logic is the study of how we ought to reason. Mastery of logic is useful not only to philosophers, but to all those who go on to engage in any kind of persuasive practice (i.e., all of you). The goal of this course will be to familiarize students with the standards and common mistakes of reasoning, and to prepare philosophy majors for further study in philosophical logic or other branches of the discipline. After a brief overview of arguments in general, the first half of the semester will be devoted to deductive logicâsyllogistic and propositional logic. These two units will give students a working knowledge of validity and soundness. For the last third of the semester, we will turn our attention to the more widely applicable inductive logic, through which we will broaden our understanding of the standards of reason. This course will satisfy the logic requirement for undergraduate Philosophy majors.

title: Christian Creeds
description: In the past thirty years or so there has been a lot of work within philosophy on various Christian doctrines, including the rationality of Christian belief, the divine attributes, creation, the Trinity, the Incarnation, the Atonement, resurrection of the body, and heaven and hell. We are going to read and discuss together some of this work. Our aim is to use the tools of philosophy to try to make sense of various ideas central to the Christian creeds. We are not trying to use philosophy to prove that the Christian creeds are true; we are asking, rather, if the propositions of the Christian creeds are true, what light philosophical investigation can shed on them. I should note up front that I am a Christian and will not pretend to be neutral on the question of the literal truth of the creeds. But anybody with an interest in these questions, whether or not he or she shares these commitments, is welcome to take part in these investigations.

title: Metaphysics
description: In contemporary philosophy metaphysics means the philosophical inquiry into what seem to be the most fundamental features of us and reality in general and into questions of ultimate explanation. This course is an introduction to a number of key issues in metaphysics as they have been treated in Anglo-American philosophy (with some incursions from the Antipodes) in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. We will focus on issues selected from the following list: (1) The problem of universals. Different things seem to share common properties â two white pieces of paper, for example, seem to share a property: whiteness. Are there really such common properties? If so, what sort of things are they? (2) Past, present and future. We experience time as flowing: what is now present once was not present but future, and it will be past. But does time really flow? Is the flow of time in some sense a psychological phenomenon, or does it characterize reality apart from the mind? How does this issue relate to the question of the reality of the past and future? (3) Free will. We like to think we have free will. But what is free will? Is it true, as some philosophers argue, that we can only have free will if our free acts are caused in a way utterly different from causation in other cases? (4) Mind and Body. What sort of thing are we? Are we just complex physical organisms or are we to be identified with something else, a non-physical mind or soul? (5) Ultimate explanation. Why is there this world? More fundamentally, why is there anything at all? Are these questions to be answered by reference to something many would call a god?

title: Philosophy of Education (CBL)
description: TBA

title: Political Philosophy
description: The purposes of this course are to enable you to find your way around the field of political philosophy, identify its main intellectual problems and solutions and apply its characteristic patterns of thought when reading new texts or understanding current political issues. Weâll do this by examining some classical, modern and contemporary works of political philosophy. Weâll start with the classical philosophers, Plato and Aristotle, and see how their views are transformed by the medieval thinker, Aquinas. Then we will read the modern social contract theorists, Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau, and their critic, Hume. Weâll cover Kant and Mill, noting the fundamental contrast in the way they ground the political ideals of freedom and autonomy. Weâll end with the contemporary philosophers Rawls, Nozick, Taylor and Habermas, focusing on the contrast between the liberal and communitarian conceptions of public life. One organizing theme is social justice, that is, how the benefits and burdens of collective life should be distributed. Another theme concerns the different notions of freedom as the absence of constraints, participation in political life and obedience to a law one gives oneself. Every conception of social justice and human freedom presupposes a theory of human nature and a social ideal â a vision of the aims or purposes of social cooperation and collective life generally. Another important goal of the course is to make explicit and evaluate these competing visions of human nature and the social life. At the end, you should have a sense of how these philosophers understand the fundamental political notions of justice, democracy, liberty and equality. Throughout we will have an eye on contemporary applications of these notions.

title: East/West: Reason & the Self
description: Some claims that have played a central role in the development of certain eastern philosophies (such as Buddhism and Daoism) are regarded as heterodoxy in the West. For example, whereas Descartes famously argued that the most fundamental belief about which he was directly and non-inferentially certain was that he existed, the belief in anatman (or, no-self) lies at the heart of traditional Buddhist philosophy. From no-self and idealism (rather than materialism) to arguments about the cause of suffering and the limits of reason, topics that have typically been found on the fringes of western philosophy have played a prominent role in several eastern philosophies. This course is designed to critically examine these claims: the cause of suffering, no self, non-being, and the denial of non-contradiction. These are claims that are in many ways central to questions about who we are and how we out to live, claims that many of us in the west have never thought to question. The course will include readings from traditional eastern philosophies (E.g., Buddhist sutras, the Dao de ching, Zen sayings) in order to better understand how these claims fit within specific philosophical systems as well readings from classic western philosophy (E.g., Plato, Aristotle, Epicurus) in hopes of better understanding why these claims seem counter-intuitive to many western readers. The central enterprise of the course, however, will be an examination of recent defenses of these traditionally eastern claims by western philosophers such as Perry, Siderits, and Parfit; as well as, to a lesser degree, recent criticisms of these claims by western philosophers such as Korsgaard.

title: Philosophy of Language
description: This course will provide an overview of issues in contemporary analytic philosophy of language, focusing on the nature of meaning, the relation between language and thought, and the role of context in language.

title: State/Indvd Freedom
description: Why is the state justified in limiting individual freedom? This course will introduce students to political philosophy by focusing on this central question. Since the work of John Stuart Mill, many political philosophers have argued to the state can legitimately limit individual freedom to avoid harm to others. We will begin the course by exploring Millâs work and then discuss how contemporary philosophers have responded to the same question. In contemporary philosophy, a central question is whether the state should remain neutral toward religious, moral and philosophical views. For example, does respect for different religious views require deregulated home-schooling? Does neutrality require the legalization of same-sex marriage? After considering arguments for neutrality, we will study critics of the principle of state neutrality. We will approach this topic in a variety of ways, including reading philosophical texts, watching movies and documentaries, and discussing and debating.

title: The State and Individual Freedom
description: Why is the state justified in limiting individual freedom? This course will introduce students to political philosophy by focusing on this central question. After considering arguments for neutrality, we will study critics of the principle of state neutrality. We will approach this topic in a variety of ways, including reading philosophical texts, watching movies and documentaries, and discussing and debating. This course will be taught during Summer 2010 Presession.

title: Philosophy and Star Trek
description: Star Trek is very philosophical. What better way, then, to learn philosophy, than to watch Star Trek, read philosophy, and hash it all out in class? That's the plan. This course is basically an introduction to certain topics in metaphysics and epistemology philosophy, centered around major philosophical questions that come up again and again in Star Trek. In conjunction with watching Star Trek, we will read excerpts from the writings of great philosophers, extract key concepts and arguments and then analyze those arguments. The questions that we will wrestle with include: I. Is time travel possible? Could we go back and kill our grandmothers? What is the nature of time? II. Could reality be radically different from what "we" (I?) think? Could we be brains in vats? III. What is the relation between a person's mind and his functioning brain--are they separate substances or identical? Can persons survive death? Can computers think? Is Data a person? IV. What is a person? When do we have one person, and when do we have two (think of the episodes where people "split" or are "fused")? V. Do people have free will, or are they determined by the laws of nature to do exactly what they wind up doing, while believing they have free will? Or both? What is free will? Course requirements: Four papers, 1000 words long, typed, double-spaced, on topics suggested a week in advance in class. Participation in discussions.

title: The British Empiricists
description: This course will examine the central issues in Locke, Berkeley, and Hume, with a particular focus on their theories of mind, language and knowledge.

title: The British Empiricists
description: This course will examine the central issues in Locke, Berkeley, and Hume, with a particular focus on their theories of mind, language and knowledge.

title: Meaning & Thought
description: Concepts are the fundamental building blocks of thought. You have a concept of whales and a concept of mammals, and these two concepts are key components of your belief that whales are mammals. Moreover, it is natural to suppose that the meanings of our words are essentially derived from our concepts. But just what are concepts? Where do they come from and how do they relate to one another? How is it that concepts, which are in (or part of) the mind, can be about things in the external world? In this course, weâll examine various answers to these questions, with a particular emphasis on how these answers impact cognitive science. It is recommended, though not required, that the student have completed one general philosophy course prior to taking this class.

title: Democracy and Religion
description: This course aims to introduce students to social and political philosophy through an investigation of the ideal of democracy and the challenge of religion. The course is divided into three parts. In part I, four fundamental concepts of social and political philosophy are introduced: âlibertyâ, âequalityâ, âjusticeâ, and âauthorityâ. For each of these concepts, we will look at an influential text in the history of Western thought (classical or modern), as well as get introduced to and assess competing interpretations of that concept. This should equip students with the conceptual apparatus we will be relying on for the rest of the course. In part II, the ideal of democracy is approached as the preferred social and political arrangement that promises to grant us legitimate political authority, place liberty and equality at its core, and achieve justice. We will look at the republican, liberal, and deliberative views of democracy, and assess them while taking a pluralistic social context as our starting point - though we will also be questioning this starting point. Finally, in part III we will introduce some of the challenges of religion to the preferred ideal of democracy by looking at contemporary debates of multiculturalism and citizenship with a focus on the demands made by Islamic groups and intellectuals.

title: Plato & Aristotle: Political Phil
description: The course will focus on a close reading and active discussion of Plato's REPUBLIC & LAWS, & Aristotle's POLITICS. Course requirements include: (1) a number of short essays; (2) group presentations; (3) active class participation.

title: Problems of Democracy
description: Why do we care about democracy? Why should anyone? What is it that we value when we value democratic life? These are fundamental questions about what sort of society we should want to be a part of, and the Western tradition of thought has much to say about them. In this course we will encounter and evaluate ancient, modern and âpost-modernâ conceptions of what politics is all about. Our aim will be to deepen and sharpen our ability to think through the problems and possibilities of politics generally, and of democratic life in particular, as they have been identified by a diverse assortment of thoughtful voicesâboth those of writers and of political actorsâthroughout the Western political tradition. These voices range from Plato, Jean-Jacques Rousseau and J.S. Mill to Judith Butler, Malcolm X and Emma Goldman. We will find that reflecting philosophically about the problems of putting democracy into practice helps us to develop a more sophisticated understanding of our own commitment to it. Along the way we will explore the following sorts of questions: What exactly are the benefits of democratic governance? What are its costs? Should we obey the law? Why? What is the role of resistance and conflict in a democratic society? Can it make such a society freer and more humane, or must it always be counter-productive? Are politics personal? Where, if anywhere, do politics end and the privacy of the individual begin? Must we overlook or lay aside what makes us different from others, such as our gender, ethnic or religious identities, when casting a vote or engaging with others in politics? Was Thomas Jefferson right to insist that âevery generation needs a new revolutionâ? The courseâs graded content will include one short and two medium-length written assignments as well as class participation, of which contributions to in-class discussions of the assigned material will form a major part.

title: Causation & Casual Interference
description: Does smoking cause lung cancer? Instinctively, after decades of scientific research and public health campaigns, we are likely to answer in the affirmative. Yet not all smokers develop lung cancer and not all individuals with lung cancer smoke. So what do we mean when we say smoking âcausesâ lung cancer? What constitutes a causal relationship? What methods do we have for identifying such relationships? The answers to these questions will have important implications for our abilities to explain, predict, intervene on, and assign responsibility for events. In this course, we will use readings, class discussions, and online modules to explore and critically examine classical and contemporary accounts of causation and causal inference. We will then use these tools to analyze âappliedâ problems of causal inference, including legal responsibility, health claims, and policy interventions.

title: Other (Kinds of) Minds
description: How do we know what other people are thinking, given that we seem to have only indirect access to their beliefs and desires? Do we have any reason for thinking that other people have minds that are fundamentally like our own? Philosophers have often asked what it takes to have a mind, and this turns out to be a very hard question. The problem is, although we use often psychological terms like âbeliefâ, âdesireâ, and âintentionâ to explain the behavior of multinational corporations and governments, people and household pets, laptop computers and automobiles, some of these appeals are surely intended in a metaphorical or figurative tone of voice. In this course, we will examine a variety of philosophical and psychological attempts to explain what it means to have a mind. We will then turn to questions about the kinds of minds that other entities possess. We will ask whether it is possible to understand the minds of persons with serious mental illnesses or cognitive disabilities; we will ask about the assumptions and presuppositions that underlie the study of non-human minds (including apes, monkeys, cats, rats, snakes, spiders, and cockroaches); and we will ask whether it is possible to build an intelligent machine.

title: Hallucinating
description: We all make mistakes. We all sometimes remember things that didn't really happen and sometimes forget things that really did. We all get confused by optical illusions, and phantom pains and itches. We all sometimes believe false things other people tell us. We all sometimes confuse our dreams for reality. We all sometimes believe things because we really want them to be, even though we have good reasons for thinking they aren't. And sometimes we're just plain hallucinating. How can we be sure that we're not mistaken about everything? What kinds of thing can we know for sure? What is knowledge anyway? We'll look at some old, not so old, and even less old answers to and ways of thinking about these questions. Some of these will come from philosophers. But some will come as well from science, science fiction, literature, film, art, etc. The views we come across will be very different, and we'll spend a lot of time trying to sort out our own reactions to these questions. We'll all probably end up with different answers ourselves---but at least they'll be our own answers, and we'll have good reasons for them. (That's the philosophy part.) Here are just a few of the things we'll be looking at: â¢ The films The Matrix and Existenz * Kafka's ``The Metamorphosis"; Thompson's Fear and Loathing in Los Vegas; Burroughs' Naked Lunch; Ellison's The Invisible Man; etc. * Hume's A Treatise of Human Nature (parts) * Recent research in cognitive science and psychology (of individuals and groups) * Some selections from Stanislaw Lem (the good stuff) * The Star Trek pilot episode, ``The Cage" * Some more philosophy stuff * Parts of Don Quixote * Religion and other mass delusions.

title: Evolutionary Biology
description: ISSUES IN EVOLUTIONARY BIOLOGY Phil-196-01 Jay Reuscher This course is about a variety of philosophical problems located around the axis of two seemingly irreducible perspectives on the relation between brain and mind. One is the first person viewing point that claims to report reliably about the operations and structures of human consciousness from the privileged perspective of an immediate internal observer. The other is the third person viewing point of neuroscience and evolutionary biology and psychology. This perspective bases its claims on the results of empirical research. At this time, it seems to be agreed that there is no available passage between these two perspectives that would allow one to read the specific content of the other. This course intends to provide the students with an orderly introduction to these two perspectives and some of the conflicts they seem to generate. The first perspective will be represented by Kantâs Critique of Pure Reason and his Critique of Judgment. The second perspective will be represented by Darwinism and Philosophy, Evolution and the Human Mind, and What Darwin Got Wrong. The implications of what one has to say about the relation between these two views or zones of information have momentous consequences for the traditional conception of a human being. Once I see how many students are in the class, Iâll be able to design a strategy for looking at some current attempts at an integrating theory. What one will have to do to get a grade for the course will also have to wait until I know the size of the class. I prefer to use discussions based on student reports about their readings in current material of their choice as the basis for the grade but that requires a small group as its condition. Required Texts: Kant. Critique of Pure Reason and Critique of Judgment. [Both are available in the book store.] Evolution and the Human Mind. Peter Carruthers and Andrew Chamberlain, Editors. Cambridge (0-521-7890-7 pbk.) [Order from Amazon.] Darwinism and Philosophy. Vitori Hosler and Christian Illiers, Editors. Notre Dame Press (0-268-03073-1 pbk.) [Order from Amazon.] What Darwin Got Wrong. Jerry Fodor and Masimo Piatelli-Palmerini. Farrar, Straus and Giroux (978-0-374-28879-2) [Order from Amazon.]

title: Philosophy of Freedom
description: This is a collectively-led course which focuses on the theory and practice of participatory freedom. Our initial readings will center on the work of Jacques Ranciere: his book, The Ignorant Schoolmaster, and Todd May's two short books on Ranciere's work: The Political Thought of Jacques Ranciere and Contemporary Political Movements and The Thought of Jacques Ranciere. These books lay out an idea of radical democracy that conceives of political freedom as essentially beginning in a methodological presumption of absolute equality. The third book looks at real world examples that have attempted to put ideas like this into practice. The rest of the course will explore applications of this idea to concrete areas of our own lives. We will also criticize and attempt to enrich this approach to political philosophy and, as a sort of micro-experiment, put the idea into practice in the course. That is, after the initial readings, the entire course - readings, course structure, assignments, etc. - will be the result of a collaborative process involving everyone in the course. Professor Lance will serve as facilitator of the initial parts of this process, but all will be equally involved in decision-making. This methodology means that students will need a greater level of commitment to the course than is usual. It does not signal an "easier" course. Quite the contrary. It signals that aspects of the labor involved in a course typically taken up solely by the professor are now the collective responsibility of everyone. We will pick additional topics - especially domains of life that can be examined in terms of the concrete ways that they foster or inhibit freedom and in terms of the ways that they could be reformed to foster greater freedom. We will work through the best ways to approach those topics. And we will devise projects - writing, but not necessarily only writing - that we will each engage in throughout the course. Students will be expected to begin the preliminary work of sorting out course content in the summer. Professor Lance will be in touch with everyone enrolled prior to the beginning of Fall so that this decision-making process can get going without our losing large hunks of the course time. Two prior courses taught by Professor Lance are relevant background to this course â Anarchism and Free Will â but these are not required pre-requisites. Students are encouraged to get in touch with Professor Lance if they have any questions about the content or nature of the course. (This course also counts as a JUPS elective. It is not a gen ed philosophy course.)

title: Ethical Theory
description: This is an ethical theory course designed for philosophy majors. The course will survey some of the major ethical theories, touching on the goals and methods of ethical theory, but focusing more on the substantive differences between the theories and attempting to adjudicate among them. We shall read representives from virtue theory, Kantianism, and utilitarianism. Among the authors read will be Aristotle (virtue theory), Kant (Kantianism), Bentham, Mill, and Sidgwick (utilitarianism), as well as contemporary writers who represent some of these respective traditions, such as Nussbaum, Rawls, and Singer.

title: Philosophy of Law
description: This is an upper-division undergraduate course on some central problems of the philosophy of law, problems centered on the role that the lawâs authority ought to play in our proper understanding of it. The first (and somewhat longer) part of the course is concerned with analytical jurisprudence. Here we will be considering various theories of the nature of law using the idea of lawâs authority as a guidepost. Thus, we will be asking: What is authority? What are the various ways that persons, or institutions, can bear authority? What sorts of authority, if any, are essential to law? And how should our account of the nature of law be shaped by the constraint that law be authoritative in these particular ways? The second (and somewhat shorter) part of the course is concerned with normative jurisprudence. Here we will be asking whether law really bears legitimate authority over those subject to it; and if so, how far that authority extends; and how whatever authority the law has is to be explained. Thus, we will be asking: What are the various ways in which claims to authority can be made good? Does the law show itself to be authoritative by any of these ways? How, then, should we understand the scope and limits of legitimate legal authority? Course requirements include (a) a medium-length midterm paper, (b) a longer final paper, (c) some presentations in class, and (d) faithful, prepared, and active attendance through the whole semester.

title: Post-Structuralism: Derrida-Zizek
description: The Political Legacy of (Post-/)Structuralism: From Derrida to Å½iÅ¾ek. The term "structuralism" (and post-structuralism alike) refers to a political, literary and aesthetic expansion of continental philosophy that developed in the second half of the 20th century parallel to early analytic philosophy. It is known for a critical review of normative concepts in classical philosophy and combines its insight with phenomenology. Derrida is known as the founder of "deconstruction", Foucault as the founder of the methodology of "discourse analysis" and Slavoj Zizek is known as a political philosopher who shifts psychoanalytic insights of Jacques Lacan into the domain of political philosophy. In the class we will read seminal texts from the aforementioned authors to understand what the political legacy of structuralism is all about and combine them with texts of other contemporary philosophers whose theories dwell on these insights. In doing so we will see how contemporary philosophers like Badiou, Mouffe and Laclau take a stand against the once proclaimed "death of the subject" and re-evaluate subjectivity as a politically productive excess. Books: Ferdinand de Saussure: Course in General Linguistics (London 1983) Friedrich Nietzsche: The Nietzsche Reader, Oxford 2006. Jacques Derrida, Writing and Difference, University of Chicago Press 1980 Jacques Derrida: The Derrida Reader, Edinburgh 1998. Jacques Derrida: Margins of Philosophy, University of Chicago Press 1985. Foucault / Rabinow (Hg.): The Foucault Reader. Alain Badiou: St. Paul. The Foundation of Univesalism, Stanford UP 2003. Badiou / Zizek: Philosophy in the Present, Polity 2009. Alain Badiou: Metapolitics, Verso 2005 Ernesto Laclau, Emancipation(s), London / New York: Verso 1996, Peter Hallward, Badiou: A Subject to Truth, Minnesota: University of Minnesota Press 2003 Chantal Mouffe: The Return of the Political, Verso 2005 Ernesto Laclau / Chantal Mouffe: Hegemony and Socialist Strategy Slavoj Å½iÅ¾ek: The Zizek-Reader Slavoj Å½iÅ¾ek: The Puppet and the Dwarf, MIT Press 2003 Slavoj Å½iÅ¾ek: Violence, Profile Books 2008. Slavoj Å½iÅ¾ek: The Sublime Object of Ideology, Verso 2009

title: Kantian View of Mind/Brain
description: The overarching claim of Kantâs critical philosophy is that a naÃ¯ve or uncritical application of philosophy to questions raised by developments in science not only fails to throw any useful light on the issues, it can actually make matters worse by producing puzzles for which there are no solutions and creating logical pressure against beliefs one has good reasons not to abandon. In this course, we will explore various tactical moves Kant built into his system that can be used to finesse some of the toughest problems that trouble current discussions about the relation between brain and mind. We do not intend to promote Kantâs philosophy but to use it to illustrate the point that not all approaches to these issues can be effective regardless of the intensity of the convictions that support them. The logistics of the course (i.e., what you have to do to get a grade) will depend on the size of the class. The smaller the class the greater the flexibility in its use of discussions based on presentations. I prefer to leave as much room as possible for students to read and report on the latest and best material in those topics in which they have a special interest. Required texts: Critique of Pure Reason. Kant. St. Martinâs Press (978-0-230-0188-4 pbk.) [available in the Levi book store] The Character of Consciousness. David Chalmers Oxford, 2010 (978-0-19-531111-2 pbk.) [get it through Amazon]

title: Dante and the Christian Imagination
description: This course will examine the question of the meaning of human freedom on the basis of careful reading of major portions of Dante Alighieri's La Divina Commedia. The study will be an integrated approach that draws together elements from the disciplines of literature, psychology (especially the theories of Jung), philosophy, and theology. Based on an analysis of the text and discussion of selected critical literature, students will be asked to reflect on Dante's interpretation of the nature of freedom, how it functions in the formation of personal identity, and the role of imagination in the formation of culture and worldviews. In this context we will discuss the specifically Christian and medieval character of Dante's imagination as well as the problem of finding appropriate metaphors to situate these issues in the transformed historical context of contemporary life. The basic assumption of the course is that Commedia, while framed in terms of the fourteenth-century medieval culture, can speak vividly and directly to modern readers in terms of human experiences which are universal and fundamental, regardless of differences in time and place. Fall.

title: Honors Thesis Res Tutorial
description: Permission required.

title: Text Seminar: Platoâs Phaedrus
description: Platoâs Phaedrus is a literary and philosophical masterpiece. Ostensibly a set of speeches about love, the dialogue both explores and performs the question of how talk can make a difference in a life. Topics include rhetoric, philosophy, myth, writing, the structure of the soul, self-knowledge, madness and inspiration. Because the Phaedrus shows more than says its claims, the question of how to read the text appropriately will be constantly raised and reformulated as we read. Accordingly, we shall have to read the text more than once. We begin by getting a sense for Platoâs philosophy and the character of Socrates, reading both the Apology and Phaedo. After reading the Phaedrus through quickly and familiarizing ourselves with its main ideas and argumentative and rhetorical trajectory, we will read it again. We will work through the text slowly and carefully, and pay particular attention to how the dialogue performs its argument. In the final part of the course, we will discuss relevant secondary literature while preparing final research papers. Course requirements will likely include: a presentation, an annotated bibliography, a final paper proposal, and a final research paper. Texts: Plato, Phaedrus, tr. Alexander Nehamas and Paul Woodruff, Hackett. 0-87220-220-8 Plato, Five Dialogues, tr. G.M.A. Grube, revised John M. Cooper. Second Edition. Hackett. 0-87220-6

title: Text Seminar: Hume's Treatise
description: A course on Hume's "A Treatise of Human Nature"

title: Foundation of Education (CBL)

title: 19th Century German Philosophy
description: Hegel, Marx, and Nietzsche are the central figures of nineteenth century German philosophy. Hegel put forward the project of speculative science as pure, objective thought. Marx criticized Hegel for neglecting the real conditions of manâs empirical life, and conceived philosophy as socio-economic enquiry. Nietzsche, in turn, called into doubt the values of true and good as such, putting them into the perspective of will. By offering mainly primary source readings, the course will not only help to assess the three master thinkers, but also lead to a deeper understanding of todayâs philosophical challenges.

title: 20th Century European Philosophy
description: Over the course of the 20th Century, European philosophers became increasingly interested in the ways in which the categories in terms of which we make sense of our world are determined by history, society, power and technology. From this perspective, even concepts as fundamental as human nature, sexuality and presence are open to question. To understand how this can be, we need to understand how we get our sense of what there is and who and what we are â that is, we need to understand how truth happens. In this course, we will explore the happening of truth through close readings of a handful of major texts by Heidegger, Sartre, Beauvoir, Freud, Foucault and Derrida. Through this, students will gain familiarity with some of the core philosophical movements in 20th Century European thought (Phenomenology, Existentialism, Feminism, Psychoanalysis, Post-Structuralism, Deconstruction). The goals of the course are: to develop a solid competence with major texts and concepts that have profoundly influenced the tradition and which continue to inform it in the 21st Century; to appreciate the ways in which thinkers in this tradition take up, respond to and reorient one anotherâs ideas; to acquire conceptual tools for thinking about how things come to make sense in the ways that they do; and to engage in a radical questioning of concepts and categories that may seem to be beyond question.

title: Ethics & Economics
description: This course is an effort at interdisciplinary thinking about the possible conflicting demands of efficiency and justice. In a first section we will look at the history of economic thought. We will start by reading Adam SmithâS The Wealth of Nations looking for arguments for efficiency while not overlooking Smithâs concern for the common good. Then we will read some crucial publications by neo-liberal economists who defend the role of the government so as to keep the free market efficient (anti-trust laws, banking regulations, etc.). Next we will read a couple of chapters in Marxâs Capital illustrating great injustices in the capitalist system. In a second section we consult current economic theory. We will begin by summarizing the requirements for economic efficiency, as defined by the economic profession (welfare economics). We will then present the economic argument for market failure, particularly in the case of public goods. With the concept of merit good we encounter the explicit ethical dimension in economic thought. This concept will allow us to articulate the problems of property rights, anti-trust legislation, subsidies for education and social legislation. In a third section we will look at philosophical reflections upon economics as done by either philosophers or philosophically inclined economists (e.g., Mill, Hegel, Rawls, Sen, Baier, Buchanan, Friedman, Anderson, Richardson)(Note we will not be able to read all of them. We will at least mention them if we do not read them). The implementation of ethical principles takes place within the context of existing ethos pattern (Olson, Briefs). We will also study how religious ethics plays an important role in the implementation of social justice. Readings: Smith, Adam (1981) An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations. Liberty Classics edition. R. H. Campbell, A. S. Skinner and W. B. Todd, eds. Indianapolis: Liberty Fund. Ver Eecke, W. (Editor).(2007). An Anthology regarding Merit Goods. The Unfinished Ethical Revolution in Economic Theory [Compiled with Commentary by Wilfried Ver Eecke]. West Lafayette: Purdue University Press. (This book is available at very reduced price in Amazon.com). A number of articles by economists, philosophers, and social scientists will be put on reserve in the library or made available electronically. There will be three take home exams, blackboard discussion and a research paper. For undergraduates the research paper is expected to be 5 pages. For graduates the research paper is expected to be between 15 and 20 pages.

title: Symbolic Logic
description: A study of a formal system representing rules of good reasoning. The concepts of consequence, validity, derivability, and truth under an interpretation will be examined. This course is also part of the interdisciplinary curriculum in Cognitive Science. This course satisfies the major's requirement in logic. Fall.

title: History of Ancient/Medieval Philosophy
description: This course surveys some of the major themes of ancient Greek and Medieval Philosophy: Knowledge and Opinion, Being and Becoming, God and the First Causes, Cosmos, Soul and Immortality, Reason and Faith. The continuity between the two periods will be stressed. We will read works of Parmenides, Heraclitus, Plato, Aristotle, Plotinus, Proclus, Augustine, Boethius, Anselm, Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite, and Aquinas. Medieval Philosophy was born from the creative interaction of biblical faith with Greek philosophy. Until the thirteenth century, Neoplatonism (which attempted to unify the Platonic and Aristotelian traditions) provided the dominant philosophical framework for speculative theology. The most illustrious of the Neoplatonists, Plotinus (205-270 A.D.), profoundly influenced Augustine (354-430), especially in regard to the mind's approach to divine reality through interior recollection. Augustine himself dominated subsequent western theology, as can be seen in the writings of Anselm of Canterbury (1033-1109), until, in the late twelfth century, the translation and introduction of Aristotle into the Latin west, often via the Islamic philosophical tradition of the Arabs, provoked a philosophical revolution in regard to the prevailing Augustinianism. But the âChristian Aristotelianismâ of Thomas Aquinas (1225-1274), which marks a new stage in the development of medieval philosophy, was, in fact, a creative synthesis of the Augustinian-Neoplatonic tradition (especially as mediated through the Procline Neoplatonism of Pseudo-Dionysius) with Aristotle. Of special interest is Aquinasâs critique, in the name of Aristotle, of the Aristotelians (the so-called âLatin Averroistsâ) in the Arts Faculty at Paris. Course requirements consist of: (1) Four short papers (3â3Â½ type-written pages) due during the semester: 20 points each (= 80/170); (2) One group presentation: 30 points for each individual presentation in group performance (= 30/170); (3) One medium-sized essay (10 pages): 60 points (= 60/170). The students are expected to keep up with the reading and to be able to engage in active discussion of the assigned material for each class session.

title: History of Modern Philosophy

title: Phil of Religion
description: If my religion is right, is yours wrong? If God is so good, why is there so much evil in the world? Are there any good reasons to think there is life after death? Are miracles real or just made up? What is going on in religious experience as found throughout the world? Does God exist? Does contemporary science support or contradict religion? In this course, we will be looking at some of the classical and contemporary questions that philosophers have and still are debating about religion. We will be looking at some of what the best minds in the Western world have come up, but even more importantly we will be evaluating their reasons for holding the views that they do. On each question, we will examine alternative views, both pro and con. Everyone should take the time and make the effort once in their lives to look at what they believe about God, religion and the afterlife, since how one answers these questions can have a tremendous impact on how one approaches a whole host of other vital questions, such as the meaning of life, what it is the good life, is there an afterlife, and where, after all, should I put my talents, time and energy.

title: Philosophy of Biology
description: Perhaps the most important thing we have learned in the last 200 years is that we are natural creatures just like all the others on the planet and that there are no ``essences" that constitute the various species---no such thing as ``dogness" or ``catness" or even ``humanness". While this profound result was established by Darwin and Wallace in the 1850s, its full significance is still not entirely clear. Beyond the fact that, as Theodosius Dobzhansky puts it, ``nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution," lies the fact that it is still quite difficult to make sense of evolution. This course will be concerned principally with contemporary theories of evolution (with a little history), and we will focus on: the structure and confirmation of evolutionary theories; the implications of such theories for our self-conception as knowing subjects; controversies both within and without the theories. Of particular interest will be: Levels of selection debates. What the heck is natural selection anyway? How and where does it act? on species? groups? individuals? genes? So-called intelligent design theories. The connection between ``evo-devo" (the science of the organismal development) and natural selection. Are there other mechanisms beside natural selection responsible for evolution? Laws of biology. Are there any? Living systems seem so specialized and contingent that it's hard to see what a true law of nature could be that applies to biology. Laws are supposed to be universal and exceptionless. Is that really possible in biology? We will look at these issues principally from the perspective of philosophers. That is, we will be attempting on the one hand to clarify and evaluate the concepts involved in the theory and on the other hand to try our hands at adjudicating and understanding the continuing debates in and about the theory. Very little in the way of biological background will be required, but students should have completed at least their first two philosophy courses.

title: Philosophy of Language
description: A study of the nature of meaning and reference from a philosophical perspective, focusing on the relationships among meanings, referents, properties, idea, conventions, intentions, the uses of language and speech acts. Topics in reference, or how words refer (what Helen Keller finally realized) include names and descriptions, the distinction between sense and reference, the puzzle of non-referring terms, causal theories of reference. Topics in meaning include: the identification of meaning with truth conditions, the nature of propositions, theories of linguistic understanding, and the roles of mind and world in determining meaning, the distinction between meaning and implication, the roles of context and convention in language use, speaker meaning versus linguistic meaning and speech act theory. Requirements: one major oral presentation and two 7-page papers. Text: A.P. Martinich: The Philosophy of Language, 4th ed.

title: Political Philosophy
description: A study of central works in political philosophy. We will closely read works of Machiavelli, Hobbes, Locke, Mill, Marx. In addition, we will read some contemporary work that illustrates major themes of each. Weâll end the semester with a study of the work of John Rawls.

title: Global Justice/The Environment
description: The focus of this course is on global justice and the effect of what ecologists call the âbuilt environmentâ on human health and a sustainable human habitat. The built environment includes the various ways in which human beings fundamentally alter the natural environment for purposes of human habitation. For example, they clear forests, drain wetlands, build sprawling cities, alter the natural course of rivers and watersheds and the hydrologic cycle itself, construct and rely upon geographically extensive transportation systems, and produce food in places distant from the point of consumption. The social and economic organization of food, energy and water â the FEW problem â has enormous ongoing, chronic impact on human health (e.g., longevity, burden of disease), as well as potential for longer-term adverse impact on the planetary and local habitats necessary for sustaining human life itself. Philosophical controversies over the requirements global justice among peoples and nations are at the center of the course, but equally indispensible to the course is an introduction to economic policies of the World Bank and International Monetary Fund, the General Agreement on Trade and Tariff (GATT), international business investment practices, and some basic principles of environmental science, including climate science, nanotechnology, and hydrology. The course is open to both undergraduates and graduate students. The course requirements include class participation and periodic presentations, as well as a final project that links issues of global justice to some concrete environmental issue. All of the materials for the course, including documentary film, web resources, and academic and popular articles, are available digitally either through Blackboard or as e-reserves.

title: Topics in Metaphysics
description: This course will not be a broad survey of topics in metaphysics but will instead be a close examination of a number of closely related areas of fundamental concern to the metaphysician: existence, modality, and time. Topics to be considered include: What is existence? Is there is just a single notion of existence? Should we say that some things, although they do not exist, nonetheless in some sense are? The notion of ontological commitment. The analysis of modal notions in terms of possible worlds. Different theories of possible worlds. Possible individuals.. Objectivist versus subjectivist accounts of the flow of time. The reality of the past, present and future: presentist, growing-block and block theories of time. We will be primarily reading the 20th and 21st-century literature on these topics.

title: Hegel
description: Hegel is sometimes referred to as being the most influential philosopher in the last 200 years. We will read important parts of his Phenomenology of Spirit and of his Philosophy of Right. We will also show the deep connection between his Phenomenology and psychoanalysis and between his Philosophy of Right and philosophy of economics and of capitalism. We will make brief references to his Logic. We will read at least the following passages in the Phenomenology of Spirit : -The section on consciousness as an alternative to Kantâs theory of perceptual knowledge, and discuss Hegelâs presentation of a theory which denies that laws of natural science provide a complete comprehension of nature. -The section on self-consciousness, which includes the influential passages on the âmaster-slave dialecticâ, Stoicism, Skepticism, and Unhappy Consciousness. We will briefly indicate how the passage of the âmaster-slaveâ deeply influenced such divergent thinkers as Marx and Lacan. The passage on the Unhappy Consciousness presents religious practice, from Judaism to Protestantism, as a pragmatic solution for the performative inconsistencies of Skepticism. -The passage on âPhrenology,â which can be read as a Hegelian criticism of a line of thinking which attempts to give being to the mind by looking to matter (e.g., the skull). It stands as a criticism of certain positions on the mind-body problem such as the belief that mental illnesses are simply diseases of the brain. We will see how Hegelâs ideas can be used or not for a philosophical criticism of the official classification of mental illnesses in the DSM-IV and the projected DSM-V. -The passage on the âLaw of the Heartâ as a critical reading of âRomantic revolutionary movements.â Here, Hegel develops a concept of law that is necessarily at odds with the wishes of individuals. -The passage on âThe Legal Statusâ as a dialectical analysis of the fate of the Roman Empire. As the political structure and the Spirit of the U.S. is in many ways similar to that of Rome, this passage may be read as a warning for the vulnerable trends in a system relying very much upon the rule of law. -The passage on the âFrench Revolutionâ as a fundamental objection to libertarianism and anarchism (including Rousseau). -The passage on âMoralityâ as a criticism of Kantian moral theory. It defends a morality of desire as opposed to a morality of duty. In the U.S., Hawthorne made a similar transition to the appreciation of a morality of desire in âThe Scarlet Letterâ several decades later. Hegel also sees the connection between moral intention and language and locates the highest moral act in the linguistic act of asking and giving forgiveness. We will pay attention to the fact that Hegel makes desire an essential part of his conception of the person . This has been used by the great Hegel scholar Hyppolite to show the overlap in conception of the human person in Hegel and psychoanalysis. On the other hand, both Lacan and Fairbairn made use of Hegel to re-conceptualize Freudâs insights. We will also read Hegelâs Philosophy of Right. We will study his analysis of the will, his analysis of the function of property for human freedom, his argument for the superiority of morality to the freedom created by property rights and his argument that morality cannot guarantee a peaceful society . We will then analyze his conception of the three ethical institutions: the family, the economic order and the political order. Hegel is one of the first philosophers to have given a place to a reflection on capitalism in his systematic philosophy. Given his criticism of Rousseau, Hegel looked to the British economists to find a domain in society where more individual freedom could be tolerated without producing what Hegel called the disastrous consequences of Rousseauâs political ideas. Hegel found the free market to be such a domain. Notwithstanding his criticism of the free market one can argue that for Hegel the (supervised and corrected) free market combined with constitutional government is the realization of the good society. We will make use of the contemporary economic concepts of public good and merit good to show the relevance of Hegelâs abstract insights in the economic order of his time: emerging capitalism.

title: Medical Ethics
description: Theories of Medical Ethics is a course combining lecture presentation and class discussion. The objective of the course is to explore various cultural, religious, social, and professional traditions that contain more or less systematic positions on ethical issues in medicine. While some of these traditions are richly developed and have medical ethical systems that may be justifiably called "theories of medical ethics," others have only rudimentary positions in the area. The course will explore the mainstream of Western medical ethics as articulated by physicians and physicians' professional groups as well as a wide range of other secular and religious traditions including: Judaism Roman Catholicism Protestantism Liberal Western political philosophy (including contract theory and the Patients' Rights Movement) Socialist and Post-Soviet perspectives Islam Chinese thought Hinduism Japanese medical ethics Professional ethical traditions in health care (nursing, social work, pharmacy, dentistry, etc.) Each student in Phil 481 will be responsible for all the reading in the course and will be expected to prepare two written statements. The first will be a detailed comparison of the metaethical theories of at least four major writers or traditions examined in class. This paper will be due no later than the Tuesday before spring break. The second written statement will be a final examination, which will be a take-home exam. The final exam may be written whenever and wherever the student chooses, but must be submitted on the day the final exam is scheduled or before. It will consist of three essay questions and considerable choice will be permitted.
